title: Employment Rights Act 1996
desc: The principal statute governing individual employment rights in Great Britain. Consolidates earlier employment protection legislation and covers employment particulars, protection of wages, time off rights, maternity rights, notice, unfair dismissal, and redundancy. Extensively amended since enactment including by ERA 2025.

sections.title: Statement of initial employment particulars
sections.summary: Requires every employer to provide a written statement of employment particulars to every employee and worker on or before the first day of employment. The statement must contain prescribed information about the employment relationship including: names, start date, job title, pay, hours, holiday, sick pay, pensions, notice, disciplinary/grievance procedures, and (since April 2020) working pattern, probationary period, benefits, and training obligations. This is a day-one right for WORKERS as well as employees (since 6 April 2020). The statement is not itself the employment contract — it is evidence of the terms agreed. Failure to provide a compliant statement triggers a mandatory financial penalty (2–4 weeks' pay) where the employee succeeds in any other tribunal claim (Employment Act 2002 s.38).
sections.text:
(1) Where a worker begins employment with an employer, the employer shall give to the worker a written statement of particulars of employment.

(2) The statement may (subject to section 2(4)) be given in instalments and (whether or not given in instalments) shall be given not later than the beginning of the employment.

(3) The statement shall contain particulars of—

(a) the names of the employer and worker,

(b) the date when the employment began, and

(c) in the case of a statement given to an employee, the date on which the employee's period of continuous employment began (taking into account any employment with a previous employer which counts towards that period).

(4) The statement shall also contain particulars, as at a specified date not more than seven days before the statement is given, of—

(a) the scale or rate of remuneration or the method of calculating remuneration,

(b) the intervals at which remuneration is paid,

(c) any terms and conditions relating to hours of work including any terms and conditions relating to normal working hours, the days of the week the worker is required to work, whether or not such hours or days may be variable, and if they may be, how they vary or how that variation is to be determined,

(d) any terms and conditions relating to any of the following: entitlement to holidays (including public holidays) and holiday pay, incapacity for work due to sickness or injury (including any provision for sick pay), pensions and pension schemes,

(da) any other paid leave to which the worker is entitled or, in the case of an employee, any other paid leave to which the employee is entitled,

(e) the length of notice which the worker is obliged to give and entitled to receive to terminate the contract of employment,

(f) the title of the job which the worker is employed to do or a brief description of the work for which the worker is employed,

(g) where the employment is not intended to be permanent, the period for which it is expected to continue or, if it is for a fixed term, the date when it is to end,

(h) either the place of work or, where the worker is required or permitted to work at various places, an indication of that and of the address of the employer,

(j) any terms and conditions relating to the worker working outside the United Kingdom for a period of more than one month,

(k) any collective agreements which directly affect the terms and conditions of the employment,

(l) where the worker is required to work outside the United Kingdom for a period of more than one month, the period for which they are to work outside the United Kingdom, the currency in which they are to be paid, any additional remuneration or benefits, and any terms relating to their return to the United Kingdom,

(m) any training entitlement provided by the employer,

(n) any part of any training entitlement which the employer requires the worker to complete, and any part of that training entitlement which the employer will not bear the cost of,

(o) any probationary period, including any conditions and its duration,

(p) any benefits provided by the employer that do not fall within another paragraph of this subsection.

(5) Subsection (4)(d)(ii) does not apply to a worker who is not an employee.

(6) The particulars required by subsections (3) and (4) (other than those falling within subsection (4)(d)(i) to (iii), (da), (m), (n) and (p)) shall be included in a single document.
sections.keyPrinciple: The s.1 statement is the foundation of documentation compliance. It is NOT the employment contract — it is evidence of the contract's terms. The contract itself may exist orally, by conduct, or in writing. However, in tribunal proceedings, the s.1 statement is treated as strong evidence of what was agreed. Inconsistencies between the statement and the employer's actual practice undermine credibility. The statement must be provided on or BEFORE the first day of employment — not within two months as was the case before April 2020. Failure to provide a compliant statement does not create a standalone claim, but triggers a mandatory 2–4 weeks' pay penalty (EA 2002 s.38) whenever the employee succeeds in any other tribunal claim.

mandatoryParticulars.note: Section 1(3)–(4) lists the mandatory particulars. Since April 2020, the list has been significantly expanded. The particulars in s.1(6) must be included in a SINGLE document — they cannot be spread across multiple documents or referred to in a handbook.

items: Date continuous employment began (employees only — s.1(3)(c))
Scale or rate of remuneration, or calculation method (s.1(4)(a))
Hours of work — including working days, whether variable, and how variation is determined (s.1(4)(c)) — EXPANDED April 2020
Job title or brief description of work (s.1(4)(f))
Expected duration or end date if not permanent (s.1(4)(g))
Probationary period — conditions and duration (s.1(4)(o)) — NEW April 2020
Benefits not covered elsewhere (s.1(4)(p)) — NEW April 2020
Training entitlement and employer-required training (s.1(4)(m), (n)) — NEW April 2020

penalty.mechanism: Where an employee succeeds in a tribunal claim listed in Schedule 5 of the EA 2002 (which includes virtually all employment tribunal claims — unfair dismissal, discrimination, wages deductions, whistleblowing, etc.), and the tribunal finds that the employer was in breach of s.1 at the time proceedings were begun, the tribunal MUST award a minimum of 2 weeks' pay and MAY award up to 4 weeks' pay. The award is MANDATORY (minimum 2 weeks) if non-compliance is found — the tribunal has no discretion to award less.
penalty.practicalNote: The s.38 penalty means that EVERY tribunal claim carries an additional financial risk for employers who have not provided compliant s.1 statements. An employer defending an unfair dismissal claim where they failed to provide a s.1 statement faces the compensatory award PLUS 2–4 weeks' pay on top. The penalty is modest in isolation but its automatic application across all claim types creates cumulative exposure.

versions.text: [Original version — employees only. Must be provided within 2 months of start. Limited list of mandatory particulars. No requirement for probationary period, benefits, working pattern detail, or training.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating EPCA 1978 s.1)
versions.policyRationale: The 1996 Act consolidated the requirement from EPCA 1978. The obligation applied to employees only (not workers), with a 2-month window for provision. The list of mandatory particulars was narrower than the current version.

versions.text: [Current version — employees AND workers. Must be provided on or before the FIRST DAY of employment. Expanded list including: working pattern (days, variability), probationary period, benefits, training, other paid leave.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights (Employment Particulars and Paid Annual Leave) (Amendment) Regulations 2018 (SI 2018/1378)
versions.policyRationale: The 2018 Regulations (in force 6 April 2020) made three transformative changes: (1) extended the obligation to workers as well as employees; (2) made provision a day-one obligation (not within 2 months); (3) added new mandatory particulars including working pattern detail, probationary period, benefits, training, and other paid leave. The changes were part of the Good Work Plan response to the Taylor Review of Modern Working Practices (2017).

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025's zero-hours and low-hours contract provisions (new ss.27BA–27BT) will require additional information in the s.1 statement for workers on such contracts — including details of the guaranteed hours offer mechanism, shift notification requirements, and compensation for cancelled shifts. The exact amendments to s.1 will be specified in regulations.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Zero-hours provisions not yet in force as at March 2026
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — employers using zero-hours or low-hours contracts will need to update their s.1 statements to include the new mandatory information when the ERA 2025 provisions are commenced.

ACAS guidance on written statements

leadingCases.name: Mears v Safecar Security Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The s.1 statement is not itself the employment contract. It is merely evidence of the terms of the contract. Where the statement is incomplete or inaccurate, the tribunal must look at all the evidence — including the statement, oral agreements, custom and practice, and the conduct of the parties — to determine the actual contractual terms.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the written statement of particulars is not a contractual document — it is a statement of evidence of the terms that have been agreed. Where the statement conflicts with what was actually agreed (orally or by conduct), the actual agreement prevails over the written statement.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on the legal status of the s.1 statement. Establishes that the statement is evidential, not contractual. This distinction matters because: (1) an employer who fails to provide a s.1 statement has not failed to provide a contract — the contract exists by virtue of the agreement, however made; (2) the statement can be challenged if it does not accurately reflect what was agreed; (3) the tribunal may look behind the statement to determine the true terms.

leadingCases.name: Cawley v South Wales Electricity Board
leadingCases.principle: Where the employer provides a s.1 statement and the employee does not object to its contents within a reasonable time, the statement is strong evidence that the employee accepted the terms as stated — but it is not conclusive. The employee may still challenge the accuracy of the statement.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that a s.1 statement that has been signed or acknowledged by the employee without objection carries significant evidential weight. However, it remains possible for the employee to show that the statement does not accurately reflect what was agreed.
leadingCases.significance: Practical guidance on the evidential weight of the s.1 statement. Employers should ensure employees sign or acknowledge the statement — this strengthens its evidential value. Employees who receive an inaccurate statement should object promptly.

leadingCases.name: Eagland v British Telecommunications plc
leadingCases.principle: Terms set out in a collective agreement may be incorporated into the individual employment contract through the s.1 statement. Where the statement refers to a collective agreement for certain terms (e.g., pay scales, hours), those terms become part of the individual contract.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the s.1 statement may incorporate terms from collective agreements by reference. The incorporated terms become contractually binding on the individual employee. However, not all terms of a collective agreement are apt for incorporation — only terms that are suitable for individual application (such as pay, hours, holidays) rather than collective or procedural terms.
leadingCases.significance: Important for employers whose s.1 statements cross-reference collective agreements, staff handbooks, or policies. The cross-referenced terms may become contractually binding — the employer must consider carefully which external documents are incorporated by reference and which are merely informational.

leadingCases.name: Southern Cross Healthcare Co Ltd v Perkins
leadingCases.principle: The employment tribunal has jurisdiction under s.11 to determine what particulars SHOULD have been included in the s.1 statement — but it does not have jurisdiction to interpret disputed contractual terms. Where the core question is what the contract means (rather than what the statement should contain), the claim is properly a breach of contract matter, not a s.11 reference.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the tribunal's s.11 jurisdiction is limited to determining what the s.1 statement should have contained. It does not extend to resolving disputed questions of contractual interpretation. Where the employer and employee disagree about what a contractual term means, the tribunal cannot resolve that dispute under s.11.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the boundary of the s.11 jurisdiction. Important because employees sometimes bring s.11 references as a tactical vehicle to resolve contractual disputes during employment (since breach of contract claims in the tribunal can only be brought after termination). Southern Cross prevents this — the s.11 reference is limited to determining what the statement should contain, not what the contract means.

leadingCases.name: Stefanko v Maritime Hotel Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The EA 2002 s.38 penalty (2–4 weeks' pay) is MANDATORY where the employer has failed to provide a compliant s.1 statement and the employee succeeds in any qualifying tribunal claim. The tribunal must award a minimum of 2 weeks' pay — it has no discretion to award less or to decline to make the award.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the s.38 penalty is automatic and mandatory once the two conditions are met: (1) the employee succeeds in a claim listed in EA 2002 Sch.5; and (2) the employer was in breach of s.1 at the time the proceedings were begun. The tribunal must award at least 2 weeks' pay. The discretion relates only to whether to increase the award to 4 weeks.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the automatic nature of the s.38 penalty. Employers who fail to provide compliant s.1 statements face an additional financial penalty on top of any other award in EVERY tribunal claim they lose. This makes s.1 compliance a baseline risk management measure.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Written statement of employment particulars
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on what must be included in the s.1 statement, the distinction between the principal statement and supplementary information, and the consequences of non-compliance.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment contracts and conditions
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the employer's obligation to provide a written statement, the required content, and the difference between the statement and the employment contract.

commonErrors: Not providing the statement on or before the first day of employment — since 6 April 2020, the s.1 statement is a DAY-ONE obligation. It must be provided on or before the date employment begins, not within two months. Employers who still operate on a two-month window are applying the pre-2020 law

Not providing the statement to WORKERS as well as employees — since 6 April 2020, the obligation extends to workers (s.230(3)), not just employees. Employers who engage casual workers, zero-hours workers, or other limb (b) workers must provide them with a s.1 statement from day one

Treating the s.1 statement as the employment contract — Mears v Safecar Security [1983] establishes that the statement is EVIDENCE of the terms, not the contract itself. The contract may exist orally or by conduct. However, the statement carries strong evidential weight, particularly if signed by the employee. Inconsistencies between the statement and actual practice undermine the employer's credibility in tribunal

Not including the expanded particulars added in April 2020 — many employers' template statements still omit: working pattern and days (s.1(4)(c)), probationary period (s.1(4)(o)), benefits (s.1(4)(p)), training obligations (s.1(4)(m)–(n)), and other paid leave (s.1(4)(da)). These are now MANDATORY

Not providing the principal particulars in a SINGLE document — s.1(6) requires that the principal particulars (listed in the statute) be included in a single document. Spreading them across multiple documents, or referring to a handbook for core terms, does not comply with the single-document requirement for the principal statement

Overlooking the EA 2002 s.38 penalty — where an employee succeeds in ANY qualifying tribunal claim and the employer was in breach of s.1, the tribunal MUST award a minimum of 2 weeks' pay (and may award 4 weeks). This penalty is automatic and mandatory (Stefanko [2018]). Every non-compliant s.1 statement adds 2–4 weeks' pay to the employer's exposure in ANY tribunal claim

Not updating the statement when terms change — s.4 requires the employer to provide a written statement of any changes to the particulars within ONE MONTH of the change. Employers who change pay, hours, location, or other terms without updating the s.1 statement are in ongoing breach

Including disciplinary and grievance procedures in the principal statement when they should be in a separate document — while disciplinary and grievance information must be provided (s.3), it may be given in a reasonably accessible separate document and may be provided within 2 months (not day one). However, many employers unnecessarily include this in the principal statement, which can create contractual incorporation risks if the procedures are then treated as contractual terms (Eagland v BT [1993])

Not recognising that the s.1 statement interacts with zero-hours and low-hours contract reforms — ERA 2025's new provisions on guaranteed hours, shift notification, and shift cancellation compensation will require additional information in the s.1 statement for affected workers. Employers should monitor the commencement of these provisions and prepare updated templates

Assuming that providing a contract of employment satisfies the s.1 obligation automatically — a contract of employment MAY satisfy the s.1 obligation if it contains ALL the mandatory particulars. But many contracts omit some of the expanded 2020 requirements (probationary period, benefits list, training, working pattern variability). Employers should audit their contracts against the full s.1(3)–(4) list

Not providing continuous employment information for employees — s.1(3)(c) requires the statement to include the date the employee's period of continuous employment began, taking into account any employment with a previous employer that counts towards that period. This is relevant for TUPE transfers, associated employers, and other continuity-preserving situations. Workers (who are not employees) are not entitled to this particular

sections.title: Note about disciplinary procedures and pensions
sections.summary: The written statement must include a note about disciplinary rules, grievance procedures, and pensions.
sections.text:
(1) The statement required by section 1 shall include a note—
(a) specifying any disciplinary rules applicable to the employee, or referring the employee to the provisions of a document specifying such rules which is reasonably accessible to the employee,
(b) specifying, by description or otherwise—
(i) a person to whom the employee can apply if dissatisfied with any disciplinary decision relating to him, and
(ii) a person to whom the employee can apply for the purpose of seeking redress of any grievance relating to his employment, and the manner in which any such application should be made,
(c) where there are further steps consequent on any such application, explaining those steps or referring to the provisions of a document explaining them which is reasonably accessible to the employee.
refs: ACAS Code on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures 2015
s.1 ERA 1996

sections.title: Statement of changes
sections.summary: Employer must give written statement of any change in particulars within one month of the change.
sections.text:
(1) If, after the material date, there is a change in any of the matters particulars of which are required by sections 1 to 3 to be included or referred to in a statement under section 1, the employer shall give to the employee a written statement containing particulars of the change.

(2) The statement under subsection (1) shall be given at the earliest opportunity and, in any event, not later than one month after the change.
refs: s.1 ERA 1996
TUPE Regulations 2006 (Changes on Transfer)

sections.title: References to employment tribunals
sections.summary: Employee may refer to employment tribunal if employer fails to provide statement or statement is inaccurate. Tribunal may award 2–4 weeks' pay.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employer does not give an employee a statement as required by section 1, 4 or 8, within the time required by the section in question, the employee may require a reference to be made to an employment tribunal to determine what particulars ought to have been included in a statement so as to comply with the requirements of the section in question.

(4) Where on a reference under this section an employment tribunal finds—
(a) that the employer has failed to give the employee any statement required by this Part, or
(b) that a statement given does not comply with the requirements of this Part,
the tribunal shall make a declaration as to what the particulars should be.
refs: Employment Tribunals Act 1996
s.38 Employment Act 2002 (Tribunal Award)

sections.title: Right not to suffer unauthorised deductions
sections.summary: Prohibits employers from making deductions from a worker's wages unless the deduction is: (a) required or authorised by statute (e.g., income tax, NI, student loans, attachment of earnings); (b) required or authorised by a relevant provision of the worker's contract (provided the worker has been given a copy of the provision or had it explained before the deduction); or (c) the worker has previously signified in writing their agreement to the deduction. A failure to pay the whole or any part of wages that are 'properly payable' is treated as a deduction (s.13(3)). This is a WORKER right — it applies to employees and workers, not just employees. There is no qualifying period. There is no cap on the amount that can be recovered. One of the three most frequently litigated provisions in the employment tribunal system.
sections.text:
(1) An employer shall not make a deduction from wages of a worker employed by him unless—

(a) the deduction is required or authorised to be made by virtue of a statutory provision or a relevant provision of the worker's contract, or

(b) the worker has previously signified in writing his agreement or consent to the making of the deduction.

(2) In this section "relevant provision", in relation to a worker's contract, means a provision of the contract comprised—

(a) in one or more written terms of the contract of which the employer has given the worker a copy on an occasion prior to the employer making the deduction in question, or

(b) in one or more terms of the contract (whether express or implied and, if express, whether oral or in writing) the existence and effect, or combined effect, of which in relation to the worker the employer has notified to the worker in writing on such an occasion.

(3) Where the total amount of wages paid on any occasion by an employer to a worker employed by him is less than the total amount of the wages properly payable by him to the worker on that occasion (after deductions), the amount of the deficiency shall be treated for the purposes of this Part as a deduction made by the employer from the worker's wages on that occasion.

(4) Subsection (3) does not apply in so far as the deficiency is attributable to an error of any description on the part of the employer affecting the computation by him of the gross amount of the wages properly payable by him to the worker on that occasion.

(5) For the purposes of this section a relevant provision of a worker's contract having effect by virtue of a variation of the contract does not operate to authorise the making of a deduction on account of any conduct of the worker, or any other event occurring, before the variation took effect.

(6) For the purposes of this section an agreement or consent signified by a worker does not operate to authorise the making of a deduction on account of any conduct of the worker, or any other event occurring, before the agreement or consent was signified.

(7) This section does not affect any right of the employer under section 69(2) to recover an amount from the worker.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 13 is deceptively simple in its wording but generates a very high volume of tribunal claims because the scope of 'wages' is broad (defined in s.27 to include salary, statutory payments, holiday pay, bonuses, commission, and other payments connected with employment), the threshold for establishing a 'deduction' is low (any shortfall between what was properly payable and what was actually paid — s.13(3)), and the right applies to all workers from day one with no cap on recovery. The critical question in most cases is whether the sum was 'properly payable' — if the employer disputes the amount owed, the tribunal may need to determine the contractual entitlement before it can assess whether a deduction occurred.

included: Any fee, bonus, commission, holiday pay, or other emolument referable to the employment
Statutory maternity pay (SMP), statutory paternity pay, statutory adoption pay, statutory shared parental pay
Payments on suspension on medical grounds (s.64)
Payments ordered by the tribunal under s.128 (interim relief)
excluded: Benefits in kind (unless their value is treated as wages by the contract)
wagesDefinition.note: The definition is broad. The key question is whether the payment is 'referable to the employment' — i.e., connected with the work the individual does. Holiday pay is wages. Commission is wages. A discretionary bonus may or may not be wages — it depends on whether the employer has a contractual obligation to pay it. If the bonus is truly discretionary (no contractual entitlement), non-payment is not a deduction. If the bonus is contractually required or has been consistently paid such that a term can be implied, it is wages.

lawfulDeductions.note: Only three categories of deduction are lawful under s.13(1). If a deduction does not fall within one of these categories, it is unlawful and the worker can claim the amount as an unauthorised deduction.

statutory.description: Deductions required or authorised by statute — income tax (PAYE), national insurance contributions, student loan repayments, attachment of earnings orders, child support deductions, and any other deduction required by law.
statutory.practicalNote: Statutory deductions are the employer's obligation — the employer is acting as agent for HMRC or the relevant authority. The worker's remedy for any dispute about the amount of a statutory deduction is against the authority, not the employer.

contractual.description: Deductions required or authorised by a relevant provision of the worker's contract. The provision must be comprised in written terms that were given to the worker BEFORE the deduction (s.13(2)(a)), or in terms whose existence and effect were notified to the worker in writing before the deduction (s.13(2)(b)).
contractual.practicalNote: The contractual authorisation must be in place BEFORE the deduction. A retrospective contractual variation cannot authorise a deduction for conduct or events that occurred before the variation (s.13(5)). This means the employer cannot insert a deduction clause after the event giving rise to the deduction.

consent.description: The worker has previously signified in writing their agreement or consent to the deduction. The consent must be given BEFORE the deduction is made.
consent.practicalNote: Written consent must be genuine and informed. Consent obtained under duress or without the worker understanding what they are consenting to may be challenged. Like contractual authorisation, consent cannot operate retrospectively — it cannot authorise a deduction for events that occurred before the consent was given (s.13(6)).

seriesOfDeductions.principle: Where a worker has suffered a series of deductions, the tribunal may consider the entire series — not just the most recent deduction — provided the claim is presented within three months of the last deduction in the series (s.23(2)). However, the tribunal cannot consider deductions where the payment date was more than two years before the date the claim was presented (s.23(4A) — the 'two-year backstop').
seriesOfDeductions.seriesTest: The Supreme Court in Chief Constable of PSNI v Agnew [2023] UKSC 33 (a Northern Ireland case on holiday pay, applied in GB) held that deductions in a series need not be of the same legal character — they need only form a factual series. A gap of three months or more between deductions does not necessarily break the series if there is a sufficient factual connection.
seriesOfDeductions.twoYearBackstop: Section 23(4A), inserted by the Deduction from Wages (Limitation) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/3322), limits the tribunal's jurisdiction to deductions where the payment date was within the two years ending with the date the claim was presented. This prevents very old claims from being brought through the series mechanism.
seriesOfDeductions.recentDevelopment: In Deksne v Ambitions Ltd [2024] EAT 171, the EAT applied the Agnew approach and held that all holiday pay shortfalls within the two-year backstop period formed part of a series, confirming that the series concept applies broadly to repeated underpayments of the same type.

versions.text: [The substantive provisions of s.13(1)–(7) have not been materially amended since the 1996 Act was enacted. The section consolidated the protection originally introduced by the Wages Act 1986 s.1, which itself replaced the Truck Acts 1831–1940. The definition of 'wages' in s.27 has been amended to include additional statutory payments, but the core deductions protection in s.13 remains as originally enacted.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating Wages Act 1986 s.1)
versions.policyRationale: ERA 1996 consolidated the wages protection provisions from the Wages Act 1986. The Wages Act 1986 itself replaced the Truck Acts 1831–1940 (which prohibited payment of wages in 'truck' — goods rather than money — and required wages to be paid in full). The modern s.13 protection is broader: it covers all deductions, not just payment in kind, and applies to all workers, not just manual labourers.

historicalContext.truckActs: The Truck Acts 1831–1940 were the original wages protection legislation, passed to prevent employers from paying workers in goods or tokens redeemable only at the employer's shop ('truck'). The Acts required wages to be paid in the 'current coin of the realm' and prohibited unauthorised deductions. The Wages Act 1986 repealed the Truck Acts and replaced them with a more modern framework, which was subsequently consolidated into ERA 1996 Part II.
historicalContext.wagesAct1986: The Wages Act 1986 introduced the modern three-category framework for lawful deductions (statutory, contractual, consent) and extended protection to all workers. It also allowed wages to be paid by any method (not just cash), reflecting the shift to bank transfers. ERA 1996 ss.13–27 are a consolidation of the Wages Act 1986 provisions.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.13. However, the extension of the time limit for presenting employment tribunal claims from 3 months to 6 months (not yet commenced) will affect s.13 claims — workers will have longer to bring deduction claims. Additionally, the zero-hours and low-hours contract provisions in ERA 2025 will interact with s.13 where employers fail to pay guaranteed hours or fail to compensate for cancelled shifts.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Time limit extension not yet commenced as at March 2026
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the extended time limit will increase the window within which workers can bring deduction claims. The zero-hours provisions will create new categories of deduction claim where employers fail to comply with guaranteed hours obligations.

refs: ERA 1996 s.13(1) (the prohibition on unauthorised deductions)
ERA 1996 s.13(2) (definition of 'relevant provision' — contractual authorisation must precede the deduction)
ERA 1996 s.13(3) (shortfall in wages properly payable = deemed deduction)
ERA 1996 s.13(4) (computation error exception)
ERA 1996 s.13(5)–(6) (no retrospective authorisation)
ERA 1996 s.14 (excepted deductions — overpayment of wages, statutory payments, tribunal orders, industrial action)
ERA 1996 s.23 (complaints to employment tribunals — 3-month time limit, series of deductions)
ERA 1996 s.23(4A) (two-year backstop for series of deductions)
ERA 1996 s.27 (definition of 'wages' — broad definition including salary, holiday pay, commission, bonuses, statutory payments)
NMWA 1998 (national minimum wage — underpayment may also be a s.13 deduction)
WTR 1998 Reg.16 (holiday pay calculation — underpayment of holiday pay is a s.13 deduction)
EqA 2010 s.66 (equal pay — underpayment due to sex discrimination may be pursued as a s.13 claim)
Deduction from Wages (Limitation) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/3322) — inserted s.23(4A) two-year backstop
Delaney v Staples [1992] UKHL 2
Chief Constable of PSNI v Agnew [2023] UKSC 33

leadingCases.name: Delaney v Staples (t/a De Montfort Recruitment)
leadingCases.principle: A payment in lieu of notice (PILON) made by the employer on termination is not 'wages' within the meaning of Part II and therefore cannot be claimed as an unauthorised deduction under s.13. PILON is damages for breach of contract (failure to give notice), not wages for work done or to be done. However, if the contract expressly provides for a PILON as a contractual entitlement, it may qualify as wages.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that 'wages' under Part II means payments made to a worker in respect of their employment — payments for work done or to be done. A payment in lieu of notice, where no contractual PILON clause exists, is damages for wrongful dismissal, not wages. It falls outside Part II. However, Lord Browne-Wilkinson left open the possibility that a contractual PILON clause might bring the payment within the definition of wages.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational UKHL authority on the boundary of 'wages' under Part II. Establishes that not every payment connected with employment is 'wages' — the payment must be for work done or to be done. This distinction is critical for employers calculating final payments on termination: a PILON without a contractual clause is not recoverable as a deduction claim, but a shortfall in salary, holiday pay, or commission is.

leadingCases.name: New Century Cleaning Co Ltd v Church
leadingCases.principle: For a deduction claim to succeed, the worker must establish that the wages were 'properly payable' — i.e., that there was a contractual obligation to pay the amount claimed. The tribunal must determine what was contractually owed before assessing whether a deduction occurred.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the s.13(3) test requires the tribunal to determine the total amount of wages 'properly payable' on the relevant occasion. This requires the tribunal to ascertain the worker's contractual entitlement. Where the amount is disputed, the tribunal must determine the contractual position before it can find that a deduction has been made.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the 'properly payable' threshold. The tribunal cannot simply take the worker's claim at face value — it must determine whether the employer was contractually obliged to pay the amount claimed. This is the step that makes s.13 claims factually intensive — the tribunal often needs to interpret the employment contract, bonus scheme, commission structure, or overtime terms to determine what was owed.

leadingCases.name: Chief Constable of the Police Service of Northern Ireland v Agnew
leadingCases.principle: In determining whether deductions form a 'series', the deductions need not be of the same legal character — they need only form a factual series. A gap of three months or more between deductions does not automatically break the series if there is a sufficient factual connection. The series concept should be applied broadly and purposively.
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that holiday pay underpayments over a period of years constituted a series of deductions. The series was not broken by gaps between holiday periods. The concept of a 'series' should be interpreted broadly — the deductions need only share a common factual link (in this case, the systematic failure to include overtime in the holiday pay calculation).
leadingCases.significance: THE landmark UKSC authority on the 'series of deductions' concept. Although a Northern Ireland case, its principles apply in England, Wales, and Scotland. Agnew dramatically expanded the scope of series-of-deductions claims by removing the requirement that deductions be of the same legal character and by taking a broad approach to what constitutes a factual series. Combined with the two-year backstop (s.23(4A)), this means workers can claim up to two years of underpayments as a single series.

leadingCases.name: Deksne v Ambitions Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Applying the Agnew approach, all of the claimant's holiday pay shortfalls within the two-year backstop period formed part of a series of deductions. The EAT substituted its own findings for those of the tribunal, confirming that the series concept applies broadly to repeated underpayments of the same type.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the tribunal had erred in finding that certain holiday pay shortfalls did not form part of a series. Applying Agnew, the shortfalls were part of a factual series — they arose from the same systematic underpayment of holiday pay. All deductions within the two-year backstop period were within the tribunal's jurisdiction.
leadingCases.significance: The first significant EAT application of Agnew principles in an England and Wales case. Confirms that the broad Agnew approach to series of deductions applies in the GB jurisdiction and that systematic underpayments (particularly of holiday pay) will readily satisfy the series test.

leadingCases.name: Agarwal v Cardiff University and Cardiff and Vale University Local Health Board
leadingCases.principle: The employment tribunal does not have jurisdiction to hear a s.13 claim where determining whether a deduction was made requires the tribunal to construe the employment contract. Where the amount 'properly payable' depends on a disputed interpretation of the contract, the claim is properly a breach of contract claim, not a deductions claim.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT followed Southern Cross Healthcare v Perkins [2011] EWCA and held that the tribunal has no jurisdiction under s.13 where the dispute is essentially about the interpretation of the contract — for example, whether the worker was entitled to full pay during sickness absence. In such cases, the worker must bring a breach of contract claim (which can only be brought in the tribunal after termination of employment).
leadingCases.significance: Establishes an important jurisdictional boundary. Not every underpayment dispute can be pursued under s.13 — if the core question is what the contract means (rather than whether a known entitlement was paid), the claim may fall outside the tribunal's s.13 jurisdiction. This matters because s.13 claims can be brought during employment (unlike breach of contract claims, which require termination).

leadingCases.name: Mendy v Manchester City Football Club Ltd
leadingCases.principle: High-profile case in which the claimant brought a s.13 claim for £11.5 million in unpaid wages during a period of suspension. The club had stopped paying the claimant's wages while he was suspended pending criminal proceedings. The case demonstrates the breadth of s.13 — there is no cap on recovery, and the provision covers suspension pay where the contract requires it.
leadingCases.heldText: The tribunal considered whether the club was contractually obliged to pay the claimant during his suspension and whether the non-payment constituted an unauthorised deduction. The case highlighted the interaction between s.13 and the employer's contractual obligations during suspension.
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates the scale of potential s.13 claims — there is no upper limit on the amount recoverable. Also illustrates the interaction between s.13 and suspension: where the contract requires the employer to pay during suspension, non-payment is an unauthorised deduction regardless of the reason for the suspension.

leadingCases.name: Gill v Ford Motor Co Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The tribunal can award financial compensation for injury to feelings in whistleblowing detriment claims (s.47B), but s.13 claims are limited to the amount of the deduction — there is no power to award compensation for distress, inconvenience, or injury to feelings in a deductions claim.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the remedy for an unauthorised deduction is limited to repayment of the amount deducted. The tribunal has no power under s.24 to award interest, aggravated damages, or compensation for non-financial loss. The remedy is purely restitutionary — the worker gets back what was improperly taken.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the purely financial nature of the s.13 remedy. Unlike unfair dismissal or discrimination claims, there is no compensatory element beyond the amount of the deduction itself. This limits the strategic value of s.13 claims to straightforward recovery of underpaid amounts — but the absence of a cap means the amounts can be very large.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Deductions from pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on when employers can and cannot make deductions from wages, the requirement for prior written authorisation, and how to raise a complaint.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Deductions from your pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the types of deductions employers can make, including statutory deductions, and workers' rights to challenge unauthorised deductions.

commonErrors: Assuming that any shortfall in expected pay can be claimed as an unauthorised deduction — the sum must be 'properly payable' (s.13(3)). If the amount is genuinely disputed and depends on the interpretation of the contract, the tribunal may lack jurisdiction under s.13 (Agarwal [2017]). The claim may need to be brought as a breach of contract claim instead

Making deductions without prior written authorisation — both contractual and consent-based authorisations must be in place BEFORE the deduction is made. A retrospective contractual variation cannot authorise a deduction for events that occurred before the variation (s.13(5)). The employer cannot insert a deduction clause after the fact

Not recognising that a failure to pay the correct amount IS a deduction — s.13(3) deems any shortfall between the amount properly payable and the amount actually paid to be a deduction. This includes: underpayment of salary, failure to pay overtime, failure to pay holiday pay in full, failure to pay the correct commission, failure to pay a contractually required bonus

Assuming s.13 only applies to employees — s.13 applies to WORKERS, not just employees. This includes casual workers, agency workers who satisfy the s.230(3) worker definition, zero-hours workers, and gig economy workers who are found to be workers. The scope is broader than unfair dismissal

Not recognising that there is NO CAP on the amount recoverable — unlike the unfair dismissal compensatory award (capped at s.124), the s.13 remedy is the full amount of the deduction with no upper limit. The Mendy v Manchester City case involved a claim for £11.5 million. The only limitation is the two-year backstop for series of deductions (s.23(4A))

Treating a discretionary bonus as 'wages properly payable' without establishing the contractual entitlement — if a bonus is genuinely discretionary (no contractual obligation to pay), non-payment is not a deduction. But if the bonus is contractually required, or has been consistently paid such that a contractual term can be implied, it is wages and non-payment is a deduction

Not understanding the series of deductions concept — under s.23(3), a worker who has suffered a series of deductions can claim the entire series, provided the claim is presented within three months of the last deduction. Agnew [2023] UKSC confirmed that deductions in a series need not be of the same legal character — they need only form a factual series. The two-year backstop (s.23(4A)) limits the look-back period

Confusing s.13 with breach of contract — s.13 is a statutory claim that can be brought DURING employment. Breach of contract claims in the tribunal can only be brought AFTER termination (Employment Tribunals Extension of Jurisdiction Order 1994). This makes s.13 tactically valuable for workers who are still employed and want to challenge underpayment without resigning

Overlooking the s.14 exceptions — s.14 lists categories of deduction that are exempt from s.13, including: overpayment of wages or expenses (s.14(1)(a)), payments required by statute (s.14(1)(b)), deductions pursuant to industrial action (s.14(5)), and payments pursuant to tribunal orders. Employers can recover overpayments without s.13 authorisation, but must do so reasonably

Not recognising the interaction between s.13 and holiday pay claims — underpayment of holiday pay (whether calculated incorrectly under WTR 1998 Reg.16 or simply not paid) is an unauthorised deduction under s.13. The Agnew and Deksne cases confirm that systematic holiday pay underpayments form a series of deductions claimable up to two years back

Making deductions from final pay without contractual authority — deductions from wages owed on termination (e.g., for unreturned equipment, training costs, notice period shortfall) require the same prior written authorisation as any other deduction. The fact that the employment has ended does not create a right to make deductions — the employer must have contractual or written consent authorisation in place

sections.title: Excepted deductions
sections.summary: Section 13 does not apply to deductions to recover overpayments, or deductions required by statutory provision.
sections.text:
(1) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer—
(a) to recover an overpayment of wages, or
(b) to recover an overpayment in respect of expenses incurred by the worker in carrying out his employment.

(2) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer in consequence of any disciplinary proceedings if those proceedings were held by virtue of a statutory provision.

(3) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer in pursuance of a requirement imposed on the employer by a statutory provision to deduct and pay over to a public authority amounts determined by that authority as being due to it from the worker.
refs: s.13 ERA 1996 (Unauthorised Deductions)

sections.title: Complaints to employment tribunals
sections.summary: Worker may complain to employment tribunal within 3 months of deduction that employer has made an unauthorised deduction from wages.
sections.text:
(1) A worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that his employer has made a deduction from his wages in contravention of section 13.

(2) Subject to subsection (4), an employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this section unless it is presented before the end of the period of three months beginning with the date of payment of the wages from which the deduction was made.
refs: Employment Tribunals Act 1996 (Time Limits)

sections.title: Meaning of "protected disclosure"
sections.summary: A qualifying disclosure is protected where made by a worker in accordance with ss.43C–43H.
sections.text: In this Act "protected disclosure" means a qualifying disclosure (as defined by section 43B) which is made by a worker in accordance with any of sections 43C to 43H.
refs: s.43B ERA 1996 (Qualifying Disclosure)
s.103A ERA 1996 (Automatic Unfair Dismissal for Protected Disclosure)
PIDA 1998

sections.title: Disclosures qualifying for protection
sections.summary: Defines the six categories of 'qualifying disclosure' for whistleblowing protection under Part IVA. A qualifying disclosure is any disclosure of information which, in the reasonable belief of the worker, is made in the public interest AND tends to show one or more of: (a) a criminal offence; (b) breach of a legal obligation; (c) a miscarriage of justice; (d) danger to health and safety; (da) sexual harassment has occurred, is occurring, or is likely to occur (ERA 2025 s.23 — IN FORCE 6 APRIL 2026); (e) environmental damage; (f) deliberate concealment of information relating to any of the above. Whistleblowing protection is a day-one right — no qualifying period. Compensation for whistleblowing dismissal (s.103A) is UNCAPPED. This is the provision that determines whether a disclosure qualifies for protection — if it does not meet the s.43B test, the worker has no whistleblowing claim regardless of how they were treated.
sections.text:
(1) In this Part a "qualifying disclosure" means any disclosure of information which, in the reasonable belief of the worker making the disclosure, is made in the public interest and tends to show one or more of the following—

(a) that a criminal offence has been committed, is being committed or is likely to be committed,

(b) that a person has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to comply with any legal obligation to which he is subject,

(c) that a miscarriage of justice has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur,

(d) that the health or safety of any individual has been, is being or is likely to be endangered,

(da) that sexual harassment of an individual has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur,

(e) that the environment has been, is being or is likely to be damaged, or

(f) that information tending to show any matter falling within any one of the preceding paragraphs has been, is being or is likely to be deliberately concealed.

(2) For the purposes of subsection (1), it is immaterial whether the relevant failure occurred, occurs or would occur in the United Kingdom or elsewhere, and whether the law applying to it is that of the United Kingdom or of any other country or territory.

(3) A disclosure of information is not a qualifying disclosure if the person making the disclosure commits an offence by making it.

(4) A disclosure of information in respect of which a claim to legal professional privilege (or, in Scotland, to confidentiality as between client and professional legal adviser) could be maintained in legal proceedings is not a qualifying disclosure if it is made by a person to whom the information had been disclosed in the course of obtaining legal advice.

(5) In this Part "the relevant failure", in relation to a qualifying disclosure, means the matter falling within paragraphs (a) to (f) of subsection (1).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 43B is the definitional gateway for all whistleblowing claims. Every element must be satisfied: (1) there must be a 'disclosure' — the worker must communicate information; (2) the information must be 'information' with sufficient factual content, not a bare allegation without specificity (Kilraine [2018]); (3) the worker must have a 'reasonable belief' that the information tends to show one of the six categories — the belief need not be correct (Babula [2007]); (4) the worker must reasonably believe the disclosure is 'in the public interest' (inserted by ERRA 2013 — interpreted broadly in Chesterton [2017]). If any element fails, the disclosure does not qualify and there is no whistleblowing protection.

qualifyingCategories.note: Section 43B(1)(a)–(f) identifies six categories of relevant failure (seven from 6 April 2026, when s.43B(1)(da) is inserted). The categories are deliberately broad — they cover virtually any form of wrongdoing. Each category uses the past/present/future formulation: 'has been', 'is being', 'is likely to' — so disclosures about anticipated future failures are also protected.

criminalOffence.scope: Any criminal offence — theft, fraud, assault, regulatory offences, health and safety offences, tax evasion, bribery, modern slavery. No requirement that the offence has been prosecuted or that charges have been brought.

legalObligation.scope: Breach of ANY legal obligation — contractual, statutory, regulatory, common law. This is the broadest category. It covers breach of employment contract, breach of statutory duties (health and safety, equality, data protection), breach of regulatory obligations (FCA, PRA, CQC), and breach of common law duties. The legal obligation must be a LEGAL obligation — a moral or ethical obligation does not qualify (Sim v Manchester Action on Street Health [2003] EAT).
legalObligation.practicalNote: This is the most frequently relied-upon category. A worker who discloses that the employer is breaching a contractual term, a statutory regulation, or a common law duty is making a qualifying disclosure under s.43B(1)(b), provided the other elements (reasonable belief, public interest, information) are satisfied.

miscarriageOfJustice.scope: A miscarriage of justice has occurred, is occurring, or is likely to occur. Rarely used in employment tribunal proceedings but included for completeness.

healthAndSafety.scope: Danger to the health or safety of any individual — not limited to employees or to the workplace. Covers: unsafe working practices, defective equipment, inadequate training, exposure to hazardous substances, risk to members of the public. The health and safety concern does not need to be proven correct — the worker must reasonably believe that a danger exists.

sexualHarassment.scope: That sexual harassment of an individual has occurred, is occurring, or is likely to occur. INSERTED BY ERA 2025 s.23(2). IN FORCE 6 APRIL 2026. This is a new standalone category — previously, workers disclosing sexual harassment had to frame the disclosure under another category (typically legal obligation or health and safety). From 6 April 2026, sexual harassment is explicitly listed as a qualifying category in its own right.
sexualHarassment.practicalNote: This is live intelligence: from 6 April 2026, any worker who discloses information about sexual harassment (whether directed at themselves or at another individual) will be making a qualifying disclosure under s.43B(1)(da), provided they reasonably believe the disclosure is in the public interest. Workers who raise concerns about sexual harassment will be protected from detriment (s.47B) and dismissal (s.103A) — with UNCAPPED compensation. Employers must update whistleblowing policies to explicitly reference sexual harassment as a qualifying category before 6 April 2026.

environmentalDamage.scope: The environment has been, is being, or is likely to be damaged. Covers: pollution, waste disposal breaches, environmental regulatory non-compliance, damage to ecosystems.

deliberateConcealment.scope: Information tending to show any of the above matters has been, is being, or is likely to be deliberately concealed. This is a 'cover-up' category — it protects workers who disclose that evidence of wrongdoing is being hidden, destroyed, or suppressed.

publicInterestTest.insertedBy: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 (c.24) s.17
publicInterestTest.rationale: Before ERRA 2013, a worker could make a qualifying disclosure about a breach affecting only themselves (e.g., a personal contractual dispute). ERRA 2013 inserted the words 'is made in the public interest' to prevent purely personal grievances from qualifying as whistleblowing. However, the Court of Appeal in Chesterton [2017] interpreted 'public interest' broadly — a disclosure may be in the public interest even if the worker's predominant motive is personal, provided it has a sufficient element of wider public concern.
publicInterestTest.chestertonTest: The tribunal should consider four factors: (1) the numbers in the group whose interests the disclosure served; (2) the nature of the interests affected and the extent to which they are affected; (3) the nature of the wrongdoing disclosed; (4) the identity of the alleged wrongdoer. A disclosure about a matter affecting only one employee may still be in the public interest if the nature of the wrongdoing is sufficiently serious.
publicInterestTest.source: Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979

informationVsAllegation.principle: A qualifying disclosure must contain 'information' — factual content with sufficient specificity to be capable of tending to show one of the relevant failures. A bare allegation without factual content does not qualify. However, the distinction is not rigid — a statement that is both an allegation and a conveyance of information may qualify if it has sufficient factual content.
informationVsAllegation.kilraineTest: The key question is whether the disclosure has 'sufficient factual content and specificity such as is capable of tending to show' one of the relevant failures. It is not helpful to draw a rigid distinction between 'information' and 'allegation' — the question is always whether the factual content is sufficient.
informationVsAllegation.source: Kilraine v London Borough of Wandsworth [2018] EWCA Civ 1436; Cavendish Munro Professional Risks Management Ltd v Geduld [2010] ICR 325 (EAT)

versions.text:
(1) In this Part a "qualifying disclosure" means any disclosure of information which, in the reasonable belief of the worker making the disclosure, tends to show one or more of the following—

(a) that a criminal offence has been committed, is being committed or is likely to be committed,
(b) that a person has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to comply with any legal obligation to which he is subject,
(c) that a miscarriage of justice has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur,
(d) that the health or safety of any individual has been, is being or is likely to be endangered,
(e) that the environment has been, is being or is likely to be damaged, or
(f) that information tending to show any matter falling within any one of the preceding paragraphs has been, is being or is likely to be deliberately concealed.
versions.amendedBy: Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (c.23) s.1 — inserted Part IVA into ERA 1996
versions.policyRationale: PIDA 1998 created the whistleblowing framework by inserting Part IVA (ss.43A–43L) into ERA 1996. The original s.43B required only 'reasonable belief' — there was no public interest requirement. This meant a worker could make a qualifying disclosure about a purely personal matter (such as their own contractual dispute) and receive whistleblowing protection. The absence of a public interest filter was criticised as allowing the whistleblowing framework to be used as a vehicle for personal grievances.

versions.text: (1) In this Part a "qualifying disclosure" means any disclosure of information which, in the reasonable belief of the worker making the disclosure, is made in the public interest and tends to show one or more of the following— [categories (a)–(f) unchanged]
versions.amendedBy: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 (c.24) s.17
versions.policyRationale: ERRA 2013 inserted the words 'is made in the public interest' into s.43B(1). This was intended to prevent purely personal grievances from qualifying as whistleblowing disclosures. However, the Court of Appeal in Chesterton [2017] interpreted 'public interest' broadly, holding that even a disclosure motivated primarily by personal interest may be in the public interest if it has a sufficient element of wider concern.

versions.text: (1) In this Part a "qualifying disclosure" means any disclosure of information which, in the reasonable belief of the worker making the disclosure, is made in the public interest and tends to show one or more of the following— [categories (a)–(d) unchanged] (da) that sexual harassment of an individual has occurred, is occurring or is likely to occur, [(e)–(f) unchanged]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 2025 (c.36) s.23(2)
versions.policyRationale: ERA 2025 s.23 inserts s.43B(1)(da) — sexual harassment as a standalone qualifying category. Commencement date: 6 April 2026. The Government cited evidence that 26% of people who experience sexual harassment report that it happened at work, and half of affected workers choose not to report incidents. The explicit inclusion removes any ambiguity about whether sexual harassment disclosures qualify for whistleblowing protection.

pendingAmendment.effect: Inserts s.43B(1)(da) — sexual harassment as a qualifying disclosure category. IN FORCE 6 APRIL 2026. From that date, any worker who discloses information about sexual harassment (directed at themselves or any other individual) in circumstances where they reasonably believe the disclosure is in the public interest, is making a qualifying disclosure entitled to full whistleblowing protection.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: CONFIRMED — in force 6 April 2026
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: HIGH — employers must update whistleblowing policies before 6 April 2026 to include sexual harassment as an explicit qualifying category. Failure to do so creates a risk that workers who report sexual harassment are not identified as whistleblowers, leading to inadequate protection and potential detriment/dismissal claims with uncapped compensation.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 s.23 is one of the first ERA 2025 provisions to be commenced — it comes into force on 6 April 2026, one week from today. Employers must ensure their whistleblowing policies explicitly include sexual harassment as a qualifying category by that date. The practical significance extends beyond the policy update: from 6 April 2026, any worker who reports sexual harassment and is subsequently subjected to detriment or dismissal will have a whistleblowing claim with UNCAPPED compensation — in addition to any harassment or discrimination claim under EqA 2010.

ACAS guidance on whistleblowing
Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979
Kilraine v London Borough of Wandsworth [2018] EWCA Civ 1436

leadingCases.name: Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed
leadingCases.principle: The 'public interest' test inserted by ERRA 2013 should be interpreted broadly. A disclosure may be in the public interest even if the worker's predominant motive is personal, provided it has a sufficient element of wider public concern. The tribunal should consider four factors: the numbers affected, the nature of the interests, the nature of the wrongdoing, and the identity of the wrongdoer.
leadingCases.heldText: Underhill LJ held that whether a disclosure is in the public interest is a matter for the tribunal to assess, taking into account the worker's reasonable belief. A disclosure about how a company's managing agents calculated their commission — affecting over 100 managers — was held to be in the public interest, even though the claimant's primary motive was personal. The Court rejected the argument that the public interest test required the disclosure to affect the 'public at large'.
leadingCases.significance: THE leading authority on the ERRA 2013 public interest requirement. Chesterton established that the test is not restrictive — it requires only a reasonable belief that the disclosure is in the public interest, not that the public at large is affected. This significantly reduced the concern that ERRA 2013 had narrowed whistleblowing protection. The four-factor test provides a structured framework that all tribunals now apply.

leadingCases.name: Kilraine v London Borough of Wandsworth
leadingCases.principle: A qualifying disclosure must contain 'information' with sufficient factual content and specificity to be capable of tending to show one of the relevant failures. The distinction between 'information' and 'allegation' is not rigid — a statement that is both an allegation and a conveyance of information may qualify — but a bare allegation without factual content does not.
leadingCases.heldText: Sales LJ held: 'In order for a statement or disclosure to be a qualifying disclosure according to this language, it has to have a sufficient factual content and specificity such as is capable of tending to show one of the matters listed in sub-section 43B(1).' The Court clarified that the previous EAT guidance in Cavendish Munro — which drew a sharp distinction between 'information' and 'allegation' — was too rigid. The real question is whether the disclosure has sufficient factual content.
leadingCases.significance: Replaced the rigid Cavendish Munro distinction with a more flexible and purposive approach. The practical test is now: does the disclosure have sufficient factual content and specificity? A statement like 'you are breaching health and safety laws' — without specifying how — may fail the test. But a statement like 'the fire exits on the third floor are blocked by storage boxes, creating a fire risk' — which combines allegation and factual content — will qualify.

leadingCases.name: Babula v Waltham Forest College
leadingCases.principle: The worker's belief that the information tends to show a relevant failure need not be CORRECT. It must be REASONABLE. A worker who reasonably but wrongly believes that a criminal offence has been committed is still making a qualifying disclosure — the protection does not depend on the information being true.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the s.43B(1) test is whether the worker has a 'reasonable belief' — not whether the disclosed information is actually correct. The worker reasonably believed that racial discrimination amounted to a criminal offence (it does not, in the relevant circumstances). His belief was wrong, but it was reasonable. The disclosure qualified.
leadingCases.significance: A critical protection for whistleblowers. Without this principle, workers would be deterred from making disclosures for fear that their information might turn out to be incorrect. Babula ensures that good-faith, reasonable disclosures are protected even if the underlying facts prove to be wrong. The test is the reasonableness of the BELIEF, not the accuracy of the information.

leadingCases.name: Cavendish Munro Professional Risks Management Ltd v Geduld
leadingCases.principle: The original EAT guidance on the information/allegation distinction: 'the ordinary meaning of giving information is conveying facts'. A statement that the employer was acting in breach of contract, without specifying what the breach was or giving factual details, was held to be an allegation, not information.
leadingCases.heldText: Slade J held: 'The ordinary meaning of giving "information" is conveying facts. In the course of the hearing before us, a useful illustration was given in the form of a communication from a hypothetical hospital worker. The statement "the wards have not been cleaned for the past two weeks" conveys information. The statement "you are not complying with health and safety requirements" is an allegation.'
leadingCases.significance: Although the rigid Cavendish Munro distinction was softened by Kilraine [2018], the ward-cleaning illustration remains the most frequently cited example of the difference between information and allegation. The practical message survives: factual specificity matters. Workers who disclose specific facts are better protected than those who make general complaints.

leadingCases.name: Darnton v University of Surrey
leadingCases.principle: The 'reasonable belief' in s.43B(1) is a SUBJECTIVE test with an objective element. The tribunal must first consider what the worker actually believed, and then assess whether that belief was objectively reasonable. The worker's personal characteristics, knowledge, and experience are relevant to the reasonableness assessment.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the tribunal must: (1) identify what the worker believed; (2) assess whether, given the worker's knowledge and experience, that belief was reasonable. The test is not whether a reasonable person in the abstract would have held the belief, but whether it was reasonable for THIS worker to hold it.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the calibration of the reasonableness test. A junior worker with limited legal knowledge may reasonably believe that conduct amounts to a criminal offence when a lawyer would not. Conversely, a compliance officer with specialist knowledge may be held to a higher standard of what is 'reasonable' to believe. This prevents employers from defeating whistleblowing claims by arguing that a legally sophisticated person would not have held the same belief.

leadingCases.name: Ibrahim v HCA International Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The 'reasonable belief' and 'public interest' requirements are separate elements that must both be satisfied. The tribunal must assess: (1) did the worker subjectively believe the disclosure was in the public interest? (2) was that subjective belief objectively reasonable? A disclosure motivated entirely by personal interest may fail the public interest test even if the underlying information relates to a matter of genuine public concern.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the tribunal must make separate findings on (a) the worker's subjective belief that the disclosure was in the public interest, and (b) the objective reasonableness of that belief. Both elements are required. The reasonable belief test is not demanding — but it must be addressed.
leadingCases.significance: Clarifies the analytical structure of the public interest test after Chesterton. The tribunal must address both the subjective and objective elements separately. In practice, this means that a worker who genuinely believes their disclosure serves the public interest, and whose belief is objectively reasonable, will satisfy the test — even if the worker also has a personal motive.

leadingCases.name: Bolton School v Evans
leadingCases.principle: The manner in which a disclosure is made does not affect whether it is a qualifying disclosure — but it may be relevant to the reasonableness of the employer's response. A worker who hacks into an employer's computer system to obtain evidence of a security flaw has made a qualifying disclosure, but the employer may fairly dismiss them for the method used to obtain the information, provided the dismissal is not ON THE GROUND of the disclosure itself.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that a teacher who hacked into the school's computer system to demonstrate that it was insecure had made a qualifying disclosure about a danger to the safety of children's data. However, the school was entitled to discipline and dismiss him for the method (the hacking), provided the reason for dismissal was the hacking, not the disclosure.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the critical distinction between the CONTENT of a disclosure (protected) and the MANNER of obtaining the information (may not be protected). Employers can legitimately take action against a worker for misconduct in how they obtained the information, provided they can show the action was because of the misconduct, not because of the disclosure itself. This is a frequently litigated question.

leadingCases.name: Jesudason v Alder Hey Children's NHS Foundation Trust
leadingCases.principle: For a detriment claim under s.47B, the worker must show that the protected disclosure was the GROUND for the detrimental treatment — it must materially influence the employer's treatment of the worker. The disclosure does not need to be the sole or even the principal ground, but it must be more than trivially connected to the treatment.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the test for causation in whistleblowing detriment claims is whether the protected disclosure materially influenced (in the sense of being more than a trivial influence on) the employer's treatment of the worker. This is a lower threshold than 'but for' causation but still requires a material connection.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the causation test in whistleblowing detriment claims. Important because many whistleblowing cases involve mixed motives — the employer may have legitimate performance or conduct concerns alongside the worker's disclosure. The 'material influence' test means the worker succeeds if the disclosure was a significant (more than trivial) factor in the treatment, even if other factors also contributed.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Whistleblowing at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on what constitutes whistleblowing, how to make a disclosure, and employer obligations to protect whistleblowers.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Whistleblowing for employees
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on whistleblower rights, how to make a disclosure, and the list of prescribed persons to whom disclosures may be made under s.43F.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Prescribed persons list
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The complete list of prescribed persons and bodies to whom workers can make protected disclosures under s.43F. Updated by SI 2025/604 to include additional prescribed persons for sanctions-related disclosures.

commonErrors: Not recognising that whistleblowing protection is a DAY-ONE right — there is no qualifying period for whistleblowing detriment (s.47B) or whistleblowing dismissal (s.103A — s.108(3)(ff) exempts it from the qualifying period). An employee dismissed on their first day for making a protected disclosure has a claim with UNCAPPED compensation

Treating any internal complaint as a 'protected disclosure' — not every grievance or complaint qualifies. The disclosure must contain INFORMATION (factual content with sufficient specificity — Kilraine [2018]), the worker must REASONABLY BELIEVE it tends to show one of the six categories, and the worker must reasonably believe it is in the PUBLIC INTEREST. A personal grievance about contractual terms may not satisfy the public interest test

Assuming the information disclosed must be TRUE — Babula [2007] confirms that the worker's belief need only be REASONABLE, not correct. A worker who reasonably but wrongly believes that conduct amounts to a criminal offence is still making a qualifying disclosure. The test is the reasonableness of the belief, not the accuracy of the information

Drawing a rigid distinction between 'information' and 'allegation' — Kilraine [2018] softened the earlier Cavendish Munro approach. The real question is whether the disclosure has 'sufficient factual content and specificity'. A statement like 'you are not complying with health and safety' may fail. A statement like 'the fire exits are blocked by storage boxes' will qualify. Workers should be advised to include specific facts

Not recognising that whistleblowing dismissal compensation is UNCAPPED — s.124(1A) excludes s.103A dismissals from the compensatory award cap. There is no upper limit. This is fundamentally different from ordinary unfair dismissal (capped at the lower of £115,115 or 52 weeks' pay). A single whistleblowing dismissal can result in a six- or seven-figure award

Conflating the reason for dismissal with the manner of disclosure — Bolton School v Evans [2006] establishes that the employer may legitimately discipline or dismiss for the MANNER in which information was obtained (e.g., hacking, theft of documents), provided the reason is genuinely the misconduct, not the disclosure itself. But the burden of proving the reason rests on the employer

Not updating whistleblowing policies to include sexual harassment — ERA 2025 s.23 inserts s.43B(1)(da) from 6 APRIL 2026. From that date, sexual harassment is an explicit qualifying category. Employers who have not updated their whistleblowing policies by that date risk failing to recognise and protect disclosures about sexual harassment

Applying the public interest test too restrictively — Chesterton [2017] interpreted 'public interest' broadly. A disclosure affecting a group of employees (even a small group) may be in the public interest. A disclosure about a single employer's systematic practice that could affect other workplaces may be in the public interest. The test is not whether the 'public at large' is affected

Not recognising that the 'cover-up' category in s.43B(1)(f) is itself a qualifying disclosure — a worker who discloses that evidence of wrongdoing is being deliberately concealed is making a qualifying disclosure even if they cannot prove the underlying wrongdoing. The concealment itself is the relevant failure

Not recognising that contractual gagging clauses are VOID — s.43J renders any contractual term unenforceable insofar as it purports to prevent a worker from making a protected disclosure. NDAs, confidentiality clauses, and settlement agreement terms cannot prevent a worker from making a qualifying disclosure to a prescribed person or to the employer

Treating whistleblowing and grievance procedures as the same thing — they are legally distinct. A whistleblowing disclosure is a disclosure of information about wrongdoing affecting the public interest. A grievance is a personal complaint about treatment. However, the same communication can be both. Employers must assess whether a communication contains a qualifying disclosure, even if it is framed as a personal grievance

sections.title: Disclosure to prescribed person
sections.summary: Qualifying disclosure to a prescribed regulator (e.g. HSE, FCA, HMRC) is protected provided the worker reasonably believes the relevant failure falls within the regulator's remit.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if—
(a) the worker makes it to a person prescribed by an order made by the Secretary of State for the purposes of this section, and
(b) the worker reasonably believes—
(i) that the relevant failure falls within any description of matters in respect of which that person is so prescribed, and
(ii) that the information disclosed, and any allegation contained in it, are substantially true.
refs: Public Interest Disclosure (Prescribed Persons) Order 2014
s.43G ERA 1996 (Wider Disclosure)
ACAS Whistleblowing at Work Guidance
