sections.title: Protected disclosures — right not to suffer detriment
sections.summary: Worker has right not to be subjected to any detriment by employer on the ground that the worker has made a protected disclosure.
sections.text:
(1) A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by his employer done on the ground that the worker has made a protected disclosure.

(1A) A worker (W) has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, done by another worker of W's employer in the course of that other worker's employment, on the ground that W has made a protected disclosure.
refs: s.43A ERA 1996 (Protected Disclosure)
s.103A ERA 1996 (Automatic Unfair Dismissal for Protected Disclosure)
Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979

sections.title: Right to time off for public duties
sections.summary: Employees who hold certain public positions (magistrates, local councillors, members of statutory tribunals) are entitled to reasonable time off.
sections.text:
(1) An employer shall permit an employee of his to take time off during the employee's working hours for the purpose of performing any of the following duties—
(a) any duties as a justice of the peace,
(b) any duties as a member of—
(i) a local authority,
(ii) a statutory tribunal,
(iii) a health authority or NHS foundation trust.

(2) The amount of time off which an employee is to be permitted to take under this section are those that are reasonable in all the circumstances having regard in particular to how much time off is required for the performance of the duties, how much time off the employee has already been permitted, and the circumstances of the employer's business.
refs: s.51 ERA 1996 (Complaints to Tribunal)
TULRCA 1992 ss.168–170 (Trade Union Time Off)

sections.title: Right to time off to look for work or arrange training
sections.summary: Employee under notice of redundancy with 2 years' service is entitled to reasonable paid time off to look for new employment or arrange training.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is given notice of dismissal by reason of redundancy is entitled to be permitted by his employer to take reasonable time off during the employee's working hours before the end of the notice period in order to—
(a) look for new employment, or
(b) make arrangements for training for future employment.

(2) An employee is not entitled to take time off under this section unless, on whichever is the later of the date on which the notice is given, the employee has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years.
refs: s.53 ERA 1996 (Right to Remuneration for Time Off)
s.135 ERA 1996 (Redundancy Payments)

sections.title: Right to time off for ante-natal care
sections.summary: Pregnant employee is entitled to paid time off to keep ante-natal appointments made on advice of a registered medical practitioner, midwife, or nurse.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who—
(a) is pregnant, and
(b) has, on the advice of a registered medical practitioner, registered midwife or registered nurse, made an appointment to attend at any place for the purpose of receiving ante-natal care,
is entitled to be permitted by her employer to take time off during the employee's working hours in order to enable her to keep the appointment.

(2) An employee is not entitled to take time off under this section to keep an appointment unless, if her employer requests her to do so, she produces for her employer's inspection a certificate from a registered medical practitioner, registered midwife or registered nurse stating that the employee is pregnant, and an appointment card showing that the appointment has been made.
refs: s.56 ERA 1996 (Remuneration During Ante-Natal Care)
Maternity and Parental Leave Regulations 1999
Children and Families Act 2014 (Partner Ante-natal Appointments)

sections.title: Time off for dependants
sections.summary: Employee is entitled to reasonable unpaid time off to deal with unexpected problems involving a dependant.
sections.text:
(1) An employee is entitled to be permitted by his employer to take a reasonable amount of time off during the employee's working hours in order to take action which is necessary—
(a) to provide assistance on an occasion when a dependant falls ill, gives birth or is injured or assaulted,
(b) to make arrangements for the provision of care for a dependant who is ill or injured,
(c) in consequence of the death of a dependant,
(d) because of the unexpected disruption or termination of arrangements for the care of a dependant, or
(e) to deal with an incident which involves a child of the employee and which occurs unexpectedly in a period during which an educational establishment which the child attends is responsible for him.

(2) This section does not apply unless the employee tells his employer the reason for his absence as soon as reasonably practicable.
refs: s.57B ERA 1996 (Complaints to Tribunal)
ACAS Time Off for Dependants Guidance

sections.title: Ordinary maternity leave
sections.summary: Every employee is entitled to 26 weeks' ordinary maternity leave regardless of length of service.
sections.text:
(1) An employee is entitled to ordinary maternity leave if she satisfies any conditions which may be prescribed and has complied with any requirements imposed by or under regulations made by the Secretary of State.

(3) Ordinary maternity leave continues for the period of 26 weeks beginning with the day on which it begins, unless the employee is dismissed or the period ends earlier.
refs: s.73 ERA 1996 (Additional Maternity Leave)
Maternity and Parental Leave Regulations 1999
SMP Regulations 1986 (Statutory Maternity Pay)

sections.title: Additional maternity leave
sections.summary: An employee entitled to ordinary maternity leave is also entitled to 26 weeks' additional maternity leave, giving a total of 52 weeks.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who satisfies prescribed conditions is entitled to additional maternity leave.

(2) Additional maternity leave—
(a) commences on the day after the last day of the employee's ordinary maternity leave period, and
(b) continues for the period of 26 weeks beginning with that day.
refs: s.71 ERA 1996 (Ordinary Maternity Leave)
s.74 ERA 1996 (Redundancy During Maternity Leave)
Maternity and Parental Leave Regulations 1999

sections.title: Redundancy during maternity leave
sections.summary: Where employee on maternity leave is redundant, employer must offer her any suitable available vacancy in preference to other redundant employees.
sections.text:
(1) This section applies where, during an employee's ordinary or additional maternity leave period, it is not practicable by reason of redundancy for the employer to continue to employ her under her existing contract of employment.

(2) Where there is a suitable available vacancy, the employee is entitled to be offered (before the end of her employment under her existing contract) alternative employment with her employer or his successor, or an associated employer, under a new contract of employment which complies with subsection (3).

(3) The new contract of employment must be such that the work to be done under it is of a kind which is both suitable in relation to the employee and appropriate for her to do in the circumstances, and its provisions as to the capacity and place in which she is to be employed are not substantially less favourable to her than if she had continued to be employed under the previous contract.
sections.eraNote: Regulation 29A of the Maternity and Parental Leave Regulations 1999 (as amended) provides that an employee on maternity leave is a priority candidate for any suitable alternative employment during a redundancy exercise. ERA 2025 further strengthens these protections.
refs: MPL Regulations 1999 reg.29A
Sefton Borough Council v Wainwright [2015]

sections.title: Parental leave
sections.summary: Employee with one year's service who is the parent of a child under 18 is entitled to 18 weeks' unpaid parental leave in respect of each child.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who has been continuously employed for a period of not less than a year, and has, or expects to have, responsibility for a child, is entitled, in accordance with this section, to be absent from work on parental leave for the purpose of caring for that child.

(2) An employee is entitled to 18 weeks' parental leave in respect of each child in relation to whom he satisfies the conditions in subsection (1)(b).
refs: Maternity and Parental Leave Regulations 1999
Children and Families Act 2014 (Shared Parental Leave)
ERA 2025 (Paternity Leave Reform — Not Yet In Force)

sections.title: Statutory right to request flexible working
sections.summary: Provides every employee with a day-one statutory right to request a change to their terms and conditions relating to hours, times, or place of work. The right was originally introduced by the Employment Act 2002 (for parents/carers only with 26 weeks' service), extended to all employees by the Children and Families Act 2014 (retaining 26 weeks' service), and fundamentally reformed by the Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 (removing the service requirement and allowing two requests per year, in force 6 April 2024). ERA 2025 s.9 introduces a further reform: the employer's refusal must be REASONABLE — not merely based on one of the eight statutory grounds. This is a RIGHT TO REQUEST, not a right to flexible working — but the reforms are progressively narrowing the employer's discretion to refuse.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying employee may apply to his employer for a change in his terms and conditions of employment if—

(a) the change relates to—

(i) the hours he is required to work,

(ii) the times when he is required to work,

(iii) where, as between his home and a place of business of his employer, he is required to work, or

(iv) such other aspect of his terms and conditions of employment as the Secretary of State may specify by regulations, and

(b) his purpose in applying is to achieve a change mentioned in paragraph (a) on a permanent basis.

(2) An application under this section must—

(a) state that it is such an application,

(b) specify the change applied for and the date on which it is proposed the change should become effective.

(4) An employee may not—

(a) make more than two applications under this section to the same employer during any period of 12 months;

(b) make an application under this section to an employer if another such application to the same employer is proceeding.

(5) In this section, section 80G and section 80H "qualifying employee" means an employee who—

(a) satisfies such conditions as to duration of employment as the Secretary of State may specify by regulations, and

(b) is not an agency worker (other than one who is, by virtue of regulation 3A(6) of the Agency Workers Regulations 2010, to be regarded as a qualifying employee for the purposes of this section).

(8) In subsection (1)(a), the reference to an employee's terms and conditions of employment includes a reference to any of the following which is not a term and condition of the employee's employment—

(a) the amount of the employee's remuneration,

(a)(i) any remuneration of the employee payable by way of pension or otherwise in connection with a member of the employee's family suffering from illness or disability,

(a)(ii) any remuneration of the employee payable by way of pension in respect of the employee's retirement, or

(b) the employee's hours of work.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 80F creates a RIGHT TO REQUEST flexible working, not a right to flexible working itself. The employer must consider the request through a structured statutory process (s.80G) and can only refuse on one of eight specified business grounds. However, the ERA 2025 reforms add a new layer: from commencement, the employer's refusal must also be REASONABLE — the employer must explain why the ground for refusal applies and why it is reasonable to refuse on that ground. The right applies to employees only (not workers) but from day one of employment with no qualifying period.

reformTimeline.note: Section 80F has been reformed three times in two years — the most rapidly evolving provision in the ERA 1996.

phases.instrument: Employment Act 2002 s.47 (inserted s.80F into ERA 1996)
phases.changes: Right to request flexible working introduced — limited to parents/carers of children under 6 (or disabled children under 18). 26 weeks' qualifying period. One request per year.
phases.rationale: Part of the Work-Life Balance campaign. Initially targeted at parents to address the childcare barrier to employment.

phases.changes: Extended the right to ALL employees — not just parents/carers. 26 weeks' qualifying period retained. One request per year retained.
phases.rationale: Recognised that flexible working benefits all employees, not just parents. Removed the parental/carer restriction.

phases.changes: (1) Day-one right — 26-week qualifying period removed. (2) Two requests per year — increased from one. (3) No business impact explanation — employee no longer required to explain what effect the request will have on the employer. (4) Employer must respond within 2 months — reduced from 3 months. (5) Employer must consult before refusing — new duty to consult with the employee before rejecting the request.
phases.rationale: The most significant reform to flexible working rights since their introduction. Reflects the post-pandemic shift in working patterns and the recognition that flexible working is now a mainstream expectation.

phases.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 (c.36) s.9 (commenced for specified purposes by SI 2026/3 reg.2(6))
phases.changes: Introduces a REASONABLENESS test for refusal. The employer must: (1) state the ground for refusal; (2) explain why refusal is reasonable on that ground. Consultation requirements to be set out in regulations. Full commencement expected 2027.
phases.commencementStatus: Partially commenced 6 January 2026 for specified purposes (enabling regulations). Full commencement expected 2027. Government consultation on process published 5 February 2026, open until 30 April 2026.
phases.rationale: Shifts the balance further towards the employee. Under the pre-ERA 2025 framework, the employer merely had to identify a ground for refusal from the statutory list — no requirement to demonstrate that the refusal was reasonable. Under ERA 2025, the employer must show that the ground applies AND that it is reasonable to refuse.

refusalGrounds.note: The employer may only refuse a request on one or more of these eight grounds. ERA 2025 adds the requirement that the refusal on these grounds must also be REASONABLE.
grounds: A detrimental effect on ability to meet customer demand
An inability to reorganise work among existing staff
An inability to recruit additional staff
Insufficiency of work during the periods the employee proposes to work
refusalGrounds.practicalNote: Under the current (pre-ERA 2025 full commencement) framework, the employer must identify a ground but the tribunal's scrutiny is limited — it asks whether the employer's decision was based on incorrect facts or whether the employer failed to follow the statutory procedure. Under the ERA 2025 framework, the tribunal will also assess whether the refusal was REASONABLE — a significantly higher bar.

indirectSexDiscrimination.principle: Refusal of a flexible working request may constitute indirect sex discrimination under EqA 2010 s.19 if the requirement to work in a particular pattern (e.g., full-time, office-based) disproportionately disadvantages women (who bear a disproportionate share of caring responsibilities) and the employer cannot objectively justify the requirement.
indirectSexDiscrimination.practicalNote: This is the most significant legal risk associated with flexible working refusal. Even if the employer correctly applies the s.80G procedure and identifies a valid ground, the refusal may still be unlawful indirect sex discrimination if it cannot be objectively justified. The compensation for indirect discrimination is uncapped and includes injury to feelings.
indirectSexDiscrimination.keyCase: Indirect sex discrimination claims arising from flexible working refusals are frequently brought alongside s.80H tribunal complaints. The combined exposure can be significant.

disabilityReasonableAdjustments.principle: Where an employee with a disability requests flexible working as a reasonable adjustment under EqA 2010 s.20, the employer's obligations go beyond the s.80F framework. A reasonable adjustment request engages a positive duty on the employer to make the adjustment unless it would cause unjustifiable hardship. The s.80G refusal grounds do not override the reasonable adjustment duty.
disabilityReasonableAdjustments.practicalNote: An employer who refuses a flexible working request that is also a reasonable adjustment request faces parallel liability: a s.80H complaint for procedural failure AND a disability discrimination claim for failure to make reasonable adjustments. The discrimination claim is uncapped.

automaticUnfairDismissal.principle: Dismissal of an employee because they made (or proposed to make) a flexible working request is automatically unfair under s.104C. No qualifying period is required. This protection applies from day one of employment.

versions.text: [Original version — right limited to parents/carers. 26 weeks' qualifying period. One request per 12 months. Employee must explain impact on employer.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Act 2002 s.47 (inserted Part 8A including s.80F)
versions.policyRationale: Introduced the statutory right to request flexible working for the first time. Limited to parents and carers as a targeted work-life balance measure.

versions.text: [Extended to all employees. 26 weeks' qualifying period retained. One request per 12 months. Employee must explain impact on employer. Employer must respond within 3 months.]
versions.amendedBy: Children and Families Act 2014 (c.6) s.131
versions.policyRationale: Extended flexible working to all employees. Recognised that flexible working benefits all workers, not just parents.

versions.text: [Current version — day-one right. Two requests per 12 months. No business impact explanation required. Employer must respond within 2 months. Employer must consult before refusing.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 (c.33) s.1
versions.policyRationale: The most significant reform — day-one right, two requests, no employee burden to explain impact, employer must consult. Reflects the post-pandemic normalisation of flexible working.

pendingAmendment.effect: Introduces a REASONABLENESS test for employer refusal. The employer must: (1) state the statutory ground for refusal; (2) explain why it is reasonable to refuse on that ground. The employer must also follow consultation steps to be set out in regulations. A new ground of complaint is added (s.80H(1)(c)) — that the employer's refusal notification was unreasonable.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Partially commenced 6 January 2026 for specified purposes (enabling regulations — SI 2026/3 reg.2(6)). Full commencement expected 2027. Government consultation published 5 February 2026, open until 30 April 2026.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: HIGH — the reasonableness test fundamentally changes the employer's position. Under the current framework, the employer merely selects a ground from the statutory list. Under ERA 2025, the employer must demonstrate that the refusal is REASONABLE — bringing the test closer to the objective justification standard used in indirect discrimination cases. Employers who reflexively refuse flexible working requests without genuine business analysis will face increased tribunal exposure.

sections.eraNote: s.80F has been reformed three times in two years (ER(FW) Act 2023 in April 2024, ACAS Code revised April 2024, ERA 2025 s.9 partially commenced January 2026). The trajectory is clear: the employer's discretion to refuse is being progressively narrowed. The combination of the reasonableness test (ERA 2025), the ACAS Code (tribunal must consider it), and the indirect discrimination route (EqA 2010) means that employers who refuse flexible working requests without a documented, evidence-based business case face significant legal risk from multiple directions simultaneously.
refs: ERA 1996 s.80F (right to request — this section)
ERA 1996 s.80G (employer's duties — process, decision period, grounds for refusal)
ERA 1996 s.80H (complaint to employment tribunal)
ERA 1996 s.80I (remedies — compensation up to 8 weeks' pay)
ERA 1996 s.104C (automatically unfair dismissal for making a flexible working request — day-one right)
Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 (c.33) (in force 6 April 2024)
ERA 2025 s.9 (reasonableness test — partially commenced 6 January 2026)
ACAS Code of Practice on Requests for Flexible Working (revised 6 April 2024)
Flexible Working Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/1398)
Children and Families Act 2014 s.131 (extended right to all employees)
Employment Act 2002 s.47 (original insertion of Part 8A)
EqA 2010 s.19 (indirect discrimination — flexible working refusal may disproportionately affect women)
EqA 2010 s.20 (reasonable adjustments — flexible working as a disability adjustment)
SI 2026/3 reg.2(6) (ERA 2025 s.9 commencement for specified purposes)
Government consultation on flexible working reforms (published 5 February 2026, closes 30 April 2026)

leadingCases.name: Commotion Ltd v Rutty
leadingCases.principle: The employer's obligation under s.80G is procedural — to follow the statutory process correctly. Where the employer has identified a valid ground for refusal and followed the correct procedure, the tribunal's scrutiny is limited. The tribunal does not apply a 'range of reasonable responses' test or conduct a merits review of the employer's decision.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the tribunal's role in a s.80H complaint is limited to: (1) whether the employer followed the statutory procedure; (2) whether the refusal was based on incorrect facts; and (3) whether the employer identified a permissible ground for refusal. The tribunal does not conduct a wider assessment of the reasonableness of the refusal.
leadingCases.significance: Established the limited scope of tribunal scrutiny under the pre-ERA 2025 framework. This is the case that ERA 2025 s.9 is designed to change — by introducing a reasonableness test, ERA 2025 will expand the tribunal's role beyond the purely procedural review established in Commotion.

leadingCases.name: Hardy & Hansons plc v Lax
leadingCases.principle: A requirement to work full-time may constitute indirect sex discrimination under EqA 2010 s.19 (then the Sex Discrimination Act 1975) if it disproportionately disadvantages women and cannot be objectively justified by the employer. The objective justification test requires the employer to show that the requirement is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the employer's refusal to allow a senior employee to work reduced hours after returning from maternity leave was capable of constituting indirect sex discrimination. The tribunal must assess whether the full-time requirement was a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim — not merely whether a 'reasonable' employer might have imposed it.
leadingCases.significance: THE leading authority on the interaction between flexible working refusal and indirect sex discrimination. Establishes that even where the s.80G process is followed correctly, the refusal may be unlawful discrimination. The objective justification test under EqA 2010 is MORE demanding than the s.80G grounds — the employer must show that the refusal is a PROPORTIONATE means of achieving a LEGITIMATE aim, not merely that a business ground applies.

leadingCases.name: British Telecommunications plc v Meier
leadingCases.principle: An employer's blanket refusal to consider flexible working for a category of employees (e.g., all employees in a particular role or location) without individually assessing each request may be both a procedural failure under s.80G and indirect discrimination.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the employer's approach of applying a blanket policy against home working for certain roles, without considering individual requests on their merits, was capable of constituting indirect sex discrimination and a failure to comply with the s.80G procedural requirements.
leadingCases.significance: Important for employers with blanket 'return to office' or 'no home working' policies. Each flexible working request must be considered individually on its merits. A blanket refusal, without individual assessment, fails both the s.80G process and the indirect discrimination defence.

leadingCases.name: Dobson v North Cumbria Integrated Care NHS Foundation Trust
leadingCases.principle: Community nurses who were required to work flexibly across a wider geographical area could bring indirect sex discrimination claims where the requirement disproportionately affected women with caring responsibilities. The tribunal must assess the particular disadvantage suffered by the claimant group, not just the workforce as a whole.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the tribunal must focus on whether the particular provision, criterion, or practice (PCP) — in this case, the requirement to work flexibly across a wider area — put women at a particular disadvantage compared with men. The fact that some women were able to comply with the PCP did not negate the disproportionate impact on women as a group.
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates that the indirect discrimination analysis applies not just to refusals of flexible working requests but also to IMPOSITIONS of working patterns that disadvantage those with caring responsibilities. The interaction works both ways — refusal to allow flexibility AND requirement to accept unwanted flexibility may both be discriminatory.

leadingCases.name: Thompson v Scancrown Ltd (t/a Manors)
leadingCases.principle: An employee dismissed after requesting flexible working may claim automatically unfair dismissal under s.104C — the reason for dismissal was the making of the request. The s.104C protection is a day-one right with no qualifying period.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that s.104C protects employees from dismissal for making (or proposing to make) a flexible working request. The protection applies from day one. The employer bears the burden of showing a different reason for the dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the day-one automatic unfair dismissal protection for employees who make flexible working requests. Important because s.104C claims bypass the s.108 qualifying period — so even before ERA 2025 repeals s.108, employees dismissed for requesting flexible working have day-one protection.

leadingCases.name: Glover v Lacoste UK Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Refusal of a flexible working request made by a mother returning from maternity leave, where the request was to work from home on certain days, constituted indirect sex discrimination. The employer's requirement for full-time office presence was not objectively justified.
leadingCases.heldText: The tribunal held that the employer's insistence on full-time office attendance for a marketing role, despite the employee having successfully worked from home during the pandemic, was not a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim. The full-time office requirement put women at a particular disadvantage because women bear a disproportionate share of childcare responsibilities.
leadingCases.significance: A recent high-profile tribunal decision that illustrates the post-pandemic landscape. Employers who require full-time office attendance must be able to objectively justify that requirement — the fact that a role was successfully performed from home during the pandemic undermines the argument that office attendance is essential.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice on Requests for Flexible Working (revised 6 April 2024)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The statutory Code of Practice — the tribunal must take it into account when considering s.80H complaints. Revised from 6 April 2024 to reflect the ER(FW) Act 2023 changes. Covers: handling the request, the duty to consult, the decision-making process, and communicating the outcome.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Guide to handling flexible working requests
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Non-statutory companion guide providing detailed practical guidance on the revised process, including template letters, decision-making frameworks, and appeal procedures.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Flexible working: employer guide
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the employer's obligations under the reformed s.80F, including the new consultation requirements.

commonErrors: Treating the right to request as a right to flexible working — s.80F creates a right to REQUEST, not a right to RECEIVE flexible working. The employer may refuse on one of the eight statutory grounds (s.80G(1)(b)). However, ERA 2025 adds the requirement that refusal must be REASONABLE — so the employer's discretion is narrowing

Not recognising that this is now a DAY-ONE right — the ER(FW) Act 2023 removed the 26-week qualifying period from 6 April 2024. Any employee can make a statutory request from their first day of employment. Employers who still apply a qualifying period requirement are acting unlawfully

Not consulting before refusing — the ER(FW) Act 2023 requires the employer to consult with the employee before refusing a request. A refusal without prior consultation is a procedural failure that the employee can challenge under s.80H. The ACAS Code provides detailed guidance on what consultation involves

Applying a blanket policy to refuse all requests of a certain type (e.g., 'no home working for customer-facing roles') — each request must be assessed individually on its merits. A blanket refusal without individual assessment is both a s.80G procedural failure and potentially indirect discrimination (BT v Meier [2019])

Ignoring the indirect sex discrimination risk — refusal of a flexible working request may constitute indirect sex discrimination under EqA 2010 s.19 if the requirement to work in a particular pattern disproportionately disadvantages women. The compensation for indirect discrimination is UNCAPPED and includes injury to feelings. Hardy v Hansons [2005] establishes that the employer must objectively justify the refusal, not merely identify a business ground

Not responding within 2 months — the ER(FW) Act 2023 reduced the response deadline from 3 months to 2 months. An employer who fails to respond within 2 months has failed to comply with s.80G and the employee can bring a s.80H complaint

Not knowing that employees can now make TWO requests per year — the ER(FW) Act 2023 increased the allowance from one request per 12 months to two. Employers who tell employees they have already used their 'one request' are applying the old law

Not recognising the automatically unfair dismissal protection — dismissal for making (or proposing to make) a flexible working request is automatically unfair under s.104C. This is a day-one right with no qualifying period. An employee dismissed on day one for requesting flexible working has a claim

Not preparing for the ERA 2025 reasonableness test — from full commencement (expected 2027), the employer will need to: (1) state the ground for refusal; (2) explain why refusal is reasonable on that ground. The Government consultation published 5 February 2026 (open until 30 April 2026) will determine the consultation steps. Employers should begin developing evidence-based decision-making frameworks now

Overlooking the disability reasonable adjustment interaction — where an employee with a disability requests flexible working as a reasonable adjustment (EqA 2010 s.20), the employer's obligations go BEYOND the s.80F framework. The reasonable adjustment duty requires the employer to make the adjustment unless it would cause unjustifiable hardship. The s.80G refusal grounds do not override this duty

Using the pandemic as a reason to refuse rather than a reason to accept — many employers successfully operated with employees working from home during 2020–2021. The fact that a role was performed from home during the pandemic undermines the argument that office attendance is essential for that role. Tribunals will scrutinise this (Glover v Lacoste [2023])

sections.title: Rights of employer and employee to minimum notice
sections.summary: Establishes the statutory minimum notice periods for terminating employment. After one month's continuous employment, the employer must give: one week's notice for service between one month and two years; one additional week per complete year of service for service between two and twelve years; twelve weeks' notice for service of twelve years or more. The employee must give one week's notice regardless of length of service (once the one-month qualifying period is met). The contract may provide for LONGER notice but cannot provide for LESS than the statutory minimum. Dismissal without proper notice (and without lawful justification such as gross misconduct) is wrongful dismissal — a breach of contract claim available from day one with no qualifying period. Section 86(6) preserves the right to dismiss without notice for the conduct of the other party (gross misconduct). The effective date of termination (EDT) under s.97(2) may be extended by the statutory minimum notice period for the purpose of determining qualifying periods — but not where the employer was entitled to dismiss without notice under s.86(6).
sections.text:
(1) The notice required to be given by an employer to terminate the contract of employment of a person who has been continuously employed for one month or more—

(a) is not less than one week's notice if his period of continuous employment is less than two years,

(b) is not less than one week's notice for each year of continuous employment if his period of continuous employment is two years or more but less than twelve years, and

(c) is not less than twelve weeks' notice if his period of continuous employment is twelve years or more.

(2) The notice required to be given by an employee who has been continuously employed for one month or more to terminate his contract of employment is not less than one week.

(3) Any provision for shorter notice in any contract of employment with a person who has been continuously employed for one month or more has effect subject to subsections (1) and (2); but this section does not prevent either party from waiving his right to notice on any occasion or from accepting a payment in lieu of notice.

(4) Any contract of employment of a person who has been continuously employed for three months or more which is a contract for a term certain of one month or less shall have effect as if it were for an indefinite period; and, accordingly, no notice shall be required to give effect to any term of the contract under which it terminates on the expiry of that term.

(5) Subsections (1) and (2) do not apply in relation to a contract made in contemplation of the performance of a specific task which is not expected to last for more than three months unless the employee has been continuously employed for a period of more than three months.

(6) This section does not affect any right of either party to a contract of employment to treat the contract as terminable without notice by reason of the conduct of the other party.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 86 establishes the FLOOR — the minimum notice that must be given. The contract may require longer notice but can never require less (s.86(3)). Where the contract specifies less than the statutory minimum, the statutory minimum overrides the contractual term. However, s.86(3) also expressly preserves the right of either party to WAIVE notice or ACCEPT a payment in lieu of notice (PILON) on any occasion. Section 86(6) preserves the common law right to dismiss without notice for gross misconduct — this is critical because it interacts with the EDT calculation under s.97(2).

employer.underOneMonth: No statutory minimum notice — either party can terminate without notice during the first month of employment
employer.twoToTwelveYears: Not less than 1 week's notice for each complete year of continuous employment (s.86(1)(b)). E.g., 5 years = 5 weeks; 8 years = 8 weeks; 11 years = 11 weeks
employer.twelveYearsOrMore: Not less than 12 weeks' notice (s.86(1)(c)) — the statutory maximum. Regardless of whether the employee has 12, 20, or 30 years' service, the statutory minimum is capped at 12 weeks
employer.contractualNotice: The contract may require the employer to give MORE notice than the statutory minimum. Contractual notice periods of 1, 3, 6, or 12 months are common for senior employees. The contractual period applies where it exceeds the statutory minimum.

employee.afterOneMonth: Not less than 1 week's notice (s.86(2)) — regardless of length of service. An employee with 20 years' service is only required to give 1 week's statutory notice, even though the employer must give 12 weeks
employee.contractualNotice: The contract may require the employee to give longer notice. Contractual notice of 1 or 3 months is common. The contractual period applies where it exceeds the statutory minimum.

grossMisconductException.principle: Section 86 does not affect the right of either party to treat the contract as terminable without notice because of the conduct of the other party. This preserves the common law right to dismiss summarily (without notice) for gross misconduct — conduct so serious that it constitutes a repudiatory breach of the employment contract.
grossMisconductException.practicalNote: The s.86(6) exception is critical for two reasons: (1) it permits summary dismissal for genuine gross misconduct (theft, fraud, violence, serious safety violations, gross insubordination); (2) where s.86(6) applies, the employee cannot rely on s.97(2) to extend the EDT by the statutory notice period — Lancaster & Duke v Wileman [2017] EAT. However, the employer must actually be ENTITLED to dismiss without notice — simply labelling the reason as 'gross misconduct' does not engage s.86(6) if the conduct would not justify summary dismissal at common law.

edtInteraction.principle: Where an employee is dismissed without notice, s.97(2) deems the effective date of termination to be the date on which the statutory minimum notice would have expired IF the employer had given notice. This 'artificial' extension of the EDT can be critical for employees dismissed just short of a qualifying period (e.g., dismissed after 1 year 51 weeks — the statutory notice extension of 1 week could push the EDT past the 2-year unfair dismissal qualifying date).
edtInteraction.wileman: Lancaster & Duke Ltd v Wileman [2017] UKEAT — the EAT held that s.97(2) takes the WHOLE of s.86 into account, including s.86(6). Where the employer was entitled to dismiss without notice under s.86(6) (genuine gross misconduct), the employee cannot use s.97(2) to extend the EDT. The EDT is the actual date of summary dismissal.
edtInteraction.practicalNote: This interaction is the most litigated aspect of s.86. Employers who dismiss without notice near a qualifying period boundary must be prepared for the employee to argue that the EDT should be extended under s.97(2). But if the dismissal was for genuine gross misconduct, the employer can resist the extension under s.86(6)/Wileman.

wrongfulDismissal.definition: Wrongful dismissal occurs when the employer terminates employment without giving the notice the employee is contractually or statutorily entitled to, and without lawful justification for summary dismissal (gross misconduct). It is a BREACH OF CONTRACT claim — distinct from unfair dismissal (which is a statutory fairness claim).
wrongfulDismissal.dayOneRight: Wrongful dismissal is a day-one contractual right — there is no qualifying period. An employee dismissed without proper notice on their second day of employment has a wrongful dismissal claim.
wrongfulDismissal.tribunalCap: Wrongful dismissal claims in the Employment Tribunal are capped at £25,000 (the tribunal's contractual jurisdiction limit). Claims exceeding £25,000 must be brought in the County Court or High Court.
wrongfulDismissal.damages: The measure of damages is the net pay (and benefits) the employee would have received during the notice period they should have been given. There is no award for injury to feelings, no basic award, and no Polkey reduction — the claim is purely contractual.
wrongfulDismissal.distinctionFromUnfairDismissal: An employer may dismiss FAIRLY under s.98 but still WRONGFULLY if notice was not given. Conversely, an employer may give proper notice but dismiss UNFAIRLY (e.g., unfair process). The two claims are independent and may be pursued in parallel.

pilon.title: Payment in Lieu of Notice (PILON)
pilon.principle: Section 86(3) permits either party to waive their right to notice or to accept a payment in lieu of notice. A PILON allows the employer to terminate employment immediately while compensating the employee for the notice period they would have worked.
pilon.contractualVsNonContractual: Where the employment contract contains an express PILON clause, the employer can exercise it to terminate immediately — this is not a breach of contract. Where there is NO PILON clause, making a payment instead of giving notice is technically a breach of contract by the employer (even though the employee receives payment). The employee could in theory claim wrongful dismissal — though the loss is limited to the notice pay already offered.
pilon.taxTreatment: Since 6 April 2018 (Finance (No.2) Act 2017, ITEPA 2003 s.402A–402E), ALL PILON — whether contractual or non-contractual — is treated as earnings and is subject to income tax and employee National Insurance contributions through the Post-Employment Notice Pay (PENP) calculation. The pre-2018 planning opportunity (non-contractual PILON qualifying for the £30,000 tax-free termination payment exemption) has been closed.

versions.text: [The substantive provisions of s.86(1)–(6) have not been materially amended since the 1996 Act consolidated the provisions from EPCA 1978 s.49 (originally from the Contracts of Employment Act 1963). The notice scale (1 week per year, max 12 weeks for employer; 1 week for employee) has remained unchanged since 1963.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating EPCA 1978 s.49, originally Contracts of Employment Act 1963 s.1)
versions.policyRationale: The statutory minimum notice scale has been in place since 1963 — over 60 years without change. The 12-week maximum for employer notice has never been increased. The 1-week employee notice has never been increased. These are among the most stable provisions in UK employment law.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does NOT change statutory notice periods. However, the reduction of the unfair dismissal qualifying period from 2 years to 6 months (expected January 2027) will affect the strategic significance of the s.97(2) EDT extension. Currently, the EDT extension is critical for employees dismissed just short of the 2-year qualifying period. When the qualifying period drops to 6 months, the EDT extension will matter for employees dismissed just short of 6 months instead.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: ERA 2025 does not amend s.86
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW for the provision itself (unchanged), but the wrongful dismissal claim gains relative importance as ERA 2025 removes the compensatory award cap — employers who dismiss without notice will face: (1) wrongful dismissal claim (notice pay); (2) potential unfair dismissal claim with UNCAPPED compensation; (3) the s.97(2) EDT extension argument.

refs: ERA 1996 s.86 (this section — minimum notice periods)
ERA 1996 s.87–91 (rights during notice period — pay, benefits, continuity)
ERA 1996 s.97(2) (EDT extension by statutory minimum notice — critical interaction)
ERA 1996 s.97(4) (EDT for trial period cases)
ERA 1996 s.108 (qualifying period for unfair dismissal — interaction with s.97(2))
ERA 1996 s.86(6) (gross misconduct exception — contract terminable without notice for conduct)
ERA 1996 s.1(4)(e) (notice periods must be included in the s.1 statement of particulars)
ERA 1996 s.95(1)(a) (dismissal includes expiry of fixed-term contract — notice implications)
ITEPA 2003 ss.402A–402E (PENP — tax treatment of PILON, in force 6 April 2018)
Finance (No.2) Act 2017 (introduced the PENP rules for PILON taxation)
Employment Tribunals Extension of Jurisdiction (England and Wales) Order 1994 (SI 1994/1623) (wrongful dismissal in tribunal — £25,000 cap)

leadingCases.name: Lancaster & Duke Ltd v Wileman
leadingCases.principle: Where the employer was entitled to dismiss without notice under s.86(6) (genuine gross misconduct), the employee cannot use s.97(2) to extend the EDT by the statutory notice period. Section 97(2) takes the whole of s.86 into account, including s.86(6). If the dismissal was for genuine gross misconduct justifying summary dismissal, the EDT is the actual date of dismissal — it is not artificially extended.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that s.97(2) must be read together with s.86(6). Where an employer dismisses summarily for gross misconduct and the conduct is such that the employer was entitled at common law to dismiss without notice, the employee has no right to statutory notice under s.86(1) — and therefore s.97(2) cannot extend the EDT. The employee who was dismissed two days before her two-year anniversary could not artificially extend the EDT to reach the qualifying period.
leadingCases.significance: THE leading authority on the s.86(6)/s.97(2) interaction. Critical for employers dismissing employees near qualifying period boundaries. However, the employer cannot simply LABEL the dismissal as gross misconduct — the tribunal will examine whether the conduct actually justified summary dismissal at common law. If not, s.97(2) DOES extend the EDT.

leadingCases.name: Lenz v Starlight Starbright Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The s.97(2) extension applies automatically where notice was not given — the employee does not need to make a separate application or satisfy a threshold. Where the employer dismisses without the statutory minimum notice and is not entitled to dismiss without notice under s.86(6), the EDT is automatically extended to the date on which the statutory notice would have expired.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that s.97(2) operates automatically. Where the employer fails to give statutory notice and has no s.86(6) defence, the EDT is extended by operation of law. This extension can have significant consequences for the employee's qualifying period.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the automatic nature of the s.97(2) extension — the employee does not need to invoke it. Combined with Wileman, this establishes the complete framework: extension is automatic UNLESS s.86(6) applies.

leadingCases.name: Gisda Cyf v Barratt
leadingCases.principle: The effective date of termination for the purpose of calculating time limits and qualifying periods is the date on which the employee receives NOTICE of dismissal (or at least has a reasonable opportunity to learn of it), not necessarily the date the employer sends the dismissal letter.
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that where an employee is dismissed by letter, the EDT is the date on which the employee reads the letter or has a reasonable opportunity to read it — not the date the letter was sent or posted. This applies to the calculation of the 3-month time limit for unfair dismissal and the qualifying period.
leadingCases.significance: THE definitive UKSC authority on determining the EDT where dismissal is communicated by letter. Critical for time-limit calculations and qualifying period determinations. An employer who sends a dismissal letter on Friday evening cannot rely on the letter arriving on Saturday as the EDT if the employee does not read it until Monday.

leadingCases.name: Société Générale v Geys
leadingCases.principle: Where a contract of employment contains a PILON clause and the employer exercises it, the employment terminates on the date specified by the employer — not on the date the PILON payment is made. The contract must be clear about when termination takes effect. An employer who purports to dismiss with immediate effect while making a PILON must ensure the contractual mechanism for exercising the PILON clause has been correctly followed.
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that the employer's purported exercise of the PILON clause was ineffective because it did not comply with the contractual requirements for invoking the clause. The employment continued until the employer properly terminated it. This had significant consequences for the employee's entitlement to a termination payment worth millions.
leadingCases.significance: THE leading UKSC authority on the mechanics of PILON. Demonstrates that employers must follow the contractual requirements precisely when exercising a PILON clause. A defective exercise may mean the employment continues — with significant financial consequences for both parties, particularly where bonus or vesting dates fall during the period.

leadingCases.name: Cerberus Nuclear Ltd v Maycock
leadingCases.principle: In wrongful dismissal cases, the tribunal must determine whether the employee's conduct actually constituted gross misconduct justifying summary dismissal at common law — not merely whether the employer had a reasonable belief in gross misconduct. The test for wrongful dismissal is different from the s.98(4) 'range of reasonable responses' test for unfair dismissal.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the wrongful dismissal test requires the tribunal to make its own finding of fact about whether the conduct occurred and whether it justified summary dismissal — not merely to assess whether the employer's belief was reasonable. This is a more rigorous standard than the unfair dismissal test.
leadingCases.significance: Critical distinction between the wrongful dismissal test (tribunal's own finding) and the unfair dismissal test (range of reasonable responses). An employer may succeed on unfair dismissal (reasonable belief in misconduct) but FAIL on wrongful dismissal (the conduct did not actually justify summary dismissal).

leadingCases.name: Delaney v Staples
leadingCases.principle: A payment in lieu of notice is properly characterised as damages for breach of contract (where there is no contractual PILON clause), not as wages. This has consequences for the tax treatment and for the scope of the s.13 wages deductions regime.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that where an employer dismisses without notice and without a contractual PILON clause, the payment made to the employee in respect of the notice period is damages for breach of contract, not wages. As such, it falls outside the scope of the s.13 wages deduction provisions.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational UKHL authority on the legal characterisation of PILON. Establishes that non-contractual PILON is damages, not wages. This distinction has been partly overtaken by the 2018 PENP tax rules (which treat all PILON as earnings for tax purposes regardless of contractual basis), but remains relevant for the scope of s.13 and for the characterisation of the employer's conduct.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Notice periods
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on statutory and contractual notice periods for employers and employees.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Notice periods
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on notice periods, garden leave, and PILON.

commonErrors: Not knowing the statutory minimum notice scale — after 1 month's service: 1 week's notice for service up to 2 years; 1 additional week per year for service between 2 and 12 years; 12 weeks' maximum for service of 12+ years. The employee's statutory obligation is always 1 week regardless of service. These scales have been unchanged since 1963

Thinking that the contract can provide LESS notice than the statutory minimum — s.86(3) overrides any contractual provision for shorter notice. If the contract says '1 week' but the employee has 8 years' service, the employee is entitled to 8 weeks' notice. The contractual term is void to the extent it falls below the statutory minimum

Dismissing without notice and without a PILON clause — where the employer dismisses with immediate effect and makes a payment instead of notice, but the contract contains no PILON clause, this is technically WRONGFUL DISMISSAL (a breach of contract). The employee may claim notice pay as damages. An express PILON clause in the contract eliminates this risk (Société Générale v Geys [2012] UKSC)

Not recognising that wrongful dismissal is a DAY-ONE right — unlike unfair dismissal (which requires 2 years' service, reducing to 6 months under ERA 2025), wrongful dismissal is a breach of contract claim available from day one. An employee dismissed without proper notice on their second day of employment has a wrongful dismissal claim. The Employment Tribunal jurisdiction is capped at £25,000; larger claims go to the civil courts

Confusing wrongful dismissal with unfair dismissal — wrongful dismissal (did the employer give proper notice or have grounds for summary dismissal?) and unfair dismissal (was the dismissal fair under s.98?) are SEPARATE claims. An employer may dismiss FAIRLY but WRONGFULLY (fair reason, inadequate notice) or UNFAIRLY but not WRONGFULLY (unfair process, proper notice given). Employees commonly pursue both in parallel

Assuming that labelling a dismissal as 'gross misconduct' automatically removes the notice obligation — s.86(6) permits dismissal without notice for the conduct of the other party, but the conduct must ACTUALLY justify summary dismissal at common law. The tribunal will examine whether the conduct was genuinely so serious that it constituted a repudiatory breach. Simply labelling the reason as gross misconduct does not engage s.86(6) (Cerberus Nuclear v Maycock [2019])

Not understanding the s.97(2) EDT extension — where an employee is dismissed without notice and s.86(6) does not apply, the EDT is automatically extended by the period of statutory minimum notice under s.97(2). This can push the employee past a qualifying period boundary (e.g., from 1 year 51 weeks to 2 years). However, Lancaster & Duke v Wileman [2017] establishes that the extension does NOT apply where the employer was entitled to dismiss without notice under s.86(6)

Not applying the correct test for wrongful dismissal — the wrongful dismissal test requires the tribunal to make its own finding on whether the conduct occurred and whether it justified summary dismissal (Cerberus Nuclear [2019]). This is DIFFERENT from the s.98(4) unfair dismissal test (which asks whether the employer's belief was reasonable and the process fair). An employer may win on unfair dismissal but LOSE on wrongful dismissal

Overlooking the tax treatment of PILON since 6 April 2018 — ALL PILON (contractual or non-contractual) is now subject to income tax and NIC through the Post-Employment Notice Pay (PENP) calculation. The pre-2018 planning opportunity (non-contractual PILON qualifying for the £30,000 tax-free exemption) has been closed by Finance (No.2) Act 2017

Not knowing that the employee's statutory notice is always 1 week — regardless of length of service. The employee's obligation under s.86(2) is 1 week's notice after 1 month's service. Employers who try to impose statutory notice obligations on employees that mirror the employer's scale (e.g., '8 weeks' notice for 8 years' service') are applying the wrong provision — the employee's statutory minimum is always 1 week

Not including notice period information in the s.1 statement — s.1(4)(e) requires the statement of employment particulars to include the notice periods for both employer and employee. Failure to include this information is a s.1 breach, triggering the EA 2002 s.38 penalty (2–4 weeks' pay) if any other claim succeeds

Not recognising that ERA 2025 does NOT change statutory notice periods — the notice scale in s.86 is unchanged by ERA 2025. However, the combination of the reduced qualifying period (6 months from 2027) and the removal of the compensatory award cap makes notice compliance even more critical — wrongful dismissal claims will increasingly be accompanied by uncapped unfair dismissal claims

sections.title: Right to written statement of reasons for dismissal
sections.summary: An employee who is dismissed by their employer is entitled, on request, to be provided with a written statement giving particulars of the reasons for the dismissal. The employer must provide the statement within 14 days of the request. The employee must have been continuously employed for at least two years at the effective date of termination (s.92(3)) — UNLESS the employee is dismissed while pregnant or during maternity leave, in which case the right is AUTOMATIC (no request required) and there is NO qualifying period (s.92(4)–(4A)). Unreasonable failure to provide written reasons, or provision of inadequate or untrue reasons, entitles the employee to a mandatory award of 2 weeks' pay (s.93(2)). The written statement is admissible as evidence in any proceedings. ERA 2025 aligns the qualifying period with the new 6-month unfair dismissal qualifying period — employees dismissed during the qualifying period or initial period will not have the right (except for pregnancy/maternity).
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is dismissed by his employer is entitled, if he requests it, to be provided by his employer within fourteen days of the request with a written statement giving particulars of the reasons for the dismissal.

(2) The employer of an employee is bound to provide a written statement under this section even if the employee has not requested one, if—

(a) the employee is dismissed at any time while she is pregnant, or

(b) the employee is dismissed at any time after the end of her ordinary maternity leave period or additional maternity leave period in circumstances in which her contract of employment is by virtue of the relevant regulations, as defined by section 235(2A), treated as continuing during the period of leave.

(3) An employee does not have a right to a written statement under this section unless on the effective date of termination the employee has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with that date.

(4) Subsection (3) does not apply if the employee is a person to whom subsection (2) applies.

(4A) Subsection (3) does not apply if the dismissal is treated as unfair by virtue of any of the following provisions—

(a) section 99 (leave for family reasons),
(b) section 100 (health and safety cases),
(c) section 101A (working time cases),
(d) section 103A (protected disclosure),
(e) section 104C (flexible working).

(5) A written statement under this section is admissible in evidence in any proceedings.

(6) Subject to subsections (7) and (8), in this section and in section 93 "the effective date of termination" has the same meaning as it has for the purposes of Part X.

(7) Where the employee is dismissed with notice, the effective date of termination is the date on which the notice expires.

(8) Where the employee is dismissed without notice, the effective date of termination is the date on which the termination takes effect.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 92 forces transparency — it compels the employer to commit to a reason for dismissal in writing. This is strategically significant because the written statement is ADMISSIBLE AS EVIDENCE in any subsequent tribunal proceedings. The reason stated in the s.92 statement will be scrutinised against the reason the employer advances in the s.98(1) proceedings. If the employer states one reason in the s.92 statement and advances a different reason at tribunal, their credibility is severely damaged. The 14-day deadline is strict. Unreasonable failure triggers a mandatory 2 weeks' pay award — the tribunal has no discretion to award less.

onRequest.scope: The employee must REQUEST the written statement. The employer must provide it within 14 days of the request. The qualifying period (currently 2 years, reducing to 6 months under ERA 2025) must be met.
onRequest.practicalNote: The request can be oral or in writing — there is no prescribed form. Best practice is to make the request in writing (email is sufficient) to create an evidence trail.

automatic.scope: Where the employee is dismissed while PREGNANT or during/after MATERNITY LEAVE, the employer must provide the written statement AUTOMATICALLY — without the employee requesting it. No qualifying period applies (s.92(4)). This reflects the particularly vulnerable position of employees dismissed during pregnancy or maternity and the high frequency of discrimination in this context.

remedy.unreasonableFailure: Where the tribunal finds that the employer unreasonably failed to provide a written statement, or that the particulars provided are inadequate or untrue, it shall make an award of 2 WEEKS' PAY. This is a MANDATORY award — the tribunal must make it. The week's pay is subject to the statutory cap (£719 from 6 April 2025).
remedy.maximumAward: 2 × £719 = £1,438 (from 6 April 2025). While modest, the significance of the s.93 award is that it is awarded IN ADDITION to any unfair dismissal compensation. It is a separate, standalone remedy.
remedy.practicalNote: The real significance of s.92/93 is not the 2 weeks' pay — it is the EVIDENTIAL value. The employer's written statement of reasons is admissible evidence. If the employer states a different reason at tribunal, the inconsistency is devastating for the employer's credibility.

evidentialSignificance.principle: s.92(5) provides that the written statement is admissible in evidence in any proceedings. This means: (1) the employee can use the employer's stated reason against them if the reason changes; (2) the tribunal will scrutinise any discrepancy between the s.92 statement and the reason advanced in the s.98(1) proceedings; (3) the employer's reluctance or refusal to provide written reasons creates an adverse inference — the tribunal may infer that the employer cannot justify the dismissal.
evidentialSignificance.practicalNote: Many employers avoid providing s.92 statements precisely because they fear committing to a reason that can be scrutinised. This fear is well-founded — but the remedy for non-provision (2 weeks' pay) combined with the adverse inference makes avoidance counterproductive.

versions.text: [The substantive provisions of s.92 were amended by the Employment Act 2002 (to add the automatic provision for pregnancy/maternity) and the Employment Relations Act 1999 (to reduce the qualifying period from 2 years to 1 year, then restored to 2 years by the Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 2012 (SI 2012/989)). The current qualifying period is 2 years.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted; amended by ERA 1999, EA 2002, SI 2012/989
versions.policyRationale: The qualifying period for written reasons has tracked the unfair dismissal qualifying period — it was reduced to 1 year in 1999 and restored to 2 years in 2012 (alongside the unfair dismissal qualifying period). The pregnancy/maternity automatic provision was added in 2002 to address the particular vulnerability of employees dismissed in these circumstances.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 amends s.92 to align the qualifying period with the new 6-month unfair dismissal qualifying period. However, this is NOT a universal day-one right. Under ERA 2025, employees dismissed DURING the qualifying period (first 6 months) or the 'initial period of employment' (which begins after the qualifying period) will NOT have the right to a written statement of reasons — unless they are dismissed for an automatically unfair reason (s.99, s.100, s.103A, s.104C) or while pregnant/on maternity leave. The practical effect: the s.92 right will be available from 6 months' service instead of 2 years, but not from day one (except for pregnancy/maternity and auto-unfair).
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Expected January 2027 (aligned with unfair dismissal qualifying period reduction)
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the extension from 2 years to 6 months significantly increases the number of employees who can request written reasons. However, the exclusion of employees dismissed during the qualifying period and initial period limits the practical impact during the early stages of employment.

refs: ERA 1996 s.92 (this section — right to written statement of reasons for dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.93 (complaint to tribunal for unreasonable failure — mandatory 2 weeks' pay award)
ERA 1996 s.92(2) (automatic provision — pregnancy and maternity, no request needed)
ERA 1996 s.92(3) (qualifying period — currently 2 years, reducing to 6 months under ERA 2025)
ERA 1996 s.92(4)–(4A) (exceptions to qualifying period — pregnancy/maternity, auto-unfair reasons)
ERA 1996 s.92(5) (written statement admissible in evidence in any proceedings)
ERA 1996 s.98(1) (employer must show reason for dismissal — consistency with s.92 statement is critical)
ERA 2025 (amends qualifying period to align with 6-month unfair dismissal qualifying period)
SI 2012/989 (restored qualifying period to 2 years from 6 April 2012)
ERA 1996 s.227 (week's pay cap — £719 from 6 April 2025, applied to s.93 award)

leadingCases.name: Catherine Haigh Harlequin Hair Design v Seed
leadingCases.principle: The written statement must contain sufficient detail to enable the employee to understand the reason for dismissal. A vague or generic statement (e.g., 'misconduct' without specifying the conduct) may be found to be 'inadequate' under s.93(1), triggering the mandatory 2 weeks' pay award.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the employer's written statement of reasons must provide the employee with enough information to understand why they were dismissed. A one-word statement such as 'misconduct' or 'redundancy' without further particulars may be inadequate.
leadingCases.significance: THE leading authority on what constitutes an 'adequate' s.92 statement. Establishes that the statement must give sufficient particulars — not merely a label. The employer should identify the specific conduct, the specific capability concern, or the specific redundancy circumstances.

leadingCases.name: Horsley v Cascades Kingston Mill Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The s.93 award of 2 weeks' pay is mandatory once the tribunal finds an unreasonable failure to provide written reasons or that the reasons given were inadequate or untrue. The tribunal has no discretion to decline to make the award or to vary the amount.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the s.93 award is not discretionary. Once the statutory conditions are met (unreasonable failure, inadequate, or untrue), the tribunal MUST award 2 weeks' pay.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the mandatory nature of the s.93 remedy. Important because tribunals sometimes overlook the s.93 claim or treat it as discretionary — it is not.

leadingCases.name: Gilham v Kent County Council (No.2)
leadingCases.principle: The written statement of reasons is admissible as evidence of the employer's reason for dismissal. If the employer advances a different reason at tribunal, the inconsistency will be examined and may undermine the employer's credibility.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the s.92 statement is admissible in evidence and that the tribunal should consider any inconsistency between the reasons stated in writing and the reasons advanced at the hearing.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the evidential function of the s.92 statement. The statement pins the employer to a reason — changing the reason later damages credibility. This is why s.92 is strategically significant even though the financial remedy (2 weeks' pay) is modest.

leadingCases.name: Harvard Securities plc v Younghusband
leadingCases.principle: The employer's failure to provide written reasons within 14 days is 'unreasonable' unless the employer can show a good reason for the delay. The 14-day deadline is strict — it runs from the date of the employee's request, not from the date the employer received the request.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the 14-day deadline should be treated strictly. An employer who ignores or delays responding to a s.92 request without good reason has unreasonably failed to provide the statement.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the strict nature of the 14-day deadline. Employers should have a process for responding to s.92 requests promptly — delegating the request to HR or management does not excuse delay.

leadingCases.name: Abernethy v Mott, Hay and Anderson
leadingCases.principle: The 'reason' for dismissal is the set of facts or beliefs known to the employer that caused them to dismiss the employee. This definition applies equally to s.92 (written reasons) and s.98(1) (reason for dismissal in unfair dismissal proceedings). The s.92 statement should reflect the same reason that the employer would advance under s.98(1).
leadingCases.heldText: Cairns LJ defined the 'reason for dismissal' as 'a set of facts known to the employer, or it may be of beliefs held by him, which cause him to dismiss the employee.' This definition governs both the s.92 statement and the s.98 proceedings.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational definition of 'reason for dismissal' — cross-referenced in both s.92 and s.98. The s.92 statement should articulate the Abernethy reason: the facts or beliefs that caused the dismissal. This creates a binding consistency requirement between the written statement and the tribunal defence.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Dismissal: what you need to know
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance covering the employer's obligation to provide written reasons for dismissal, including the 14-day deadline and the pregnancy/maternity automatic provision.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Dismissal: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the right to written reasons for dismissal.

commonErrors: Not providing written reasons when requested — an employee with the qualifying period (currently 2 years, reducing to 6 months under ERA 2025) who requests written reasons for their dismissal is entitled to receive them within 14 DAYS. Unreasonable failure triggers a MANDATORY award of 2 weeks' pay (s.93). The employer cannot refuse on the basis that the reason is 'confidential' or 'sensitive'

Not providing written reasons AUTOMATICALLY for pregnancy/maternity dismissals — s.92(2) requires the employer to provide written reasons WITHOUT the employee requesting them when the employee is dismissed while pregnant or during/after maternity leave. There is NO qualifying period for this automatic right (s.92(4)). Many employers are unaware of this automatic obligation

Providing vague or generic reasons — Catherine Haigh v Seed [1990] establishes that the statement must contain sufficient detail for the employee to understand the reason. A one-word answer ('misconduct', 'redundancy', 'capability') without further particulars is inadequate and triggers the s.93 mandatory award

Stating a different reason in the s.92 statement from the reason advanced at tribunal — the s.92 statement is ADMISSIBLE AS EVIDENCE (s.92(5)). If the employer states 'redundancy' in the written reasons but argues 'capability' at tribunal, the inconsistency severely damages the employer's credibility (Gilham v Kent [1985]). The s.92 statement pins the employer to a reason

Not responding within 14 days — the 14-day deadline runs from the date of the employee's request. Harvard Securities v Younghusband [1990] confirms that the deadline is strict. An employer who ignores or delays the response without good reason has 'unreasonably failed' under s.93

Thinking the s.93 award is discretionary — the 2 weeks' pay award is MANDATORY once the tribunal finds unreasonable failure, inadequate reasons, or untrue reasons (Horsley v Cascades [2009]). The tribunal has no discretion to decline the award or reduce it

Not recognising the evidential significance — the real value of the s.92 statement is not the 2 weeks' pay (modest) but the EVIDENCE it creates. The employer's written commitment to a reason for dismissal can be used against them in unfair dismissal proceedings, discrimination proceedings, and wrongful dismissal proceedings. An employer who provides a carefully crafted s.92 statement has committed to a position; an employer who refuses has created an adverse inference

Assuming ERA 2025 makes s.92 a universal day-one right — ERA 2025 aligns the s.92 qualifying period with the new 6-month unfair dismissal qualifying period, but employees dismissed DURING the qualifying period (first 6 months) or the 'initial period of employment' will NOT have the right to written reasons — unless dismissed while pregnant/on maternity leave or for an automatically unfair reason. s.92 is NOT becoming a day-one right for all employees

Not recognising that s.92(4A) exempts automatically unfair dismissal categories from the qualifying period — employees dismissed for reasons falling within s.99 (family leave), s.100 (health and safety), s.101A (working time), s.103A (whistleblowing), or s.104C (flexible working) are entitled to written reasons regardless of length of service. This is important because these employees often have the strongest need for written reasons

Not understanding the interaction between s.92 and the Abernethy definition — Abernethy v Mott [1974] defines the 'reason for dismissal' as the facts or beliefs that caused the employer to dismiss. The s.92 statement should articulate this Abernethy reason. If it articulates a different reason, the inconsistency between the s.92 statement and the employer's s.98(1) case is exposed

Overlooking the s.92 claim when advising on unfair dismissal — the s.93 award (2 weeks' pay) is awarded IN ADDITION to any unfair dismissal compensation. Many representatives forget to include the s.92/93 claim alongside the unfair dismissal claim. It adds up to £1,438 (at the 2025/26 rate) with no additional evidential burden — the tribunal simply checks whether the employer provided adequate reasons within 14 days

sections.title: The right not to be unfairly dismissed
sections.summary: Every employee has the right not to be unfairly dismissed by their employer. This is THE foundational employment right in UK law — introduced by the Industrial Relations Act 1971 and consolidated in the ERA 1996. The right applies to EMPLOYEES only (not workers) and currently requires two years' continuous employment (s.108(1)), subject to exceptions for automatically unfair dismissals (which have no qualifying period). ERA 2025 reduces the qualifying period from two years to SIX MONTHS from 1 January 2027 — the most significant change to unfair dismissal law since 1971. The Government's original proposal for day-one rights was dropped. The 'initial period of employment' concept (lighter-touch dismissal during early employment) was also dropped. The compensatory award cap was removed entirely. The power to change the qualifying period by secondary legislation was removed — future changes require primary legislation. An estimated 6.3 million additional employees will gain unfair dismissal protection.
sections.text:
(1) An employee has the right not to be unfairly dismissed by his employer.

(2) Subsection (1) has effect subject to the following provisions of this Part (in particular, sections 108 to 110) and to the provisions of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 (in particular, sections 237 to 239).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 94 CREATES the right. It does not define what makes a dismissal unfair — that is the function of s.98 (fairness), s.99–105 (automatically unfair reasons), and the body of case law interpreting them. Section 94 is subject to the qualifying period in s.108 — the employee must satisfy the qualifying period before they can exercise the right. The section is deliberately simple: it states the right and signposts the provisions that qualify it. Its brevity belies its transformative significance — before 1971, employers could dismiss employees at will (subject only to notice and contract).

architecturalPosition.principle: Section 94 is the GATEWAY provision. An employee who wishes to claim unfair dismissal must first establish that they have the s.94 right (by satisfying the qualifying period or establishing an automatically unfair reason). Once the right is established, the tribunal proceeds to assess fairness under s.98 (or automatically unfair dismissal under s.99–105). The remedies flow from ss.112–127 (reinstatement, re-engagement, basic award, compensatory award).
architecturalPosition.claimPath: s.94 (right exists?) → s.95 (was the employee dismissed?) → s.108 (qualifying period satisfied?) → s.98 (was the dismissal fair?) → ss.119–124 (remedy)

current.effectiveSince: 6 April 2012 (Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 2012 (SI 2012/989))
current.history: The qualifying period has fluctuated: 6 months (1971–1974), 1 year (1974–1985), 2 years (1985–1999), 1 year (1999–2012), 2 years (2012–present)

era2025.transitionalProvisions: NONE — all employees with 6 months' service on 1 January 2027 gain protection immediately. An employee hired on 1 July 2026 will gain protection on 1 January 2027. An employee hired on 1 January 2027 will need to complete 6 months (gaining protection around 1 July 2027).
era2025.dayOneDropped: The Government's original proposal (in the Employment Rights Bill) was to make unfair dismissal a DAY-ONE right, with an 'initial period of employment' during which a lighter-touch dismissal procedure would apply. The House of Lords refused to pass this provision. On 27 November 2025, the Government dropped both day-one rights and the initial period concept, agreeing to a 6-month qualifying period instead.
era2025.initialPeriodDropped: The 'initial period of employment' (IPE) concept — which would have created a separate, lighter-touch dismissal regime for new employees — was dropped entirely from the final Act. There is NO initial period. From 6 months' service, the full s.98 fairness test applies with no modification.
era2025.secondaryLegislationPowerRemoved: ERA 2025 removes the power to change the qualifying period by statutory instrument (Order). Future changes to the qualifying period will require primary legislation (an Act of Parliament). This makes the 6-month qualifying period more durable than previous periods, which were changed by secondary legislation (e.g., SI 2012/989).
era2025.capRemoved: As part of the same compromise, ERA 2025 removes the compensatory award cap entirely (s.124(1)). The cap was the lower of 52 weeks' pay or £118,223.
