From 1 January 2027, the compensatory award is UNCAPPED for all unfair dismissal claims — not just automatically unfair dismissals.
era2025.impact: The Government estimates that 6.3 million additional employees will gain unfair dismissal protection. An estimated 9,000 additional ACAS Early Conciliation referrals per year and approximately 3,000 additional tribunal claims per year are anticipated. This is the largest expected impact on the tribunal system of any ERA 2025 measure.

whoIsProtected.employees: s.94 applies to EMPLOYEES only — individuals who work under a contract of employment (s.230(1)). Workers who are not employees (s.230(3)(b)) do NOT have unfair dismissal rights.
exclusions: Workers who are not employees (s.230(3)(b) — limb (b) workers)
Members of the armed forces (with limited exceptions)
Employees with illegal contracts (but see Hounga v Allen [2014] UKSC — illegality does not automatically defeat all claims)

historicalSignificance.introduction: The right not to be unfairly dismissed was introduced by the Industrial Relations Act 1971 (s.22). Before 1971, employers had an unfettered common law right to dismiss employees at will (subject only to contractual notice). The 1971 Act created, for the first time, a statutory right to challenge dismissal on the grounds of fairness — a right that had no equivalent in common law.
historicalSignificance.consolidation: The right was consolidated in EPCA 1978 s.54, then in ERA 1996 s.94. The core right has remained unchanged since 1971 — it is the qualifying period, the fairness test, and the remedies that have evolved.
historicalSignificance.era2025Significance: ERA 2025 represents the most significant reform to unfair dismissal since the right was created in 1971. The combination of: (1) reducing the qualifying period from 2 years to 6 months; (2) removing the compensatory award cap; and (3) locking in the qualifying period through primary legislation — fundamentally changes the balance between employer flexibility and employee protection.

versions.text:
(1) An employee has the right not to be unfairly dismissed by his employer.
(2) Subsection (1) has effect subject to the following provisions of this Part (in particular, sections 108 to 110) and to the provisions of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 (in particular, sections 237 to 239).
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating EPCA 1978 s.54, originally Industrial Relations Act 1971 s.22)
versions.policyRationale: Section 94 has not been amended since its enactment. It is the qualifying period (s.108) that has changed, not the right itself. The right remains as stated in 1996 (and substantively as stated in 1971): 'An employee has the right not to be unfairly dismissed by his employer.'

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 s.25 reduces the qualifying period from 2 years to 6 months by amending s.108(1). ERA 2025 also: (1) removes the compensatory award cap (s.124(1)); (2) removes the power to change the qualifying period by secondary legislation; (3) drops the 'initial period of employment' concept entirely. The combined effect is that from 1 January 2027, every employee with 6 months' continuous service has the full right not to be unfairly dismissed, with uncapped compensation, and this position is locked in by primary legislation.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: 1 January 2027 confirmed. No transitional provisions. All employees with 6 months' service on 1 January 2027 gain protection immediately.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: VERY HIGH — the most significant change to unfair dismissal law since 1971. 6.3 million additional employees protected. Uncapped compensation. Employers who currently rely on the 2-year qualifying period to dismiss freely during probation will face unfair dismissal exposure from 6 months. Every employer must review: probation policies, performance management frameworks, dismissal procedures, and the documentation of fair reasons from the earliest stage of employment.

refs: ERA 1996 s.94 (this section — the right not to be unfairly dismissed)
ERA 1996 s.95 (definition of dismissal — actual, constructive, expiry of fixed-term)
ERA 1996 s.98 (fairness of dismissal — the substantive test)
ERA 1996 s.99–105 (automatically unfair reasons — no qualifying period)
ERA 1996 s.108 (qualifying period — currently 2 years, reducing to 6 months from 1 January 2027)
ERA 1996 s.111 (time limit for presenting complaint — currently 3 months, extending to 6 months from October 2026)
ERA 1996 ss.112–117 (remedies — reinstatement, re-engagement, additional award)
ERA 1996 ss.119–124 (compensation — basic award, compensatory award, cap)
ERA 2025 s.25 (amends s.108(1) — qualifying period 2 years → 6 months)
ERA 2025 (removes s.124(1) compensatory award cap — uncapped from 1 January 2027)
ERA 2025 (removes power to change qualifying period by secondary legislation)
Industrial Relations Act 1971 s.22 (original introduction of the right — first time in UK law)
SI 2012/989 (increased qualifying period from 1 year to 2 years from 6 April 2012)
TULRCA 1992 ss.237–239 (dismissal and industrial action — interaction with s.94(2))

leadingCases.name: Western Excavating (ECC) Ltd v Sharp
leadingCases.principle: The right not to be unfairly dismissed extends to CONSTRUCTIVE dismissal — where the employer's conduct is so serious that it constitutes a repudiatory breach of the employment contract, and the employee resigns in response, the employee is deemed to have been dismissed under s.95(1)(c). The employee's resignation is treated as a dismissal for the purposes of s.94.
leadingCases.heldText: Lord Denning MR held that an employee is constructively dismissed if the employer commits a fundamental breach of the contract of employment and the employee resigns in response to that breach. The contract test — not the 'unreasonableness' test — is the correct approach.
leadingCases.significance: Cross-referenced from s.95. Establishes that the s.94 right extends to constructive dismissal — the employee does not have to be formally dismissed to exercise the right. The employer's repudiatory breach triggers the s.94 protection.

leadingCases.name: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd
leadingCases.principle: A dismissal may be unfair because of procedural failings even if the employer had a substantively fair reason. The question under s.98(4) is whether the employer acted reasonably in treating the reason as sufficient — and an employer who fails to follow a fair procedure has not acted reasonably. However, the compensatory award may be reduced if the employer can show that a fair procedure would have made no difference to the outcome (the 'Polkey reduction').
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that procedural fairness is an integral part of the s.98(4) assessment. An employer cannot argue that a procedural failing is irrelevant because the outcome would have been the same — but the tribunal may reduce the compensatory award to reflect the chance that a fair procedure would still have resulted in dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: THE landmark UKHL authority on procedural fairness in unfair dismissal. Cross-referenced from s.98 and s.123. Polkey establishes that the s.94 right requires not just a fair reason but a fair process — and that the remedy reflects the probability of a fair outcome.

leadingCases.name: Gisda Cyf v Barratt
leadingCases.principle: The effective date of termination (EDT) — from which the qualifying period and time limit are calculated — is the date on which the employee receives notice of dismissal or has a reasonable opportunity to learn of it. This is a statutory concept that may differ from the contractual termination date.
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that the EDT is determined by reference to when the employee actually learns of the dismissal, not when the employer sends the communication. This protects the employee's s.94 rights by ensuring that the qualifying period and time limit are calculated from a date within the employee's knowledge.
leadingCases.significance: Cross-referenced from s.86 and s.111. Ensures that the employer cannot manipulate the EDT to defeat the employee's s.94 rights by, for example, sending a dismissal letter to an unoccupied address.

leadingCases.name: Hogg v Dover College
leadingCases.principle: A unilateral and fundamental change to the employee's terms and conditions of employment may constitute a dismissal for the purposes of s.94/s.95, even if the employer does not formally terminate the contract. If the employer imposes entirely new terms that bear no resemblance to the original contract, the original contract has been terminated by the employer's conduct.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that where the employer fundamentally changed the employee's role (from full-time teacher to part-time tutor), this was not a variation of the existing contract but a termination of one contract and the imposition of a new one. The employee was dismissed under s.95(1)(a).
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates that the s.94 right can be engaged even without a formal dismissal letter. An employer who fundamentally changes the employee's role, pay, or status has effectively dismissed the employee — giving rise to s.94 rights.

leadingCases.name: Kwik Save Stores Ltd v Swain
leadingCases.principle: The s.94 right is a personal right that cannot be contracted out of. Any agreement that purports to waive the right to claim unfair dismissal is void under s.203(1) — unless it is a valid settlement agreement (s.203(2)(f)) or an ACAS-conciliated settlement (s.203(2)(e)).
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the right not to be unfairly dismissed is a statutory right that cannot be excluded by agreement. An employer who includes a waiver clause in a contract or settlement that does not comply with s.203 requirements cannot enforce that waiver.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the non-waivable nature of the s.94 right. Important for employers using compromise agreements or exit packages — the agreement must comply with s.203 formalities (settlement agreement or ACAS settlement) to be valid.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Unfair dismissal
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on unfair dismissal rights, including the qualifying period, the fairness test, and the remedies.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Dismissal: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the right not to be unfairly dismissed, including automatically unfair reasons.

commonErrors: Assuming that the right to unfair dismissal is a day-one right under ERA 2025 — the Government's original proposal for day-one rights was DROPPED during parliamentary ping-pong in November 2025. ERA 2025 sets a 6-MONTH qualifying period, not day one. However, automatically unfair dismissals (s.99, s.100, s.103A, etc.) remain genuine day-one rights with no qualifying period

Not knowing that the 'initial period of employment' (IPE) concept was DROPPED — the Employment Rights Bill originally proposed an 'initial period' during which a lighter-touch dismissal procedure would apply. This concept was dropped entirely from the final Act. From 6 months' service, the FULL s.98 fairness test applies — there is no lighter-touch regime

Not recognising that the compensatory award cap is being REMOVED — ERA 2025 removes the s.124(1) cap entirely from 1 January 2027. ALL unfair dismissal claims will carry UNCAPPED compensation — not just automatically unfair dismissals. Combined with the 6-month qualifying period, this fundamentally changes the employer's risk calculation

Not knowing that the power to change the qualifying period by secondary legislation has been REMOVED — ERA 2025 removes the power to vary the qualifying period by Order (statutory instrument). Future changes require primary legislation (an Act of Parliament). This makes the 6-month period more durable than any previous qualifying period

Thinking that employees hired before 1 January 2027 are not affected — there are NO transitional provisions. ALL employees with 6 months' service on 1 January 2027 gain protection immediately. An employee hired on 1 July 2026 gains protection on 1 January 2027. Employers must assess their entire workforce

Continuing to operate on the assumption that 'you can dismiss freely in the first two years' — from 1 January 2027, the free dismissal window closes at 6 months. Employers must have fair reasons and fair processes from the 6-month mark. Informal probationary dismissals, 'not the right fit' reasoning, and undocumented decisions will carry unfair dismissal exposure

Not recognising that s.94 applies to EMPLOYEES only — workers who are not employees (s.230(3)(b) limb (b) workers) do NOT have unfair dismissal rights under s.94. They may have other protections (discrimination, whistleblowing detriment under s.47B, wages deductions under s.13) but not the s.94 right. Employment status under s.230 is the gateway

Not understanding that s.94 creates the RIGHT but s.98 defines FAIRNESS — s.94 is two sentences. It creates the right and signposts the qualifying provisions. The substance — what makes a dismissal fair or unfair — is in s.98 (potentially fair reasons, reasonableness) and s.99–105 (automatically unfair reasons). A claim under 'section 94' is actually tested under s.98

Overlooking the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures — the tribunal must take the Code into account when considering the reasonableness of the dismissal under s.98(4). A failure to follow the Code may lead to an uplift in the compensatory award of up to 25% (s.207A TULRCA). The Code is integral to the s.94 right in practice

Not appreciating the scale of the ERA 2025 change — the Government estimates that 6.3 million additional employees will gain unfair dismissal protection. An estimated 9,000 additional ACAS Early Conciliation referrals per year and approximately 3,000 additional tribunal claims per year are anticipated. This is the largest expected impact on the tribunal system of any ERA 2025 measure

Not knowing that the right was only introduced in 1971 — before the Industrial Relations Act 1971, there was NO statutory right not to be unfairly dismissed. Employers could dismiss at will (subject only to contractual notice). The s.94 right is 55 years old — young by UK employment law standards. ERA 2025 represents the most significant reform to this right in its entire history

Not recognising that the s.94 right cannot be waived or contracted out of — any agreement purporting to exclude the right to claim unfair dismissal is void under s.203(1), unless it is a valid settlement agreement (s.203(2)(f)) or an ACAS-conciliated settlement (s.203(2)(e)). Employers who include blanket waiver clauses in contracts or exit packages without complying with s.203 formalities cannot enforce those waivers (Kwik Save v Swain [1997])

sections.title: Circumstances in which an employee is dismissed
sections.summary: Defines 'dismissal' for the purposes of Part X (unfair dismissal). Three circumstances constitute dismissal: (a) the employer terminates the contract with or without notice; (b) a fixed-term contract expires without renewal; (c) the employee terminates the contract (with or without notice) in circumstances where they are entitled to do so by reason of the employer's conduct — constructive dismissal. The employee must establish that a dismissal occurred under one of these three limbs before the fairness test in s.98 is engaged. Constructive dismissal under s.95(1)(c) is one of the most heavily litigated areas of employment law, generating a vast body of case law on the implied term of mutual trust and confidence, the 'last straw' doctrine, affirmation, and the employee's response to the breach.
sections.text:
(1) For the purposes of this Part an employee is dismissed by his employer if (and, subject to subsection (2) and section 96, only if)—

(a) the contract under which he is employed is terminated by the employer (whether with or without notice),

(b) he is employed under a limited-term contract and that contract terminates by virtue of the limiting event without being renewed under the same contract, or

(c) the employee terminates the contract under which he is employed (with or without notice) in circumstances in which he is entitled to terminate it without notice by reason of the employer's conduct.

(2) An employee shall be taken to be dismissed by his employer for the purposes of this Part if—

(a) the employer gives notice to the employee to terminate his contract of employment, and

(b) at a time within the period of that notice the employee gives notice to the employer to terminate the contract of employment on a date earlier than the date on which the employer's notice is due to expire;

and the reason for the dismissal is to be taken to be the reason for which the employer's notice is given.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 95 is the gateway provision. Without a 'dismissal' within the meaning of s.95(1), there is no jurisdiction to consider fairness under s.98. The three limbs cover: express dismissal by the employer (s.95(1)(a)), expiry of a fixed-term contract (s.95(1)(b)), and constructive dismissal (s.95(1)(c)). The burden of proving that a dismissal occurred rests on the employee. In constructive dismissal cases, this means the employee must prove: (1) a repudiatory breach of contract by the employer; (2) that the employee resigned IN RESPONSE to that breach; and (3) that the employee did not affirm the contract after the breach by delaying too long before resigning.

constructiveDismissal.doctrinalFoundation: Constructive dismissal under s.95(1)(c) requires the application of general contractual principles. The employee must show that the employer committed a repudiatory breach of an express or implied term of the contract, and that the employee resigned in response to that breach without undue delay (which would amount to affirmation). The most commonly relied-upon implied term is the implied term of mutual trust and confidence, established by the House of Lords in Malik v BCCI [1997] UKHL 23.

impliedTermOfTrustAndConfidence.definition: The employer shall not, without reasonable and proper cause, conduct itself in a manner calculated or likely to destroy or seriously damage the relationship of trust and confidence between employer and employee.
impliedTermOfTrustAndConfidence.source: Malik v Bank of Credit and Commerce International SA [1997] UKHL 23; Woods v WM Car Services (Peterborough) Ltd [1981] ICR 666 (EAT)
impliedTermOfTrustAndConfidence.significance: This implied term exists in every contract of employment. It cannot be excluded by express terms. A breach of this term is always a fundamental (repudiatory) breach — there is no such thing as a minor breach of the implied term of trust and confidence (Morrow v Safeway Stores plc [2002] IRLR 9). This means that once the employee establishes conduct that is 'calculated or likely to destroy or seriously damage' the relationship, the breach is automatically fundamental and entitles the employee to resign.

lastStrawDoctrine.definition: Where a series of acts by the employer, viewed cumulatively, amounts to a breach of the implied term of trust and confidence, the employee is entitled to treat the final act (the 'last straw') as the trigger for resignation — even if the last straw, viewed in isolation, would not itself constitute a breach. The last straw must contribute to the breach; it need not be unreasonable or blameworthy, but it must not be utterly trivial or innocuous (London Borough of Waltham Forest v Omilaju [2004] EWCA Civ 1493).
lastStrawDoctrine.source: Omilaju v London Borough of Waltham Forest [2004] EWCA Civ 1493; Lewis v Motorworld Garages Ltd [1986] ICR 157 (CA)

kaurFramework.source: Kaur v Leeds Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust [2018] EWCA Civ 978
questions: (1) What was the most recent act (or omission) on the part of the employer which the employee says caused, or triggered, his or her resignation?
(2) Has he or she affirmed the contract since that act?
(3) If not, was that act (or omission) by itself a repudiatory breach of contract?

(4) If not, was it nevertheless a part (applying the approach explained in Omilaju) of a course of conduct comprising several acts and omissions which, viewed cumulatively, amounted to a repudiatory breach of the Malik term? (If it was, there is no need for any separate consideration of a possible previous affirmation.)
(5) Did the employee resign in response (or partly in response) to that breach?
kaurFramework.significance: The Kaur five-question framework is now the standard analytical tool applied by all tribunals in constructive dismissal cases. Question (4) is particularly important: in a last straw case, previous affirmation of earlier breaches is irrelevant — the employee may rely on the TOTALITY of the employer's conduct, including previously affirmed acts, provided the last straw forms part of the series.

affirmation.definition: An employee who continues to work without protest after a repudiatory breach may be taken to have affirmed the contract, losing the right to resign and claim constructive dismissal based on that breach. However, affirmation is not easily established — mere continuation of employment, particularly where the employee is protesting or pursuing a grievance, does not necessarily constitute affirmation (W E Cox Toner (International) Ltd v Crook [1981] ICR 823).
affirmation.source: W E Cox Toner (International) Ltd v Crook [1981] ICR 823 (EAT); Western Excavating (ECC) Ltd v Sharp [1978] QB 761 (CA)
affirmation.practicalNote: The interaction between affirmation and the last straw doctrine is critical. Under Kaur, in a last straw case, the employee can rely on previously affirmed breaches as part of the cumulative course of conduct — provided there is a new act forming part of the series. This means that affirmation of individual acts does not prevent the employee from relying on the overall pattern.

noRangeTest.principle: The 'range of reasonable responses' test that applies to s.98(4) does NOT apply to the question of whether the employer committed a repudiatory breach. The tribunal asks a single question: did the employer's conduct amount to a breach of the implied term of trust and confidence? If yes, the employee was entitled to resign. The employer cannot argue that its conduct fell within a 'range of reasonable employer conduct' — this is a contractual test, not a reasonableness test.
noRangeTest.source: Buckland v Bournemouth University Higher Education Corporation [2010] EWCA Civ 121
noRangeTest.significance: Buckland establishes a critical asymmetry in unfair dismissal law. When assessing whether the employer's decision to dismiss was fair (s.98(4)), the tribunal applies the range of reasonable responses test — which is employer-favourable. But when assessing whether the employer's conduct amounts to a repudiatory breach (s.95(1)(c)), the tribunal applies an objective contractual test with NO range — which is employee-favourable. This means constructive dismissal claims can succeed even where the employer's conduct might be considered within the range of reasonable responses under s.98(4).

versions.text:
(1) For the purposes of this Part an employee is dismissed by his employer if (and, subject to subsection (2) and section 96, only if)—

(a) the contract under which he is employed is terminated by the employer (whether with or without notice),

(b) he is employed under a contract for a fixed term and that term expires without being renewed under the same contract, or

(c) the employee terminates the contract under which he is employed (with or without notice) in circumstances in which he is entitled to terminate it without notice by reason of the employer's conduct.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted
versions.policyRationale: The 1996 Act consolidated the constructive dismissal provision from EPCA 1978 s.55(2)(c). The wording of s.95(1)(c) — 'entitled to terminate it without notice by reason of the employer's conduct' — was carried forward unchanged. The three limbs of dismissal (express, fixed-term expiry, constructive) have remained constant since the original enactment.

versions.text:
(1) For the purposes of this Part an employee is dismissed by his employer if (and, subject to subsection (2) and section 96, only if)—

(a) the contract under which he is employed is terminated by the employer (whether with or without notice),

(b) he is employed under a limited-term contract and that contract terminates by virtue of the limiting event without being renewed under the same contract, or

(c) the employee terminates the contract under which he is employed (with or without notice) in circumstances in which he is entitled to terminate it without notice by reason of the employer's conduct.
versions.amendedBy: Fixed-term Employees (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2002 (SI 2002/2034)
versions.policyRationale: The Fixed-term Employees Regulations 2002 replaced 'a contract for a fixed term' with 'a limited-term contract' and 'that term expires' with 'that contract terminates by virtue of the limiting event'. This broadened s.95(1)(b) to cover not just time-limited contracts but also task-limited and event-limited contracts — ensuring that the expiry of any form of limited-term contract constitutes a dismissal for unfair dismissal purposes.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.95. However, the repeal of s.108 (qualifying period) and the introduction of the initial period of employment regime fundamentally change the PRACTICAL significance of s.95. Under the current law, many constructive dismissal claims fail at the s.108 gateway because the employee lacks two years' service. Once s.108 is repealed, every employee will be able to claim constructive dismissal from day one — significantly increasing the volume of s.95(1)(c) claims and the exposure for employers whose conduct breaches the implied term of trust and confidence.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: ERA 2025 ss.3–6 and Schedule 2 not yet in force as at March 2026
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: HIGH — the removal of the qualifying period will substantially increase constructive dismissal claims. Employers who currently rely on the two-year qualifying period as a barrier to constructive dismissal claims from short-service employees will face day-one exposure for any conduct that breaches the implied term.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 does not change what constructive dismissal IS — the Malik term, the Western Excavating contract test, the Kaur framework, and the Omilaju last straw doctrine all continue to apply unchanged. What ERA 2025 changes is WHO CAN CLAIM. Currently, only employees with two years' service can bring a constructive dismissal claim (because s.108 requires two years for s.94, and s.95 defines dismissal for the purposes of s.94). Once s.108 is repealed, any employee — including a day-one employee — can resign and claim constructive dismissal if the employer's conduct amounts to a repudiatory breach.
refs: ERA 1996 s.94 (the right not to be unfairly dismissed — s.95 defines 'dismissal' for the purposes of s.94)
ERA 1996 s.95(1)(a) (express dismissal by the employer — with or without notice)
ERA 1996 s.95(1)(b) (expiry of a limited-term contract without renewal — constitutes dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.95(1)(c) (constructive dismissal — employee terminates by reason of employer's conduct)
ERA 1996 s.95(2) (counter-notice during employer's notice period — still treated as dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.96 (failure to permit return after childbirth — deemed dismissal, now largely spent)
ERA 1996 s.97 (effective date of termination — determines qualifying period calculation and time limits)
ERA 1996 s.98 (fairness test — applied AFTER s.95 dismissal is established)
ERA 1996 s.108 (qualifying period — must be satisfied before s.95 dismissal can be tested under s.98, to be repealed by ERA 2025)
ERA 1996 s.111 (time limit for presenting complaint — 3 months from effective date of termination)
ERA 1996 s.136 (definition of dismissal for redundancy payment purposes — similar but not identical to s.95)
ERA 2025 Schedule 2 (repeal of s.108 — will remove the qualifying period barrier to constructive dismissal claims)
ACAS Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015) — relevant to whether employer's disciplinary conduct breaches the implied term
EqA 2010 s.39(2)(c)/(d) (discrimination by dismissal — a constructive dismissal may simultaneously be a discriminatory dismissal)

leadingCases.name: Western Excavating (ECC) Ltd v Sharp
leadingCases.principle: Constructive dismissal requires a CONTRACTUAL analysis, not a reasonableness analysis. The employee must show that the employer committed a repudiatory breach of an express or implied term of the contract, that the employee resigned in response to that breach, and that the employee did not delay so long as to affirm the contract.
leadingCases.heldText: Lord Denning MR held: 'If the employer is guilty of conduct which is a significant breach going to the root of the contract of employment, or which shows that the employer no longer intends to be bound by one or more of the essential terms of the contract, then the employee is entitled to treat himself as discharged from any further performance. If he does so, then he terminates the contract by reason of the employer's conduct. He is constructively dismissed.' The Court of Appeal rejected the alternative 'reasonableness test' which would have asked simply whether the employer acted unreasonably — holding that such a test would be too uncertain and would blur the distinction between constructive dismissal (s.95) and fairness (s.98).
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on constructive dismissal. Established the 'contract test' that has governed every constructive dismissal case for over 45 years. The distinction between the contract test (s.95) and the reasonableness test (s.98) is fundamental to the structure of unfair dismissal law. Western Excavating makes clear that the employee must first establish a contractual breach (s.95), and only then does the tribunal consider fairness (s.98).

leadingCases.name: Malik v Bank of Credit and Commerce International SA (in compulsory liquidation)
leadingCases.principle: There is an implied term in every contract of employment that the employer shall not, without reasonable and proper cause, conduct itself in a manner calculated or likely to destroy or seriously damage the relationship of trust and confidence between employer and employee.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords confirmed the existence of the implied term of mutual trust and confidence and held that it was a term of fundamental importance in the employment relationship. Lord Steyn described it as a term 'implied by law as a necessary incident of the contract of employment'. The implied term imposes a mutual obligation — both employer and employee are bound by it — but in practice it is almost always invoked by employees in constructive dismissal claims.
leadingCases.significance: The leading UKHL authority on the implied term of trust and confidence. Although the implied term had been recognised earlier (notably in Woods v WM Car Services [1981] and Courtaulds Northern Textiles v Andrew [1979]), Malik provided the definitive House of Lords endorsement. The implied term is now the basis for the vast majority of constructive dismissal claims — employees typically allege breach of the Malik term rather than breach of an express contractual term.

leadingCases.name: Morrow v Safeway Stores plc
leadingCases.principle: Any breach of the implied term of mutual trust and confidence is necessarily a fundamental (repudiatory) breach. There is no concept of a 'minor' breach of the Malik term — the very nature of the term means that any breach goes to the root of the contract.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the implied term of trust and confidence is, by its very formulation, concerned only with conduct that is 'calculated or likely to destroy or seriously damage' the employment relationship. Conduct that meets this threshold is, by definition, a fundamental breach of contract. There is therefore no need for the employee to separately establish that the breach was 'fundamental' or 'repudiatory' — once the Malik term is breached, the breach is automatically fundamental.
leadingCases.significance: Eliminates a step in the constructive dismissal analysis that had caused confusion. Before Morrow, some tribunals required the employee to prove both (a) a breach of the implied term AND (b) that the breach was fundamental. Morrow clarifies that these are one and the same — any breach of the Malik term is by definition fundamental.

leadingCases.name: London Borough of Waltham Forest v Omilaju
leadingCases.principle: In a 'last straw' constructive dismissal, the final act need not itself be a breach of contract or even unreasonable — it need only be part of a series of acts which, cumulatively, amount to a breach of the Malik term. However, the last straw must not be 'utterly trivial' or an 'innocuous act' — it must be capable of contributing, however slightly, to the cumulative breach.
leadingCases.heldText: Dyson LJ set out the last straw doctrine at paragraph 19: 'The quality that the final straw must have is that it should be an act in a series whose cumulative effect is to amount to a breach of the implied term. I do not use the phrase "an act in a series" in a technical sense. The act does not have to be of the same character as the earlier acts. Its essential quality is that, when taken in conjunction with the earlier acts, it amounts to a breach of the implied term of trust and confidence. It must contribute something to that breach, although what it adds may be relatively insignificant.' At paragraph 22: 'The last straw must not be an innocuous act, nor must it be something which amounts to a completely innocuous act on the part of the employer.'
leadingCases.significance: THE leading authority on the last straw doctrine. Dyson LJ's formulation at paragraphs 19–22 is cited in every last straw constructive dismissal case. The practical importance is that employees can build a constructive dismissal claim from a series of acts, none of which in isolation would be sufficient — provided the final act crosses the threshold from 'utterly innocuous' to 'contributing something'. Confirmed and applied by Kaur [2018].

leadingCases.name: Kaur v Leeds Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust
leadingCases.principle: In the normal constructive dismissal case, the tribunal should ask five questions: (1) What was the most recent act or omission that the employee says triggered the resignation? (2) Has the employee affirmed the contract since that act? (3) If not, was that act itself a repudiatory breach? (4) If not, was it part of a course of conduct which, viewed cumulatively, amounted to a breach of the Malik term? (5) Did the employee resign in response to that breach? In a last straw case (question 4), there is no need to consider whether the employee previously affirmed individual earlier acts — the employee may rely on the totality of the employer's conduct.
leadingCases.heldText: Underhill LJ held: 'In the normal case where an employee claims to have been constructively dismissed it is sufficient for a tribunal to ask itself the following questions: (1) What was the most recent act (or omission) on the part of the employer which the employee says caused, or triggered, his or her resignation? (2) Has he or she affirmed the contract since that act? (3) If not, was that act (or omission) by itself a repudiatory breach of contract? (4) If not, was it nevertheless a part (applying the approach explained in Omilaju) of a course of conduct comprising several acts and omissions which, viewed cumulatively, amounted to a (repudiatory) breach of the Malik term? (If it was, there is no need for any separate consideration of a possible previous affirmation, for the reason given at the end of para. 45 above.) (5) Did the employee resign in response (or partly in response) to that breach?'
leadingCases.significance: The definitive EWCA framework for analysing constructive dismissal claims. The five questions provide a structured analytical tool that all tribunals now follow. The critical innovation at question (4) is the clarification that in a last straw case, previous affirmation of individual acts is irrelevant — the employee may rely on the ENTIRE course of conduct. This resolved a long-standing uncertainty about whether affirmation of earlier breaches prevented reliance on them in a cumulative claim.

leadingCases.name: Buckland v Bournemouth University Higher Education Corporation
leadingCases.principle: The 'range of reasonable responses' test does not apply to the question of whether the employer committed a repudiatory breach of contract. The tribunal must make its own objective assessment of whether the employer's conduct amounted to a breach of the Malik term. The employer cannot argue that its conduct fell within a range of reasonable employer conduct.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the tribunal must itself assess whether the employer's conduct was 'calculated or likely to destroy or seriously damage' the relationship of trust and confidence. This is an objective question — it is not for the employer to argue that some employers might have behaved in the same way. The range of reasonable responses test is confined to s.98(4) (the reasonableness of the decision to dismiss) and has no application to s.95(1)(c) (whether the employer's conduct amounted to a repudiatory breach).
leadingCases.significance: Establishes a critical asymmetry in unfair dismissal law. Under s.98(4), the employer benefits from the range of reasonable responses test — the tribunal asks whether the decision fell WITHIN the range, not whether it was the BEST decision. But under s.95(1)(c), there is no such protection — the tribunal makes its own objective assessment. This means constructive dismissal claims are structurally more favourable to the employee than direct unfair dismissal claims at the breach stage. Also confirmed that the employer cannot 'cure' a repudiatory breach — once the breach has occurred, the employee's right to resign crystallises immediately.

leadingCases.name: W E Cox Toner (International) Ltd v Crook
leadingCases.principle: An employee who continues to work for a significant period after a repudiatory breach by the employer, without protesting or reserving their position, may be taken to have affirmed the contract — waiving the right to resign and claim constructive dismissal based on that breach. However, merely continuing to work while pursuing a grievance or seeking to resolve the issue does not amount to affirmation.
leadingCases.heldText: Browne-Wilkinson J held that mere continued working does not, by itself, conclusively demonstrate affirmation. The tribunal must consider whether the employee's conduct, viewed objectively, demonstrated an intention to continue with the contract notwithstanding the breach. Factors relevant to affirmation include: the length of the delay, whether the employee protested, whether a grievance was raised, and whether the employee was seeking to resolve the situation.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on affirmation in constructive dismissal. Provides the analytical framework for tribunals assessing whether delay defeats a constructive dismissal claim. The practical message for employees is: if the employer commits a repudiatory breach, the employee must resign promptly or at least make clear they are not accepting the breach. For employers, the message is: the passage of time may save you from a constructive dismissal claim, but only if the employee genuinely accepted the new position without protest.

leadingCases.name: Nottinghamshire County Council v Meikle
leadingCases.principle: A constructive dismissal may simultaneously constitute disability discrimination where the employer's repudiatory breach arises from a failure to make reasonable adjustments or from conduct related to the employee's disability. The employee can pursue both unfair dismissal and discrimination claims arising from the same resignation.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that where an employee with a disability resigns by reason of the employer's failure to make reasonable adjustments, and that failure amounts to a repudiatory breach of the implied term, the resignation is both a constructive dismissal (s.95(1)(c)) and an act of discrimination (EqA 2010 s.39(2)(c)). The discrimination compensation is uncapped, while the unfair dismissal compensatory award is subject to the s.124 cap.
leadingCases.significance: Critical for employers: a constructive dismissal involving a disabled employee carries dual liability — the capped UD compensatory award PLUS the uncapped discrimination compensation (including injury to feelings). Many constructive dismissal claims by disabled employees are now pleaded as both UD and discrimination to access the uncapped jurisdiction.

leadingCases.name: Hogg v Dover College
leadingCases.principle: Where an employer unilaterally imposes a fundamental change to the terms of employment — such as a significant reduction in hours, status, or responsibilities — the employee does not need to resign to claim dismissal. The imposition of the new terms can itself constitute a termination of the old contract (an express dismissal under s.95(1)(a)) coupled with an offer of re-engagement on new terms.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that where an employer told a teacher that his duties would be 'seriously curtailed' and that he would no longer hold his existing position, this amounted to a dismissal under s.95(1)(a) — not merely a breach entitling constructive dismissal. The old contract was terminated by the employer's unilateral imposition of fundamentally different terms.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes an important alternative route for employees facing fundamental contractual changes. Instead of claiming constructive dismissal (s.95(1)(c)), the employee can argue that the employer's imposition of new terms actually terminated the old contract (s.95(1)(a)). This is tactically significant because an express dismissal under s.95(1)(a) does not require the employee to resign — they can continue working under the new terms while pursuing their unfair dismissal claim based on the termination of the old contract.

regulatoryGuidance.title: If your employer has done something seriously wrong (constructive dismissal)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on what constitutes constructive dismissal, when to resign, and how to raise a grievance before resigning. Advises employees to raise a formal grievance before resigning where possible, as this may strengthen their position and demonstrates they did not simply walk away.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Relevant to constructive dismissal in two ways: (1) the employer's failure to follow the Code in a disciplinary process may itself constitute or contribute to a breach of the implied term; (2) the employee's failure to raise a grievance before resigning may result in a reduction to the compensatory award under TULRCA 1992 s.207A.

commonErrors: Confusing the contractual test (s.95(1)(c)) with the reasonableness test (s.98(4)) — constructive dismissal requires a REPUDIATORY BREACH OF CONTRACT, not merely unreasonable conduct. The employer's conduct must be 'calculated or likely to destroy or seriously damage' the relationship of trust and confidence (Malik [1997]). Conduct that is unreasonable but does not reach this threshold does not give rise to constructive dismissal

Assuming that if the employer's conduct was within the range of reasonable responses, it cannot be a repudiatory breach — Buckland v Bournemouth University [2010] confirms that the range of reasonable responses test does NOT apply to the constructive dismissal analysis. The tribunal makes its own objective assessment of whether the Malik term was breached

Treating delay as automatically defeating a constructive dismissal claim — Cox Toner v Crook [1981] establishes that mere continuation of employment is not conclusive affirmation. The tribunal considers whether the employee was protesting, pursuing a grievance, or seeking to resolve the situation. An employee who delays while exhausting the internal grievance procedure may not have affirmed

Overlooking the last straw doctrine — Omilaju [2004] and Kaur [2018] establish that an employee may rely on a series of acts which, viewed cumulatively, amount to a breach of the Malik term. No single act in the series needs to be a breach on its own. The last straw must contribute something to the breach but need not be unreasonable — it just must not be utterly trivial

Not recognising that a fair disciplinary process CANNOT constitute or contribute to a breach of the implied term — Kaur [2018] confirms that a disciplinary process conducted 'in perfectly proper fashion' cannot be a last straw or part of a cumulative breach, even if the employee disagrees with the outcome. This is a critical defence for employers who have followed their procedures correctly

Assuming the employee must resign immediately — the law requires the employee to resign 'in response to' the breach, but does not require immediate resignation. The employee may take a reasonable time to consider their position, seek advice, and attempt to resolve the issue through a grievance. What constitutes 'reasonable' depends on the circumstances

Failing to recognise that the employer cannot 'cure' a repudiatory breach — Buckland [2010] confirms that once the employer has committed a repudiatory breach, the employee's right to resign crystallises immediately. The employer cannot subsequently withdraw the breach or offer to remedy the situation — the employee has an irrevocable right to treat the contract as terminated. However, the employee may CHOOSE to affirm — the right is the employee's, not the employer's

Not connecting constructive dismissal with discrimination claims — Meikle [2004] establishes that a constructive dismissal arising from discriminatory conduct (particularly failure to make reasonable adjustments for disability) gives rise to BOTH a capped unfair dismissal claim AND an uncapped discrimination claim. Many employers focus on the UD exposure and overlook the potentially much larger discrimination liability

Treating s.95(1)(a) and s.95(1)(c) as mutually exclusive — Hogg v Dover College [1990] establishes that where an employer unilaterally imposes fundamentally different terms, the employee may have been expressly dismissed under s.95(1)(a) (termination of the old contract) rather than constructively dismissed under s.95(1)(c). This is tactically significant because the employee does not need to resign

Failing to advise employees to raise a grievance before resigning — while not strictly required, the ACAS Code expects it, and a failure to raise a grievance may result in a reduction of the compensatory award by up to 25% under TULRCA 1992 s.207A. The employer's response to the grievance may also itself constitute or contribute to the repudiatory breach

Not documenting the employer's conduct and the employee's responses — constructive dismissal cases are intensely fact-sensitive. The tribunal will examine the chronology in detail: what did the employer do, when, how did the employee respond, was a grievance raised, how long before the resignation? Contemporaneous documentary evidence is critical for both sides

sections.title: General — fairness of dismissal: reason and reasonableness
sections.summary: The governing provision for all ordinary unfair dismissal claims. Establishes a two-stage test: (1) the employer must show the reason (or principal reason) for the dismissal, and that the reason is one of five potentially fair reasons (capability, conduct, redundancy, statutory bar, or some other substantial reason); (2) the tribunal must determine whether the employer acted reasonably or unreasonably in treating that reason as sufficient to dismiss, applying the 'range of reasonable responses' test and having regard to the size and administrative resources of the employer, equity, and the substantial merits of the case. ERA 2025 ss.3–6 will introduce the 'initial period of employment' framework — a new regime governing dismissal during a statutory probation period — when commenced.
sections.text:
(1) In determining for the purposes of this Part whether the dismissal of an employee is fair or unfair, it is for the employer to show—

(a) the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal, and

(b) that it is either a reason falling within subsection (2) or some other substantial reason of a kind such as to justify the dismissal of an employee holding the position which the employee held.

(2) A reason falls within this subsection if it—

(a) relates to the capability or qualifications of the employee for performing work of the kind which he was employed by the employer to do,

(b) relates to the conduct of the employee,

(ba) is retirement of the employee,

(c) is that the employee was redundant, or

(d) is that the employee could not continue to work in the position which he held without contravention (either on his part or on that of his employer) of a duty or restriction imposed by or under an enactment.

(2A) Subsections (1) and (2) are subject to sections 98ZA to 98ZF.

(3) In subsection (2)(a)—

(a) "capability", in relation to an employee, means his capability assessed by reference to skill, aptitude, health or any other physical or mental quality, and

(b) "qualifications", in relation to an employee, means any degree, diploma or other academic, technical or professional qualification relevant to the position which he held.

(4) In any other case where the employer has fulfilled the requirements of subsection (1), the determination of the question whether the dismissal is fair or unfair (having regard to the reason shown by the employer)—

(a) depends on whether in the circumstances (including the size and administrative resources of the employer's undertaking) the employer acted reasonably or unreasonably in treating it as a sufficient reason for dismissing the employee, and

(b) shall be determined in accordance with equity and the substantial merits of the case.

(6) Subsection (4) is subject to—

(a) sections 98A to 107B of this Act, and

(b) sections 152, 153, 238 and 238A of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 (dismissal on ground of trade union membership or activities or in connection with industrial action).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 98 does not ask whether the tribunal would have dismissed. It asks whether the employer's decision to dismiss fell within the range of reasonable responses open to a reasonable employer in those circumstances. The burden of proving the reason rests on the employer (s.98(1)). The burden of proving reasonableness is neutral — neither side bears it (s.98(4)). Procedural fairness is an integral part of the reasonableness assessment — a dismissal for a fair reason carried out through an unfair process is itself unfair.

fairReasons.note: Section 98(2) identifies four specific potentially fair reasons, and s.98(1)(b) adds a fifth residual category. Identifying the correct reason is the first step — the tribunal's approach to reasonableness differs depending on the category.

capability.scope: Covers both performance-based capability (skill, aptitude) and health-based capability (health or any other physical or mental quality). These are analytically distinct: performance capability requires evidence of underperformance and an opportunity to improve; health capability requires medical evidence, consultation with the employee, and consideration of reasonable adjustments under EqA 2010 where disability is engaged.
capability.keyCase: Alidair Ltd v Taylor [1978] ICR 445 (CA) — capability assessed by reference to the standard required for the job, not the employee's absolute ability
capability.practicalNote: Capability dismissals for ill health carry significant discrimination risk. Where the employee's health condition constitutes a disability under EqA 2010 s.6, the employer must consider reasonable adjustments (EqA 2010 s.20) before concluding that dismissal is the only option. Failure to do so may render the dismissal both unfair under s.98(4) and discriminatory under EqA 2010 s.15 (discrimination arising from disability).

conduct.scope: Covers all forms of employee misconduct including gross misconduct warranting summary dismissal and less serious misconduct warranting warnings and a staged disciplinary process. The ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures is the benchmark for procedural fairness in conduct cases.
conduct.keyCase: British Home Stores Ltd v Burchell [1978] IRLR 379 (EAT) — the three-stage test: (1) genuine belief in misconduct; (2) reasonable grounds for that belief; (3) reasonable investigation
conduct.practicalNote: The Burchell test governs misconduct dismissals but does not replace the statutory test in s.98(4). In Taylor v OCS Group [2006] EWCA Civ 702, the Court of Appeal emphasised that the tribunal must assess overall fairness — investigation, hearing, and decision together — not mechanically apply Burchell as a checklist.

redundancy.scope: Redundancy is defined separately in s.139. Where the reason for dismissal is redundancy, the dismissal is potentially fair — but the employer must still satisfy s.98(4) reasonableness through fair selection criteria, adequate individual consultation, and a genuine search for alternative employment.
redundancy.keyCase: Williams v Compair Maxam Ltd [1982] ICR 156 (EAT) — the five principles for a fair redundancy: maximum warning, union consultation on criteria, objective criteria, fair application, alternative employment search
redundancy.practicalNote: See redundancy-intelligence.json for the complete cross-cutting redundancy intelligence briefing. The redundancy intelligence briefing maps the full intersection of s.98, s.139, s.105, TULRCA s.188, EqA 2010, and TUPE Reg.7.

statutoryBar.scope: Where the employee cannot continue to work in their position without contravening a legal requirement — for example, a driver who loses their driving licence, or a worker who loses their right to work in the UK. The employer must still act reasonably: consider whether alternative employment is available that does not require the breached qualification or entitlement.
statutoryBar.practicalNote: Post-Brexit, this category has increased significance for employers of EU/EEA nationals whose immigration status changes. Loss of the right to work in the UK is a statutory bar ground, but the employer must still investigate and consult before dismissing.

sosr.scope: Some Other Substantial Reason — the residual category. The reason must be 'of a kind such as to justify the dismissal of an employee holding the position which the employee held'. This is a broad category that catches legitimate business reasons not falling within the four specific categories. Common SOSR reasons include: business reorganisation falling short of redundancy, personality clashes destroying working relationships, pressure from a major client to remove an employee, expiry of a fixed-term contract, and refusal to accept a contractual change.
sosr.keyCase: Hollister v National Farmers' Union [1979] ICR 542 (CA) — reorganisation amounting to SOSR; RS Components v Irwin [1973] ICR 535 — refusal to accept restrictive covenants as SOSR
sosr.practicalNote: SOSR is not a 'catch-all' that allows dismissal for any reason the employer finds compelling. The reason must be 'substantial' and must be of a kind that justifies dismissal of someone in the employee's particular position. The tribunal still applies the full s.98(4) reasonableness test. ERA 2025 introduces specific provisions for dismissal during the 'initial period of employment' — these interact with SOSR where the reason relates to the employee's suitability.

versions.text:
(1) In determining for the purposes of this Part whether the dismissal of an employee is fair or unfair, it is for the employer to show—

(a) the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal, and

(b) that it is either a reason falling within subsection (2) or some other substantial reason of a kind such as to justify the dismissal of an employee holding the position which the employee held.

(2) A reason falls within this subsection if it—

(a) relates to the capability or qualifications of the employee for performing work of the kind which he was employed by the employer to do,

(b) relates to the conduct of the employee,

(c) is that the employee was redundant, or

(d) is that the employee could not continue to work in the position which he held without contravention (either on his part or on that of his employer) of a duty or restriction imposed by or under an enactment.

(4) Where the employer has fulfilled the requirements of subsection (1), the determination of the question whether the dismissal is fair or unfair (having regard to the reason shown by the employer)—

(a) depends on whether in the circumstances (including the size and administrative resources of the employer's undertaking) the employer acted reasonably or unreasonably in treating it as a sufficient reason for dismissing the employee, and

(b) shall be determined in accordance with equity and the substantial merits of the case.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted
versions.policyRationale: The 1996 Act consolidated the unfair dismissal fairness test from EPCA 1978 s.57. Four potentially fair reasons were specified. The reasonableness test in s.98(4) was carried forward without substantive change from EPCA 1978. No retirement reason existed at this stage.

versions.text: [As above, but with the insertion of: s.98(2)(ba) 'is retirement of the employee'; s.98(2A) 'Subsections (1) and (2) are subject to sections 98ZA to 98ZF'; and s.98(3A) providing that where the reason for dismissal is retirement, fairness is determined under s.98ZG rather than s.98(4).]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Equality (Age) Regulations 2006 (SI 2006/1031) Sch.8 para.22
versions.policyRationale: The Age Regulations 2006 (implementing the EU Framework Directive 2000/78/EC) inserted retirement as a fifth potentially fair reason (s.98(2)(ba)) and created a separate fairness regime for retirement dismissals (ss.98ZA–98ZG). This allowed employers to retire employees at 65 (the default retirement age) using a simplified procedure. The retirement reason was subsequently repealed in 2011.

versions.text: [Current version — retirement subsections repealed. s.98(2)(ba), (3A) repealed by Employment Equality (Repeal of Retirement Age Provisions) Regulations 2011 (SI 2011/1069). s.98(4) wording amended to 'In any other case where' by the same Regulations.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Equality (Repeal of Retirement Age Provisions) Regulations 2011 (SI 2011/1069)
versions.policyRationale: The Coalition Government abolished the default retirement age of 65. The retirement reason (s.98(2)(ba)) was repealed, and the separate retirement fairness regime (ss.98ZA–98ZG, except 98ZA which was retained for other purposes) was removed. Employers can no longer dismiss employees by reason of retirement using the simplified procedure. An employer-justified retirement age (EJRA) may still be lawful where the employer can show it is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim under EqA 2010, but this must be assessed under the ordinary s.98(4) reasonableness test and the Equality Act — not under the former statutory retirement regime.

pendingAmendment.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 ss.3–6 and Schedule 2
pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 introduces the 'initial period of employment' (IPE) — a statutory probation period during which the employer may dismiss using a 'lighter touch' process. ERA 2025 s.3 inserts new sections into Part X creating a distinct fairness regime for dismissals during the IPE. The full s.98(4) reasonableness test continues to apply after the IPE ends. The IPE does NOT create a new qualifying period — the employee has the right to claim unfair dismissal from day one, but the procedural requirements and compensation during the IPE are different from post-IPE dismissals.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Not yet in force as at March 2026. The Government's stated intention is Autumn 2026 at the earliest. The duration of the IPE (expected ~9 months) and the procedural requirements are subject to consultation.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: CRITICAL — transforms the s.98 landscape. Every employer will need to understand the distinction between IPE dismissals (lighter process, lower compensation) and post-IPE dismissals (full s.98(4) reasonableness test). The ACEI unfair dismissal category will require recalibration.

initialPeriodDetail.lighterProcess: The employer will need to: (1) hold a meeting with the employee to discuss concerns; (2) allow the employee to be accompanied; (3) make a decision. This is simpler than the full ACAS Code procedure but is NOT a free hand to dismiss without any process.
initialPeriodDetail.validReasons: Dismissal during the IPE must be for a reason 'related to the employee' — capability, conduct, or the employee's suitability for the role. Reasons NOT related to the employee (redundancy, business reorganisation) are not covered by the IPE regime and must satisfy the full s.98(4) test even during the IPE.
initialPeriodDetail.compensation: Expected to be lower than the standard compensatory award for dismissals during the IPE, but the exact quantum is subject to consultation.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 ss.3–6 fundamentally restructure the fairness assessment for short-service employees. The interaction between the IPE regime and the existing s.98(4) test is the most complex area of the ERA 2025 reforms. Employers who dismiss during the IPE for a reason NOT related to the employee (e.g., redundancy) will face the full s.98(4) test regardless of the employee's length of service. This is a critical distinction that many employers will misunderstand.
refs: ERA 1996 s.94 (the right not to be unfairly dismissed — s.98 determines whether the dismissal of that right was fair)
ERA 1996 s.95 (definition of dismissal — must be established before s.98 is engaged)
ERA 1996 s.98(2)(a)–(d) (the four specific potentially fair reasons)
ERA 1996 s.98(1)(b) (SOSR — the residual 'some other substantial reason' category)
ERA 1996 s.98(4) (the reasonableness test — the operative provision in every ordinary UD case)
ERA 1996 s.98(6) (subjects s.98(4) to the automatically unfair dismissal provisions and TULRCA)
ERA 1996 ss.98ZA–98ZF (provisions retained from the retirement regime — limited current application)
ERA 1996 s.99–107B (automatically unfair reasons — bypass s.98(4) entirely)
ERA 1996 s.108 (qualifying period — gateway condition for engaging s.98, to be repealed by ERA 2025)
ERA 1996 s.111A (pre-termination negotiations — inadmissible evidence in s.98(4) proceedings)
ERA 1996 s.123 (compensatory award calculation — 'just and equitable' — informed by s.98(4) findings)
ERA 1996 s.124 (compensatory award cap — applied to the s.123 award)
ERA 1996 s.139 (definition of redundancy — relevant to s.98(2)(c) reason)
ERA 2025 ss.3–6 (initial period of employment — not yet in force)
ERA 2025 Schedule 2 (repeal of s.108 — not yet in force)
TULRCA 1992 ss.152–153 (trade union reasons — automatically unfair under s.98(6))
TULRCA 1992 ss.238, 238A (industrial action dismissals — subject to separate regime under s.98(6))
ACAS Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)
EqA 2010 s.15 (discrimination arising from disability — parallel claim in health capability dismissals)
EqA 2010 s.20 (reasonable adjustments — duty engaged in health capability cases)

leadingCases.name: Iceland Frozen Foods Ltd v Jones
leadingCases.principle: The tribunal must not substitute its own view of the correct decision for that of the employer. The function of the tribunal is to determine whether the decision to dismiss fell within the range of reasonable responses which a reasonable employer might have adopted. If there are two possible reasonable responses — one to dismiss and one not to dismiss — the employer's decision to dismiss is not unfair merely because the tribunal would have chosen not to dismiss.
leadingCases.heldText: Browne-Wilkinson J held: 'The correct approach for the industrial tribunal to adopt in answering the question posed by section 98(4) is as follows: (1) the starting point should always be the words of section 98(4) themselves; (2) in applying the section an industrial tribunal must consider the reasonableness of the employer's conduct, not simply whether they (the members of the industrial tribunal) consider the dismissal to be fair; (3) in judging the reasonableness of the employer's conduct an industrial tribunal must not substitute its decision as to what was the right course to adopt for that of the employer; (4) in many (though not all) cases there is a band of reasonable responses to the employee's conduct within which one employer might reasonably take one view, another quite reasonably take another; (5) the function of the industrial tribunal, as an industrial jury, is to determine whether in the particular circumstances of each case the decision to dismiss the employee fell within the band of reasonable responses which a reasonable employer might have adopted.'
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on s.98(4). The 'range of reasonable responses' (or 'band of reasonable responses') test has governed every ordinary unfair dismissal case since 1983. Despite periodic judicial criticism (notably by Morison J in Haddon v Van den Bergh Foods [1999] ICR 1150, which was overruled), the test was emphatically reaffirmed by the Court of Appeal in Post Office v Foley; HSBC v Madden [2000] ICR 1283. The Iceland test remains the law.

leadingCases.name: British Home Stores Ltd v Burchell
leadingCases.principle: In misconduct dismissals, the employer must show: (1) a genuine belief that the employee committed the misconduct; (2) reasonable grounds for that belief; (3) a reasonable investigation to sustain that belief. The tribunal does not determine whether the employee actually committed the misconduct — only whether the employer's belief was reasonably held after a reasonable investigation.
leadingCases.heldText: Arnold J held: 'What the tribunal have to decide every time is, broadly expressed, whether the employer who discharged the employee on the ground of the misconduct in question (usually, though not necessarily, parsing under section 57(2)(b)) entertained a reasonable suspicion amounting to a belief in the guilt of the employee of that misconduct at that time. That is really stating shortly and compendiously what is in fact more than one element. First of all, there must be established by the employer the fact of that belief; that the employer did believe it. Secondly, that the employer had in his mind reasonable grounds upon which to sustain that belief. And thirdly, we think, that the employer, at the stage at which he formed that belief on those grounds, at any rate at the final stage at which he formed that belief on those grounds, had carried out as much investigation into the matter as was reasonable in all the circumstances of the case.'
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on misconduct dismissals. The three-stage Burchell test is cited in virtually every misconduct case. Note: Burchell was decided before the burden of proof on reasonableness was made neutral (by Employment Act 1980 s.6, now reflected in s.98(4)). The test remains valid but the burden of proving reasonableness no longer falls on the employer — it is neutral. The three-stage framework is now understood as a guide to the tribunal's assessment, not as a burden on the employer.

leadingCases.name: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Procedural fairness is an integral part of the s.98(4) reasonableness assessment. An employer who fails to follow a fair procedure — consultation in redundancy, investigation in misconduct, warnings in capability — cannot argue that the outcome would have been the same regardless. The procedural failure makes the dismissal unfair. However, the tribunal may reduce the compensatory award to reflect the percentage chance that the employee would have been dismissed in any event if a fair procedure had been followed (the 'Polkey reduction').
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the question of whether a dismissal was fair or unfair must be assessed by reference to what the employer actually did, not by reference to what would have happened if they had acted differently. An employer who omitted a basic procedural step could not escape a finding of unfair dismissal by arguing it would have made no difference. However, the tribunal could take account of the procedural failure when assessing compensation — reducing the compensatory award by the percentage chance that the employee would have been dismissed anyway.
leadingCases.significance: One of the most important UKHL employment law decisions. Established two principles that operate in every unfair dismissal case: (1) procedural fairness is constitutive of reasonableness, not separate from it; (2) the 'Polkey reduction' — the tribunal may reduce compensation where a fair procedure would have made no difference to the outcome. The Polkey reduction is applied in a very high proportion of successful unfair dismissal claims. Software 2000 Ltd v Andrews [2007] ICR 825 (EAT) provides guidance on how tribunals should approach the Polkey assessment.

leadingCases.name: Abernethy v Mott, Hay and Anderson
leadingCases.principle: The 'reason for dismissal' is the set of facts known to the employer, or the set of beliefs held by the employer, which caused the employer to dismiss the employee. It is not the label the employer attaches to the reason.
leadingCases.heldText: Cairns LJ held: 'A reason for dismissal of an employee is a set of facts known to the employer, or it may be of beliefs held by him, which cause him to dismiss the employee.' The Court of Appeal made clear that the tribunal must identify the real reason for the dismissal by examining the facts and beliefs that actually operated on the employer's mind, not the characterisation the employer chose to give to the reason.
leadingCases.significance: The foundational authority on what constitutes a 'reason for dismissal' under s.98(1). This definition is applied at the first stage of every unfair dismissal case. It prevents employers from disguising the true reason — for example, labelling a discriminatory dismissal as a redundancy or a personality-driven dismissal as a capability issue.

leadingCases.name: Devis v Atkins
leadingCases.principle: The tribunal can only consider the reason that was known to the employer at the time of the dismissal. Evidence of misconduct discovered after the dismissal cannot be used to justify the dismissal — although it may be relevant to the compensatory award (reducing it to nil if the employee would inevitably have been dismissed had the information been known).
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that fairness under s.98(4) must be assessed by reference to the reason the employer relied on at the time of dismissal. An employer cannot retrospectively justify a dismissal by pointing to misconduct discovered afterwards. However, post-dismissal evidence may reduce the compensatory award under what is now s.123(1).
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the temporal focus of the fairness assessment — the tribunal looks at the reason and circumstances at the point of dismissal, not with hindsight. The interaction between Devis (no retrospective justification for fairness) and the Polkey reduction (compensation may be reduced for inevitable dismissal) is a sophisticated area that frequently arises in practice.

leadingCases.name: Post Office v Foley; HSBC Bank plc v Madden
leadingCases.principle: The Court of Appeal emphatically reaffirmed the range of reasonable responses test from Iceland Frozen Foods, overruling the EAT's attempt in Haddon v Van den Bergh Foods [1999] ICR 1150 to move towards a 'pure reasonableness' test.
leadingCases.heldText: Mummery LJ, delivering the judgment of the Court, held that the range of reasonable responses test was not a gloss on the statutory test in s.98(4) — it was the correct application of the statutory test. The EAT in Haddon had been wrong to suggest that the Iceland test had been applied incorrectly or that it created an unhelpful 'mantra'. The range of reasonable responses approach applies both to the substantive decision to dismiss and to the procedure by which the decision was reached.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive EWCA endorsement of the range of reasonable responses test. After Foley/Madden, there is no room for argument that the tribunal can substitute its own view. The test applies to BOTH the substance of the decision AND the procedure. This case settled a long-running debate about whether the Iceland test was compatible with the statutory language of s.98(4).

leadingCases.name: Taylor v OCS Group Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The tribunal must assess the overall fairness of the dismissal — not mechanically apply the Burchell test as a checklist. Where the investigation was inadequate but the hearing was fair, or vice versa, the tribunal must step back and assess whether the totality of the process rendered the dismissal fair or unfair.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the Burchell test is a useful guide but the ultimate question is always the statutory test in s.98(4) — did the employer act reasonably in all the circumstances? A failure at one stage of the Burchell analysis does not automatically render the dismissal unfair if deficiencies were remedied at a later stage (for example, an inadequate initial investigation supplemented by a thorough appeal hearing).
leadingCases.significance: Important corrective to an overly mechanical application of Burchell. Establishes that the tribunal must look at the whole picture — not just tick boxes. Particularly relevant where the appeal hearing cures defects in the first-instance hearing.

leadingCases.name: W Weddel & Co Ltd v Tepper
leadingCases.principle: Where two employees are suspected of misconduct and the employer cannot identify which one is responsible, the employer may fairly dismiss both — provided the employer has carried out a reasonable investigation and genuinely cannot identify the individual culprit.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that where misconduct has undoubtedly occurred and a reasonable investigation has been carried out but the employer still cannot determine which of a group of employees was responsible, it may be reasonable to dismiss all members of the group. The employer must be able to show: (1) a genuine act of misconduct; (2) a thorough investigation; (3) all members of the group could reasonably be suspected; (4) the employer genuinely could not identify the culprit.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on 'group dismissals' (sometimes called 'blanket dismissals') where individual culpability cannot be established. Frequently relied upon in retail, warehouse, and cash-handling environments where stock loss or theft has occurred but the individual responsible cannot be identified. The investigation must be genuinely thorough — a superficial investigation followed by blanket dismissal will be unfair.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The ACAS Code is the primary regulatory guidance for s.98(4) purposes. Employment tribunals are required by TULRCA 1992 s.207A to take the Code into account when assessing reasonableness. An unreasonable failure to follow the Code may result in a 25% uplift to the compensatory award (or a 25% reduction if the employee unreasonably failed to follow it). The Code covers: establishing facts, informing the employee of the case, holding a hearing, allowing accompaniment, deciding the outcome, providing the right of appeal.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Guide: Discipline and grievances at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Non-statutory companion guide to the Code of Practice, providing detailed practical guidance on investigation, suspension, hearing conduct, appeals, and record-keeping. Not admissible in tribunal proceedings in the same way as the Code, but widely used as a benchmark for good practice.

commonErrors: Substituting the tribunal's (or the employer's solicitor's) view for the employer's own assessment — the s.98(4) test asks whether the employer's decision fell within the range of reasonable responses, NOT whether it was the decision the tribunal would have made. An employer's decision to dismiss is not unfair merely because another employer might not have dismissed (Iceland Frozen Foods v Jones [1983])

Treating the Burchell three-stage test as the statutory test — Burchell is a guide for misconduct cases, not a substitute for s.98(4). The statutory test is always the words of s.98(4) itself: did the employer act reasonably in all the circumstances? (Taylor v OCS Group [2006])

Failing to identify the correct potentially fair reason — if the employer cannot articulate a reason falling within s.98(2) or SOSR, the dismissal is automatically unfair regardless of how fair the procedure was. The reason must be identified before reasonableness is assessed (Abernethy v Mott, Hay and Anderson [1974])

Labelling the reason incorrectly — calling a performance-managed exit 'redundancy' or dressing up a personality clash as 'capability' will fail at the s.98(1) stage when the tribunal examines the real reason. The tribunal looks at the facts and beliefs that caused the dismissal, not the label (Abernethy [1974])

Omitting basic procedural steps — investigation, notification of charges, hearing, right to be accompanied, decision, appeal. Procedural fairness is integral to the s.98(4) assessment (Polkey [1987]). Even where the employer had overwhelming grounds for dismissal, a failure to follow a fair procedure renders the dismissal unfair — though the compensatory award may be reduced (the Polkey reduction)

Not following the ACAS Code of Practice — the tribunal must take the Code into account under TULRCA 1992 s.207A. An unreasonable failure to follow the Code may result in a 25% uplift to the compensatory award. The Code requires: establishing facts through investigation, informing the employee of the problem, holding a meeting, allowing accompaniment, deciding the outcome, and providing the right of appeal

Assuming the employer's investigation must achieve the standard of a criminal investigation — the Burchell test requires a 'reasonable' investigation, not a perfect one.
