The scope of the investigation must be proportionate to the nature and seriousness of the allegation and to the size and resources of the employer (s.98(4) expressly requires the tribunal to consider 'the size and administrative resources of the employer's undertaking'). A small employer is not held to the same investigatory standard as a large corporation

Not recognising that the range of reasonable responses test applies to the PROCEDURE as well as the SUBSTANTIVE decision — Post Office v Foley [2000] confirmed that the test applies to both. This means the tribunal asks: 'would a reasonable employer have conducted the procedure in this way?' — not: 'was this the best procedure?'

Ignoring the employee's length of service as a relevant factor — while not determinative, long service is a relevant circumstance under s.98(4). A single act of misconduct by an employee with 20 years' unblemished service may not justify dismissal where the same act by a probationary employee would. The tribunal considers all the circumstances

Dismissing without considering alternatives — particularly in capability and conduct cases, the tribunal will consider whether the employer considered alternatives to dismissal such as warnings, retraining, redeployment, or demotion. Dismissal is not reasonable if a less drastic sanction would have been appropriate within the range of reasonable responses

Using post-dismissal evidence to justify the dismissal — Devis v Atkins [1977] prohibits this. The fairness assessment is fixed at the point of dismissal. Evidence discovered afterwards may reduce compensation but cannot retrospectively make an unfair dismissal fair

Not preparing for the ERA 2025 initial period of employment — when commenced, employers will need two distinct fairness frameworks: the IPE regime (lighter process, for reasons related to the employee) and the full s.98(4) regime (for all other reasons and for all dismissals after the IPE ends). Conflating the two will create avoidable exposure

sections.title: Leave for family reasons — automatically unfair dismissal
sections.summary: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or principal reason) for the dismissal is of a prescribed kind, or the dismissal takes place in prescribed circumstances. The 'prescribed' reasons and circumstances are set out in regulations — primarily Regulation 20 of the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312). They cover: pregnancy, childbirth, maternity leave, adoption leave, shared parental leave, parental leave, paternity leave, parental bereavement leave, time off for dependants, and (since 6 April 2024) failure to offer priority suitable alternative employment during the 18-month protected period following childbirth. Day-one right (s.108(3)(b)). This is the provision with the highest discrimination crossover — pregnancy/maternity dismissals almost always engage BOTH s.99 (automatically unfair dismissal) AND EqA 2010 s.18 (pregnancy and maternity discrimination — uncapped compensation and injury to feelings).
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of this Part as unfairly dismissed if—

(a) the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is of a prescribed kind, or

(b) the dismissal takes place in prescribed circumstances.

(2) In this section "prescribed" means prescribed by regulations made by the Secretary of State.

(3) A reason or set of circumstances prescribed under this section must relate to—

(a) pregnancy, childbirth or maternity,

(b) ordinary, compulsory or additional maternity leave,

(c) ordinary or additional adoption leave,

(d) shared parental leave,

(e) parental leave,

(ea) paternity leave,

(eb) parental bereavement leave,

(f) time off under section 57A, or

(g) time off under section 57ZJ or 57ZL.

(4) Regulations under this section may—

(a) make different provision for different cases or circumstances;

(b) apply any enactment, in such circumstances as may be specified and subject to any conditions specified, in relation to persons regarded as unfairly dismissed by reason of section (1).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 99 is a FRAMEWORK provision — it establishes that dismissal for prescribed family-related reasons is automatically unfair, but delegates the detail of those reasons to regulations. The primary regulations are the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312), Regulation 20 (unfair dismissal) and Regulation 10 (redundancy — priority for suitable alternative employment). The provision is a day-one right (s.108(3)(b)) — no qualifying period is required. In practice, pregnancy/maternity dismissals are almost always pursued under BOTH s.99 AND EqA 2010 s.18 because the discrimination route provides uncapped compensation and injury to feelings awards that are not available under the unfair dismissal route alone.

prescribedReasons.source: Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312), Reg.20
prescribedReasons.note: Regulation 20 sets out the prescribed reasons for automatically unfair dismissal under s.99. A dismissal is automatically unfair if the reason or principal reason is connected to any of the following:

reasons.description: The employee is pregnant, has recently given birth, or the dismissal is connected to pregnancy or childbirth. This includes dismissal for pregnancy-related sickness absence, taking time off for antenatal appointments, or any other reason connected to the fact of pregnancy.

The employee took or sought to take ordinary or additional maternity leave, or any benefits connected to maternity leave. This includes dismissal for exercising rights under the maternity leave provisions, including the right to return to the same or a similar job.
The employee took or sought to take ordinary or additional adoption leave.
reasons.regulation: Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002, Reg.29

reasons.description: The employee took or sought to take shared parental leave.

The employee took or sought to take paternity leave (birth or adoption). Since 6 April 2024, paternity leave can be taken within 52 weeks of birth (extended from 56 days). ERA 2025 makes paternity leave a day-one right.
reasons.regulation: Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002, Reg.29

reasons.description: The employee took or sought to take parental leave (up to 18 weeks unpaid per child). ERA 2025 makes parental leave a day-one right.
The employee took or sought to take parental bereavement leave (Jack's Law — 2 weeks' leave following the death of a child under 18). In force since 6 April 2020.
reasons.regulation: Parental Bereavement Leave and Pay Regulations 2020

reasons.description: The employee took or sought to take time off for dependants under s.57A (reasonable time off to deal with emergencies involving dependants — including illness, death, breakdown of care arrangements, and school incidents).

reasons.category: Redundancy during protected period — failure to offer suitable alternative
reasons.description: Since 6 April 2024 (Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023), the employer failed to offer a suitable alternative vacancy to an employee who was pregnant, on maternity/adoption/shared parental leave, or within the 18-month post-birth/adoption protected period. If a suitable alternative vacancy exists and is not offered to the protected employee with priority, the redundancy dismissal is automatically unfair.

redundancyProtection.title: Enhanced redundancy protection — 18-month protected period
redundancyProtection.instrument: Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 (in force 6 April 2024, via Maternity Leave, Adoption Leave and Shared Parental Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2024 (SI 2024/468))

protectedPeriod.pregnancy: From the date the employee notifies the employer of the pregnancy until 18 months after the child's date of birth (or expected week of childbirth if actual date not notified). If miscarriage occurs within the first 24 weeks, the protected period ends 2 weeks after the pregnancy ends.
protectedPeriod.maternityLeave: From the start of maternity leave until 18 months after the child's date of birth.
protectedPeriod.adoptionLeave: From the start of adoption leave until 18 months after placement.
protectedPeriod.sharedParentalLeave: From the start of shared parental leave. If less than 6 weeks taken, protection ends on the last day of leave. If 6 weeks or more, protection extends 18 months from birth/placement.

redundancyProtection.obligation: During the protected period, if the employee's role is genuinely redundant AND a suitable alternative vacancy exists (with the employer or an associated employer), the employer MUST offer the protected employee the vacancy with PRIORITY over other employees who are also at risk of redundancy. Failure to offer the suitable alternative vacancy makes the redundancy dismissal automatically unfair under s.99.
redundancyProtection.keyDistinction: The protection does NOT prevent the employer from making the protected employee redundant. If no suitable alternative vacancy exists, the employer can proceed with the redundancy (following a fair process). The protection is about PRIORITY ACCESS to suitable alternatives, not immunity from redundancy.

eqa2010Interaction.protectedPeriod: The protected period under EqA 2010 s.18 runs from the start of pregnancy until the end of additional maternity leave (or, if the employee does not take maternity leave, until 2 weeks after the end of pregnancy). During this period, treating the employee unfavourably because of pregnancy or a pregnancy-related illness is pregnancy/maternity discrimination — no comparator is required.
eqa2010Interaction.significance: Pregnancy/maternity dismissals should ALWAYS be pursued under BOTH s.99 AND EqA 2010 s.18 because: (1) discrimination compensation is UNCAPPED (there is no cap on the compensatory award for discrimination); (2) injury to feelings awards are available under discrimination but NOT under unfair dismissal; (3) the discrimination burden of proof (EqA s.136) is more favourable to the employee than the unfair dismissal burden; (4) discrimination claims can be brought by workers as well as employees.
eqa2010Interaction.practicalNote: The compensatory award for ordinary unfair dismissal under s.99 IS subject to the cap in s.124(1) (unlike s.100 and s.103A which are uncapped). However, the parallel EqA 2010 s.18 discrimination claim provides uncapped compensation. This is why the dual-route strategy is critical — the s.99 claim establishes automatic unfairness, while the EqA claim provides uncapped compensation.

versions.text: [Current version — framework provision delegating prescribed reasons to regulations. The section has been amended to add new categories as new family leave rights were introduced: adoption leave (2002), shared parental leave (2014), parental bereavement leave (2020), neonatal care leave (2025).]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Relations Act 1999 s.9 and Sch.4 (substituted s.99); subsequently amended by EA 2002, CLFA 2014, PBLPA 2018, and Neonatal Care (Leave and Pay) Act 2023
versions.policyRationale: s.99 was originally inserted by TURERA 1993 (limited to pregnancy and maternity). The Employment Relations Act 1999 substituted the current framework version, expanding the scope to all family leave rights through a regulation-making power. Subsequent Acts have added new leave categories to s.99(3).

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 introduces ENHANCED dismissal protection for pregnant employees and new mothers. The Government proposes to make dismissals of pregnant women or those on maternity leave automatically unfair EXCEPT in specified circumstances — going beyond the current s.99 protection (which only catches dismissals WHERE THE REASON IS the pregnancy/leave). The ERA 2025 framework will: (1) restrict or remove some potentially fair reasons for dismissal (s.98(2)) for protected employees; (2) extend the protected period to at least 6 months after return from maternity leave; (3) potentially require the employer to satisfy a higher standard (such as showing 'significant detrimental effect on the business') before dismissing a protected employee. Two options were consulted on: (a) narrowing the fair reasons for dismissal for protected employees; (b) requiring a higher fairness threshold.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Government consultation published November 2025, closed 15 January 2026. Secondary legislation expected 2027. ERA 2025 provides the enabling power; regulations will contain the detail.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: VERY HIGH — this is the most significant expansion of pregnancy/maternity protection since the introduction of maternity leave itself. Employers will need to fundamentally reconsider whether and how they can dismiss protected employees. The combination of the existing s.99 auto-unfair dismissal, the 2024 redundancy protection (18-month priority period), and the ERA 2025 enhanced dismissal protection creates a near-total shield for pregnant employees and new mothers — broken only by the narrowest specified circumstances.

reformTimeline.note: Pregnancy/maternity dismissal protection has been reformed three times in three years — the fastest-evolving area of employment protection alongside flexible working.

phases.instrument: Employment Relations Act 1999 s.9 / Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312)
phases.changes: Substituted s.99 with the current framework provision. Regulation 20 set out the prescribed reasons. Regulation 10 provided redundancy priority for employees on maternity leave.

phases.instrument: Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 / SI 2024/468
phases.changes: Extended redundancy priority protection to: (1) pregnant employees from the date pregnancy is notified; (2) employees who have recently given birth — for 18 months after the birth; (3) adoption and shared parental leave (6+ weeks) — for 18 months after placement.

phases.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 (regulations under enabling power)
phases.changes: Enhanced dismissal protection — dismissals of pregnant women and new mothers to be automatically unfair except in specified narrow circumstances. At least 6-month post-return protected period. Potential restriction or removal of some s.98(2) fair reasons for protected employees.

refs: ERA 1996 s.99 (this section — framework provision for family leave auto-unfair dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.108(3)(b) (s.99 exempt from qualifying period — day-one right)
ERA 1996 s.105(1) (selection for redundancy on family leave grounds — also auto-unfair)
ERA 1996 s.47C (detriment for family leave reasons — the parallel detriment provision)

Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002 (SI 2002/2788) — Reg.29 (unfair dismissal)
Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/3050) — Reg.43 (unfair dismissal)

leadingCases.name: Brown v Stockton-on-Tees Borough Council
leadingCases.principle: Where an employee is selected for redundancy because she is pregnant, the dismissal is automatically unfair. The employer cannot treat pregnancy as a factor in redundancy selection — it is an impermissible reason for selection regardless of the employer's business justification.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that selecting a woman for redundancy because of her pregnancy was automatically unfair. Lord Griffiths stated that to allow pregnancy to be a factor in redundancy selection would be to permit direct discrimination on grounds of sex.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational UKHL authority on pregnancy and redundancy. Establishes the absolute principle: pregnancy can never be a permissible factor in redundancy selection. Pre-dates the current s.99 framework but the principle it establishes is now codified in both s.99/Reg.20 and EqA 2010 s.18.

leadingCases.name: Caledonia Bureau Investment & Property v Caffrey
leadingCases.principle: Dismissal connected to IVF treatment is capable of being a pregnancy-related dismissal. The concept of 'pregnancy' for the purposes of automatic unfair dismissal protection extends to cover the IVF process and early stages of assisted reproduction, not just confirmed pregnancy.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the protection against dismissal for pregnancy-related reasons should be interpreted broadly to include the IVF process. An employee dismissed because she needed time off for IVF treatment was potentially dismissed for a pregnancy-related reason.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes that 'pregnancy' in the context of s.99/Reg.20 is not limited to confirmed pregnancy — it extends to the process of becoming pregnant through assisted reproduction. Important for employers who dismiss employees undergoing fertility treatment.

leadingCases.name: Atkins v Coyle Personnel plc
leadingCases.principle: Dismissal for pregnancy-related sickness absence is automatically unfair under s.99/Reg.20. An employer cannot dismiss an employee for sickness absence where the absence is caused by pregnancy-related illness, even if the employer applies its normal sickness absence policy. The pregnancy-related nature of the illness makes the dismissal automatically unfair.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that dismissing an employee because of sickness absence that was caused by pregnancy or a pregnancy-related condition is dismissal for a reason connected to pregnancy — automatically unfair under s.99.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the absolute rule: pregnancy-related sickness cannot be a reason for dismissal. An employer who applies its normal sickness trigger to pregnancy-related absence is dismissing for a pregnancy-related reason — automatically unfair. This applies even if the employer treats all sickness equally — the pregnancy-related absence must be disregarded.

leadingCases.name: Visa International Service Association v Paul
leadingCases.principle: Where an employee on maternity leave is made redundant and the employer fails to offer a suitable alternative vacancy that exists (in breach of Regulation 10 of the 1999 Regulations), the dismissal is automatically unfair under s.99. The employer must proactively offer the vacancy — the employee does not need to apply for it.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that Regulation 10 imposes a positive obligation on the employer to offer the suitable alternative vacancy to the employee on maternity leave. The employee is not required to apply through the normal recruitment process. Failure to offer the vacancy makes the dismissal automatically unfair.
leadingCases.significance: THE leading authority on the Regulation 10 obligation to proactively offer suitable alternative vacancies. Critical since the 2024 extension of the protected period — employers must now proactively identify and offer suitable alternatives to pregnant employees and those within the 18-month post-birth period, not just those on maternity leave.

leadingCases.name: Simpson v Endsleigh Insurance Services Ltd
leadingCases.principle: An employee on maternity leave must be consulted about a redundancy that affects her role, even though she is absent from the workplace. Excluding an employee from consultation because she is on maternity leave is both a failure of the redundancy process (making the dismissal unfair under s.98(4)) and potentially pregnancy/maternity discrimination under EqA 2010.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that excluding an employee from redundancy consultation because she was on maternity leave was unfair. The employer had a duty to keep the employee informed and to consult her about the redundancy proposals — just as it would have done had she been at work.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes that maternity leave does not excuse the employer from its duty to consult. An employer who restructures while an employee is on maternity leave and presents her with a fait accompli on her return is likely to face both an unfair dismissal claim and a discrimination claim.

leadingCases.name: Sefton Borough Council v Wainwright
leadingCases.principle: The Regulation 10 duty to offer a suitable alternative vacancy applies where the employee's role is redundant — not where the employee's role continues but has been restructured. The duty is triggered by a redundancy situation affecting the employee's role, not by a general reorganisation.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT clarified that Regulation 10 applies where 'it is not practicable by reason of redundancy' to permit the employee to return to her existing role. If the role still exists but has been restructured, Regulation 10 is not engaged — the employee has a right to return to the same or a similar job under Regulation 18.
leadingCases.significance: Important boundary case — distinguishes between genuine redundancy (Reg.10 applies — priority for alternative) and restructuring without redundancy (Reg.18 applies — right to return to same/similar job). The distinction matters because the remedies and obligations are different.

leadingCases.name: Johal v Commissioner of Police of the Metropolis
leadingCases.principle: The tribunal must carefully examine whether the real reason for dismissal is connected to pregnancy or maternity leave, even where the employer presents a facially neutral reason. Where the timing of the dismissal closely follows a pregnancy announcement or return from maternity leave, the tribunal should scrutinise the employer's stated reason with particular care.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the tribunal must look beyond the employer's stated reason and examine the factual matrix, including the timing of the dismissal in relation to the pregnancy or maternity leave. Close temporal proximity between the pregnancy-related event and the dismissal may give rise to an inference that the pregnancy/maternity was the real reason.
leadingCases.significance: Recent 2024 EAT authority reinforcing the principle that tribunals must scrutinise the real reason for dismissal in pregnancy cases, not simply accept the employer's stated reason. The temporal proximity principle — dismissal closely following a pregnancy announcement — creates a strong inference of pregnancy-related dismissal.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy protection for pregnancy and new parents
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive ACAS guidance on the extended redundancy protection from 6 April 2024, including the 18-month protected period, the priority obligation, and worked examples.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Pregnancy and maternity discrimination: how it affects you at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: EHRC research report finding that 1 in 9 mothers were dismissed, made redundant, or treated so poorly they felt they had to leave. Cited in the ERA 2025 consultation document as the evidence base for enhanced protection.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Maternity leave and pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on maternity leave entitlements, statutory maternity pay, and the right to return to work.

commonErrors: Not recognising that s.99 dismissals are AUTOMATICALLY unfair — the tribunal does not apply the s.98(4) reasonableness test. If the reason or principal reason for dismissal is one of the prescribed family leave reasons, the dismissal is unfair regardless of the employer's procedure or business justification. No qualifying period is required (s.108(3)(b) — day-one right)

Not pursuing the PARALLEL EqA 2010 s.18 discrimination claim alongside s.99 — s.99 unfair dismissal compensation IS subject to the s.124(1) cap (unlike s.100 and s.103A). However, EqA 2010 s.18 discrimination compensation is UNCAPPED and includes injury to feelings. Pregnancy/maternity dismissals should ALWAYS be pursued under BOTH routes

Applying normal sickness absence policies to pregnancy-related absence — Atkins v Coyle Personnel [2008] establishes the absolute rule: pregnancy-related sickness cannot be a reason for dismissal. An employer who triggers its sickness absence policy for pregnancy-related illness is dismissing for a pregnancy-related reason — automatically unfair. The pregnancy-related absence must be disregarded entirely from sickness records

Not proactively offering suitable alternative vacancies during the protected period — since 6 April 2024, the 18-month post-birth protected period requires the employer to PROACTIVELY offer suitable alternative vacancies to pregnant employees and those recently returned from maternity leave. The employee does not need to apply (Visa International v Paul [2004]). Failure to offer an available suitable alternative makes the redundancy dismissal automatically unfair

Not understanding the 18-month protected period — since 6 April 2024 (Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023), redundancy priority protection runs from the date the employer is notified of pregnancy until 18 months after the child's date of birth. Many employers are unaware of this extended period and fail to check whether employees undergoing redundancy are within the protected window

Excluding employees on maternity leave from redundancy consultation — Simpson v Endsleigh [2011] establishes that employees on maternity leave must be consulted about redundancy proposals. Presenting the employee with a fait accompli on her return is both unfair and discriminatory

Treating IVF and fertility treatment as unrelated to pregnancy — Caledonia Bureau v Caffrey [1998] establishes that IVF treatment falls within the scope of pregnancy-related protection. Dismissing an employee for time off for IVF or fertility treatment is capable of being a pregnancy-related dismissal

Confusing the s.99 protected period with the EqA 2010 protected period — the EqA 2010 s.18 protected period runs from the start of pregnancy to the end of maternity leave (or 2 weeks post-pregnancy if no maternity leave). The s.99/Reg.20 protection is broader — it covers dismissal connected to pregnancy at any time, not just during the protected period. After the EqA protected period ends, the employee may still be protected under s.99 if the reason for dismissal is connected to her pregnancy or maternity leave

Using pregnancy as a factor in redundancy selection — Brown v Stockton-on-Tees [1988] UKHL establishes the absolute principle: pregnancy can never be a permissible factor in redundancy selection. This includes using pregnancy-related absence in selection scoring, treating maternity leave as a negative factor, or selecting the pregnant employee because she will be 'unavailable'

Not preparing for the ERA 2025 enhanced dismissal protection — ERA 2025 will restrict or remove some of the fair reasons for dismissal for pregnant employees and new mothers for at least 6 months after return from maternity leave. The Government consultation (closed 15 January 2026) proposed either narrowing the fair reasons or requiring a higher standard of justification. Employers should begin reviewing their dismissal processes for protected employees now

Not recognising that ERA 2025 makes paternity leave and parental leave day-one rights — prior to ERA 2025, paternity leave required 26 weeks' service by the 15th week before the expected week of childbirth. ERA 2025 removes this requirement. Dismissal for taking day-one paternity or parental leave will be automatically unfair under s.99

Not tracking the protected period end date accurately — the 18-month protected period requires the employer to know the child's date of birth and to track the 18-month window from that date. If the employee has not notified the actual date of birth, the period runs from the expected week of childbirth. Employers must have systems to track these dates accurately across the workforce

sections.title: Health and safety cases — automatically unfair dismissal
sections.summary: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or principal reason) for the dismissal falls within one of five health and safety categories: (a) carrying out designated health and safety activities; (b) performing functions as a health and safety representative or safety committee member; (c) bringing health and safety matters to the employer's attention in the absence of a representative or committee; (d) leaving, proposing to leave, or refusing to return to the workplace in circumstances of serious and imminent danger; (e) taking or proposing to take appropriate steps to protect themselves or others from serious and imminent danger. UNCAPPED compensation (s.124(1A)). Day-one right (s.108(3)(c)). Interim relief available (ss.128–132). The provision gained transformative significance during the COVID-19 pandemic.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of this Part as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that—

(a) having been designated by the employer to carry out activities in connection with preventing or reducing risks to health and safety at work, the employee carried out (or proposed to carry out) any such activities,

(b) being a representative of workers on matters of health and safety at work or member of a safety committee—

(i) in accordance with arrangements established under or by virtue of any enactment, or

(ii) by reason of being acknowledged as such by the employer,

the employee performed (or proposed to perform) any functions as such a representative or a member of such a committee,

(c) being an employee at a place where—

(i) there was no such representative or safety committee, or

(ii) there was such a representative or safety committee but it was not reasonably practicable for the employee to raise the matter by those means,

he brought to his employer's attention, by reasonable means, circumstances connected with his work which he reasonably believed were harmful or potentially harmful to health or safety,

(d) in circumstances of danger which the employee reasonably believed to be serious and imminent and which he could not reasonably have been expected to avert, he left (or proposed to leave) or (while the danger persisted) refused to return to his place of work or any dangerous part of his place of work, or

(e) in circumstances of danger which the employee reasonably believed to be serious and imminent, he took (or proposed to take) appropriate steps to protect himself or other persons from the danger.

(2) For the purposes of subsection (1)(e) whether steps which an employee took (or proposed to take) were appropriate is to be judged by reference to all the circumstances including, in particular, his knowledge and the facilities and advice available to him at the time.

(3) Where the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal of an employee is that specified in subsection (1)(e), he shall not be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the employer shows that it was (or would have been) so negligent for the employee to take the steps which he took (or proposed to take) that a reasonable employer might have dismissed him for taking (or proposing to take) them.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 100 protects employees who act on health and safety concerns from being dismissed for doing so. It applies to five distinct categories of activity — from designated H&S representatives carrying out their duties, to any employee who leaves a dangerous workplace or takes steps to protect themselves or others from serious and imminent danger. The protection is AUTOMATIC — the tribunal does not apply the s.98(4) reasonableness test. The employee's belief in the danger must be REASONABLE but need not be CORRECT. The employer's response to the danger is critical — where the employer has taken adequate steps to avert the danger, it becomes harder for the employee to show a reasonable belief that the danger is serious and imminent AND that they could not reasonably avert it.

fiveCategories.note: Section 100(1)(a)–(e) identifies five categories of health and safety activity that are protected from dismissal. Each has a different scope and different requirements.

categoryA.title: Designated H&S activities
categoryA.scope: The employee has been designated by the employer to carry out health and safety activities (e.g., fire warden, first aider, H&S coordinator) and is dismissed for carrying out or proposing to carry out those activities.
categoryA.practicalNote: The designation must come from the employer. An employee who self-appoints as a safety activist is not 'designated' under s.100(1)(a) — but may be protected under s.100(1)(c) or (e).

categoryB.title: H&S representatives and safety committee members
categoryB.scope: The employee is a health and safety representative (elected under the Safety Representatives and Safety Committees Regulations 1977 or the Health and Safety (Consultation with Employees) Regulations 1996) or a member of a safety committee, and is dismissed for performing or proposing to perform their representative functions.

categoryC.title: Raising H&S concerns in the absence of representatives
categoryC.scope: Where there is no H&S representative or safety committee (or it is not reasonably practicable to raise concerns through them), the employee brings health and safety concerns to the employer's attention by reasonable means. The employee must reasonably believe the circumstances are harmful or potentially harmful to health or safety.
categoryC.practicalNote: This is the 'general whistleblowing for health and safety' provision — it protects any employee who raises H&S concerns directly with the employer. Unlike s.43B whistleblowing, s.100(1)(c) does not require a 'public interest' test — the concern can be personal.

categoryD.title: Leaving or refusing to return to the workplace — serious and imminent danger
categoryD.scope: The employee leaves, proposes to leave, or refuses to return to the workplace (or a dangerous part of it) in circumstances of danger which the employee REASONABLY BELIEVED to be SERIOUS AND IMMINENT and which they COULD NOT REASONABLY HAVE BEEN EXPECTED TO AVERT.
categoryD.rodgersFramework: The Court of Appeal in Rodgers v Leeds Laser Cutting [2022] EWCA Civ 1659 set out four questions for s.100(1)(d): (1) Did the employee believe that there were circumstances of serious and imminent danger at the workplace? (2) If so, was that belief reasonable? (3) Could the employee reasonably have been expected to avert the danger? (4) Did they leave, propose to leave, or refuse to return because of the perceived danger — and was that the reason or principal reason for the dismissal?
categoryD.covidSignificance: Rodgers is the first EWCA decision on s.100 and COVID-19. The Court held that while COVID-19 could in principle constitute serious and imminent danger, the employer's safety measures (risk assessment, social distancing, PPE) meant the employee could not show a reasonable belief that the WORKPLACE danger was serious and imminent — even though the community danger was. The employer's response to the risk is critical to the defence.

categoryE.title: Taking appropriate steps to protect from serious and imminent danger
categoryE.scope: The employee takes or proposes to take appropriate steps to protect themselves or other persons from danger which they reasonably believe to be serious and imminent. The steps must be 'appropriate' — judged by reference to all circumstances including the employee's knowledge, facilities, and advice available (s.100(2)).
categoryE.negligenceDefence: s.100(3) provides a unique defence: the employee is NOT automatically unfairly dismissed under s.100(1)(e) if the employer shows that it was 'so negligent' for the employee to take the steps they took that a reasonable employer might have dismissed them for it. This is the ONLY automatically unfair dismissal provision that contains a statutory defence based on the employee's conduct.
categoryE.practicalNote: The s.100(3) negligence defence is narrow — it requires the employee's steps to be SO negligent that a reasonable employer MIGHT have dismissed. This is a high bar. An employee who takes disproportionate steps (e.g., shutting down an entire production line over a minor issue) may fall within s.100(3), but an employee who takes reasonable precautions will not.

uncappedCompensation.effect: The compensatory award cap does NOT apply to s.100 dismissals. The award is unlimited.

noQualifyingPeriod.effect: s.100 dismissals are exempt from the qualifying period. Day-one right.

interimRelief.effect: Interim relief is available for s.100(1)(a) and (b) cases (designated activities and representatives). The 7-day time limit applies.

versions.text: [The substantive provisions of s.100(1)–(3) have not been materially amended since the 1996 Act was enacted. The section consolidated the protection originally introduced by TULRCA 1992 s.152 (as inserted by the Trade Union Reform and Employment Rights Act 1993), which itself implemented the EC Framework Directive 89/391/EEC on health and safety at work. Minor amendments have been made to cross-references but the five categories and the negligence defence remain as originally enacted.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating TURERA 1993 provisions implementing EC Directive 89/391/EEC)
versions.policyRationale: The EC Framework Directive 89/391/EEC required member states to ensure that workers who leave their workstation in circumstances of serious, imminent, and unavoidable danger cannot be placed at any disadvantage. The UK implemented this through TURERA 1993, which was consolidated into ERA 1996 s.100. The provision has remained substantially unchanged for 30 years — the recent litigation has been about APPLICATION to new circumstances (COVID-19), not about statutory amendment.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.100. However, the repeal of s.108 will not affect s.100 claims (which are already exempt from the qualifying period). The wider ERA 2025 reforms — particularly the extension of the time limit from 3 to 6 months — will give employees longer to bring s.100 claims. The zero-hours contract provisions may also interact with s.100 where zero-hours workers face H&S risks.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Time limit extension not yet commenced
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW for the provision itself (unchanged), but the post-pandemic awareness of s.100 rights remains elevated — employers should expect continued claims in any future public health emergency or significant workplace safety incident.

sections.eraNote: s.100 has been unchanged since 1996 but the COVID-19 pandemic transformed its practical significance. Before 2020, s.100 claims were relatively uncommon. During and after the pandemic, s.100 became one of the most high-profile provisions in the ERA. Rodgers v Leeds Laser Cutting [2022] EWCA Civ 1659 — the first Court of Appeal ruling on s.100 and COVID — established that while a pandemic CAN engage s.100, the employer's risk assessment and safety measures are critical to the defence. An employer who takes adequate steps to mitigate the risk can defeat a s.100 claim even where the community danger is serious and imminent.
refs: ERA 1996 s.100(1)(a)–(e) (the five categories of protected H&S activity)
ERA 1996 s.100(2) (appropriateness of steps — judged by reference to all circumstances)
ERA 1996 s.100(3) (negligence defence — unique to s.100(1)(e))
ERA 1996 s.44 (right not to be subjected to detriment in H&S cases — the parallel detriment provision)
ERA 1996 s.108(3)(c) (s.100 exempt from qualifying period — day-one right)
ERA 1996 s.124(1A) (compensatory award cap does NOT apply to s.100 — uncapped)
ERA 1996 ss.128–132 (interim relief — available for s.100(1)(a) and (b))
ERA 1996 s.105(3) (selection for redundancy on H&S grounds — also uncapped)
HSWA 1974 (Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 — the general duty framework)
EC Framework Directive 89/391/EEC (the origin of the s.100 protection)
Safety Representatives and Safety Committees Regulations 1977 (SI 1977/500)
Health and Safety (Consultation with Employees) Regulations 1996 (SI 1996/1513)
Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3242)
Rodgers v Leeds Laser Cutting Ltd [2022] EWCA Civ 1659

leadingCases.name: Rodgers v Leeds Laser Cutting Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The first Court of Appeal decision on s.100 and COVID-19. While the COVID-19 pandemic could in principle constitute circumstances of serious and imminent danger engaging s.100(1)(d), the employer's risk assessment and safety measures meant the employee could not show a reasonable belief that the WORKPLACE danger was serious and imminent. The employer's response to the risk is critical.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal set out four questions for s.100(1)(d): (1) Did the employee believe there were circumstances of serious and imminent danger at the workplace? (2) If so, was that belief reasonable? (3) Could the employee reasonably have been expected to avert the danger? (4) Did they leave or refuse to return because of the perceived danger, and was that the reason for dismissal? On the facts, Mr Rodgers had a general fear of COVID-19 in the community but could not show a reasonable belief in serious and imminent danger at the WORKPLACE, where the employer had implemented adequate safety measures.
leadingCases.significance: THE landmark COVID/s.100 case. Establishes that a pandemic or public health emergency CAN engage s.100(1)(d), but the employer's safety measures are critical to the defence. An employer who conducts a proper risk assessment and implements adequate safety measures can defeat a s.100(1)(d) claim — because the employee cannot show that they could not reasonably avert the workplace danger. Also establishes that s.100(1)(d) can apply to dangers arising OUTSIDE the workplace (such as a pandemic) — but the employee's belief must still relate to danger at or connected to the workplace.

leadingCases.name: Harvest Press Ltd v McCaffrey
leadingCases.principle: The employee's belief in the existence of serious and imminent danger must be assessed objectively — was the belief REASONABLE? The belief need not be correct, but it must be one that a reasonable person in the employee's position, with the employee's knowledge, could have held.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the test is whether the employee's belief in the existence of serious and imminent danger was reasonable. The tribunal must consider what the employee knew at the time and whether, on the basis of that knowledge, the belief was one that a reasonable person could have formed. The danger need not actually have existed — the test is reasonable belief, not actual danger.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the objective element of the 'reasonable belief' test. Important because employees may overestimate danger (especially in unfamiliar situations) — the question is whether their belief was reasonable in the circumstances, not whether it was correct.

leadingCases.name: Oudahar v Esporta Group Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The 'appropriate steps' taken by an employee under s.100(1)(e) must be appropriate in the circumstances. Refusing to carry out an instruction that the employee considers unsafe may constitute an 'appropriate step' — but the employee's response must be proportionate to the perceived danger.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that a lifeguard who refused to operate the swimming pool alone (because he considered it unsafe) had taken appropriate steps to protect pool users from danger. His refusal was proportionate to the perceived risk and was therefore protected under s.100(1)(e).
leadingCases.significance: Illustrates the scope of 'appropriate steps' under s.100(1)(e). Refusal to carry out a task the employee considers dangerous is a legitimate protective step — not insubordination. The employee does not have to comply with the instruction first and complain afterwards.

leadingCases.name: Balfour Kilpatrick Ltd v Acheson
leadingCases.principle: An employee designated to carry out health and safety activities under s.100(1)(a) is protected from dismissal for performing those activities, even if the employer disagrees with the employee's approach or conclusions. The protection extends to the manner in which the activities are carried out, provided the activities themselves fall within the designation.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the protection in s.100(1)(a) extends to all activities in connection with preventing or reducing risks to health and safety that fall within the employee's designation. The employer cannot dismiss the employee for the results or conclusions of their H&S activities simply because the employer disagrees with them.
leadingCases.significance: Protects designated H&S employees from dismissal for doing their job — even if the employer finds their findings inconvenient. This is crucial for employees who identify compliance failures or risks that the employer would prefer to ignore.

leadingCases.name: Masiak v City Restaurants (UK) Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Section 100(1)(c) — raising H&S concerns in the absence of representatives — protects an employee who brings health and safety concerns to the employer's attention by reasonable means. The concern must relate to circumstances connected with the employee's work that they reasonably believe are harmful or potentially harmful.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that an employee who raised concerns about unsafe working conditions with management was protected under s.100(1)(c). The protection applies where the employee brings genuine H&S concerns to the employer's attention and is subsequently dismissed for doing so.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EAT authority on s.100(1)(c) — the 'general H&S whistleblowing' provision. Important because s.100(1)(c) does not require a 'public interest' test (unlike s.43B whistleblowing) — the employee can raise personal safety concerns and still be protected.

leadingCases.name: Kerr v Nathan's Wastesavers Ltd
leadingCases.principle: In assessing whether the employee reasonably believed there were circumstances of serious and imminent danger, the tribunal must consider the employee's subjective knowledge at the time and whether that subjective belief was objectively reasonable. The assessment is contextual — what a particular employee in that workplace, with their knowledge and experience, would have believed.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT emphasised that the reasonable belief assessment under s.100(1)(d) and (e) is contextual and must take account of what the employee actually knew at the relevant time, not what they later discovered or what an expert would have known.
leadingCases.significance: Recent EAT guidance on the reasonable belief assessment in the post-COVID landscape. Confirms that the test remains contextual and fact-specific — there is no blanket rule about when danger is 'serious and imminent'.

leadingCases.name: Sinclair v Trackwork Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Where an employee raises concerns about being required to work alone in circumstances they consider dangerous, and is dismissed for raising those concerns, the dismissal may be automatically unfair under s.100(1)(c) (raising concerns) or s.100(1)(e) (taking protective steps). The employee does not need to have exhausted internal grievance procedures before raising H&S concerns.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the employee's refusal to work alone in a potentially dangerous environment was a legitimate exercise of his s.100 rights. The dismissal for that refusal was automatically unfair.
leadingCases.significance: Recent authority confirming that lone working arrangements raise genuine s.100 issues. Employers who require employees to work alone in potentially hazardous environments must ensure adequate risk assessments are in place — dismissal for refusal to work alone in genuinely dangerous conditions is likely to be automatically unfair.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Consulting employees on health and safety
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: HSE guidance on the employer's duty to consult employees on health and safety matters — the context within which s.100 rights operate.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Health and safety at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on health and safety obligations and employee rights, including the right not to be dismissed for raising H&S concerns.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Managing risks and risk assessment at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: HSE guidance on risk assessment — the employer's risk assessment is critical to defending s.100 claims (Rodgers [2022]).

commonErrors: Not recognising that s.100 dismissals are AUTOMATICALLY unfair — the tribunal does not apply the s.98(4) reasonableness test. If the reason or principal reason for dismissal was one of the five H&S categories, the dismissal is unfair regardless of the employer's procedure. The only statutory defence is s.100(3) (negligence by the employee under s.100(1)(e) only)

Not recognising that compensation is UNCAPPED — s.124(1A) excludes s.100 dismissals from the compensatory award cap. Combined with the day-one right (s.108(3)(c)), this makes s.100 one of the highest-exposure categories of dismissal

Assuming that COVID-19 or a pandemic automatically engages s.100(1)(d) — Rodgers v Leeds Laser Cutting [2022] EWCA establishes that a pandemic CAN engage s.100(1)(d), but the employee must show a reasonable belief in serious and imminent danger at the WORKPLACE, not merely in the community. The employer's risk assessment and safety measures are critical to the defence

Not conducting a proper risk assessment — the employer's response to health and safety risks is the primary defence to a s.100 claim. Rodgers [2022] shows that an employer who conducts a proper risk assessment and implements adequate safety measures can defeat a s.100(1)(d) claim. An employer who fails to risk-assess faces both H&S regulatory liability AND increased s.100 exposure

Dismissing an employee for raising H&S concerns, even if the concerns turn out to be wrong — the test under s.100(1)(c) is whether the employee REASONABLY BELIEVED the circumstances were harmful or potentially harmful. The belief need not be correct (Harvest Press v McCaffrey [1999]). Dismissing an employee for raising a concern that turns out to be unfounded is still automatically unfair if the belief was reasonable

Not recognising the distinction between s.100(1)(d) and s.100(1)(e) — s.100(1)(d) (leaving/refusing to return) requires that the employee could NOT reasonably have been expected to AVERT the danger. s.100(1)(e) (taking protective steps) requires that the steps were APPROPRIATE. These are different tests — an employee who stays at the workplace and takes protective steps is assessed under (e), not (d)

Overlooking the s.100(3) negligence defence — this is unique to s.100(1)(e). If the employee took steps that were SO negligent that a reasonable employer might have dismissed them for taking those steps, the dismissal is not automatically unfair. But this is a very high bar — the employee's steps must be extremely negligent, not merely disproportionate or misguided

Treating an employee's refusal to carry out an unsafe task as insubordination — Oudahar v Esporta [2011] establishes that refusing to carry out an instruction the employee considers unsafe is a legitimate protective step under s.100(1)(e), not insubordination. The employee does not have to comply first and complain afterwards

Dismissing a designated H&S employee for the conclusions of their H&S work — Balfour Kilpatrick v Acheson [2003] protects designated H&S employees from dismissal for performing their designated activities, even if the employer disagrees with their findings. An employer cannot dismiss a fire warden for identifying fire risks that the employer would prefer to ignore

Not recognising that s.100(1)(c) does not require a 'public interest' test — unlike s.43B whistleblowing (which requires reasonable belief that the disclosure is in the public interest), s.100(1)(c) protects employees who raise PERSONAL health and safety concerns. An employee who says 'I feel unsafe in this workplace' is protected even if the concern does not have a wider public dimension

Assuming the danger must be physical — while most s.100 cases involve physical danger, the provision is not expressly limited to physical harm. Mental health risks arising from working conditions could potentially engage s.100, particularly where the working conditions create a risk of psychiatric injury — though this remains a developing area

Not applying for interim relief within 7 days — interim relief is available for s.100(1)(a) and (b) cases (designated activities and representatives) but must be applied for within 7 days of the effective date of termination. This deadline is frequently missed, particularly where the employee takes time to seek legal advice

sections.title: Protected disclosure — automatically unfair dismissal
sections.summary: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee made a protected disclosure. This is AUTOMATICALLY unfair — the tribunal does not apply the s.98(4) reasonableness test. No qualifying period is required (s.108(3)(ff) — day-one right). The compensatory award is UNCAPPED (s.124(1A)). Interim relief is available (ss.128–132). This provision completes the whistleblowing claim path: s.43B defines the qualifying disclosure, ss.43C–43H determine how it is made, and s.103A provides the dismissal remedy. Section 47B provides the parallel detriment remedy for workers (including non-employees).
sections.text: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed for the purposes of this Part if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee made a protected disclosure.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 103A asks a single question: was the reason (or principal reason) for the dismissal that the employee made a protected disclosure? If yes, the dismissal is automatically unfair — there is no further consideration of reasonableness, procedure, or proportionality. The burden of proving the reason rests on the employer (s.98(1) applies). If the employer cannot show a potentially fair reason, and the employee has raised a credible case that the disclosure was the reason, the tribunal may infer that the disclosure was the principal reason (Kuzel v Roche [2008]). Critically, the Supreme Court in Royal Mail v Jhuti [2019] held that even where the decision-maker did not know about the disclosure, the tribunal must look behind the ostensible reason if a more senior manager manipulated the process to conceal the true reason.

uncappedCompensation.effect: The compensatory award cap in s.124(1) does NOT apply to s.103A dismissals. The compensatory award is unlimited — it is calculated under s.123 ('just and equitable') with no statutory ceiling. This is the same uncapped treatment as health and safety dismissals (s.100) and blacklisting dismissals (s.105(6A)). A single whistleblowing dismissal can result in a six- or seven-figure award where the employee's losses are significant.

interimRelief.effect: An employee who presents a s.103A complaint may apply for interim relief within 7 days of the effective date of termination. If the tribunal considers it 'likely' that the s.103A claim will succeed at final hearing, it may order the employer to reinstate or re-engage the employee pending the hearing, or to continue paying the employee's wages. Interim relief is a powerful and unusual remedy — it is available for very few types of claim (s.103A, trade union dismissals under TULRCA s.161, and a small number of other automatically unfair reasons).
interimRelief.practicalNote: The 7-day time limit for interim relief is extremely tight — it runs from the effective date of termination, not from the date the employee became aware of the right. The 'likely to succeed' threshold is lower than the balance of probabilities but higher than a mere arguable case. Interim relief applications are heard urgently, typically within days of presentation.

noQualifyingPeriod.effect: The s.108 qualifying period does NOT apply to s.103A dismissals. An employee dismissed on their first day for making a protected disclosure has a claim with uncapped compensation. This is a day-one right.

burdenOfProof.principle: The burden of proving the reason for dismissal rests on the employer under s.98(1). In a s.103A case, the employer must show a potentially fair reason for the dismissal. If the employer fails to prove its reason, and the employee has raised a credible case that the disclosure was the reason, the tribunal MAY (but is not obliged to) infer that the s.103A reason was the true reason. However, the failure of the employer to prove its reason does NOT automatically mean the s.103A reason is established — the tribunal must still make a positive finding on the reason.
burdenOfProof.kuzelFramework: Kuzel v Roche Products Ltd [2008] EWCA Civ 380 established the approach: (1) Has the employee shown that there is a real issue about whether the employer's stated reason was the true one? (2) Has the employer proved its potentially fair reason? (3) If the employer has failed to prove its reason, the tribunal may infer the s.103A reason — but is not obliged to do so. The tribunal must make a finding of fact on the actual reason for dismissal.
burdenOfProof.distinction: The whistleblowing burden of proof is NOT the same as the discrimination burden of proof (EqA 2010 s.136). In discrimination cases, if the employee proves facts from which discrimination could be inferred, the burden shifts to the employer to prove a non-discriminatory reason. In s.103A cases, the burden remains on the employer to prove its reason — but if it fails, the tribunal does not automatically adopt the employee's suggested reason.

hiddenReasonDoctrine.source: Royal Mail Ltd v Jhuti [2019] UKSC 55
hiddenReasonDoctrine.principle: Where a manager in the hierarchy of responsibility determines that an employee should be dismissed because of a protected disclosure, but deliberately conceals the true reason from the decision-maker behind a fictitious reason (such as poor performance), the tribunal should look behind the fictitious reason and attribute the real reason to the employer — even if the decision-maker genuinely believed the fictitious reason.
hiddenReasonDoctrine.practicalNote: Jhuti is one of the most significant whistleblowing decisions. It means employers cannot insulate themselves from s.103A liability by ensuring the decision-maker is unaware of the disclosure. If anyone in the management chain manipulated the process to punish the whistleblower, the employer is liable. However, Henderson v GCRM [2025] EAT clarified that the Jhuti principle applies to s.103A (employer liability for dismissal) but does NOT extend to s.47B(1A) (personal liability of individual co-workers for detriment) — an innocent decision-maker cannot be held personally liable under s.47B.

s103AVsS47B.principle: s.103A provides a remedy for DISMISSAL (employees only, against the employer). s.47B provides a remedy for DETRIMENT (workers and employees, against the employer and potentially against individual co-workers). Where an employee is dismissed, the primary route is s.103A. An employee cannot bring a s.47B detriment claim against the employer where the detriment is the dismissal itself — s.47B(2) excludes dismissal from the detriment provisions. However, an employee CAN bring both: (1) a s.103A claim for the dismissal; AND (2) a s.47B claim for pre-dismissal detriments (such as being ostracised, denied promotion, or subjected to disciplinary action because of the disclosure).
s103AVsS47B.recentDevelopment: In Rice v Wicked Vision Ltd / Barton Turns v Treadwell [2025] EWCA Civ 1466, the Court of Appeal reviewed whether an employee who brings a s.103A dismissal claim can also bring a s.47B detriment claim where the alleged detriment IS the dismissal. This clarified the boundary between the two provisions.
s103AVsS47B.practicalNote: The strategic importance of the s.103A/s.47B distinction is significant. s.103A claims can only be brought by employees and only against the employer. s.47B claims can be brought by workers (including non-employees) and against individual co-workers as well as the employer. Workers who are not employees and who are dismissed cannot bring a s.103A claim — but they may be able to bring a s.47B detriment claim for the termination of their engagement.

versions.text: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed for the purposes of this Part if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee made a protected disclosure.
versions.amendedBy: Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (c.23) s.5 — inserted s.103A into ERA 1996
versions.policyRationale: PIDA 1998 created the whistleblowing framework by inserting Part IVA (qualifying and protected disclosures) and s.103A (automatically unfair dismissal for whistleblowing) into ERA 1996. The section has not been materially amended since its insertion — its single sentence has remained unchanged for over 25 years. The jurisprudential development has been entirely through case law, not statute.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.103A. However, ERA 2025 s.23 inserts s.43B(1)(da) (sexual harassment as a qualifying disclosure, in force 6 April 2026), which expands the range of disclosures that can found a s.103A claim. Additionally, the repeal of s.108 will not affect s.103A claims (which are already exempt from the qualifying period under s.108(3)(ff)), but it will increase the overall volume of unfair dismissal claims, making the distinction between ordinary unfair dismissal (capped) and s.103A (uncapped) even more strategically significant.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: s.43B(1)(da) in force 6 April 2026. s.108 repeal expected Autumn 2026.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the expansion of qualifying disclosure categories (sexual harassment) increases the scope of s.103A. Employers who dismiss employees who have reported sexual harassment will face potential s.103A claims with uncapped compensation from 6 April 2026.

sections.eraNote: s.103A is a single sentence that has remained unchanged since 1999 — but the case law it has generated is immense. The Supreme Court's decision in Jhuti [2019] fundamentally changed the landscape by establishing the 'hidden reason' doctrine. The 2025 EAT decisions in Henderson v GCRM and the EWCA decision in Rice v Wicked Vision continue to refine the boundaries between s.103A and s.47B. The provision's significance will increase further as ERA 2025 expands the qualifying disclosure categories and removes the unfair dismissal qualifying period — making the uncapped s.103A route even more strategically valuable relative to the capped ordinary unfair dismissal route.
refs: ERA 1996 s.103A (this section — automatically unfair dismissal for protected disclosure)
ERA 1996 s.43A (definition of 'protected disclosure' — qualifying disclosure made under ss.43C–43H)
ERA 1996 s.43B (qualifying disclosure — the gateway provision that s.103A relies on)
ERA 1996 s.47B (detriment for protected disclosure — the parallel worker remedy)
ERA 1996 s.47B(2) (detriment does not include dismissal — employees must use s.103A)
ERA 1996 s.98(1) (burden of proving reason for dismissal — applies to s.103A)
ERA 1996 s.108(3)(ff) (s.103A exempt from qualifying period — day-one right)
ERA 1996 s.124(1A) (compensatory award cap does NOT apply to s.103A — uncapped)
ERA 1996 ss.128–132 (interim relief — available for s.103A complaints)
ERA 1996 s.123(6A) (tribunal may reduce award by up to 25% if disclosure not made in good faith)
ERA 2025 s.23 (s.43B(1)(da) — sexual harassment as qualifying disclosure, in force 6 April 2026)
PIDA 1998 s.5 (inserted s.103A into ERA 1996)
Kuzel v Roche Products Ltd [2008] EWCA Civ 380
Royal Mail Ltd v Jhuti [2019] UKSC 55

leadingCases.name: Royal Mail Ltd v Jhuti
leadingCases.principle: Where a manager in the chain of command determines that an employee should be dismissed for making a protected disclosure but conceals the true reason from the decision-maker behind a fictitious reason (poor performance), the tribunal must look behind the fictitious reason and attribute the true reason to the employer. The decision-maker's honest but manipulated belief does not determine the 'reason for dismissal' — the employer's real reason does.
leadingCases.heldText: Lord Wilson (delivering the unanimous judgment of the Supreme Court) held: 'In a case in which a manager (A) in the hierarchy of responsibility above the employee determines that she should be dismissed for making a protected disclosure but hides that reason behind a fictitious alternative, and in which the employee's line manager (B) is instructed by A to bring about the dismissal on grounds of the fictitious alternative, it is the real reason for the dismissal, namely the making of the protected disclosure, which the tribunal must determine was the reason for the dismissal under s.103A.'
leadingCases.significance: THE most important s.103A case. Jhuti prevents employers from engineering a 'clean' dismissal by ensuring the decision-maker is kept ignorant of the disclosure while a more senior manager orchestrates the dismissal from behind the scenes. The principle extends beyond whistleblowing — the Supreme Court noted that the same approach to identifying the 'reason for dismissal' applies to all unfair dismissal cases under s.98. However, Henderson v GCRM [2025] EAT clarified that Jhuti applies to s.103A but NOT to s.47B personal liability.

leadingCases.name: Kuzel v Roche Products Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The burden of proof in s.103A cases: (1) the employer bears the burden of proving the potentially fair reason for dismissal (s.98(1)); (2) if the employee raises a real issue that the employer's stated reason is not the true reason, and the employer fails to prove its reason, the tribunal MAY infer that the s.103A reason is the true reason — but is not obliged to do so; (3) the tribunal must make a positive finding on the actual reason for dismissal.
leadingCases.heldText: Mummery LJ held that the whistleblowing provisions must be seen in the overall context of unfair dismissal law. The burden of proof is not the same as in discrimination cases. The employer must prove its reason. If it fails, the tribunal may draw an inference that the s.103A reason was the true reason — but the tribunal is not compelled to do so. The tribunal must still make a finding of fact about the real reason for dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: THE leading authority on the burden of proof in s.103A cases. Establishes that the whistleblowing burden is NOT a reversal of the burden of proof (as in discrimination). The employer must prove its reason — but if it fails, the tribunal exercises judgment rather than automatically finding for the employee on s.103A. This makes s.103A claims harder for employees than discrimination claims in the burden-of-proof analysis.

leadingCases.name: Eiger Securities LLP v Korshunova
leadingCases.principle: Where the tribunal rejects the employer's stated reason for dismissal and the employee has advanced a s.103A case, the tribunal should go on to consider whether the s.103A reason has been established. The tribunal should not simply stop after finding the employer's reason to be untrue — it must make a positive finding on the actual reason.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that where the employer fails to establish a potentially fair reason and the employee has advanced a plausible s.103A case, the tribunal should consider the s.103A case on its merits. The rejection of the employer's reason creates a vacuum that the tribunal must fill with a positive finding.
leadingCases.significance: Practical guidance on the sequencing of the tribunal's analysis in s.103A cases. Reinforces Kuzel: the tribunal must make a positive finding, not simply default to one side or the other.

leadingCases.name: Henderson v GCRM Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The Jhuti hidden reason doctrine applies to s.103A claims (employer liability for dismissal) but does NOT extend to s.47B(1A) claims (personal liability of individual co-workers for detriment). An innocent decision-maker who was manipulated by a colleague cannot be held personally liable under s.47B — only the manipulator and the employer (vicariously) can be liable.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that it cannot have been the intention of Parliament to impose unlimited liability upon innocent individuals under s.47B(1A). The Jhuti principle — attributing the real reason to the employer — is appropriate for s.103A (where only the employer is liable) but not for s.47B(1A) (where individuals face personal liability). The EAT allowed Henderson's appeal on the s.103A complaint (remitting for reconsideration under Jhuti) but dismissed the detriment claims against the innocent decision-maker.
leadingCases.significance: The most recent significant authority on the Jhuti/s.47B boundary — decided in 2025. Establishes that the Jhuti imputation principle is confined to s.103A employer liability and cannot be extended to s.47B personal liability against innocent individuals. This is critical for practitioners advising on who to name as respondents in whistleblowing claims.

leadingCases.name: Rice v Wicked Vision Ltd; Barton Turns Developments Ltd v Treadwell
leadingCases.principle: The Court of Appeal reviewed the scope of whistleblowing detriment claims and whether an employee who brings a s.103A automatically unfair dismissal claim can also bring a s.47B detriment claim where the alleged detriment is the dismissal itself.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal considered the interaction between s.103A and s.47B in joined cases, providing authoritative guidance on the circumstances in which employees can pursue both routes in relation to the same dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: The most recent EWCA authority on the s.103A/s.47B overlap — decided in 2025. This case addresses a long-running question about whether employees can 'double up' claims by pursuing both the s.103A dismissal route and the s.47B detriment route for the same act of dismissal. Essential for practitioners structuring whistleblowing claims.

leadingCases.name: Fecitt v NHS Manchester
leadingCases.principle: The causation test is different for s.103A and s.47B. For s.103A, the disclosure must be the 'reason or principal reason' for the dismissal — a high threshold. For s.47B detriment, the disclosure need only be a ground for the detriment — the tribunal asks whether the disclosure 'materially influenced' the employer's treatment of the worker (a lower threshold — see also Jesudason [2020]).
leadingCases.heldText: Elias LJ held that the causation test for s.47B is lower than for s.103A. For s.47B, the question is whether the protected disclosure materially influenced (in the sense of being more than a trivial influence on) the employer's treatment. For s.103A, the question is whether the disclosure was the REASON or PRINCIPAL REASON — a higher standard requiring the disclosure to be the dominant or main cause.
leadingCases.significance: THE leading authority on the different causation tests for s.103A and s.47B. Critical for practitioners because a whistleblower may succeed on a s.47B detriment claim (lower threshold — material influence) while failing on a s.103A dismissal claim (higher threshold — principal reason). This makes the s.47B detriment route strategically important for pre-dismissal treatment.

leadingCases.name: Kong v Gulf International Bank (UK) Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Where the employee's protected disclosure is intertwined with their conduct in making the disclosure, the tribunal must carefully separate the fact of making the disclosure (protected) from the manner or conduct (potentially not protected). An employer may legitimately dismiss for the manner in which a disclosure was made (e.g., aggressive, insubordinate, or in breach of confidentiality) without the dismissal being automatically unfair under s.103A — provided the reason was genuinely the conduct, not the disclosure.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the tribunal must undertake a careful 'separability' analysis: can the employer's reason for dismissal be separated from the fact that a protected disclosure was made? If the employer dismissed because of the WAY the disclosure was communicated (e.g., aggressively or through unauthorised channels), and the manner is genuinely separable from the content, the dismissal may not be automatically unfair under s.103A. But if the manner and the disclosure are inseparable — or if the employer is using the manner as a pretext — the s.103A claim succeeds.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EWCA authority on the 'separability' question — whether the employer can distinguish between the disclosure (protected) and the conduct surrounding the disclosure (potentially not protected). This is a frequent battleground in s.103A cases where the employer argues 'we dismissed her for how she raised the concern, not for raising it'.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Whistleblowing at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on whistleblowing protections including the right not to be dismissed for making a protected disclosure.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Whistleblowing for employees
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the protections available to whistleblowers, including automatically unfair dismissal under s.103A.

commonErrors: Not recognising that s.103A dismissals are AUTOMATICALLY unfair — the tribunal does not apply the s.98(4) reasonableness test. If the reason or principal reason for dismissal was the protected disclosure, the dismissal is unfair regardless of the employer's procedure, the employee's conduct, or the size of the employer. There is no 'range of reasonable responses' analysis

Not recognising that compensation is UNCAPPED — s.124(1A) excludes s.103A dismissals from the compensatory award cap. The award is calculated under s.123 ('just and equitable') with no statutory ceiling. A single s.103A claim can result in a six- or seven-figure award. This makes s.103A the highest-exposure category of unfair dismissal

Assuming the decision-maker's ignorance of the disclosure protects the employer — Royal Mail v Jhuti [2019] UKSC established that where a senior manager manipulates the process to conceal the real reason (the disclosure) behind a fictitious reason, the tribunal must attribute the real reason to the employer. The decision-maker's honest but manipulated belief is irrelevant

Confusing the s.103A burden of proof with the discrimination burden — Kuzel v Roche [2008] establishes that the whistleblowing burden is NOT a reversal. The employer must prove its reason (s.98(1)). If it fails, the tribunal MAY infer the s.103A reason — but is not compelled to. In discrimination, if the employee proves a prima facie case, the burden SHIFTS to the employer. The s.103A burden is less favourable to the employee

Not understanding the different causation tests for s.103A and s.47B — for s.103A, the disclosure must be the REASON or PRINCIPAL REASON for dismissal (high threshold). For s.47B detriment, the disclosure need only MATERIALLY INFLUENCE the treatment (lower threshold — Fecitt [2012]). An employee may fail on s.103A but succeed on s.47B for pre-dismissal detriments

Not considering interim relief — ss.128–132 provide a uniquely powerful remedy for s.103A claims. The employee may apply within 7 DAYS of the effective date of termination for an order requiring the employer to reinstate, re-engage, or continue paying wages pending the full hearing. The 7-day time limit is extremely tight and frequently missed

Not distinguishing between the disclosure and the manner of disclosure — Kong v Gulf International Bank [2022] establishes the 'separability' analysis. An employer may legitimately dismiss for the WAY a disclosure was made (aggressive, insubordinate, breach of confidentiality) without triggering s.103A — but only if the manner is genuinely separable from the content. If the manner and disclosure are inseparable, or if manner is a pretext, s.103A applies

Treating Jhuti as extending to s.47B personal liability — Henderson v GCRM [2025] EAT clarified that the Jhuti imputation principle applies to s.103A (employer liability) but NOT to s.47B(1A) (personal liability of individual co-workers). An innocent decision-maker cannot be held personally liable under s.47B even if they were manipulated

Not advising on the s.103A/s.47B overlap — an employee should consider bringing BOTH a s.103A claim for the dismissal AND separate s.47B claims for pre-dismissal detriments (ostracism, denied promotion, disciplinary action). The s.47B claims may succeed on the lower 'material influence' threshold even if the s.103A claim fails on the higher 'principal reason' threshold

Overlooking that s.103A is a day-one right — s.108(3)(ff) exempts s.103A from the qualifying period. An employee dismissed on day one for making a protected disclosure has a claim with uncapped compensation. Many employers do not realise this

Not recognising that ERA 2025 s.23 (sexual harassment as qualifying disclosure, in force 6 April 2026) expands the scope of s.103A — from 6 April 2026, an employee dismissed for reporting sexual harassment will have a s.103A claim with UNCAPPED compensation, in addition to any harassment or discrimination claim under EqA 2010

sections.title: Redundancy — automatically unfair selection
sections.summary: An employee who is dismissed by reason of redundancy shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or principal reason) for their SELECTION for redundancy was a protected reason. The redundancy itself may be genuine — but if the employee was chosen for redundancy BECAUSE OF a protected reason (pregnancy, whistleblowing, health and safety, assertion of statutory rights, trade union membership, etc.), the dismissal is automatically unfair. This is the BRIDGE provision — it extends every automatically unfair dismissal category into the redundancy context. No qualifying period applies for most protected reasons (s.108(3)(h)). Uncapped compensation applies where the selection was for health and safety (s.100) or whistleblowing (s.103A) reasons. The minimum basic award of £8,763 applies for H&S and whistleblowing selections. ERA 2025 will add fire-and-rehire (s.104I) as a new automatically unfair reason, which will also be caught by s.105.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of this Part as unfairly dismissed if—

(a) the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee was redundant,

(b) it is shown that the circumstances constituting the redundancy applied equally to one or more other employees in the same undertaking who held positions similar to that held by the employee and who have not been dismissed by the employer, and

(c) it is shown that any of subsections (2A) to (7N) applies.
