[Subsections (2A)–(7N) list the protected reasons — each cross-references a specific automatically unfair dismissal provision]
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 105 asks a three-part question: (1) Was the employee dismissed by reason of redundancy? (2) Did the same redundancy circumstances apply equally to other employees in similar positions who were NOT dismissed? (3) Was the reason the employee was SELECTED (rather than the others) a protected reason? If all three are answered yes, the dismissal is automatically unfair — even though the redundancy was genuine. The concept is 'tainted selection' — the employer had a legitimate need to reduce headcount, but chose THIS employee for an illegitimate reason.

protectedGrounds.note: Subsections (2A)–(7N) list the protected reasons for which selection for redundancy is automatically unfair. Each cross-references the corresponding auto-unfair dismissal provision. Key categories include:

categories.note: Selection for redundancy because the employee carried out H&S activities, raised H&S concerns, or exercised rights under s.100(1)(a)–(e). UNCAPPED compensation. Minimum basic award £8,763.
Selection for redundancy because the employee made a protected disclosure. UNCAPPED compensation. Minimum basic award £8,763.

categories.subsection: s.105(1)(c) read with Reg.20(2) of SI 1999/3312
categories.note: Selection for redundancy because of pregnancy, childbirth, or maternity leave. Compensation is subject to the standard cap (but parallel EqA 2010 s.18 discrimination claim is uncapped).
Selection because of exercising working time rights (WTR 1998).
Selection because of performing workforce representative functions.
Selection because of performing duties as occupational pension scheme trustee.
Selection because of performing employee representative functions.
Selection because the employee asserted a statutory right.
Selection because of NMW enforcement or complaint.
Selection because the employee made or proposed to make a flexible working request.
Selection because of trade union membership, activities, or non-membership. Governed by TULRCA s.153, which mirrors the s.105 mechanism.

protectedGrounds.era2025Addition: ERA 2025 introduces s.104I (dismissal for refusing to agree a variation of contract — 'fire and rehire'). This will be automatically unfair from October 2026, and selection for redundancy on this ground will also be caught by s.105.

taintedSelectionConcept.principle: The employer may have a genuine redundancy situation (s.139) and may have followed a fair redundancy process (Williams v Compair Maxam). But if the reason the particular employee was SELECTED — rather than a colleague in a similar position — was a protected reason, the dismissal is automatically unfair under s.105. The redundancy is genuine; the selection is tainted.
taintedSelectionConcept.example: A department of 10 employees must reduce to 8. Two employees must be selected. The employer uses a scoring matrix. One of the two selected employees is a known whistleblower. If the tribunal finds that the whistleblowing was a factor in the selection scoring, the dismissal is automatically unfair under s.105(6A) — even if the redundancy was genuine and the process appeared fair.
taintedSelectionConcept.practicalNote: The s.105 analysis requires the tribunal to compare the dismissed employee with colleagues in similar positions who were NOT dismissed (s.105(1)(b)). If the circumstances of the redundancy applied equally to others but only the protected employee was selected, that comparison may give rise to an inference that the selection was tainted.

versions.text: [s.105 has been amended repeatedly to add new protected grounds as new automatically unfair dismissal categories have been created. The core mechanism (s.105(1)(a)–(c)) has remained unchanged since enactment. Subsections have been added by: ERA 1999, EA 2002, WTR 1998, NMW Act 1998, Employment Relations Act 2004, Children and Schools Act 2010, ERA 2025, and various statutory instruments.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted; repeatedly amended to add new protected grounds
versions.policyRationale: s.105 is designed to ensure that automatically unfair dismissal protections cannot be circumvented by dressing up a dismissal as a redundancy. Every time Parliament creates a new automatically unfair dismissal category, a corresponding subsection is added to s.105 to extend the protection to the redundancy context.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 adds s.104I (fire-and-rehire) as a new automatically unfair dismissal category (expected October 2026). This will be caught by s.105 — selection for redundancy because the employee refused to agree a contractual variation will be automatically unfair. Additionally, the reduction of the qualifying period to 6 months (January 2027) will not affect s.105 claims directly (which are already exempt from the qualifying period for most protected grounds), but it will increase the volume of ordinary unfair dismissal claims that run alongside s.105 claims.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Fire-and-rehire provisions expected October 2026
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the addition of fire-and-rehire to the auto-unfair list expands the scope of s.105. Employers who use redundancy as a mechanism to achieve contractual change (a common restructuring approach) face s.105 exposure if the selection is connected to the employee's refusal to accept the variation.

refs: ERA 1996 s.105 (this section — automatically unfair selection for redundancy)
ERA 1996 s.105(1)(a)–(c) (the three-part test: redundancy + comparators + protected reason)
ERA 1996 s.105(3) (health and safety — s.100)
ERA 1996 s.105(6A) (whistleblowing — s.103A)
ERA 1996 s.99 (family leave — protected via Reg.20(2) of SI 1999/3312)
ERA 1996 s.100 (health and safety)
ERA 1996 s.103A (whistleblowing)
ERA 1996 s.104 (assertion of statutory rights)
ERA 1996 s.104C (flexible working)
ERA 1996 s.108(3)(h) (s.105 claims exempt from qualifying period)
ERA 1996 s.120 (minimum basic award for s.105(3) and s.105(6A) selections — £8,763)
ERA 1996 s.124(1A) (compensatory award cap does NOT apply to s.105(3) and s.105(6A))
ERA 1996 s.139 (definition of redundancy — the genuine redundancy element)
TULRCA 1992 s.153 (trade union — redundancy selection on union grounds)
Williams v Compair Maxam [1982] (fair redundancy process — cross-reference)
ERA 2025 (adds s.104I fire-and-rehire — new auto-unfair ground caught by s.105)

leadingCases.name: O'Dea v ISC Chemicals Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Where the employee alleges that their selection for redundancy was for a protected reason, the tribunal must examine the reason for the SELECTION — not the reason for the redundancy itself. The redundancy may be genuine, but if the protected reason influenced WHY this particular employee was selected, the dismissal is automatically unfair.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the s.105 analysis focuses on the reason for the SELECTION, not the reason for the redundancy. The employer must show that the protected reason played no part in the selection decision.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational EWCA authority on the s.105 mechanism. Establishes the critical distinction between the reason for the redundancy (which may be genuine) and the reason for the selection (which may be tainted).

leadingCases.name: Smiths Industries Aerospace & Defence Systems v Rawlings
leadingCases.principle: Selection for redundancy on grounds of trade union membership or activities is automatically unfair under TULRCA s.153 (which mirrors s.105). The employer cannot use selection criteria that directly or indirectly penalise trade union activities — such as scoring employees lower for 'flexibility' if they have been absent on union duties.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that selection criteria that indirectly penalised trade union activities (such as counting union absence against employees in the selection scoring) rendered the selection automatically unfair.
leadingCases.significance: Illustrates how INDIRECT discrimination in selection criteria can taint the selection. Important for all protected grounds — an employer's selection matrix may appear neutral but if it disproportionately impacts employees who have exercised protected rights, the selection is tainted.

leadingCases.name: Hendy Banks City Print Ltd v Fairbrother
leadingCases.principle: Where an employee on maternity leave is selected for redundancy and the employer fails to offer a suitable alternative vacancy (in breach of Reg.10 of the 1999 Regulations), the selection is automatically unfair under s.105 read with s.99/Reg.20. The Reg.10 priority obligation applies specifically in the redundancy context.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the employer's failure to offer the employee on maternity leave a suitable alternative vacancy — when such a vacancy existed — rendered the redundancy dismissal automatically unfair under s.99/s.105.
leadingCases.significance: Connects s.105 to the Reg.10 redundancy priority obligation for maternity (now extended to 18-month post-birth period since April 2024). Failure to offer a suitable alternative to a protected employee is both a Reg.10 breach and a s.105 automatically unfair selection.

leadingCases.name: Mott v Secure Care UK Ltd
leadingCases.principle: In a s.105/s.103A case (whistleblowing redundancy selection), the causation test is whether the protected disclosure was the REASON or PRINCIPAL REASON for the selection — the same test as s.103A itself. The tribunal must not apply the lower s.47B 'material influence' test. However, the timing of the selection (immediately after a disclosure) may give rise to a strong inference.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the tribunal had applied the wrong causation test — using the s.47B 'material influence' test instead of the s.103A 'reason or principal reason' test. The case was remitted for the correct test to be applied.
leadingCases.significance: Critical clarification of the causation test for s.105/s.103A claims. The s.103A 'principal reason' test applies — not the lower s.47B 'material influence' test. This makes s.105 whistleblowing claims harder than s.47B detriment claims, but the timing and circumstances may create strong inferential evidence.

leadingCases.name: Capita Hartshead Ltd v Byard
leadingCases.principle: The s.105(1)(b) comparator requirement — that the redundancy circumstances applied equally to other employees in similar positions — must be established before the tribunal can examine the reason for selection. If the employee was the only person in their role, there is no comparator and s.105 does not apply (though s.99/100/103A may still apply directly).
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that s.105(1)(b) requires a genuine pool of comparable employees from which the claimant was selected. If the employee was unique in their role and no comparator existed, the s.105 mechanism is not engaged.
leadingCases.significance: Clarifies the scope of s.105 — it only applies where there was a selection exercise involving comparable employees. Where the employee's unique role is made redundant and no selection was needed, the claim route is the direct auto-unfair provision (e.g., s.99, s.100, s.103A), not s.105.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy: handling and managing the process
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on fair redundancy selection, including the obligation to use objective criteria that do not penalise employees for exercising protected rights.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on employee rights during redundancy including protection from selection on protected grounds.

commonErrors: Not recognising that a GENUINE redundancy can be AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR — s.105 does not require the employee to prove the redundancy is sham. The redundancy may be entirely genuine (s.139 satisfied), the process may appear fair, and the employer may have followed Williams v Compair Maxam — but if the reason the PARTICULAR employee was selected was a protected reason, the dismissal is automatically unfair

Confusing the reason for the REDUNDANCY with the reason for the SELECTION — s.105 asks why THIS employee was selected, not why the redundancy occurred. The redundancy may be driven by cost savings (a legitimate business reason), but if the employee was selected because she is pregnant or because he blew the whistle, the selection is tainted (O'Dea v ISC Chemicals [1995])

Using selection criteria that indirectly penalise protected activities — a selection matrix that appears neutral may indirectly discriminate against employees who have exercised protected rights. For example: scoring for 'attendance' that counts pregnancy-related absence; scoring for 'flexibility' that penalises employees who have refused unsafe work (s.100); scoring for 'team contribution' that penalises employees who have been absent on trade union duties (Smiths Industries v Rawlings [1996])

Not recognising that s.105 carries UNCAPPED compensation for H&S and whistleblowing selections — s.124(1A) exempts s.105(3) (H&S) and s.105(6A) (whistleblowing) from the compensatory award cap. An employer who selects a whistleblower for redundancy faces the same uncapped exposure as a direct s.103A dismissal. The minimum basic award of £8,763 also applies

Not understanding the comparator requirement — s.105(1)(b) requires that the redundancy circumstances applied equally to one or more OTHER employees in similar positions who were NOT dismissed. If the employee was the only person in their role, there is no comparator and s.105 does not apply (Capita Hartshead v Byard [2012]). The claim route is the direct auto-unfair provision instead

Applying the wrong causation test for whistleblowing selection — Mott v Secure Care [2021] clarifies that the s.103A 'reason or principal reason' test applies to s.105(6A) claims, NOT the lower s.47B 'material influence' test. The protected disclosure must be the principal reason for the selection, not merely a factor that influenced it

Not offering suitable alternative vacancies to protected employees — where a pregnancy/maternity employee is selected for redundancy and a suitable alternative vacancy exists, the employer must offer it with PRIORITY (Reg.10 of SI 1999/3312, as extended by the 2024 Regulations to the 18-month post-birth period). Failure to offer the alternative is both a Reg.10 breach and a s.105 automatically unfair selection (Hendy Banks v Fairbrother [2005])

Ignoring temporal proximity — where an employee is selected for redundancy shortly after exercising a protected right (making a disclosure, raising H&S concerns, announcing pregnancy), the timing creates a strong inference that the selection was tainted. The employer must be prepared to explain why the selection was not connected to the protected activity

Not recognising that no qualifying period applies — s.108(3)(h) exempts s.105 claims from the qualifying period for most protected grounds. An employee dismissed on day one by reason of redundancy where the selection was connected to a protected reason has a claim. This is true NOW, not just under ERA 2025

Not recognising that ERA 2025 adds fire-and-rehire as a new s.105 ground — from October 2026, s.104I (dismissal for refusing to agree a contractual variation) will be automatically unfair. Selection for redundancy because the employee refused to accept changed terms will also be caught by s.105. Employers who use redundancy as a mechanism to achieve contractual change face additional exposure

Not running the s.105 analysis alongside the s.98(4) analysis — even where s.105 is not engaged (e.g., no comparator, or the protected reason was not the principal reason for selection), the redundancy may still be ORDINARILY unfair under s.98(4) if the employer failed to follow a fair process. s.105 and s.98(4) are parallel analyses — both should be considered in every redundancy dismissal

sections.title: Qualifying period of employment
sections.summary: Currently requires two years' continuous employment before an employee can claim ordinary unfair dismissal under s.94. The qualifying period does not apply to automatically unfair dismissals (s.108(3)). ERA 2025 Schedule 2 will repeal s.108 entirely when commenced, creating day-one unfair dismissal rights — the most significant change to unfair dismissal law since the right was introduced in 1971.
sections.text:
(1) Section 94 does not apply to the dismissal of an employee unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the effective date of termination.

(2) If an employee is dismissed by reason of any such requirement or recommendation as is referred to in section 64(2), subsection (1) has effect in relation to that dismissal as if for the words "two years" there were substituted the words "one month".

(3) Subsection (1) does not apply if—

(aa) section 98ZA applies,

(b) section 99 (read with any regulations under that section) applies,

(c) subsection (1) of section 100 (read with subsections (2) and (3) of that section) applies,

(d) subsection (1) of section 101 (read with subsection (2) of that section) or subsection (3) of that section applies,

(da) section 101B applies,

(dd) section 104A applies,

(de) section 104B applies,

(df) section 104C applies,

(dg) section 104D applies,

(e) section 102 applies,

(f) section 103 applies,

(ff) section 103A applies,

(g) subsection (1) of section 104 (read with subsections (2) and (3) of that section) applies,

(gg) section 104E applies,

(gh) section 104F applies,

(gi) section 104G applies,

(gj) section 104H applies,

(gk) section 104I applies,

(gl) section 104J applies,

(gm) section 104K applies,

(h) section 105 applies,

(hh) paragraph 5(3) of the Schedule to the Reserved Forces (Safeguard of Employment) Act 1985 (c.17) applies,

(i) section 23A or 23B of the Employment Relations Act 1999 applies.
sections.keyPrinciple: The qualifying period is a gateway condition, not a merits test. Satisfying it merely opens the door to s.94; it does not determine outcome. The period has fluctuated between 6 months and 2 years since the right to claim unfair dismissal was created by the Industrial Relations Act 1971. ERA 2025 will repeal s.108 entirely — the first time in 54 years that there will be no qualifying period at all.

versions.text:
(1) Section 94 does not apply to the dismissal of an employee unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the effective date of termination.

(2) If an employee is dismissed by reason of any such requirement or recommendation as is referred to in section 64(2), subsection (1) has effect in relation to that dismissal as if for the words "two years" there were substituted the words "one month".

(3) Subsection (1) does not apply if—

(a) section 84 or 96(1) applies,

(b) subsection (1) of section 99 (read with subsection (2) of that section) or subsection (3) of that section applies,

(c) subsection (1) of section 100 (read with subsections (2) and (3) of that section) applies,

(d) subsection (1) of section 101 (read with subsection (2) of that section) or subsection (3) of that section applies,

(e) section 102 applies,

(f) section 103 applies,

(g) subsection (1) of section 104 (read with subsections (2) and (3) of that section) applies, or

(h) section 105 applies.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating Employment Protection (Consolidation) Act 1978 s.64)
versions.policyRationale: The 1996 Act consolidated the two-year qualifying period that had been in place since the Employment Act 1985 s.6 increased it from one year. The Conservative Government's position was that a longer qualifying period encouraged employers to hire by reducing the risk of unfair dismissal claims in the early period of employment. The original s.108(3) listed 8 exceptions (automatically unfair reasons) that bypassed the qualifying period.

versions.text:
(1) Section 94 does not apply to the dismissal of an employee unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than one year ending with the effective date of termination.

(2) If an employee is dismissed by reason of any such requirement or recommendation as is referred to in section 64(2), subsection (1) has effect in relation to that dismissal as if for the words "one year" there were substituted the words "one month".

(3) Subsection (1) does not apply if— [list of exceptions, expanding through this period as new automatically unfair reasons were enacted]
versions.amendedBy: Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 1999 (SI 1999/1436)
versions.policyRationale: The Blair Government reduced the qualifying period from two years to one year as part of the Fairness at Work programme. The White Paper (Cm 3968) originally proposed a reduction to six months, but one year was the compromise reached after consultation with employer representatives. The reduction was implemented by statutory instrument under ERA 1996 s.236(3), not by primary legislation — demonstrating that the qualifying period can be changed without an Act of Parliament. The TUC argued for complete abolition; the CBI argued for retention at two years.

versions.text:
(1) Section 94 does not apply to the dismissal of an employee unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the effective date of termination.

(2) If an employee is dismissed by reason of any such requirement or recommendation as is referred to in section 64(2), subsection (1) has effect in relation to that dismissal as if for the words "two years" there were substituted the words "one month".

(3) Subsection (1) does not apply if— [current list of 20+ exceptions — see full text above]
versions.amendedBy: Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 2012 (SI 2012/989)
versions.policyRationale: The Coalition Government restored the two-year qualifying period as part of the Employment Law Review announced in the Growth Review 2011. The stated aim was to reduce the regulatory burden on employers and encourage hiring by removing the risk of unfair dismissal claims during the first two years of employment. The BIS Impact Assessment estimated the change would result in an additional 2,000-3,000 employees per year losing access to the unfair dismissal jurisdiction. The transitional provision (SI 2012/989 art.4) preserved the one-year period for employees whose continuous employment began before 6 April 2012.

pendingAmendment.effect: Repeals section 108 entirely. Does not substitute a shorter period — it removes the qualifying period concept altogether. Every employee will have the right to claim unfair dismissal from day one of employment. However, ERA 2025 introduces an 'initial period of employment' (a statutory probation period, expected to be around 9 months) during which a simplified 'lighter touch' dismissal process will apply. Compensation for unfair dismissal during the initial period is expected to be lower than for established employees.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Not yet in force as at March 2026. ERA 2025 received Royal Assent but the s.108 repeal has not been commenced. The Government's stated intention is Autumn 2026 at the earliest, potentially applying only to new joiners from the commencement date.
pendingAmendment.anticipatedDate: Autumn 2026 at the earliest — subject to consultation outcomes on the initial period of employment
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: CRITICAL — affects every employer in Great Britain. Every dismissal from day one of employment will carry unfair dismissal exposure once commenced. Employers who currently rely on the two-year qualifying period to manage out short-service employees without formal procedures will face a fundamentally different risk landscape. The ACEI unfair dismissal category will require recalibration to reflect universal day-one exposure.

initialPeriodOfEmployment.concept: ERA 2025 introduces a new concept of an 'initial period of employment' — effectively a statutory probation period — during which the employer may dismiss using a 'lighter touch' process. The period is expected to be around 9 months but the exact duration and the procedural requirements are subject to consultation.
initialPeriodOfEmployment.lighterTouchProcess: The Government has indicated that employers will need to: (1) hold a meeting with the employee to discuss concerns; (2) allow the employee to be accompanied by a trade union representative or colleague; (3) make a decision on dismissal. This is simpler than the full ACAS Code procedure but still requires a structured process — dismissal without any process will remain unfair.
initialPeriodOfEmployment.compensationDuringIPE: Compensation for unfair dismissal during the initial period is expected to be lower than the standard compensatory award, but the exact quantum is subject to consultation.
initialPeriodOfEmployment.consultationStatus: Consultation ongoing as at March 2026. Final regulations not yet laid.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 Schedule 2 repeals s.108 entirely. This is not a reduction to zero — it is a repeal. Section 108 will cease to exist. A future government wishing to reintroduce a qualifying period would need primary legislation, not a statutory instrument. This makes the change structurally permanent in a way that previous qualifying period changes (which were done by SI) were not.

pepperVHartStatements.statement: The reforms are not about making it easier to dismiss workers or to undermine job security. Day one rights are about ensuring that from the first day in a new job, workers have the security of knowing they cannot be unfairly dismissed. The initial period of employment will give employers confidence that they can assess whether a new recruit is right for the role.
pepperVHartStatements.speaker: Jonathan Reynolds MP, Secretary of State for Business and Trade
pepperVHartStatements.significance: The ministerial statement at Second Reading of the Employment Rights Bill (now ERA 2025) addressing the purpose and scope of the qualifying period removal. Admissible under Pepper v Hart [1993] UKHL 3 to establish parliamentary intent regarding the initial period of employment concept.

precedingLegislation.notes: The right to claim unfair dismissal was created for the first time. Qualifying period set at 26 weeks.

precedingLegislation.instrument: Trade Union and Labour Relations Act 1974 Sch.1 para.4
precedingLegislation.notes: The 1974 Act retained the 26-week qualifying period.

precedingLegislation.instrument: Unfair Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 1979 (SI 1979/959)
precedingLegislation.notes: Callaghan Government increased the qualifying period to 1 year.

precedingLegislation.instrument: Unfair Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 1985 (SI 1985/782)
precedingLegislation.notes: Thatcher Government increased the qualifying period to 2 years for employers with fewer than 20 employees.
Extended the 2-year qualifying period to all employers regardless of size.
Consolidated the existing 2-year qualifying period from EPCA 1978.
Blair Government reduced to 1 year under Fairness at Work programme.
Coalition Government restored 2 years under Growth Review.
Starmer Government — complete repeal. First time in 54 years with no qualifying period.

refs: ERA 1996 s.94 (the right not to be unfairly dismissed — s.108 is the gateway condition for s.94)
ERA 1996 s.108(3) (exceptions — automatically unfair reasons that bypass the qualifying period: over 20 categories)
ERA 1996 s.236(3) (power to vary qualifying period by statutory instrument — used in 1999 and 2012)
ERA 1996 s.211 (definition of continuous employment — starts from the beginning of employment, not from date of contract)
ERA 1996 s.212 (weeks counting in computing period of continuous employment)
ERA 1996 s.64(2) (health requirements — one month qualifying period under s.108(2))
ERA 2025 Schedule 2 (repeal of s.108 — not yet in force)
Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 1999 (SI 1999/1436)
Unfair Dismissal and Statement of Reasons for Dismissal (Variation of Qualifying Period) Order 2012 (SI 2012/989)
Fairness at Work White Paper (Cm 3968, 1998) — proposed 6 months, compromised at 1 year
BIS Growth Review 2011 — rationale for restoration to 2 years
House of Commons Library Research Briefing CBP-8347 (Unfair dismissal: qualifying service rule)
R v Secretary of State for Employment, ex parte Seymour-Smith [2000] UKHL 12
ACAS Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)

leadingCases.name: R v Secretary of State for Employment, ex parte Seymour-Smith and Perez
leadingCases.principle: The two-year qualifying period for unfair dismissal was indirectly discriminatory against women (because a higher proportion of women than men had less than two years' continuous service), but was justified as a proportionate means of achieving the legitimate aim of encouraging recruitment.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords, following a preliminary ruling from the CJEU (C-167/97), held that the qualifying period had a disparate adverse impact on women. However, by a 3-2 majority, the Lords held that the Government had discharged the burden of showing that the qualifying period was justified as encouraging employers to recruit by reducing the perceived risk of unfair dismissal claims. Lord Nicholls dissented, arguing the Government had not produced sufficient evidence of a causal link between the qualifying period and increased recruitment.
leadingCases.significance: The leading UKHL authority on the legality of the qualifying period. Although the challenge ultimately failed, the case established that the qualifying period IS indirectly discriminatory against women and can only be maintained if objectively justified. This has implications for the ERA 2025 initial period of employment — any new qualifying threshold will need to pass the same justification test. The dissenting judgment of Lord Nicholls on evidential burden is frequently cited in discrimination scholarship.

leadingCases.name: Secretary of State for Justice v Slee
leadingCases.principle: The qualifying period runs from the beginning of the employee's employment, not from the date they entered their current role or position. Continuous employment across different roles with the same employer counts.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that continuous employment for the purposes of s.108 is calculated from the date the employee began employment with the employer (or an associated employer), regardless of changes in role, location, or terms during that period. The employee does not need to have been in the same role for the qualifying period.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the basic but frequently misunderstood principle that the qualifying period relates to continuous employment with the employer, not continuous service in a particular role. Important for employers who restructure or redeploy during the qualifying period.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Checking if your employee can make an unfair dismissal claim
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on the qualifying period, including the exceptions for automatically unfair dismissals and the calculation of continuous employment. Confirms that the two-year qualifying period is the current position but notes the ERA 2025 changes.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Unfair dismissal: qualifying service rule (CBP-8347)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive parliamentary briefing on the history of the qualifying period from 1971 to 2025, including the full legislative chronology, the Seymour-Smith challenge, and the ERA 2025 repeal. Updated to reflect current position.

commonErrors: Assuming the qualifying period protects employers from ALL unfair dismissal claims — s.108(3) lists over 20 categories of automatically unfair dismissal that bypass the qualifying period entirely. Pregnancy, whistleblowing, health and safety, trade union activities, assertion of statutory rights, and many others are day-one rights NOW, regardless of s.108(1)

Calculating continuous employment from the date of the current contract rather than from the date employment began — if an employee has been employed by the same employer (or an associated employer) in different roles, the continuous employment runs from the original start date

Not recognising the transitional provisions — the 2012 increase from 1 year to 2 years only applies to employees whose continuous employment began on or after 6 April 2012. Employees who started before that date retain the 1-year qualifying period. Similarly, when ERA 2025 commences, transitional provisions may apply

Treating the qualifying period as a 'safe harbour' during which the employer can dismiss for any reason — even during the qualifying period, dismissals can still be challenged as automatically unfair (s.108(3)), discriminatory (EqA 2010), wrongful (breach of contract), or in breach of other statutory rights

Not preparing for the ERA 2025 repeal — s.108 will be repealed entirely, not amended. A future government cannot simply issue a statutory instrument to reinstate a qualifying period (as was done in 1999 and 2012) because there will be no s.108 to vary. Primary legislation would be required. This makes the change structurally permanent

Overlooking the interaction between the qualifying period and the effective date of termination — the qualifying period must be satisfied 'ending with the effective date of termination' (s.108(1)). Where the employer gives notice, the effective date may be the end of the notice period, not the date notice was given, potentially extending the qualifying period calculation in the employee's favour

Misunderstanding the 'initial period of employment' concept in ERA 2025 — this is NOT a new qualifying period. It is a statutory probation period during which a 'lighter touch' dismissal process applies. The employee will still have the right to claim unfair dismissal during the initial period; it is the procedural requirements and compensation that differ, not the existence of the right

Not recognising that the qualifying period does not apply to redundancy payment claims — ERA 1996 s.155 sets a separate two-year qualifying period for statutory redundancy payments, which is NOT affected by the ERA 2025 repeal of s.108. The redundancy payment qualifying period remains at two years

sections.title: Complaints to employment tribunal — time limits
sections.summary: An employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint of unfair dismissal unless it is presented before the end of three months beginning with the effective date of termination — or within such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable where it is satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the complaint to be presented within that period. The 'three months minus one day' rule means: EDT of 15 January → deadline 14 April. Before presenting a claim, the employee must first notify ACAS for mandatory Early Conciliation (since 6 April 2014), which 'stops the clock' under s.207B. From 1 December 2025, the maximum EC period is 12 weeks (previously 6 weeks). ERA 2025 extends the primary time limit from 3 months to 6 months — expected to come into force in October 2026. When combined with the 12-week EC period, the effective limitation window will extend to approximately 9–10 months.
sections.text:
(1) A complaint may be presented to an employment tribunal against an employer by any person that he was unfairly dismissed by the employer.

(2) Subject to the following provisions of this section, an employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this section unless it is presented to the tribunal—

(a) before the end of the period of three months beginning with the effective date of termination, or

(b) within such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable in a case where it is satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the complaint to be presented before the end of that period of three months.

(3) Where a dismissal is with notice, an employment tribunal shall consider a complaint under this section if it is presented after the notice is given but before the effective date of termination.

(4) In relation to a member of the naval, military or air forces of the Crown, subsection (2) has effect as if for the words 'three months' there were substituted the words 'six months'.

Section 207B (extension of time limits to facilitate conciliation before institution of proceedings) applies for the purposes of subsection (2)(a).
sections.keyPrinciple: The s.111 time limit is STRICT. The 'not reasonably practicable' test for extension is much harder to satisfy than the 'just and equitable' extension available for discrimination claims under EqA 2010 s.123. In practice, most out-of-time unfair dismissal claims are struck out. The time limit runs from the effective date of termination (EDT) — not from the date the employee became aware of the unfairness, not from the date they took legal advice, and not from the end of an internal appeal. ACAS Early Conciliation 'stops the clock' under s.207B — but only for the period between Day A and Day B, and only if the EC process starts AFTER the EDT (Raison v DF Capital [2025] EAT).

threeMonthsMinusOneDay.rule: The time limit is 'before the end of the period of three months beginning with the effective date of termination'. This means: add three calendar months to the EDT, then subtract one day.
examples: EDT 30 November → deadline 28 February (or 29 February in a leap year)
threeMonthsMinusOneDay.practicalNote: The calculation uses calendar months, not fixed 30-day periods. The deadline always falls on the corresponding date minus one day in the third subsequent month. Where the corresponding date does not exist (e.g., 31 November), the last day of the month applies.

acasEarlyConciliation.since: 6 April 2014 (Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.7, inserting ETA 1996 s.18A)
acasEarlyConciliation.mechanism: Before presenting a tribunal claim, the employee must contact ACAS and provide prescribed information. ACAS will attempt conciliation. The time between Day A (the day the employee contacts ACAS) and Day B (the day the EC certificate is issued) 'stops the clock' under s.207B.

s207B.stopTheClock: s.207B(3): In working out when the time limit expires, the period beginning with the day after Day A and ending with Day B is not to be counted. This effectively pauses the limitation clock for the duration of EC.
s207B.oneMonthGuarantee: s.207B(4): If the time limit (as extended by s.207B(3)) would still expire between Day A and one month after Day B, the time limit is extended to one month after Day B. This is a safety net ensuring at least one month after EC ends to present the claim.
s207B.raison: Raison v DF Capital Bank Ltd [2025] EAT — the EAT held that the s.207B 'stop the clock' mechanism only applies to EC days that fall AFTER the EDT. If the employee contacts ACAS before being dismissed, the days between Day A and the EDT do not count towards the s.207B extension. The clock can only be stopped if it has already started.

acasEarlyConciliation.era2025Impact: When the ERA 2025 extends the primary time limit to 6 months, combined with the 12-week EC period, the effective limitation window will extend to approximately 9–10 months from the EDT. This significantly increases the period of uncertainty for employers.

notReasonablyPracticable.test: s.111(2)(b) permits a late claim only where the tribunal is satisfied that (1) it was NOT REASONABLY PRACTICABLE for the complaint to be presented within the 3-month period; AND (2) the complaint was presented within such further period as the tribunal considers REASONABLE.
notReasonablyPracticable.palmerTest: Palmer v Southend-on-Sea Borough Council [1984] EWCA — the Court of Appeal held that 'reasonably practicable' means 'reasonably feasible'. The question is not whether it was reasonable for the employee not to present the claim in time, but whether it was FEASIBLE for them to do so. This is a stricter test than the discrimination 'just and equitable' standard.
factors: Ignorance of the right to claim — only excuses lateness if the ignorance was itself reasonable (Dedman v British Building & Engineering Appliances [1974])
Illness or incapacity — may excuse lateness if the illness genuinely prevented the employee from presenting a claim or instructing a representative
Misleading advice from a solicitor — does NOT excuse lateness; the solicitor's negligence is attributed to the employee (Dedman [1974])
Internal appeal pending — does NOT excuse lateness; the time limit runs regardless of whether an internal appeal is pending (Palmer [1984])
Postal delays — may excuse lateness if the claim was posted in time but arrived late (capital Post Office v Gallagher [1970])
Awaiting outcome of related proceedings — generally does NOT excuse lateness
notReasonablyPracticable.distinction: The 'not reasonably practicable' test (unfair dismissal) is MUCH stricter than the 'just and equitable' test (discrimination under EqA 2010 s.123). In discrimination cases, the tribunal has a broad discretion to extend time. In unfair dismissal cases, the tribunal can only extend time where it was genuinely NOT FEASIBLE to present the claim in time. This is why employees should always present their unfair dismissal claim within time, even if their discrimination claim might be extended.

versions.text: [The substantive provisions of s.111(1)–(4) have not been materially amended since the 1996 Act was enacted. The ACAS Early Conciliation mechanism (s.207B) was added by the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.7 (in force 6 April 2014). The primary time limit of 3 months has been in place since the introduction of unfair dismissal rights in 1971.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating EPCA 1978 s.67)
versions.policyRationale: The 3-month time limit reflects the policy that employment disputes should be resolved quickly. The 'not reasonably practicable' extension test is deliberately strict — it prevents employees from sitting on their rights.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 extends the primary time limit for unfair dismissal complaints from 3 months to 6 months. The 'not reasonably practicable' extension test is expected to remain unchanged. When combined with the 12-week ACAS Early Conciliation period (from 1 December 2025), the effective limitation window will extend to approximately 9–10 months from the EDT. Breach of contract claims (wrongful dismissal) in the tribunal remain at 3 months.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Expected October 2026. Will apply to dismissals with an EDT on or after the commencement date. Earlier dismissals will continue to be subject to the 3-month time limit.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: VERY HIGH — the extension from 3 to 6 months fundamentally changes the employer's risk landscape. Currently, employers know within approximately 4–5 months of a dismissal whether a claim will be brought. Under ERA 2025, the uncertainty extends to 9–10 months. This requires longer document retention, delayed settlement decisions, and more cautious provisioning.

refs: ERA 1996 s.111 (this section — time limit for unfair dismissal complaints)
ERA 1996 s.97 (effective date of termination — the date from which the time limit runs)
ERA 1996 s.207B (ACAS Early Conciliation — stop the clock mechanism)
ETA 1996 s.18A (mandatory Early Conciliation requirement — inserted by ERRA 2013 s.7)
ERA 2025 (extends primary time limit from 3 months to 6 months — expected October 2026)
Employment Tribunals (Early Conciliation: Exemptions and Rules of Procedure) (Amendment) Regulations 2025 (extends EC period to 12 weeks from 1 December 2025)
EqA 2010 s.123 (time limit for discrimination claims — 'just and equitable' extension, more generous)
Employment Tribunals Extension of Jurisdiction (England and Wales) Order 1994 (wrongful dismissal — 3-month time limit, UNCHANGED by ERA 2025)
Palmer v Southend-on-Sea Borough Council [1984] IRLR 119
Raison v DF Capital Bank Ltd [2025] EAT

leadingCases.name: Palmer v Southend-on-Sea Borough Council
leadingCases.principle: The 'not reasonably practicable' test means 'not reasonably feasible'. The question is whether it was feasible for the employee to present the claim within the 3-month time limit — not whether it was reasonable for them not to do so. This is a stricter test than the 'just and equitable' extension available for discrimination claims. An employee who is pursuing an internal appeal does NOT have an excuse for late presentation — the time limit runs regardless.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that 'reasonably practicable' does not mean 'reasonable' in the broad sense — it means 'reasonably feasible'. The tribunal must ask: was it feasible for the employee to present the claim in time? If it was feasible but the employee chose not to (e.g., because they were awaiting the outcome of an internal appeal), the claim is out of time.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational EWCA authority on the 'not reasonably practicable' test. Cited in virtually every out-of-time unfair dismissal application. Establishes both the test and the principle that internal appeals do not excuse late presentation.

leadingCases.name: Dedman v British Building & Engineering Appliances Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Ignorance of the right to claim unfair dismissal does NOT excuse late presentation unless the ignorance was itself reasonable. Where the employee had access to information (e.g., through a trade union, Citizens Advice, or legal advice) that would have informed them of the time limit, their ignorance is not reasonable. Misleading advice from a solicitor does NOT excuse late presentation — the solicitor's negligence is attributed to the employee.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that an employee who instructs a solicitor to act cannot escape the consequences of the solicitor's negligence in missing the time limit. The employee chose to instruct the solicitor and must bear the consequences of the solicitor's failure.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes two critical principles: (1) ignorance only excuses if it was reasonable (not merely genuine); (2) solicitor negligence is attributed to the employee. The second principle makes the time limit even more unforgiving — employees cannot blame their lawyers.

leadingCases.name: Raison v DF Capital Bank Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The s.207B 'stop the clock' mechanism only applies to the period of ACAS Early Conciliation that falls AFTER the effective date of termination. If the employee contacts ACAS before the EDT, the days between Day A and the EDT do not count towards the s.207B extension. The clock can only be stopped if it has already started.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that it would give claimants an inexplicable advantage if they could inflate their normal time limit by starting EC before the time limit had even begun. Section 207B is designed to prevent claimants from losing time during the normal limitation period due to EC — it does not provide a bonus extension for EC days that predate the start of the limitation period.
leadingCases.significance: The most recent significant authority on the s.207B/EC interaction — decided in 2025. Closes a potential loophole where employees could extend their time limit by starting EC before being dismissed. Particularly relevant where the employee anticipates dismissal and contacts ACAS proactively.

leadingCases.name: Robertson v Bexley Community Centre
leadingCases.principle: The 'not reasonably practicable' test is applied strictly. Where the employee was aware of the right to claim and had access to legal advice, it is almost always reasonably practicable to present the claim in time. The tribunal should not adopt a 'liberal' approach to the time limit — it is a jurisdictional requirement, not a discretionary filter.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal emphasised the strict nature of the test. The tribunal has no general discretion to extend time — it can only do so where the specific statutory condition ('not reasonably practicable') is satisfied.
leadingCases.significance: Reinforces the strict approach to the unfair dismissal time limit. Important because tribunals occasionally adopt a more sympathetic approach — Robertson confirms that sympathy is not a substitute for satisfying the statutory test.

leadingCases.name: John Lewis Partnership v Charman
leadingCases.principle: Where the employee's delay is caused by awaiting the outcome of an internal grievance or appeal, this does NOT make it 'not reasonably practicable' to present the claim in time. The tribunal claim should be presented within the time limit even if internal processes are ongoing.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that pursuing an internal appeal does not excuse late presentation of a tribunal claim. The employee can and should present the tribunal claim within the time limit and continue the internal appeal in parallel.
leadingCases.significance: Practical guidance for employees and employers — internal processes do not suspend the tribunal time limit. Employees who wait for the outcome of an appeal or grievance before claiming risk being out of time.

leadingCases.name: Marks & Spencer plc v Williams-Ryan
leadingCases.principle: In rare circumstances, the 'not reasonably practicable' test may be satisfied where the employer's internal appeal process was misleading about the time limits, or where the employer led the employee to believe that the internal process would resolve the matter. But the bar remains high — the employee must show that they were genuinely misled, not merely that they assumed the internal process would succeed.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that where the employer's appeal process was structured in such a way that the employee was misled into believing that the internal process was a substitute for a tribunal claim, it may not have been reasonably practicable to present the claim in time. But this is an exceptional circumstance — the default rule is that internal processes do not excuse late presentation.
leadingCases.significance: A rare example of the 'not reasonably practicable' test being satisfied. Important because it shows that the test is not absolute — but the circumstances must be truly exceptional. The employer's conduct in misleading the employee about the process was the key factor.

regulatoryGuidance.title: How early conciliation works
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on the mandatory Early Conciliation process including the 12-week period (from 1 December 2025), Day A and Day B, and the stop-the-clock mechanism.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Making a claim to an employment tribunal
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on employment tribunal time limits and the ACAS Early Conciliation requirement.

commonErrors: Not calculating the deadline correctly — the time limit is 'before the end of the period of three months beginning with the effective date of termination'. This means: add three calendar months to the EDT, then subtract one day. EDT of 15 January = deadline 14 April. EDT of 1 March = deadline 31 May. Getting this calculation wrong by even one day is fatal — the tribunal has no general discretion to extend

Assuming that an internal appeal or grievance extends the time limit — Palmer v Southend-on-Sea [1984] establishes that the 3-month time limit runs regardless of whether an internal appeal or grievance is pending. The employee should present the tribunal claim within the time limit AND continue the internal appeal in parallel. Waiting for the appeal outcome is NOT a valid reason for late presentation

Confusing the unfair dismissal extension test with the discrimination extension test — for unfair dismissal, the test is 'NOT REASONABLY PRACTICABLE' (strict — was it feasible to claim in time?). For discrimination, the test is 'JUST AND EQUITABLE' (broad discretion). The unfair dismissal test is much harder to satisfy. An employee whose discrimination claim is in time but whose unfair dismissal claim is late cannot assume the same extension applies to both

Assuming that ignorance of rights excuses late presentation — ignorance only excuses lateness if the ignorance was itself REASONABLE (Dedman [1974]). If the employee had access to information about the time limit (through a union, Citizens Advice, employer handbook, or legal advice) and failed to use it, their ignorance is not reasonable

Relying on a solicitor's advice as an excuse for late presentation — Dedman [1974] establishes that a solicitor's negligence in missing the time limit is attributed to the employee. The employee chose to instruct the solicitor and must bear the consequences. The employee's remedy is against the negligent solicitor, not an extension of the time limit

Not understanding that ACAS Early Conciliation is MANDATORY — since 6 April 2014, the employee must contact ACAS before presenting a tribunal claim. Failure to do so renders the claim invalid. The EC certificate number must be included on the ET1 form. From 1 December 2025, the maximum EC period is 12 weeks (previously 6 weeks)

Starting ACAS Early Conciliation before the EDT and assuming it extends the time limit — Raison v DF Capital [2025] EAT clarifies that the s.207B stop-the-clock mechanism only applies to EC days that fall AFTER the EDT. If the employee contacts ACAS before being dismissed, the days between Day A and the EDT do not count towards the extension. The clock can only be stopped if it has already started

Not knowing that ERA 2025 extends the time limit to 6 months — ERA 2025 extends the primary time limit for unfair dismissal from 3 months to 6 months (expected October 2026). Combined with the 12-week EC period, the effective limitation window will extend to approximately 9–10 months. However, this only applies to dismissals with an EDT on or after the commencement date — earlier dismissals remain subject to the 3-month limit

Not knowing that breach of contract (wrongful dismissal) claims REMAIN at 3 months — ERA 2025 extends the unfair dismissal time limit but does NOT extend the 3-month time limit for breach of contract claims in the tribunal. An employee who brings an unfair dismissal claim within the new 6-month limit but outside the 3-month wrongful dismissal limit will lose the wrongful dismissal claim

Not presenting the claim during the notice period — s.111(3) permits an employee to present an unfair dismissal complaint after notice is given but BEFORE the effective date of termination. This is useful where the employee knows they will be dismissed and wants to secure their tribunal claim before the EDT

Assuming the time limit is exactly 3 months — the limit is 3 months minus one day. EDT of 15 March = deadline 14 June, NOT 15 June. This one-day difference has defeated many claims. The same calculation applies to the 6-month limit under ERA 2025 — it will be 6 months minus one day

sections.title: Basic award — calculation
sections.summary: The basic award is the first element of compensation for unfair dismissal. It is calculated using the same formula as statutory redundancy pay (s.162): age at dismissal × complete years of continuous service (maximum 20) × capped weekly pay. The formula uses three age bands: 0.5 week's pay per year for ages under 22, 1 week's pay per year for ages 22–40, and 1.5 weeks' pay per year for ages 41 and over. Weekly pay is capped at £719 (from 6 April 2025). Maximum basic award: £21,570. The basic award is SEPARATE from the compensatory award (s.123) — both are awarded together. The basic award may be reduced for contributory fault (s.122(2)), set off against a redundancy payment already received (s.122(4)), or reduced for unreasonable refusal of a reinstatement offer (s.122(1)). A minimum basic award of £8,763 applies for certain automatically unfair dismissals (s.100, s.103A, trade union reasons).
sections.text:
(1) Subject to the provisions of this section, sections 120 to 122 and section 126, the amount of the basic award shall be calculated by—

(a) determining the period, ending with the effective date of termination, during which the employee has been continuously employed,

(b) reckoning backwards from the end of that period the number of years of employment falling within that period, and

(c) allowing the appropriate amount for each of those years of employment.

(2) In subsection (1)(c) "the appropriate amount" means—

(a) one and a half weeks' pay for a year of employment in which the employee was not below the age of forty-one,

(b) one week's pay for a year of employment (not within paragraph (a)) in which he was not below the age of twenty-two, and

(c) half a week's pay for a year of employment not within paragraph (a) or (b).

(3) Where twenty years of employment have been reckoned under subsection (1), no account shall be taken under that subsection of any year of employment earlier than those twenty years.
sections.keyPrinciple: The basic award is a formulaic, mechanical calculation — there is no discretion in the formula itself. The tribunal calculates years of service (maximum 20) backwards from the effective date of termination, assigns each year to an age band (0.5/1.0/1.5 weeks' pay), applies the weekly pay cap, and multiplies. The result is the basic award before any reductions. Reductions may be applied for contributory fault, redundancy payment set-off, or unreasonable refusal of reinstatement. The basic award recognises the employee's service and investment in the employment relationship — it is NOT designed to compensate for financial loss (that is the compensatory award's function under s.123).

autoUnfair.applies: s.100 (health & safety), s.101A(d) (workforce representative), s.102(1) (pension trustee), s.103 (employee representative), s.103A (whistleblowing), s.105 (selection for redundancy on these grounds), TULRCA s.152 (trade union reasons)

currentLimits.annualReview: The weekly pay cap, maximum compensatory award, minimum basic award, and other limits are reviewed annually by the Secretary of State (typically from April each year) in line with RPI. The increases are made by statutory instrument under the Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order.

formula.step1: Determine the employee's period of continuous employment ending with the effective date of termination (EDT).
formula.step2: Count backwards from the EDT — reckon the number of complete years of employment (maximum 20 years).
formula.step3: For each year, apply the age-band multiplier based on the employee's age during that year: (a) 1.5 weeks' pay if the employee was aged 41 or over; (b) 1 week's pay if aged 22–40; (c) 0.5 week's pay if under 22.
formula.step4: Apply the weekly pay cap (£719 from 6 April 2025) — the employee's actual gross weekly pay is used only up to the cap. If gross weekly pay exceeds £719, the cap applies.
formula.step5: Multiply total weeks × capped weekly pay = basic award (before reductions).
formula.maximumCalculation: The maximum possible basic award is 30 weeks × £719 = £21,570 (achieved by an employee aged 61+ with 20+ years' service — all 20 years at the 1.5 multiplier).
formula.example: Employee aged 48, 15 years' service, gross weekly pay £900 (capped at £719): Ages 41–48 (7 years): 7 × 1.5 × £719 = £7,549.50; Ages 33–40 (8 years): 8 × 1.0 × £719 = £5,752. Total basic award = £13,301.50.

categories.effect: Where the tribunal finds that the conduct of the employee before the dismissal was such that it would be just and equitable to reduce the basic award, it shall reduce the award by such proportion as it considers just and equitable. Typical reductions range from 25% to 100%.
categories.practicalNote: Contributory fault applies to BOTH the basic award (s.122(2)) and the compensatory award (s.123(6)). The percentages may be different for each. Conduct that occurred after the dismissal cannot be taken into account for the basic award.

categories.effect: Where the employer has already paid a redundancy payment (whether statutory or contractual), the redundancy payment is set off against the basic award. Since the basic award and the statutory redundancy payment use the SAME formula, the set-off typically extinguishes the basic award entirely in redundancy cases.

Where the employee has unreasonably refused an offer of reinstatement in all respects as if they had not been dismissed, the tribunal shall reduce the basic award to such extent as it considers just and equitable.
Where the employer has made an ex gratia payment to the employee, the tribunal may reduce the basic award by such amount as it considers just and equitable.

basicAwardVsRedundancyPay.principle: The basic award (s.119) and the statutory redundancy payment (s.162) use the SAME formula — same age bands, same weekly pay cap, same 20-year service maximum. An employee who is made redundant and receives a statutory redundancy payment will NOT also receive a basic award (the redundancy payment is set off under s.122(4)). However, an employee who is unfairly dismissed for a reason OTHER than redundancy will receive BOTH the basic award AND the compensatory award.
basicAwardVsRedundancyPay.practicalNote: In a case where the employee is dismissed by reason of redundancy AND the dismissal is found to be unfair (e.g., unfair selection process), the employee receives: (1) the statutory redundancy payment (same formula as basic award); (2) the compensatory award (s.123) — but the basic award is set off against the redundancy payment already received. The net result is: redundancy payment + compensatory award (not redundancy payment + basic award + compensatory award).

versions.text: [The core formula in s.119(1)–(3) has not been amended since the 1996 Act was enacted. The weekly pay cap and related limits are updated annually by statutory instrument. The minimum basic award provisions (s.120) have been amended to add new categories of automatically unfair dismissal as they were created.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating EPCA 1978 s.72)
versions.policyRationale: The basic award formula has remained unchanged since its introduction. The formula's purpose is to provide a mechanical, predictable element of compensation that recognises the employee's investment in the employment relationship — analogous to the redundancy payment that recognises service.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 REMOVES the compensatory award cap (s.124(1)). However, this does NOT change the basic award formula under s.119 — the basic award continues to be calculated using the same age/service/weekly pay formula with the annual limits. The practical significance is that the basic award becomes relatively less important as a proportion of total compensation — when the compensatory award was capped at £118,223, the basic award (up to £21,570) represented a meaningful proportion. When the compensatory award is uncapped, the basic award will be a smaller fraction of large awards.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Compensatory award cap removal expected January 2027
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the basic award itself is unchanged, but the removal of the compensatory award cap means that the total exposure for unfair dismissal is now potentially unlimited (basic award + uncapped compensatory award). This is a transformative change for high-earning employees where the compensatory award cap previously limited the employer's total exposure.

refs: ERA 1996 s.119 (this section — basic award calculation formula)
ERA 1996 s.120 (minimum basic award for certain automatically unfair dismissals — £8,763)
ERA 1996 s.121 (basic award reduced to 2 weeks' pay where redundancy but no entitlement to redundancy payment)
ERA 1996 s.122 (reductions — contributory fault, redundancy set-off, refusal of reinstatement, ex gratia)
ERA 1996 s.123 (compensatory award — the second element of compensation)
ERA 1996 s.124 (compensatory award cap — £118,223 or 52 weeks' pay, whichever lower; REMOVED by ERA 2025 from January 2027)
ERA 1996 s.126 (basic award and compensatory award not to overlap — prevents double recovery)
ERA 1996 s.162 (statutory redundancy payment — uses the SAME formula as the basic award)
ERA 1996 ss.220–229 (calculation of a week's pay — the methodology for determining gross weekly pay)
ERA 1996 s.227 (maximum amount of a week's pay — £719 from 6 April 2025)
Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order 2025 (SI 2025/348) — current limits
ERA 2025 (removes compensatory award cap — expected January 2027)

leadingCases.name: Dunnachie v Kingston-upon-Hull City Council
leadingCases.principle: The compensatory award under s.123 is limited to financial loss — it does not include compensation for injury to feelings, stress, or humiliation. This makes the basic award the only element of unfair dismissal compensation that does not require proof of financial loss — it is awarded as of right once unfair dismissal is established, calculated mechanically from the formula.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the compensatory award is limited to financial loss arising from the dismissal. Non-economic loss (injury to feelings, humiliation, distress) is not recoverable under the unfair dismissal regime. This means the basic award, though modest, has particular significance as the only guaranteed element of compensation — it is payable regardless of whether the employee has suffered financial loss.
leadingCases.significance: While primarily a s.123 case, Dunnachie is critical for understanding the basic award's role. The basic award is the floor — it is payable even where the employee has suffered no financial loss (e.g., where they find a higher-paying job immediately). The compensatory award covers financial loss; the basic award recognises service. Together they form the complete remedy.

leadingCases.name: Optikinetics Ltd v Whooley
leadingCases.principle: The tribunal must apply the s.122(2) contributory fault reduction to the basic award separately from the s.123(6) reduction to the compensatory award. Different percentages may be applied to each. The basic award reduction focuses on the employee's conduct BEFORE dismissal; the compensatory award reduction may take a broader view of justice and equity.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the contributory fault assessment for the basic award (s.122(2)) and the compensatory award (s.123(6)) are separate exercises. The tribunal should state the percentage reduction for each independently.
leadingCases.significance: Practical guidance on contributory fault reductions. The basic award and compensatory award may be reduced by different percentages — the tribunal must make independent findings for each.

leadingCases.name: Digital Equipment Co Ltd v Clements (No.2)
leadingCases.principle: Where the employee has received a redundancy payment and the dismissal is subsequently found to be unfair, the redundancy payment is set off against the basic award under s.122(4). Since both use the same formula, the set-off typically extinguishes the basic award entirely. The employee retains the compensatory award.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the s.122(4) set-off of a redundancy payment against the basic award is mandatory — the tribunal must apply it. The practical effect is that in unfair redundancy cases, the employee's compensation consists of the redundancy payment already received plus the compensatory award for additional losses.
leadingCases.significance: Clarifies the interaction between the basic award and the redundancy payment in unfair redundancy cases. Prevents double recovery — the employee cannot receive both a redundancy payment and a basic award calculated on the same formula.

leadingCases.name: Cadbury Ltd v Doddington
leadingCases.principle: The basic award calculation counts backwards from the effective date of termination. Each year of employment is assigned to the age band applicable during that year. Where an employee's service spans multiple age bands, the different multipliers apply to the years in each band.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the basic award is calculated by working backwards from the EDT and applying the appropriate age-band multiplier for each year. The calculation is mechanical — there is no discretion in the formula.
leadingCases.significance: Early authority establishing the mechanical nature of the basic award calculation. The formula is applied strictly — the tribunal has no discretion to adjust the formula itself (though it has discretion to reduce the result for contributory fault).

leadingCases.name: Parker Foundry Ltd v Slack
leadingCases.principle: The basic award is calculated using the employee's gross weekly pay (subject to the cap) as at the calculation date — which is the EDT or, where notice was given, the date on which notice was given if that produces a different result. The weekly pay is calculated under ss.220–229.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the weekly pay for basic award purposes is calculated as at the calculation date using the methodology in ss.220–229. This includes normal working hours pay and, where hours vary, the 12-week reference period.
leadingCases.significance: Authority on the calculation date and weekly pay methodology. Important for employees whose pay changed shortly before dismissal or whose hours varied — the ss.220–229 methodology determines the weekly pay figure used in the formula.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Calculate your statutory redundancy pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The GOV.UK statutory redundancy pay calculator uses the same formula as the basic award (s.119 mirrors s.162). Can be used to estimate the basic award.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Compensation for unfair dismissal
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on unfair dismissal compensation including the basic award formula, the compensatory award, and available reductions.

commonErrors: Confusing the basic award with the compensatory award — the basic award (s.119) is a fixed formula based on age, service, and capped weekly pay. The compensatory award (s.123) is calculated on actual financial loss ('just and equitable'). They are SEPARATE elements, both awarded together in an unfair dismissal case. The basic award recognises service; the compensatory award compensates for loss

Not recognising the minimum basic award for automatically unfair dismissals — where the dismissal is automatically unfair for health and safety (s.100), whistleblowing (s.103A), or trade union reasons (TULRCA s.152), the minimum basic award is £8,763 (from 6 April 2025). This minimum applies BEFORE any reductions — the tribunal cannot reduce the basic award below this floor in these cases

Assuming the basic award can exceed £21,570 — the maximum basic award is 30 weeks × £719 = £21,570 (from 6 April 2025). Even an employee with 40 years' service at a high salary cannot exceed this — the 20-year service cap and the £719 weekly pay cap impose firm ceilings

Not applying the redundancy payment set-off in unfair redundancy cases — s.122(4) requires the redundancy payment to be set off against the basic award. Since both use the same formula, the set-off typically extinguishes the basic award entirely. The employee receives: redundancy payment + compensatory award (not redundancy payment + basic award + compensatory award). Failing to apply the set-off is a common tribunal error

Thinking that ERA 2025's removal of the compensatory award cap changes the basic award — the ERA 2025 cap removal applies to the COMPENSATORY award (s.124). The basic award formula (s.119) is UNCHANGED. The basic award continues to be calculated using the same age/service/weekly pay formula with the same annual limits. The cap removal means the TOTAL compensation is now unlimited (basic + uncapped compensatory), but the basic award component remains capped

Not calculating the weekly pay correctly under ss.220–229 — the weekly pay for basic award purposes must be calculated using the statutory methodology in ss.220–229, not simply the employee's last payslip. For employees with normal working hours, it is the gross pay for those hours. For employees with variable hours or pay, it is the average over the 12-week reference period. Overtime is only included if contractually guaranteed. The statutory cap (£719 from 6 April 2025) applies regardless of actual pay

Not applying separate contributory fault percentages for the basic and compensatory awards — the tribunal must assess contributory fault separately for the basic award (s.122(2)) and the compensatory award (s.123(6)). Different percentages may apply. The basic award reduction focuses on pre-dismissal conduct; the compensatory award reduction takes a broader 'just and equitable' approach (Optikinetics v Whooley [1999])

Overlooking that the basic award is payable even where no financial loss has occurred — the basic award is a mechanical calculation based on service, not on loss. An employee who is unfairly dismissed but immediately finds a higher-paying job is still entitled to the full basic award. The compensatory award may be nil (because there is no loss), but the basic award is payable as of right

Not knowing the current limits — the weekly pay cap, maximum basic award, minimum basic award, and compensatory award cap are updated annually (typically from April) by statutory instrument. Using the wrong year's limits produces incorrect calculations. Current limits (from 6 April 2025): weekly pay cap £719; maximum basic award £21,570; minimum basic award £8,763; compensatory award cap £118,223

Not counting service backwards from the effective date of termination — the formula works BACKWARDS from the EDT. Each year is assigned to the age band the employee was in during that year. Counting forward from the start date produces a different (incorrect) result where the employee's service spans multiple age bands

Forgetting that only COMPLETE years of service count — nine years and eleven months counts as nine years. The final partial year is not included in the calculation. This can make a significant difference where the employee is close to a service anniversary at the time of dismissal

dynamicLimits.instruction: The figures in currentLimits are valid from the effectiveFrom date. Eileen MUST cross-reference the kl_employment_limits table (or kl_legislative_alerts for the latest Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order) at runtime before quoting any figure. If the live table has not been updated for the current year, Eileen must inform the user that the figures are from the stated limits year and that updated limits may apply if their dismissal falls in a later year.
dynamicLimits.annualSource: Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order — published annually by the Secretary of State, typically in February/March, taking effect from 6 April

incomingLimits.note: Takes effect 7 DAYS from session date (30 March 2026). The LAST increase before ERA 2025 removes the compensatory cap on 1 January 2027.

sections.title: Compensatory award
sections.summary: The operative calculation provision for the compensatory award in every unfair dismissal case. The award must be 'such amount as the tribunal considers just and equitable in all the circumstances having regard to the loss sustained by the complainant in consequence of the dismissal in so far as that loss is attributable to action taken by the employer.' This is not a punitive award — it compensates for actual financial loss. The award is subject to the s.124 cap, the ACAS Code uplift (s.124A), the Polkey reduction, contributory conduct reduction (s.123(6)), the duty to mitigate (s.123(4)), and the order of deductions established by Digital Equipment v Clements [1998].
sections.text:
(1) Subject to the provisions of this section and sections 124, 124A and 126, the amount of the compensatory award shall be such amount as the tribunal considers just and equitable in all the circumstances having regard to the loss sustained by the complainant in consequence of the dismissal in so far as that loss is attributable to action taken by the employer.

(2) The loss referred to in subsection (1) shall be taken to include—

(a) any expenses reasonably incurred by the complainant in consequence of the dismissal, and

(b) subject to subsection (3), loss of any benefit which he might reasonably be expected to have had but for the dismissal.

(3) The loss referred to in subsection (1) shall be taken to include in respect of any loss of—

(a) any entitlement or potential entitlement to a payment on account of dismissal by reason of redundancy (whether in pursuance of Part XI or otherwise), or

(b) any expectation of such a payment,

only the loss referable to the amount (if any) by which the amount of that payment would have exceeded the amount of a basic award (apart from any reduction under section 122) in respect of the same dismissal.

(4) In ascertaining the loss referred to in subsection (1) the tribunal shall apply the same rule concerning the duty of a person to mitigate his loss as applies to damages recoverable under the common law of England and Wales or (as the case may be) Scotland.

(5) In determining, for the purposes of subsection (1), how far any loss sustained by the complainant was attributable to action taken by the employer, no account shall be taken of any pressure which by—

(a) calling, organising, procuring or financing a strike or other industrial action, or

(b) threatening to do so,

was exercised on the employer to dismiss the employee; and that question shall be determined as if no such pressure had been exercised.

(6) Where the tribunal finds that the dismissal was to any extent caused or contributed to by any action of the complainant, it shall reduce the amount of the compensatory award by such proportion as it considers just and equitable having regard to that finding.

(6A) Where—

(a) the reason (or principal reason) for the dismissal is that the complainant made a protected disclosure, and

(b) it appears to the tribunal that the disclosure was not made in good faith,

the tribunal may, if it considers it just and equitable in all the circumstances to do so, reduce any award it makes to the complainant by no more than 25%.

(7) If the amount of any payment made by the employer to the employee on the ground that the dismissal was by reason of redundancy (whether in pursuance of Part XI or otherwise) exceeds the amount of the basic award which would be payable but for section 122(4), that excess goes to reduce the amount of the compensatory award.

(8) Where the amount of the compensatory award falls to be calculated for the purposes of an award under section 117(3)(a), there shall be deducted from the compensatory award any award made under section 112(5) at the time of the order under section 113.
sections.keyPrinciple: The compensatory award is compensatory, not punitive. It compensates the employee for the financial loss they have actually sustained as a consequence of the dismissal.
