The tribunal calculates what the employee has lost — in earnings, benefits, pension, and statutory protections — and awards an amount that is 'just and equitable' to compensate for that loss. The award is then subject to reductions (Polkey, contributory conduct, mitigation failure, ex gratia payments) and the s.124 cap. The award covers financial loss ONLY — injury to feelings is not compensable under s.123 (Dunnachie [2004] UKHL 36).

headsOfLoss.source: Norton Tool Co Ltd v Tewson [1973] 1 WLR 45 (NIRC) — established the framework for calculating the compensatory award by reference to specific 'heads of loss'.

heads.head: Immediate loss of earnings
heads.description: Net earnings lost from the date of dismissal to the date of the tribunal hearing (or to the date the employee found new employment, if earlier). Calculated as net weekly pay × number of weeks of loss. If the employee found lower-paid work, the loss is the difference between old and new net pay for that period.
heads.practicalNote: This is the most straightforward head of loss. The tribunal will expect evidence of the employee's net pay (payslips, P60) and, if new employment was obtained, evidence of the new salary.

heads.head: Future loss of earnings
heads.description: Net earnings the employee is likely to lose from the date of the tribunal hearing into the future, until they find equivalent employment or until a reasonable date by which they should have found it. In most cases, tribunals award 6–24 months of future loss. In exceptional cases (e.g., senior employees in specialist roles, employees with disabilities, employees dismissed close to retirement), career-long loss may be awarded.
heads.practicalNote: The tribunal must make a reasonable assessment, not a precise prediction. Career-long loss (Chagger [2010]) is exceptional and requires the employee to show that they are unlikely ever to achieve equivalent earnings — for example, because of their age, specialist skills, health condition, or the impact of the dismissal on their reputation.

heads.head: Loss of pension rights
heads.description: Loss of the employer's pension contributions from dismissal until the employee finds equivalent employment with a comparable pension scheme. For defined contribution schemes, this is typically the employer's contribution rate × salary × weeks of loss. For defined benefit schemes, the calculation is more complex — the Principles for Compensating Pension Loss (revised 2017) provides guidance.
heads.practicalNote: Pension loss is frequently under-claimed. The employer's contribution to a defined contribution scheme is a quantifiable benefit — typically 3%–10% of salary — that the employee loses immediately on dismissal. The 2017 revised guidance on pension loss replaces the old 'simplified' approach with a more actuarially rigorous methodology.

heads.head: Loss of statutory rights
heads.description: A conventional award for the loss of the accrued statutory rights (unfair dismissal qualifying period, statutory notice period, statutory redundancy entitlement) that the employee had built up and will need to rebuild in new employment. The conventional award is typically £500–£600, though there is no rigid rule.
heads.practicalNote: This is a modest conventional sum that reflects the fact that the employee will need to serve a new qualifying period in their next job before gaining protection against unfair dismissal. With the ERA 2025 repeal of the qualifying period, this head of loss may become less significant for unfair dismissal purposes — though the notice period and redundancy accrual will still apply.

heads.head: Expenses
heads.description: Reasonable expenses incurred as a consequence of the dismissal (s.123(2)(a)). This includes: costs of looking for new work (travel to interviews, postage, CV preparation), costs of retraining, costs of relocating, and any other expenses reasonably attributable to the dismissal.
heads.practicalNote: Expenses must be 'reasonably incurred' — extravagant or unnecessary expenditure will not be compensated. The employee should keep receipts and records of all dismissal-related expenditure.

heads.head: Loss of other benefits
heads.description: Loss of any benefit the employee might reasonably have expected to have but for the dismissal (s.123(2)(b)). This includes: company car (or car allowance), private health insurance, death-in-service benefit, subsidised accommodation, staff discounts, share options, and any other contractual or discretionary benefit.
heads.practicalNote: Benefits must be valued at their cost to the employee of replacing them, not at their cost to the employer of providing them. A company car benefit is valued at the cost to the employee of running an equivalent vehicle, net of any employee contribution.

polkeyReduction.principle: Where the dismissal was procedurally unfair but a fair procedure would have made no difference to the outcome (or would have led to dismissal at a later date), the tribunal may reduce the compensatory award by the percentage chance that the employee would have been dismissed in any event. Named after Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd [1987] UKHL 8.
polkeyReduction.application: The tribunal assesses the percentage chance that the employee would have been fairly dismissed if a fair procedure had been followed. If the chance is 100%, the award is reduced to nil. If 50%, the award is halved. If only 10%, the award is reduced by 10%. The tribunal may also assess that a fair procedure would have resulted in dismissal at a later date — in which case, the award covers loss only up to that date.
polkeyReduction.guidance: Software 2000 Ltd v Andrews [2007] ICR 825 (EAT) — Elias J provided detailed guidance on how tribunals should approach the Polkey assessment: (1) the tribunal should not be deterred from making a Polkey reduction merely because the assessment is difficult; (2) the tribunal should assess the percentage chance on the evidence available; (3) only in an exceptional case should the tribunal decline to make a Polkey assessment altogether.
polkeyReduction.practicalNote: Polkey reductions are extremely common — they arise in a high proportion of successful unfair dismissal claims, particularly where the reason for dismissal was genuine but the procedure was flawed. Typical reductions range from 25% to 80%.

contributoryConduct.principle: Under s.123(6), where the tribunal finds that the dismissal was 'to any extent caused or contributed to by any action of the complainant', it shall reduce the compensatory award by such proportion as it considers just and equitable. The reduction is mandatory once contributory conduct is found, but the PROPORTION is discretionary.
contributoryConduct.application: The tribunal assesses the employee's conduct that contributed to the dismissal and makes a percentage reduction. Common examples: employee committed the misconduct alleged (but was dismissed unfairly due to procedural defects), employee provoked the situation, employee failed to cooperate with the investigation. Reductions of 25%–75% are common; 100% reductions are possible but rare.
contributoryConduct.distinction: Contributory conduct (s.123(6)) is distinct from the Polkey reduction. The Polkey reduction addresses what WOULD HAVE HAPPENED with a fair procedure. Contributory conduct addresses the employee's OWN CULPABILITY. Both reductions may be applied to the same award.

mitigationFailure.principle: Under s.123(4), the tribunal applies the common law duty to mitigate loss. The employee must take reasonable steps to reduce their losses — primarily by seeking alternative employment. If the employee unreasonably fails to mitigate, the tribunal will reduce the compensatory award to reflect the loss that would have been sustained had reasonable mitigation steps been taken.
mitigationFailure.burdenOfProof: The burden of proving failure to mitigate rests on the EMPLOYER, not the employee. The employer must show that the employee acted unreasonably in failing to take specific steps to reduce their losses.
mitigationFailure.practicalNote: The employee does not have to accept the first job offered or accept a significant downgrade. The employee must take REASONABLE steps — the standard is reasonableness, not perfection. Evidence of job search activity (applications, interviews, registrations with agencies) is important.

orderOfDeductions.source: Digital Equipment Co Ltd v Clements (No.2) [1998] ICR 258 (CA)
order: 1. Calculate the total financial loss (Norton Tool heads)
2. Apply the Polkey reduction (percentage chance of fair dismissal)
4. Deduct any ex gratia payment or enhanced redundancy payment already made by the employer
5. Apply the ACAS Code uplift or reduction (s.124A — up to ±25%)
6. Apply the s.124 cap (the lower of £118,223 or 52 weeks' gross pay)
orderOfDeductions.significance: The order matters significantly. The s.124 cap is applied LAST — to the net figure after all deductions. If the cap were applied first, subsequent deductions would reduce the award further below the cap.

versions.text:
(1) Subject to the provisions of this section and sections 124, 126, 127 and 127A(1), (3) and (4), the amount of the compensatory award shall be such amount as the tribunal considers just and equitable in all the circumstances having regard to the loss sustained by the complainant in consequence of the dismissal in so far as that loss is attributable to action taken by the employer.

[subsections (2)–(7) substantially as current]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted
versions.policyRationale: The 1996 Act consolidated the compensatory award calculation from EPCA 1978 s.74. The cross-references in s.123(1) included ss.126, 127, and 127A — provisions relating to tribunal awards that were subsequently amended.

versions.text:
(1) Subject to the provisions of this section and sections 124, 124A and 126, the amount of the compensatory award shall be such amount as the tribunal considers just and equitable...

[s.124A inserted by Employment Act 2002 s.39 — ACAS Code uplift/reduction]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Act 2002 (c.22) s.39, Sch.7 para.39
versions.policyRationale: EA 2002 inserted s.124A (ACAS Code adjustment) and updated the cross-references in s.123(1). The ACAS Code uplift/reduction mechanism allows the tribunal to adjust the compensatory award by up to 25% for unreasonable failure by either party to follow the ACAS Code.

versions.text: [Current version — s.123(6A) inserted by ERRA 2013 s.18(5), allowing the tribunal to reduce the award by up to 25% where a whistleblowing disclosure was not made in good faith. s.123(8) inserted by EA 2002 for reinstatement/re-engagement award interactions.]
versions.amendedBy: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 (c.24) s.18(5)
versions.policyRationale: ERRA 2013 inserted s.123(6A) — a specific reduction for whistleblowing disclosures not made in good faith. This was introduced alongside the public interest requirement in s.43B to address concerns about disclosures made for purely personal motives. The tribunal MAY reduce the award by up to 25% if the disclosure was not made in good faith — it is a discretionary power, not mandatory.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 is expected to remove the compensatory award cap (s.124) from around January 2027. If the cap is removed, the s.123 compensatory award will be uncapped for ALL unfair dismissals, not just whistleblowing and health and safety dismissals. This will be the most significant change to unfair dismissal compensation since the cap was introduced in 1971. Additionally, the time limit for presenting unfair dismissal claims is expected to extend from 3 months to 6 months (not yet commenced).
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Cap removal expected around January 2027 — not yet commenced as at March 2026. Time limit extension also pending.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: CRITICAL — if the compensatory award cap is removed, employer exposure in unfair dismissal cases will increase significantly. High-earning employees will no longer have their compensation capped at the s.124 figure. Employers will face uncapped exposure based on actual financial loss, subject only to contributory conduct, Polkey reduction, and mitigation.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 is expected to remove the s.124 compensatory award cap from approximately January 2027. When this happens, the s.123 calculation will become the sole determinant of the compensatory award — there will be no statutory ceiling. This makes every element of the s.123 calculation (heads of loss, Polkey reduction, contributory conduct, mitigation) even more significant than it is today. Employers who currently take comfort from the cap should prepare for uncapped exposure.
refs: ERA 1996 s.123(1) (the 'just and equitable' formula — the operative calculation provision)
ERA 1996 s.123(2) (loss includes expenses and loss of benefits)
ERA 1996 s.123(4) (duty to mitigate — common law duty applied by statute)
ERA 1996 s.123(6) (contributory conduct reduction — mandatory reduction, discretionary proportion)
ERA 1996 s.123(6A) (whistleblowing good faith reduction — up to 25%)
ERA 1996 s.123(7) (deduction of excess redundancy payment)
ERA 1996 s.124 (compensatory award cap — applied AFTER all s.123 deductions)
ERA 1996 s.124A (ACAS Code uplift/reduction — up to ±25%)
ERA 1996 s.119–122 (basic award — separate calculation, NOT part of s.123)
ERA 1996 s.117 (additional compensation where reinstatement/re-engagement not complied with)
ERA 2025 (expected to remove the s.124 cap from approximately January 2027)
ACAS Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015) — s.124A uplift/reduction
Norton Tool Co Ltd v Tewson [1973] 1 WLR 45 (heads of loss framework)
Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd [1987] UKHL 8 (procedural fairness + Polkey reduction)
Digital Equipment Co Ltd v Clements (No.2) [1998] ICR 258 (order of deductions)
Principles for Compensating Pension Loss (Employment Tribunals, revised 2017)

leadingCases.name: Norton Tool Co Ltd v Tewson
leadingCases.principle: The compensatory award is compensatory, not punitive. It is calculated by reference to specific 'heads of loss': immediate loss of net earnings, future loss of net earnings, loss of pension rights, loss of statutory rights, expenses, and loss of other benefits. The award compensates for actual financial loss, not for the manner of the dismissal or for injury to feelings.
leadingCases.heldText: Sir John Donaldson (President of the NIRC) established the framework for calculating the compensatory award by reference to specific heads of loss. The award is intended to put the employee in the financial position they would have been in had they not been unfairly dismissed — subject to the duty to mitigate.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on the structure of the compensatory award calculation. The Norton Tool heads of loss remain the analytical framework used by every tribunal. Every schedule of loss submitted in an unfair dismissal case is structured around these heads.

leadingCases.name: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Where the dismissal was procedurally unfair but a fair procedure would have made no difference to the outcome, the tribunal may reduce the compensatory award by the percentage chance that the employee would have been dismissed in any event (the 'Polkey reduction'). This does not make the dismissal fair — it reduces the compensation.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that procedural unfairness renders a dismissal unfair regardless of the outcome. However, the compensatory award may be reduced to reflect the percentage chance that a fair procedure would have led to the same result.
leadingCases.significance: The Polkey reduction is applied in a very high proportion of successful unfair dismissal claims. It is the primary mechanism by which tribunals adjust awards to reflect the reality that a procedurally defective dismissal for a genuine reason would often have reached the same outcome if done properly. Cross-reference: see s.98 leadingCases entry for Polkey's significance to the fairness assessment.

leadingCases.name: Software 2000 Ltd v Andrews
leadingCases.principle: Detailed guidance on how tribunals should approach the Polkey assessment: (1) the tribunal should not be deterred from making a percentage assessment merely because the exercise is difficult or imprecise; (2) the tribunal should use its judgment to assess the percentage on the evidence; (3) only in exceptional cases should the tribunal decline to make any Polkey assessment at all; (4) a 'percentage' assessment is inherently an approximation, not a precise calculation.
leadingCases.heldText: Elias P held that the tribunal should make the best assessment it can on the available evidence, using its industrial experience and judgment. The tribunal should express the reduction as a percentage. A failure to engage with the Polkey question at all is an error of law.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EAT guidance on the mechanics of the Polkey assessment. Resolves the practical difficulty of tribunals being reluctant to make percentage assessments. Establishes that an approximate percentage is better than no assessment at all.

leadingCases.name: Chagger v Abbey National plc
leadingCases.principle: In exceptional cases, the tribunal may award 'career-long' loss — compensation reflecting the fact that the employee is unlikely ever to achieve equivalent employment. This requires evidence that the employee's career prospects have been permanently damaged by the dismissal. Career-long loss awards are rare but may be appropriate where the employee's age, specialist skills, health, or reputational damage makes re-employment at an equivalent level unlikely.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that in assessing future loss, the tribunal must consider all relevant factors including the employee's age, qualifications, health, the state of the labour market, and the impact of the dismissal on their reputation and career prospects. Where the evidence shows that the employee is unlikely to find equivalent employment, a career-long loss award is permissible — subject to appropriate discounting for contingencies.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EWCA authority on career-long loss. Important for high-value claims by senior employees, employees in specialist or niche roles, older employees approaching retirement, and employees whose dismissal has damaged their professional reputation. Career-long loss transforms the compensatory award from a modest sum to a potentially very large award.

leadingCases.name: Dunnachie v Kingston upon Hull City Council
leadingCases.principle: The compensatory award under s.123 compensates for FINANCIAL loss only. It does not include compensation for injury to feelings, humiliation, or distress. Injury to feelings is compensable only through a separate discrimination claim, not through the unfair dismissal compensatory award.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the word 'loss' in s.123(1) refers to financial loss — pecuniary loss that can be calculated in monetary terms. There is no power under s.123 to award compensation for non-economic loss. This resolves a long-running debate about the scope of the compensatory award.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive authority on the boundary of the compensatory award. Cross-reference: see s.124 leadingCases entry. The practical consequence is that an employee who has been unfairly dismissed in a humiliating or distressing manner can only recover compensation for the emotional impact through a parallel discrimination claim (if one exists), not through the unfair dismissal compensatory award.

leadingCases.name: Scope v Thornett
leadingCases.principle: The duty to mitigate (s.123(4)) requires the employee to take reasonable steps to find alternative employment. The burden of proving failure to mitigate is on the employer. The employee does not have to accept the first job offered or take a significant step down — the standard is reasonableness.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the duty to mitigate is a duty to take REASONABLE steps, not a duty to do everything conceivable. The tribunal must assess what a reasonable person in the employee's position would have done. A disabled employee may face greater difficulty in finding new employment, and this is a relevant factor in assessing the reasonableness of their mitigation efforts.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EWCA authority on the duty to mitigate in the employment context. Confirms that the burden is on the employer to prove that the employee failed to mitigate, and that the standard of reasonableness must take account of the employee's personal circumstances (health, skills, location, caring responsibilities). This is particularly important for disabled employees and employees in specialist roles.

leadingCases.name: W Devis & Sons Ltd v Atkins
leadingCases.principle: Evidence of misconduct discovered AFTER the dismissal cannot retrospectively justify the dismissal (it remains unfair). However, post-dismissal evidence may be taken into account in assessing the compensatory award under s.123(1) — the tribunal may reduce the award (potentially to nil) if the evidence shows the employee would inevitably have been dismissed had the misconduct been known.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the 'just and equitable' formula in s.123(1) permits the tribunal to take account of ALL the circumstances — including evidence of misconduct discovered after the dismissal. While the dismissal remains unfair (Devis — the fairness assessment is fixed at the point of dismissal), the compensatory award may be reduced if justice and equity require it.
leadingCases.significance: Creates the important distinction between the fairness finding (fixed at the point of dismissal) and the compensation calculation (which may take account of later evidence). This means an employer who discovers serious misconduct after dismissing for a different reason may still face a finding of unfair dismissal but pay little or no compensation. Cross-reference: see s.98 leadingCases entry for Devis's significance to the fairness assessment.

leadingCases.name: Digital Equipment Co Ltd v Clements (No.2)
leadingCases.principle: The s.124 cap is applied to the NET compensatory award AFTER all deductions (Polkey, contributory conduct, mitigation, ex gratia payments) have been made. The correct order of calculation is: (1) total financial loss; (2) Polkey reduction; (3) contributory conduct; (4) payments already made; (5) ACAS Code uplift; (6) s.124 cap.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal established the order of deductions for the compensatory award calculation. The cap is the final step, applied to the net figure after all other adjustments.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the order of deductions. Cross-reference: see s.124 leadingCases entry. The order matters because applying the cap at a different stage would produce different results — particularly where the gross loss exceeds the cap but the net loss (after deductions) falls below it.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Principles for Compensating Pension Loss (revised 2017)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The authoritative guidance on calculating pension loss in unfair dismissal cases. Replaces the earlier 'simplified' approach with a more actuarially rigorous methodology. Covers both defined contribution and defined benefit schemes.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Compensation for unfair dismissal
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on how the compensatory award is calculated, the current cap, and the reductions that may apply.

commonErrors: Treating the compensatory award as punitive — Norton Tool v Tewson [1973] establishes that the award compensates for actual financial loss, not for the employer's conduct. An employee whose loss is nil (because they immediately found equivalent employment) receives a nil compensatory award regardless of how egregious the employer's behaviour was (Dunnachie [2004])

Not calculating each head of loss separately — the tribunal calculates: immediate loss of net earnings, future loss of net earnings, loss of pension, loss of statutory rights (typically £500–£600), expenses, and loss of other benefits (car, health insurance, etc). Each head must be quantified and evidenced. Failing to claim a head of loss means it is not compensated

Applying the s.124 cap before making deductions — Digital Equipment v Clements [1998] establishes the correct order: gross loss → Polkey → contributory conduct → payments made → ACAS Code uplift → cap LAST. Getting the order wrong produces the wrong result

Not making a Polkey reduction assessment — Software 2000 v Andrews [2007] requires the tribunal to engage with the Polkey question and make a percentage assessment. The tribunal should not decline to assess the percentage merely because it is difficult. Employers should always invite the tribunal to consider a Polkey reduction where the reason for dismissal was genuine but the procedure was defective

Confusing the Polkey reduction with contributory conduct — these are different concepts applied separately. Polkey asks: what would have happened with a fair procedure? Contributory conduct (s.123(6)) asks: did the employee's own conduct contribute to the dismissal? Both may apply to the same case — for example, an employee dismissed for genuine misconduct (Polkey — perhaps 70% chance of fair dismissal) who also provoked the situation (contributory conduct — perhaps 30% reduction)

Overlooking pension loss — the employer's pension contribution is a significant benefit that the employee loses on dismissal. For a defined contribution scheme, the loss is typically the employer's contribution rate × salary × period of loss. Many employees and their representatives fail to claim pension loss at all, significantly undervaluing the claim

Not evidencing mitigation efforts — the duty to mitigate (s.123(4)) requires the employee to take reasonable steps to find new employment. The burden of proving FAILURE to mitigate is on the employer (Scope v Thornett [2006]). However, employees who cannot demonstrate any job search activity are vulnerable to a mitigation failure argument. Job applications, agency registrations, interview records, and training evidence should all be preserved

Assuming the compensatory award will always be capped at the s.124 figure — the cap does NOT apply to whistleblowing dismissals (s.103A) or health and safety dismissals (s.100). In these cases, the compensatory award is unlimited. ERA 2025 is also expected to remove the cap entirely for ALL unfair dismissals from approximately January 2027

Not recognising the ACAS Code uplift — s.124A allows the tribunal to increase the compensatory award by up to 25% for an employer's unreasonable failure to follow the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures, or to REDUCE it by up to 25% if the employee unreasonably failed to follow it. The uplift is applied BEFORE the s.124 cap (per the Digital Equipment order of deductions)

Overlooking the s.123(6A) good faith reduction for whistleblowing — where the reason for dismissal was a protected disclosure but the disclosure was not made in good faith, the tribunal may reduce the award by up to 25%. This is a separate power from the contributory conduct reduction in s.123(6)

Not claiming loss of statutory rights — the conventional award (£500–£600) is modest but it is a separate head of loss that should be claimed in every case. It reflects the loss of the accrued qualifying period and notice period that the employee had built up and will need to rebuild

sections.title: Limit of compensatory award
sections.summary: Caps the compensatory award for unfair dismissal at the LOWER of a fixed sum (currently £115,115, uprated annually by RPI under ERA 1999 s.34) or 52 weeks' gross pay. The dual cap was introduced by ERRA 2013. The cap does NOT apply to whistleblowing dismissals (s.103A), health and safety dismissals (s.100), or where the employer has failed to comply with a reinstatement or re-engagement order. ERA 2025 Schedule 3 will amend the indexation mechanism when commenced.
sections.text:
(1) The amount of—

(a) any compensation awarded to a person under section 117(1) and (2), or

(b) a compensatory award to a person calculated in accordance with section 123,

shall not exceed the amount specified in subsection (1ZA).

(1ZA) The amount specified in this subsection is the lower of—

(a) £115,115, and

(b) 52 multiplied by a week's pay of the person concerned.

(1A) Subsection (1) shall not apply to compensation awarded, or a compensatory award made, to a person in a case where he is regarded as unfairly dismissed by virtue of section 100, 103A, 105(3) or 105(6A).

(3) In a case where—

(a) a compensatory award is an award under paragraph (b) of subsection (1) of section 117, and

(b) an additional award falls to be made under paragraph (c) of that subsection,

the limit imposed by this section on the compensatory award may be exceeded to the extent necessary to enable the aggregate of the compensatory and additional awards fully to reflect the amount specified as payable under section 114(2)(a) or section 115(2)(d).

(5) The limit imposed by this section applies to the amount which the employment tribunal would, apart from this section, award in respect of the subject matter of the complaint after taking into account—

(a) any payment made by the respondent to the complainant in respect of that matter, and

(b) any reduction in the amount of the award required by any enactment or rule of law.
sections.keyPrinciple: The compensatory award cap operates as a dual ceiling: the award cannot exceed EITHER the fixed annual sum (currently £115,115) OR 52 weeks' gross pay, whichever is LOWER. For employees earning more than approximately £2,214 per week (£115,115 ÷ 52), the fixed sum is the binding cap. For employees earning less, 52 weeks' gross pay is the binding cap. Critically, certain categories of unfair dismissal are completely exempt from the cap — most importantly whistleblowing (s.103A) and health and safety (s.100) dismissals, where there is no upper limit on the compensatory award.

versions.text: (1) The amount of a compensatory award to a person calculated in accordance with section 123 shall not exceed £11,000.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted
versions.policyRationale: The original 1996 Act set the compensatory award cap at £11,000, consolidating the figure from EPCA 1978. This was a fixed sum with no automatic uprating mechanism.

versions.text:
(1) The amount of— (a) any compensation awarded to a person under section 117(1) and (2), or (b) a compensatory award to a person calculated in accordance with section 123, shall not exceed £50,000.

(1A) Subsection (1) shall not apply to compensation awarded, or a compensatory award made, to a person in a case where he is regarded as unfairly dismissed by virtue of section 100 or 105(3).
versions.amendedBy: Employment Relations Act 1999 s.34(4) and s.37(1)
versions.policyRationale: ERA 1999 made two transformative changes: (1) increased the cap from £11,000 to £50,000 — more than quadrupling it; (2) introduced the RPI-linked annual uprating mechanism under s.34, ensuring the cap would keep pace with inflation; (3) removed the cap entirely for health and safety dismissals (s.100) and subsequently for whistleblowing dismissals (s.103A, added by Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998). The cap was subsequently uprated annually by order: £51,700 (2000), £53,500 (2001), rising to £74,200 by February 2013.

versions.text:
(1) The amount of— (a) any compensation awarded to a person under section 117(1) and (2), or (b) a compensatory award to a person calculated in accordance with section 123, shall not exceed the amount specified in subsection (1ZA).

(1ZA) The amount specified in this subsection is the lower of— (a) [£115,115], and (b) 52 multiplied by a week's pay of the person concerned.

(1A) Subsection (1) shall not apply to compensation awarded, or a compensatory award made, to a person in a case where he is regarded as unfairly dismissed by virtue of section 100, 103A, 105(3) or 105(6A).
versions.amendedBy: Unfair Dismissal (Variation of the Limit of Compensatory Award) Order 2013 (SI 2013/1949) — implementing ERRA 2013 s.15
versions.policyRationale: The Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.15 introduced the dual cap mechanism. Before this, the cap was a single fixed sum (uprated annually by RPI). After this, the cap became the LOWER of: (a) the RPI-linked fixed sum; OR (b) 52 weeks' gross pay. The policy objective was to ensure that lower-paid employees' awards were proportionate to their actual earnings. For a minimum-wage worker (approximately £20,000/year), the 52-week multiple (£20,000) rather than the fixed sum (then £74,200) would be the binding cap. The 52-week multiple was chosen after consultation — alternatives considered included 26 weeks, 78 weeks, and the median annual salary. s.124(1A) was also extended to add s.105(6A) (blacklisting dismissals) to the uncapped categories.

pendingAmendment.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 Schedule 3 paras.2 and 5
pendingAmendment.effect: Amends the indexation mechanism for the compensatory award cap under ERA 1999 s.34. Omits s.34(1)(c) (the reference to s.124(1) in the indexation list) and s.34(4)–(4B) (the ERA 1999 base figure and ERRA 2013 interaction provisions), and confers a new power to amend s.34 by regulations. The practical effect is a restructuring of how the cap is uprated — the cap itself is not being abolished.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Not yet in force as at March 2026
pendingAmendment.anticipatedDate: To be confirmed by commencement order
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the cap will continue to exist but the mechanism for annual uprating will be restructured. Employers should monitor for the commencement order and any transitional provisions affecting claims straddling the changeover.

currentLimits.weeklyPayForCapPurposes: 52 × employee's gross weekly pay (no statutory cap on the week's pay figure for this calculation — unlike s.227 which caps a week's pay at £643 for basic award purposes)
bindingCapExamples: Employee earning £15,000/year: 52 × £288.46 = £15,000 is the binding cap (52-week multiple is lower)
Employee earning £40,000/year: 52 × £769.23 = £40,000 is the binding cap
Employee earning £80,000/year: 52 × £1,538.46 = £80,000 is the binding cap
Employee earning £120,000/year: £115,115 is the binding cap (fixed sum is lower than 52 × £2,307.69 = £120,000)
currentLimits.uprating: The £115,115 figure is uprated annually by reference to the September RPI under ERA 1999 s.34, with effect from the following 6 April. The 52-week multiple is calculated using the employee's actual gross weekly pay at the calculation date — it is not subject to the s.227 weekly pay cap.

uncappedCategories.note: Section 124(1A) provides that the cap does NOT apply to the following categories of automatically unfair dismissal. In these cases, the compensatory award is unlimited.

categories.reason: Health and safety — dismissed for carrying out health and safety duties, raising health and safety concerns, or leaving/refusing to return to a place of danger
Whistleblowing — dismissed for making a protected disclosure under Part IVA
Selection for redundancy on health and safety grounds
Selection for redundancy connected with blacklisting (ERA 1999 (Blacklists) Regulations 2010)

uncappedCategories.notUncapped: Pregnancy/maternity (s.99), trade union activities (TULRCA s.152), assertion of statutory rights (s.104), flexible working (s.104C), and all other automatically unfair reasons remain SUBJECT to the cap. Only health and safety, whistleblowing, and blacklisting are uncapped.

refs: ERA 1996 s.123 (calculation of the compensatory award — 'just and equitable' — s.124 caps the result)
ERA 1996 s.117 (compensation where reinstatement/re-engagement ordered but not complied with — s.124 applies to the s.117 award too)
ERA 1996 s.119–122 (basic award — separate calculation, NOT subject to the s.124 cap)
ERA 1996 s.124A (ACAS Code adjustment — applied before the s.124 cap, per s.124(5))
ERA 1996 s.227 (week's pay cap for basic award purposes — £643 — does NOT apply to the 52-week multiple in s.124(1ZA)(b))
ERA 1999 s.34 (annual RPI-linked uprating of the s.124(1ZA)(a) fixed sum)
ERA 1999 s.37 (removal of the cap for health and safety dismissals)
ERRA 2013 s.15 (introduced the dual cap — s.124(1ZA))
SI 2013/1949 (implementing order for the dual cap)
Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order 2024 (SI 2024/182) — most recent uprating
ERA 2025 Schedule 3 (amendments to indexation mechanism — not yet in force)
Dunnachie v Kingston upon Hull City Council [2004] UKHL 36 — compensatory award compensates financial loss only
Digital Equipment Co Ltd v Clements (No.2) [1998] ICR 258 — order of deductions and the cap

leadingCases.name: Dunnachie v Kingston upon Hull City Council
leadingCases.principle: The compensatory award under s.123 compensates for financial loss only — it does not include an award for injury to feelings. Injury to feelings is only compensable through a separate claim (e.g., discrimination).
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the compensatory award under s.123(1) is limited to financial loss sustained by the employee in consequence of the dismissal. There is no power to award compensation for non-economic loss such as injury to feelings, humiliation, or distress arising from the manner of the dismissal. This means the s.124 cap applies only to the financial loss element; any injury to feelings award under the Equality Act 2010 is separate and not subject to the s.124 cap.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive UKHL authority on the scope of the compensatory award. Resolves the long-running debate about whether non-economic loss is compensable under s.123. The practical consequence is that employers facing both unfair dismissal and discrimination claims face a capped compensatory award (for the UD) PLUS an uncapped injury to feelings award (for the discrimination). This makes the s.124 cap less protective than it appears for employers who dismiss in a discriminatory manner.

leadingCases.name: Digital Equipment Co Ltd v Clements (No.2)
leadingCases.principle: The s.124 cap is applied to the net compensatory award after all deductions (mitigation, contributory fault, ex gratia payments) have been made — not to the gross loss figure before deductions.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the correct order of calculation is: (1) assess the total financial loss; (2) apply deductions for mitigation, contributory conduct (s.123(6)), and payments already made; (3) then apply the s.124 cap to the resulting figure. The cap is applied last, to the net amount. This means that an employee whose gross loss exceeds the cap but whose net loss (after deductions) falls below it will receive the full net amount.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EWCA authority on the order of deductions and the application of the s.124 cap. Establishes the sequence: loss → mitigation → contributory conduct → ex gratia → cap. This matters significantly in practice because if the cap were applied first (to the gross figure), all subsequent deductions would reduce the award further below the cap.

leadingCases.name: Dattani v Chief Constable of West Mercia Police
leadingCases.principle: Where an employee brings both an unfair dismissal claim and a discrimination claim, the tribunal must apportion the loss between the two claims. The s.124 cap applies only to the unfair dismissal compensatory award — the discrimination award is uncapped.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that where the same loss is attributable to both unfair dismissal and discrimination, the tribunal must avoid double recovery but should ensure that the employee receives the benefit of the uncapped discrimination jurisdiction for losses attributable to the discriminatory act.
leadingCases.significance: Critical for employers assessing exposure in concurrent unfair dismissal and discrimination claims. The s.124 cap does not protect the employer from losses attributable to discrimination — only from losses attributable solely to the unfair dismissal element.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Compensation for unfair dismissal
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on how the compensatory award is calculated, the current cap, and the categories of dismissal where the cap does not apply.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Presidential Guidance — compensation for unfair dismissal (England and Wales)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Presidential Guidance on the approach to calculating the compensatory award, including the Polkey reduction, contributory conduct, and the application of the cap.

commonErrors: Assuming the compensatory award cap protects the employer from large awards in all cases — the cap does NOT apply to whistleblowing (s.103A) or health and safety (s.100) dismissals. In these cases, the compensatory award is unlimited. An employer who dismisses a whistleblower faces uncapped liability

Not understanding the dual cap mechanism — the cap is the LOWER of the fixed sum (£115,115) and 52 weeks' gross pay. For the majority of UK employees (those earning less than approximately £115,000/year), the 52-week multiple is the binding cap, not the fixed sum. An employee earning £30,000/year has an effective cap of £30,000, not £115,115

Confusing the s.227 week's pay cap (£643, used for the basic award calculation) with the week's pay figure used for the s.124(1ZA)(b) calculation — the 52-week multiple uses the employee's ACTUAL gross weekly pay with NO statutory cap. The s.227 cap only applies to the basic award under s.119

Applying the cap before making deductions for contributory conduct and mitigation — Digital Equipment v Clements [1998] establishes that the cap is applied LAST, after all deductions. The correct order is: gross loss → mitigation → contributory conduct → ex gratia payments → cap

Assuming the cap applies to discrimination awards — where an employee brings both unfair dismissal and discrimination claims, the s.124 cap applies only to the UD compensatory award. The discrimination compensation (including injury to feelings) is uncapped. Dunnachie [2004] confirms the compensatory award covers financial loss only; injury to feelings is compensable only through the discrimination jurisdiction

Not recognising that the cap can be exceeded where the employer fails to comply with a reinstatement or re-engagement order — s.124(3) permits the cap to be exceeded to the extent necessary to fully reflect the amount payable under s.114(2)(a) or s.115(2)(d)

Overlooking the ACAS Code uplift — under s.124A, the tribunal may increase the compensatory award by up to 25% for an employer's unreasonable failure to comply with the ACAS Code of Practice. The uplift is applied BEFORE the s.124 cap (per s.124(5)). This means a large uplift could push an otherwise below-cap award above the cap, where it is then capped

Treating the cap as protection against exposure in all unfair dismissal claims — the total exposure includes: the compensatory award (capped at s.124), the basic award (uncapped, calculated under s.119), the ACAS Code uplift (up to 25%), any discrimination award (uncapped), and any wrongful dismissal damages (uncapped). The s.124 cap applies to only one component of the total liability

Not tracking the annual uprating — the fixed sum in s.124(1ZA)(a) is uprated annually by reference to the September RPI under ERA 1999 s.34. The figure changes every April. Employers who last checked the figure more than 12 months ago may be using an outdated cap for exposure assessments

dynamicLimits.instruction: The compensatory award cap figure changes annually. Eileen MUST cross-reference the live limits table before quoting the current cap. From ERA 2025 commencement (expected January 2027), the cap is REMOVED entirely — but until commencement, the annual cap applies.

sections.title: The right to a redundancy payment
sections.summary: An employer shall pay a redundancy payment to any employee who: (a) is dismissed by the employer by reason of redundancy (s.139); OR (b) is eligible for a redundancy payment by reason of being laid off or kept on short-time working (ss.147–154). The employee must have been continuously employed for not less than two years at the relevant date (s.155). The amount is calculated under s.162 using the same formula as the basic award (s.119) — age bands × years of service × capped weekly pay (s.227). The qualifying period for redundancy payments (2 years) is NOT affected by ERA 2025's reduction of the unfair dismissal qualifying period to 6 months — this is one of the most important distinctions in the ERA 2025 landscape. The employer's obligation to pay is subject to exceptions: the employee may lose entitlement if they unreasonably refuse a suitable offer of alternative employment (s.141).
sections.text:
(1) An employer shall pay a redundancy payment to any employee of his if the employee—

(a) is dismissed by the employer by reason of redundancy, or

(b) is eligible for a redundancy payment by reason of being laid off or kept on short-time.

(2) Subsection (1) has effect subject to the following provisions of this Part (including, in particular, sections 140 to 144, 149 to 152, 155 to 161 and 164).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 135 creates the ENTITLEMENT to a redundancy payment. Three conditions must be met: (1) the employee must be dismissed by reason of redundancy (s.139) OR eligible through lay-off/short-time working (ss.147–154); (2) the employee must have been continuously employed for at least 2 years (s.155); (3) the employee must not have lost their entitlement through one of the statutory exceptions (unreasonable refusal of suitable alternative, misconduct, etc.). The redundancy payment is a STATUTORY MINIMUM — the employer may offer a more generous contractual or ex gratia redundancy payment, but cannot offer less than the statutory entitlement.

routeA.title: Dismissed by reason of redundancy
routeA.requirements: The employee must be DISMISSED (s.136) by reason of REDUNDANCY (s.139). Both elements must be established. If the employee resigns (rather than being dismissed), there is no entitlement under this route — unless the resignation is a constructive dismissal (s.136(1)(c)).

routeB.title: Lay-off or short-time working
routeB.requirements: Where the employee has been laid off or kept on short-time working for: (a) 4 or more consecutive weeks; OR (b) 6 or more weeks in a 13-week period (with not more than 3 consecutive), the employee may give notice of intention to claim a redundancy payment under s.148. The employer may serve a counter-notice under s.149 if they expect to provide full work within 4 weeks. If no valid counter-notice is served, the employee can resign and claim the redundancy payment.
routeB.practicalNote: The lay-off/short-time route is less common but strategically important in economic downturns. An employer who lays off employees for extended periods may inadvertently trigger redundancy payment obligations.

qualifyingPeriod.period: 2 years' continuous employment at the relevant date
qualifyingPeriod.era2025: The 2-year qualifying period for statutory redundancy payments is NOT affected by ERA 2025. ERA 2025 reduces the unfair dismissal qualifying period from 2 years to 6 months (s.108), but s.155 is UNCHANGED. This means: an employee dismissed by reason of redundancy after 8 months' service will be able to claim UNFAIR DISMISSAL (if the process was unfair) from 1 January 2027, but will NOT be entitled to a STATUTORY REDUNDANCY PAYMENT until they have completed 2 years' service.
qualifyingPeriod.criticalDistinction: This is one of the most important distinctions in the ERA 2025 landscape. The unfair dismissal qualifying period (s.108) and the redundancy payment qualifying period (s.155) are DECOUPLED from 1 January 2027. Before ERA 2025, both were 2 years, creating symmetry. After ERA 2025, they diverge: 6 months for unfair dismissal, 2 years for redundancy pay.

calculation.formula: The same formula as the basic award for unfair dismissal (s.119): age bands × complete years of service (max 20) × capped weekly pay (s.227). Three age bands: 0.5 weeks for under 22, 1 week for 22–40, 1.5 weeks for 41+.
calculation.crossReference: See s.227 for the authoritative annual limits table. See s.119 for the identical formula (basic award).

suitableAlternative.principle: Where the employer (or an associated employer) makes an offer of suitable alternative employment before the old contract ends, and the employee unreasonably refuses the offer, the employee loses their entitlement to a redundancy payment. The offer must be of 'suitable' employment — objectively assessed by reference to pay, status, location, hours, and the nature of the work.
suitableAlternative.trialPeriod: s.138 provides a statutory 4-week trial period in the new employment. If the employee tries the new role for up to 4 weeks and then decides it is not suitable, they can leave and still claim the redundancy payment as if they had been dismissed on the original redundancy date. The trial period may be extended by agreement for retraining purposes.
suitableAlternative.practicalNote: The 'suitability' of the alternative and the 'reasonableness' of the refusal are separate assessments. An offer may be suitable (objectively appropriate for the employee) but the refusal may be reasonable (e.g., because of personal circumstances, commuting distance, or childcare). Conversely, an offer may be unsuitable (significantly different role or location) regardless of whether the refusal was reasonable.

insolvencyProtection.principle: Where the employer is insolvent or refuses to pay the statutory redundancy payment, the employee may apply to the Secretary of State for payment from the National Insurance Fund (NIF). The Secretary of State pays the statutory redundancy payment directly to the employee and takes over the employer's liability. This ensures that employees receive their statutory entitlement even when the employer cannot pay.
insolvencyProtection.practicalNote: The NIF covers the statutory redundancy payment only — not any contractual enhancement. Employees who are owed both statutory and contractual redundancy pay will receive the statutory element from the NIF and must pursue the contractual element as an unsecured creditor in the insolvency.

setOffAgainstBasicAward.principle: Where the employer pays a statutory redundancy payment and the employee subsequently succeeds in an unfair dismissal claim, the redundancy payment is SET OFF against the basic award (s.119). Since both use the same formula, the set-off typically extinguishes the basic award entirely. The employee receives: redundancy payment + compensatory award (not redundancy payment + basic award + compensatory award).
setOffAgainstBasicAward.crossReference: See s.119 for the complete set-off analysis (Digital Equipment v Clements [1998]).

versions.text: [s.135 has not been materially amended since the 1996 Act was enacted. It consolidated the entitlement from the Redundancy Payments Act 1965 (via EPCA 1978 s.81). The statutory redundancy payment was one of the first employment protection rights created in UK law — predating unfair dismissal (1971) by six years.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating EPCA 1978 s.81, originally Redundancy Payments Act 1965)
versions.policyRationale: The statutory redundancy payment was introduced by the Redundancy Payments Act 1965 as part of the Wilson Government's industrial modernisation programme. It was designed to compensate employees for the loss of job security and to facilitate labour market flexibility by reducing resistance to redundancy. The entitlement has been in place for over 60 years.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does NOT amend s.135 or s.155. The qualifying period for statutory redundancy payments REMAINS at 2 years. However, two ERA 2025 changes affect the redundancy landscape: (1) the unfair dismissal qualifying period reduces to 6 months — creating a decoupling between the UD and redundancy payment qualifying periods; (2) the collective consultation protective award maximum DOUBLES from 90 to 180 days' pay from 6 April 2026 — significantly increasing the financial exposure for employers who fail to collectively consult.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Protective award increase: 6 April 2026. UD qualifying period: 1 January 2027. s.135/s.155: UNCHANGED.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the decoupling of qualifying periods creates a new category of employee: those with 6–24 months' service who can claim unfair dismissal but NOT statutory redundancy pay. This affects the employer's risk calculation and the employee's claim strategy.

refs: ERA 1996 s.135 (this section — the right to a redundancy payment)
ERA 1996 s.136 (dismissal — the trigger for s.135(1)(a))
ERA 1996 s.138 (trial period in new employment — 4-week statutory trial)
ERA 1996 s.139 (definition of redundancy — must be satisfied for s.135(1)(a))
ERA 1996 s.140 (exclusion for misconduct — employee may lose entitlement)
ERA 1996 s.141 (suitable alternative employment — unreasonable refusal loses entitlement)
ERA 1996 ss.147–154 (lay-off and short-time working — the s.135(1)(b) route)
ERA 1996 s.155 (qualifying period — 2 years — NOT affected by ERA 2025)
ERA 1996 s.162 (calculation of redundancy payment — same formula as s.119 basic award)
ERA 1996 s.227 (weekly pay cap — £751 from 6 April 2026)
ERA 1996 ss.166–170 (insolvency — payment from National Insurance Fund)
ERA 1996 s.122(4) (redundancy payment set off against basic award in unfair dismissal)
Redundancy Payments Act 1965 (the original statute — the first employment protection right in UK law)

leadingCases.name: Taylor v Kent County Council
leadingCases.principle: An employer who offers an employee a substantially different role (even at the same pay) may be making the employee redundant — not offering suitable alternative employment. The suitability of the alternative must be assessed by reference to the nature of the work, status, and responsibility, not just pay.
leadingCases.heldText: The court held that offering a headmaster a position as a supply teacher, even at the same pay, was not suitable alternative employment because the role was fundamentally different in status and responsibility.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on the meaning of 'suitable alternative employment' under s.141. Establishes that suitability is assessed by the nature of the work, not just pay. A significant demotion in status or responsibility may not be suitable even if pay is maintained.

leadingCases.name: Thomas & Betts Manufacturing Ltd v Harding
leadingCases.principle: The reasonableness of the employee's refusal of suitable alternative employment is assessed subjectively — taking into account the employee's personal circumstances, including domestic responsibilities, health, commuting distance, and childcare. An objectively suitable offer may be reasonably refused on personal grounds.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the employee's refusal of an offer to relocate was reasonable given her personal circumstances, including her husband's employment and her children's schooling, even though the alternative role was objectively suitable.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the subjective element of the 'reasonableness of refusal' test. Important because suitability (objective) and reasonableness of refusal (subjective) are separate assessments. An employee may reasonably refuse a suitable alternative for personal reasons.

leadingCases.name: Optare Group Ltd v TGWU
leadingCases.principle: The obligation to offer suitable alternative employment applies not just to the immediate employer but to ASSOCIATED employers (s.141(2)). An employer who has suitable vacancies at a sister company or related entity must offer them to the redundant employee.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that s.141 extends the obligation to associated employers. Vacancies across the wider corporate group must be considered when assessing whether suitable alternative employment exists.
leadingCases.significance: Extends the suitable alternative obligation across the corporate group. Important for employers who are part of a larger group — they cannot claim there is no suitable alternative if vacancies exist at an associated employer.

leadingCases.name: Willcox v Humphreys & Glasgow Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The 4-week statutory trial period under s.138 is a strict 4-calendar-week period. It cannot be extended beyond 4 weeks by agreement — though a LONGER trial period may be agreed for retraining purposes (s.138(3)). If the employee continues beyond 4 weeks without objection, they are treated as having accepted the new employment and lose their right to claim a redundancy payment.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the statutory trial period is fixed at 4 weeks. An employee who continues working beyond 4 weeks is treated as having accepted the new employment. The right to reject the alternative and claim redundancy pay is lost.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the strict 4-week boundary for the statutory trial period. Employees must make their decision within 4 weeks — continuing beyond that period is treated as acceptance.

leadingCases.name: Secretary of State for Employment v Cooper
leadingCases.principle: Where an employer becomes insolvent and cannot pay the statutory redundancy payment, the employee may apply to the Secretary of State for payment from the National Insurance Fund under s.166. The Secretary of State pays the statutory amount and assumes the employer's liability.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed the scope and operation of the NIF scheme for redundancy payments. The Secretary of State steps into the employer's shoes for the statutory payment, ensuring employees receive their entitlement regardless of the employer's solvency.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the NIF safety net for statutory redundancy payments. Important because many redundancies occur in the context of employer financial distress — the NIF ensures the statutory entitlement is honoured even if the employer cannot pay.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Calculate your statutory redundancy pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The official redundancy pay calculator, automatically applying the current year's limits.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the right to redundancy pay, suitable alternative employment, and the trial period.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on statutory and contractual redundancy pay, including the calculation and the NIF scheme.

commonErrors: Assuming that ERA 2025's reduction of the unfair dismissal qualifying period also reduces the redundancy payment qualifying period — the redundancy payment qualifying period (s.155) is 2 YEARS and is NOT affected by ERA 2025. From 1 January 2027, an employee dismissed by reason of redundancy after 8 months' service can claim UNFAIR DISMISSAL but is NOT entitled to a STATUTORY REDUNDANCY PAYMENT. This is the most important decoupling in the ERA 2025 landscape

Not offering suitable alternative employment before dismissing — s.141 requires the employer to offer suitable alternative employment if a vacancy exists (including at associated employers — Optare [2007]). Failure to offer a suitable alternative that exists is a factor in the fairness of the redundancy process (s.98(4)). For employees on maternity leave within the 18-month protected period, the employer must offer the alternative with PRIORITY (Reg.10)

Treating the statutory redundancy payment as the employer's entire obligation — the statutory payment is a MINIMUM. Many employers offer enhanced contractual redundancy packages. If the employee's contract or a collective agreement provides for more generous terms, the contractual entitlement applies. However, the statutory payment is the FLOOR — the employer cannot offer less than s.135 requires

Not recognising the lay-off / short-time working route to redundancy payment — s.135(1)(b) provides that an employee who has been laid off for 4+ consecutive weeks (or 6+ weeks in 13) can claim a redundancy payment by serving notice under s.148. This catches employers who use extended lay-offs instead of making redundancies — the employees may trigger the redundancy payment obligation themselves

Not recognising the 4-week statutory trial period — s.138 provides a 4-week trial period in new employment. If the employee tries the alternative role for up to 4 weeks and then rejects it, they can claim the redundancy payment as if they were dismissed on the original date. But if they continue beyond 4 weeks, they are treated as having accepted and lose the entitlement (Willcox v Humphreys [1976])

Confusing 'suitability' of the offer with 'reasonableness' of the refusal — these are SEPARATE assessments under s.141. An offer may be objectively suitable (similar pay, status, location) but the refusal may be reasonable (personal circumstances — Thomas & Betts v Harding [1980]). Conversely, an offer may be unsuitable regardless of the refusal. The tribunal must assess both independently

Not knowing the current maximum statutory redundancy payment — from 6 April 2026: £22,530 (30 × £751). From 6 April 2025: £21,570 (30 × £719). Using the wrong year's figure produces an incorrect calculation. The EDT determines which year's limits apply (see s.227 for authoritative limits table)

Not applying for payment from the National Insurance Fund when the employer is insolvent — ss.166–170 provide a safety net. Where the employer is insolvent or refuses to pay, the employee can apply to the Secretary of State for payment from the NIF. The NIF covers the statutory redundancy payment only — not contractual enhancements

Not recognising that an employee dismissed for misconduct during the notice period may lose their redundancy entitlement — s.140 provides that an employee who is dismissed for misconduct during the notice period (after the employer has given notice of redundancy) may lose their right to a redundancy payment. However, this only applies to misconduct occurring DURING the notice period — pre-existing misconduct does not defeat the entitlement

Not including redundancy pay information in the s.1 statement — while s.1 does not specifically require redundancy pay information, best practice is to include a reference to the employer's redundancy policy in the employment contract or staff handbook. Many employers fail to inform employees of their redundancy entitlements at the point of engagement

Not recognising that the redundancy payment is TAX-FREE up to £30,000 — statutory redundancy payments (and contractual payments) are exempt from income tax up to the first £30,000 of the total termination payment (ITEPA 2003 s.403). Above £30,000, the excess is taxable. Employers must ensure the tax treatment is correctly applied in the final payslip

sections.title: Redundancy
sections.summary: Defines 'redundancy' for the purposes of the ERA 1996 — both for redundancy payments (Part XI) and as a potentially fair reason for dismissal (s.98(2)(c)). An employee is dismissed by reason of redundancy if the dismissal is wholly or mainly attributable to: (a) the employer ceasing or intending to cease to carry on business, or ceasing to carry on business at the place where the employee was employed; OR (b) the requirements of the business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind, or to carry out work of a particular kind at the place where the employee was employed, having ceased or diminished or being expected to do so. The House of Lords in Murray v Foyle Meats [1999] described the statutory language as 'simplicity itself' — it poses two factual questions: (1) does the relevant economic situation exist? (2) was the dismissal attributable to it?
sections.text:
(1) For the purposes of this Act an employee who is dismissed shall be taken to be dismissed by reason of redundancy if the dismissal is wholly or mainly attributable to—

(a) the fact that his employer has ceased or intends to cease—

(i) to carry on the business for the purposes of which the employee was employed by him, or

(ii) to carry on that business in the place where the employee was so employed, or

(b) the fact that the requirements of that business—

(i) for employees to carry out work of a particular kind, or

(ii) for employees to carry out work of a particular kind in the place where the employee was employed by the employer,

have ceased or diminished or are expected to cease or diminish.

(2) For the purposes of subsection (1) the business of the employer together with the business or businesses of his associated employers shall be treated as one (unless either of the conditions specified in paragraphs (a) and (b) of that subsection would be satisfied without so treating them).

(4) Where—

(a) the contract under which a person is employed is treated by section 136(5) as terminated by his employer by reason of an act or event, and

(b) the employee's contract is not renewed and he is not re-engaged under a new contract of employment,

he shall be taken for the purposes of subsection (1)(b) to be dismissed by reason of redundancy if the conditions specified in paragraphs (i) or (ii) of that subsection are met.

(6) In subsection (1) "cease" and "diminish" mean cease and diminish either permanently or temporarily and for whatever reason.
sections.keyPrinciple: The s.139 test is a test of FACT, not law. The House of Lords in Murray v Foyle Meats [1999] UKHL 45 rejected the 'contract test' and the 'function test' that had complicated redundancy law for decades, and held that the statutory language asks two simple factual questions: (1) does one of the economic situations in s.139(1)(a) or (b) exist? (2) is the dismissal 'wholly or mainly attributable' to that situation? If both questions are answered yes, the employee was dismissed by reason of redundancy. The words of the statute should not be made more complicated than they are.

twoLimbs.note: Section 139(1) identifies two distinct situations that constitute redundancy. These are alternatives — only one needs to be established.

limbA.title: Cessation of business
limbA.scope: The employer has ceased, or intends to cease, carrying on the business entirely (s.139(1)(a)(i)), OR has ceased, or intends to cease, carrying on business at the place where the employee was employed (s.139(1)(a)(ii)). This covers: complete business closure, closure of a particular site/branch/depot, and relocation of the business to a new location.
limbA.practicalNote: Site closure (s.139(1)(a)(ii)) is the most straightforward redundancy situation. If the employer closes the site where the employee works, the employee is redundant — regardless of whether the same work continues at other sites. The employee may have a right to be offered alternative employment at another site, but the redundancy itself is established.

limbB.title: Diminishing need for employees
limbB.scope: The requirements of the business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind have ceased or diminished (or are expected to do so), either generally (s.139(1)(b)(i)) or at the particular place where the employee was employed (s.139(1)(b)(ii)). This covers: workforce reduction, downsizing, automation replacing human labour, reorganisation reducing headcount, and outsourcing.
limbB.practicalNote: This is the more complex limb and the one that generates most litigation. The key question is whether the employer's need for EMPLOYEES (not for the work itself) to carry out work of a particular kind has diminished. The work may still need to be done — but if it can be done by fewer employees (through efficiency, technology, reorganisation), the need for employees has diminished and the situation is a redundancy.

murrayTest.source: Murray v Foyle Meats Ltd [1999] UKHL 45
murrayTest.formulation: Lord Irvine LC: 'The language of [s.139(1)(b)] is in my view simplicity itself. It asks two questions of fact. The first is whether one or other of various states of economic affairs exists. In this case, the relevant one is whether the requirements of the business for employees to work in the slaughter hall had diminished. The second question is whether the dismissal is attributable, wholly or mainly, to that state of affairs. This is a question of causation.'
murrayTest.significance: Rejected the 'contract test' (what work could the employee be required to do under their contract?) and the 'function test' (what work did the employee actually do?) that had competed for decades. Replaced them with a simple two-stage factual analysis based on the statutory words. This is the authoritative UKHL test that all tribunals apply.

burrellThreeStage.source: Safeway Stores plc v Burrell [1997] ICR 523 (EAT)
burrellThreeStage.formulation: Peter Clark J: '(1) Was the employee dismissed? (2) Had the requirements of the employer's business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind ceased or diminished, or were they expected to? (3) Was the dismissal caused wholly or mainly by that cessation or diminution?'
burrellThreeStage.significance: The EAT in Burrell established the three-stage approach that was subsequently endorsed by the House of Lords in Murray. Burrell also clarified that the focus should be on the diminution of the need for EMPLOYEES, not on the diminution of WORK — an important distinction because work may continue but require fewer employees.

bumping.principle: A 'bumping' redundancy occurs where the employer selects Employee A for redundancy because Employee A's role is being eliminated, but instead of dismissing Employee A, the employer moves Employee A into Employee B's role and dismisses Employee B. Employee B is redundant even though Employee B's own role continues — because Employee B's dismissal is attributable to the diminished need for employees to carry out work of a particular kind (the original work done by Employee A).
bumping.authority: Murray v Foyle Meats [1999] UKHL 45 confirmed that bumping dismissals fall within s.139. The reduction in the need for work of a particular kind need not relate to the work done by the DISMISSED employee — it need only be the cause of the dismissal.
bumping.practicalNote: Bumping is legitimate but must be handled fairly under s.98(4). The employer must consider bumping as part of the search for alternative employment. In Mirab v Mentor Graphics (UK) Ltd [2018] UKEAT, the EAT held that an employer is not obliged to consider bumping in every case, but where bumping is a realistic possibility, the employer should consider it as part of a fair redundancy process.

placeOfWork.principle: The 'place where the employee was employed' (s.139(1)(a)(ii) and (b)(ii)) is determined primarily by looking at where the employee actually worked, not where the contract says they could be required to work. A mobility clause in the contract does not prevent a redundancy from arising at the employee's actual place of work.
placeOfWork.authority: Bass Leisure Ltd v Thomas [1994] IRLR 104 (EAT); Kellogg Brown & Root (UK) Ltd v Fitton [2017] UKEAT — the geographical test looks at the employee's actual workplace, though a contractual mobility clause may be relevant to whether the employer can require the employee to relocate as an alternative to redundancy.
placeOfWork.practicalNote: An employer who closes a site but has a mobility clause requiring employees to work at any site does NOT automatically avoid redundancy. The employees at the closed site are redundant under s.139(1)(a)(ii). The mobility clause may be relevant to whether the employer offered suitable alternative employment — but the redundancy itself is established.

s139VsS195.principle: The s.139 definition of redundancy is different from the s.195 definition used for collective consultation under TULRCA 1992. Section 195 defines redundancy for collective consultation purposes as dismissal 'for a reason not related to the individual concerned' — which is BROADER than s.139. A reorganisation that results in dismissals but does not involve a diminution in the need for employees may fall within s.195 (triggering collective consultation) but NOT within s.139 (not a statutory redundancy).
s139VsS195.authority: Hardy v Tourism South East [2005] IRLR 242 (EAT); Optare Group Ltd v Transport and General Workers' Union [2007] IRLR 931 (EAT)
s139VsS195.practicalNote: This is one of the most frequently misunderstood distinctions in redundancy law. Employers who reorganise without reducing headcount may think they do not need to collectively consult because 'no redundancies are involved'. But the s.195 definition catches dismissals for any reason not related to the individual — including reorganisation dismissals. The collective consultation obligation is triggered by s.195, not by s.139. See redundancy-intelligence.json RI-3.1 for the complete analysis.

versions.text: [The substantive definition in s.139(1) has not been materially amended since the 1996 Act was enacted. The section consolidated the definition from EPCA 1978 s.81, which in turn consolidated the definition from the Redundancy Payments Act 1965 s.1. Minor amendments have been made to s.139(3) (references to local education authorities updated) and s.139(7) inserted (education-related modifications), but the core s.139(1) definition remains unchanged since 1965.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted (consolidating EPCA 1978 s.81, originally Redundancy Payments Act 1965 s.1)
versions.policyRationale: The definition of redundancy has remained substantively unchanged for over 60 years — since the Redundancy Payments Act 1965 first created the statutory right to redundancy pay. The stability of the statutory language contrasts with the long-running judicial debate about how to interpret it (the 'contract test' vs 'function test' debate), which was only resolved by the House of Lords in Murray v Foyle Meats [1999].

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.139. However, two ERA 2025 changes significantly affect the practical landscape: (1) the repeal of s.108 (qualifying period) means that all employees — including those with less than two years' service — will be able to claim unfair dismissal for an unfair redundancy process from day one; (2) ERA 2025 amends the collective consultation provisions in TULRCA 1992 to remove the 'single establishment' trigger, meaning redundancies across multiple sites will be aggregated for the purposes of the collective consultation threshold.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: s.108 repeal expected Autumn 2026. Collective consultation changes subject to separate commencement.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: HIGH — the removal of the qualifying period will increase the number of employees who can challenge the fairness of the redundancy process under s.98(4). Employers who currently rely on the two-year qualifying period to dismiss short-service employees during restructuring without a full redundancy process will face day-one exposure.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 does not change WHAT redundancy IS (the s.139 definition is unchanged). But it changes WHO CAN CHALLENGE the process. Currently, employees with less than two years' service cannot claim unfair dismissal — so an employer who makes a short-service employee redundant using an unfair process faces no s.98(4) claim. Once s.108 is repealed, every employee will be able to challenge the fairness of the redundancy process from day one. This fundamentally changes the risk calculation for every restructuring exercise.
refs: ERA 1996 s.139(1)(a) (cessation of business — whole business or at particular place)
ERA 1996 s.139(1)(b) (diminishing need for employees to carry out work of a particular kind)
ERA 1996 s.139(6) ('cease' and 'diminish' include temporarily and for whatever reason)
ERA 1996 s.98(2)(c) (redundancy as a potentially fair reason for dismissal — triggers s.98(4) reasonableness test)
ERA 1996 s.105 (automatically unfair selection for redundancy — pregnancy, whistleblowing, H&S, union, etc.)
ERA 1996 s.135 (right to redundancy payment — requires s.139 redundancy + qualifying period)
ERA 1996 s.141 (renewal of contract or re-engagement — employee may lose redundancy pay if unreasonably refuses suitable alternative)
ERA 1996 s.155 (qualifying period for redundancy payment — 2 years — NOT affected by ERA 2025 s.108 repeal)
ERA 1996 s.162 (calculation of redundancy payment — based on age, service, weekly pay)
TULRCA 1992 s.188 (collective consultation obligation — triggered by s.195 definition, NOT s.139)
TULRCA 1992 s.193–194 (notification to Secretary of State — HR1 form)
TULRCA 1992 s.195 (definition of redundancy for collective consultation — BROADER than s.139)
ERA 2025 (removes single establishment trigger for collective consultation — aggregation across sites)
redundancy-intelligence.json (cross-cutting redundancy intelligence briefing — 7 stages, 12 cases)
Williams v Compair Maxam [1982] ICR 156 (EAT) (five principles for fair redundancy)
Murray v Foyle Meats Ltd [1999] UKHL 45

leadingCases.name: Murray v Foyle Meats Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The s.139(1)(b) test is 'simplicity itself'. It asks two questions of fact: (1) does one of the economic situations in s.139(1) exist (has the need for employees to carry out work of a particular kind ceased or diminished)? (2) is the dismissal wholly or mainly attributable to that state of affairs? The 'contract test' and 'function test' are both wrong — the tribunal should apply the straightforward statutory language.
leadingCases.heldText: Lord Irvine LC: 'The language of [s.139(1)(b)] is in my view simplicity itself. It asks two questions of fact. The first is whether one or other of various states of economic affairs exists. In this case, the relevant one is whether the requirements of the business for employees to work in the slaughter hall had diminished... The second question is whether the dismissal is attributable, wholly or mainly, to that state of affairs... In the present case, the tribunal found as a fact that the requirements of the business for employees to work in the slaughter hall had diminished... That, in my opinion, is the end of the matter.'
leadingCases.significance: THE definitive UKHL authority on the meaning of redundancy. Ended decades of confusion between the 'contract test' (what work could the employee be required to do under their contract?) and the 'function test' (what work did the employee actually do?). Both tests are wrong. The test is a simple factual analysis of the statutory words. Also confirmed that bumping redundancies fall within s.139 — the diminution in the need for work of a particular kind need not relate to the work done by the dismissed employee.

leadingCases.name: Safeway Stores plc v Burrell
leadingCases.principle: The three-stage approach to s.139(1)(b): (1) was the employee dismissed?
