(2) had the requirements of the employer's business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind ceased or diminished? (3) was the dismissal caused wholly or mainly by that cessation or diminution? The focus should be on the diminution of the need for EMPLOYEES, not on the diminution of WORK.
leadingCases.heldText: Peter Clark J established the three-stage approach and made the critical observation that s.139(1)(b) refers to the requirements for EMPLOYEES, not for WORK. The work may continue — but if it can be done by fewer employees, the need for employees has diminished.
leadingCases.significance: The EAT decision that laid the groundwork for the House of Lords' endorsement in Murray. Burrell's three-stage approach is the analytical framework that all tribunals apply. The employee/work distinction is critical — a reorganisation that preserves the same volume of work but requires fewer employees IS a redundancy.

leadingCases.name: Williams v Compair Maxam Ltd
leadingCases.principle: A redundancy dismissal will only be fair under s.98(4) if the employer follows fair procedures. The five principles for a fair redundancy are: (1) maximum warning and consultation; (2) consultation with the union (or employee representatives) on selection criteria; (3) selection criteria that are objectively verifiable; (4) fair application of the criteria to each employee; (5) a genuine search for alternative employment within the organisation.
leadingCases.heldText: Browne-Wilkinson J set out five principles that a reasonable employer would follow in a redundancy: '(1) The employer will seek to give as much warning as possible of impending redundancies so as to enable the union and employees to take early steps to inform themselves of the relevant facts, consider possible alternative solutions and, if necessary, find alternative employment... (2) The employer will consult the union on the selection criteria... (3) Whether or not an agreement is reached with the union, the employer will seek to establish criteria for selection which so far as possible do not depend solely upon the opinion of the person making the selection but can be objectively checked... (4) The employer will seek to ensure that the selection is made fairly in accordance with these criteria... (5) The employer will see whether, instead of dismissing an employee, the employee could be offered alternative employment.'
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on the fairness of the redundancy PROCESS (as opposed to the definition of redundancy). While s.139 defines what redundancy IS, Williams v Compair Maxam defines what a FAIR redundancy LOOKS LIKE. The five principles are cited in virtually every unfair redundancy dismissal case. Cross-reference: see redundancy-intelligence.json RI-2.1.

leadingCases.name: Shawkat v Nottingham City Hospital NHS Trust (No.2)
leadingCases.principle: The terms of the employment contract and the work actually done are relevant but not conclusive factors in applying the statutory test. The tribunal must conduct a factual analysis of whether the requirements of the business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind have diminished, looking at the reality of the situation.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal, applying Murray, confirmed that the factual analysis requires the tribunal to look at the work actually being done, the contractual terms, and the overall business context — but no single factor is determinative. The statutory test is the statutory test — it is not to be overlaid with glosses derived from the contract test or function test.
leadingCases.significance: Post-Murray EWCA confirmation of the factual approach. Important for cases where the employee's contract contains broad flexibility clauses — the contract is a relevant factor but does not determine whether a redundancy exists.

leadingCases.name: Bass Leisure Ltd v Thomas
leadingCases.principle: The 'place where the employee was employed' for the purposes of s.139(1)(a)(ii) and (b)(ii) is determined primarily by reference to where the employee actually worked, not where the contract permits the employer to require them to work. A contractual mobility clause does not prevent a redundancy from arising at the employee's actual workplace.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the 'place where the employee was employed' should be determined by looking at the factual position — where did the employee actually work? A contractual provision permitting the employer to require the employee to work elsewhere is relevant to the question of whether the employer can offer alternative employment, but does not prevent a redundancy from being established at the employee's actual place of work.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the geographical element of the redundancy test. Important for employers closing a site but relying on mobility clauses to avoid redundancy. Bass Leisure establishes that the mobility clause does not defeat the redundancy — it may affect the alternative employment question but not the definition.

leadingCases.name: Johnson v Nottinghamshire Combined Police Authority
leadingCases.principle: A business reorganisation that changes how work is done (e.g., changing shift patterns, rearranging duties, splitting one role into two part-time roles) may not constitute a redundancy if the overall requirement for employees to carry out work of a particular kind has not diminished.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that changing shift patterns for existing employees — from a two-shift system to a three-shift system — was a reorganisation, not a redundancy, because the employer's requirement for employees to carry out the same work had not diminished. The employees were required to do the same work, just at different times.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the critical distinction between REORGANISATION (same work, different arrangements — NOT redundancy) and REDUNDANCY (diminished need for employees). An employer who reorganises without reducing headcount is not making redundancies under s.139 — though the reorganisation may trigger other rights (constructive dismissal if terms are fundamentally changed, collective consultation under the broader s.195 definition).

leadingCases.name: Packman t/a Packman Lucas Associates v Fauchon
leadingCases.principle: An employer is not automatically obliged to consider bumping in every redundancy case, but where bumping is a realistic possibility, the failure to consider it may render the redundancy dismissal unfair under s.98(4).
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that whether bumping should be considered depends on the circumstances — the size and resources of the employer, the availability of alternative roles, and the practicality of bumping. A small employer is not held to the same standard as a large employer. But where bumping is an obvious alternative that a reasonable employer would have considered, failure to consider it makes the dismissal unfair.
leadingCases.significance: Practical guidance on the bumping obligation. Resolves the tension between Murray (bumping is legitimate) and the practical reality that not every employer can or should bump. The key question under s.98(4) is whether a reasonable employer in the circumstances would have considered bumping — and if so, whether the failure to bump renders the dismissal unfair.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy: handling and managing the process
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive ACAS guidance on managing redundancies fairly — consultation, selection, alternative employment, notice, and payments.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on employee rights during redundancy including statutory redundancy pay calculator, notice entitlement, and time off to look for work.

regulatoryGuidance.title: HR1 notification form — Insolvency Service
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The HR1 form that must be filed with the Insolvency Service when proposing to make 20 or more employees redundant at one establishment (TULRCA 1992 s.193–194). Failure to file is a criminal offence.

commonErrors: Applying the old 'contract test' or 'function test' instead of the Murray v Foyle Meats factual analysis — Murray [1999] UKHL rejected both tests. The correct approach is the simple two-stage factual analysis: (1) does the relevant economic situation exist? (2) is the dismissal attributable to it? The words of the statute should not be made more complicated than they are

Confusing a reduction in WORK with a reduction in the need for EMPLOYEES — Safeway v Burrell [1997] clarified that s.139(1)(b) refers to the requirements for EMPLOYEES, not for WORK. The same volume of work may continue, but if it can be done by fewer employees (through efficiency, automation, reorganisation), the need for employees has diminished (a diminution in the requirement for employees) and a redundancy exists

Assuming that a business reorganisation is automatically a redundancy — Johnson v Nottinghamshire Police [1974] establishes that a reorganisation that changes HOW work is done (shift patterns, duties, reporting lines) without reducing the number of employees needed is NOT a redundancy under s.139. Reorganisation and redundancy are legally distinct concepts

Relying on a mobility clause to avoid redundancy when closing a site — Bass Leisure v Thomas [1994] establishes that the 'place where the employee was employed' is determined by where they actually worked, not where the contract says they could be required to work. The mobility clause may be relevant to alternative employment, but the redundancy at the closed site is still established under s.139(1)(a)(ii)

Not recognising that bumping can constitute a redundancy — Murray [1999] confirmed that the employee dismissed through bumping IS redundant under s.139, even though their own role continues. The diminished need for work of a particular kind (the eliminated role) is the cause of the bumped employee's dismissal

Confusing the s.139 definition with the s.195 definition — s.139 defines redundancy for individual dismissal and redundancy payment purposes. s.195 (TULRCA 1992) defines redundancy for COLLECTIVE CONSULTATION purposes as dismissal 'for a reason not related to the individual' — which is BROADER. A reorganisation that does not involve a s.139 redundancy may still trigger the s.195 collective consultation obligation. This is one of the most common and expensive mistakes in restructuring

Not considering whether the redundancy is genuine — a sham redundancy (where the real reason for dismissal is not a diminution in the need for employees but a desire to dismiss a particular individual) is not a redundancy at all. If the tribunal finds the real reason was something other than redundancy (personality, performance, discrimination), the s.139 defence fails and the employer must justify the dismissal under the real reason

Failing to follow a fair process even though a genuine redundancy exists — establishing a s.139 redundancy is only the first step. The employer must ALSO satisfy s.98(4) by following Williams v Compair Maxam principles: warning, consultation, objective selection criteria, fair application, alternative employment search. A genuine redundancy dismissal is unfair if the process is unfair

Not recognising that the qualifying period for redundancy PAYMENTS (s.155 — 2 years) is NOT affected by the ERA 2025 repeal of s.108 — the ERA 2025 repeal of the unfair dismissal qualifying period does NOT change the qualifying period for statutory redundancy pay. An employee dismissed by reason of redundancy after 6 months' service will be able to claim unfair dismissal (if the process was unfair) but will NOT be entitled to a statutory redundancy payment

Treating temporary cessation of work as permanent — s.139(6) provides that 'cease' and 'diminish' include ceasing or diminishing TEMPORARILY. A temporary downturn in the need for employees can constitute a redundancy, but the employer should consider alternatives (short-time working, lay-off, redeployment) before making permanent redundancies in a temporary situation

Not aggregating redundancies across sites for collective consultation — ERA 2025 amends the collective consultation provisions in TULRCA 1992 to remove the 'single establishment' trigger. When commenced, redundancies across multiple sites will be aggregated for the purposes of the 20-employee threshold. Employers who plan redundancies at several sites simultaneously should assess whether the aggregate number triggers the collective consultation obligation

Not filing the HR1 notification form — TULRCA 1992 s.193–194 requires the employer to notify the Secretary of State (via the Insolvency Service HR1 form) at least 30 days before the first dismissal takes effect (for 20–99 redundancies) or at least 45 days (for 100+ redundancies). Failure to file is a CRIMINAL OFFENCE. Many employers overlook this obligation, particularly in smaller-scale redundancies just above the 20-employee threshold

sections.title: Qualifying period of employment for redundancy payment
sections.summary: An employee does not have any right to a redundancy payment unless they have been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the relevant date. This qualifying period is NOT affected by ERA 2025. While the unfair dismissal qualifying period (s.108) reduces from 2 years to 6 months from 1 January 2027, the redundancy payment qualifying period REMAINS at 2 years. This creates the most significant decoupling in the ERA 2025 landscape: from 1 January 2027, an employee with 8 months' service who is made redundant can claim UNFAIR DISMISSAL (if the process was unfair) but is NOT entitled to a STATUTORY REDUNDANCY PAYMENT. The 'relevant date' from which the 2-year period is measured is defined in s.145.
sections.text: An employee does not have any right to a redundancy payment unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the relevant date.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 155 is a SINGLE SENTENCE that creates the gateway to statutory redundancy pay. No continuous employment of 2 years = no entitlement to statutory redundancy payment, regardless of how genuine the redundancy is, how unfair the process was, or how long the employee has actually worked (if below 2 years). The provision is absolute — there are NO exceptions to the 2-year requirement for statutory redundancy pay (unlike unfair dismissal, which has numerous exceptions for automatically unfair reasons). Even automatically unfair dismissal categories (s.99, s.100, s.103A) do not exempt the employee from the 2-year redundancy PAYMENT requirement — they only exempt from the unfair DISMISSAL qualifying period.

era2025Decoupling.title: The ERA 2025 Decoupling — the most important distinction for post-January 2027 employment law

before.symmetry: Both qualifying periods were the same — 2 years. An employee who could claim unfair dismissal could also claim statutory redundancy pay (if dismissed by reason of redundancy). An employee who could not claim unfair dismissal could not claim redundancy pay. The symmetry was clean.

after.unfairDismissalQualifyingPeriod: 6 months (s.108(1) as amended by ERA 2025 s.25, from 1 January 2027)
after.decoupling: The qualifying periods DIVERGE. A new category of employee is created: those with 6–24 months' service who can claim unfair dismissal but CANNOT claim statutory redundancy pay.

practicalConsequences: An employee dismissed by reason of redundancy after 8 months' service can claim UNFAIR DISMISSAL (challenging the fairness of the process, selection, consultation) but is NOT entitled to a STATUTORY REDUNDANCY PAYMENT

The employer's exposure in an unfair redundancy case for a short-service employee is the COMPENSATORY AWARD only (no basic award, no redundancy payment set-off) — and from 1 January 2027, the compensatory award is UNCAPPED

The basic award (s.119) uses the same formula as the redundancy payment (s.162) but has a SEPARATE qualifying period (s.108, reducing to 6 months). An employee with 8 months' service who is unfairly dismissed receives a basic award (calculated on 8 months' service — modest) but no redundancy payment

Contractual or enhanced redundancy payments are unaffected by s.155 — the employer may choose to pay enhanced redundancy to employees with less than 2 years' service, but has no statutory obligation to do so

relevantDate.definition: The 'relevant date' for the purposes of s.155 is: (a) where the employee is dismissed with notice, the date on which the employer's notice expires; (b) where the employee is dismissed without notice, the date on which the termination takes effect; (c) where the employee is employed under a fixed-term contract that expires without being renewed, the date on which the term expires.
relevantDate.practicalNote: The relevant date determines whether the employee has completed 2 years' continuous employment. It is similar but not identical to the 'effective date of termination' (EDT) used for unfair dismissal under s.97. The key difference: s.97(2) allows the EDT to be extended by the statutory notice period for unfair dismissal purposes — but there is no equivalent extension for the 'relevant date' under s.145 for redundancy payment purposes. This means an employee who is dismissed without notice just short of 2 years may have the EDT extended for unfair dismissal (reaching 2 years) but NOT for redundancy payment (staying below 2 years).

continuousEmployment.principle: The 2-year period must be CONTINUOUS employment under a contract of employment (s.210). Continuity is governed by ss.210–219, which define what breaks continuity and what preserves it. Key provisions: s.212 (weeks counting towards continuity — any week in which the employee works under a contract counts); s.215 (employment abroad — may count towards continuity); s.216 (industrial action — does not break continuity but the weeks of action do not count); s.218 (transfer of undertaking, including TUPE — preserves continuity).
continuousEmployment.crossReference: See s.210 and s.212 for the detailed continuity rules.

versions.text: An employee does not have any right to a redundancy payment unless he has been continuously employed for a period of not less than two years ending with the relevant date.
versions.amendedBy: The Employment Equality (Age) Regulations 2006 (SI 2006/1031) reg.49(1), Sch.8 para.30 — repealed s.156 (upper age limit for redundancy) but did NOT amend s.155
versions.policyRationale: The 2-year qualifying period for redundancy payments has been in place since the Redundancy Payments Act 1965 (originally 2 years, briefly reduced to 1 year between 1975 and 1979, then restored to 2 years). It has tracked the unfair dismissal qualifying period historically but is now decoupled by ERA 2025. The upper age limit for redundancy pay (originally 65, then the normal retirement age) was removed in 2006 by the Age Regulations.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does NOT amend s.155. The redundancy payment qualifying period REMAINS at 2 years. ERA 2025 amends s.108 (unfair dismissal qualifying period — reduced to 6 months from 1 January 2027) but does NOT touch s.155. This is a deliberate policy choice: the Government chose to extend unfair dismissal protection to shorter-service employees but did NOT extend the financial entitlement to statutory redundancy pay. The policy rationale is that redundancy pay compensates for long-service investment in the employment relationship — it is not appropriate for employees with less than 2 years' service.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Not applicable — s.155 is UNCHANGED by ERA 2025
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: VERY HIGH — the decoupling creates a new employee category (6–24 months) and fundamentally changes the risk calculation for redundancy exercises involving shorter-service employees. Employers must understand that from 1 January 2027, short-service employees can challenge the FAIRNESS of the redundancy process (unfair dismissal) even though they are not entitled to REDUNDANCY PAY.

refs: ERA 1996 s.155 (this section — qualifying period for redundancy payment — 2 years)
ERA 1996 s.108 (qualifying period for unfair dismissal — 2 years, reducing to 6 months from 1 January 2027)
ERA 1996 s.135 (right to redundancy payment — the entitlement provision)
ERA 1996 s.139 (definition of redundancy)
ERA 1996 s.145 (the 'relevant date' for redundancy payment purposes)
ERA 1996 s.97 (the 'effective date of termination' for unfair dismissal — different from the 'relevant date')
ERA 1996 s.162 (calculation of redundancy payment — same formula as s.119 basic award)
ERA 1996 ss.210–219 (continuity of employment — governs whether the 2-year period is met)
ERA 2025 s.25 (amends s.108(1) — does NOT amend s.155)
Redundancy Payments Act 1965 (original statute — introduced the 2-year qualifying period)

leadingCases.name: Secretary of State for Employment v Globe Elastic Thread Co Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The 'relevant date' for the purposes of qualifying for redundancy payment is determined by s.145 (now s.145) and is not the same as the 'effective date of termination' for unfair dismissal purposes. The relevant date does not benefit from the s.97(2) statutory notice extension that applies to the EDT.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the relevant date for redundancy payment purposes is determined strictly by the statutory provisions — it does not include the artificial extension that applies to the EDT for unfair dismissal qualifying period purposes.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational UKHL authority on the distinction between the 'relevant date' (redundancy) and the 'effective date of termination' (unfair dismissal). Critical because an employee dismissed without notice just short of 2 years may qualify for unfair dismissal (s.97(2) extends the EDT) but NOT for redundancy pay (the relevant date is not extended).

leadingCases.name: Continuity of Employment — Ford v Warwickshire County Council
leadingCases.principle: A seasonal worker employed on a series of fixed-term contracts with gaps between them may still have continuous employment if the gaps are governed by custom and practice or by an overarching arrangement. The statutory presumption of continuity (s.210(5)) means that continuity is presumed unless shown to be broken.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that a teacher employed on successive fixed-term contracts (each covering one academic year) had continuous employment across the gaps between contracts. The presumption of continuity under s.210(5) applied, and the employer had not rebutted it.
leadingCases.significance: Important for employees with broken service patterns — the presumption of continuity means the employee may reach the 2-year threshold even where there are gaps between contracts, provided the gaps do not break continuity under ss.210–219.

leadingCases.name: Pfaffinger v City of Liverpool Community College
leadingCases.principle: The calculation of the 2-year qualifying period must be done precisely. The period ends on the 'relevant date' — the employee must have been continuously employed for a period of NOT LESS THAN two years ending on that date. One day short is fatal to the claim.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the 2-year requirement is strict. The employee must have completed 2 full years of continuous employment by the relevant date. Even one day short means no entitlement.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the strictness of the 2-year requirement. Important for employees dismissed near the 2-year anniversary — one day makes the difference between entitlement and no entitlement.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the qualifying period for statutory redundancy pay.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Calculate your statutory redundancy pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The official calculator — requires the employee to enter their start date and dismissal date. It checks the 2-year qualifying period automatically.

commonErrors: Assuming that ERA 2025's reduction of the unfair dismissal qualifying period to 6 months also reduces the redundancy PAYMENT qualifying period — s.155 is UNCHANGED by ERA 2025. The redundancy payment qualifying period REMAINS at 2 years. This is the single most important distinction in the ERA 2025 landscape. From 1 January 2027, the two qualifying periods are DECOUPLED: 6 months for unfair dismissal (s.108), 2 years for redundancy pay (s.155)

Not recognising the new category of employee created by the decoupling — from 1 January 2027, employees with 6–24 months' service can claim UNFAIR DISMISSAL but are NOT entitled to STATUTORY REDUNDANCY PAY. This is a new category that did not exist when both qualifying periods were 2 years. Employers must understand this distinction when planning redundancy exercises

Confusing the 'relevant date' (s.145) with the 'effective date of termination' (s.97) — these are different statutory dates. The EDT for unfair dismissal can be extended by s.97(2) (statutory notice period). The relevant date for redundancy pay is NOT extended in the same way (Globe Elastic Thread [1980]). An employee dismissed without notice just short of 2 years may qualify for UNFAIR DISMISSAL (EDT extended) but NOT for REDUNDANCY PAY (relevant date not extended)

Not calculating the 2-year period precisely — the requirement is 'not less than two years' ending with the relevant date. One day short is fatal (Pfaffinger [1997]). The calculation must be exact — employers who dismiss employees one or two days before their 2-year anniversary to avoid redundancy pay obligations must ensure their calculation is correct

Assuming that automatically unfair dismissal categories exempt the employee from the redundancy payment qualifying period — they do NOT. Section 108(3) lists the exceptions to the unfair DISMISSAL qualifying period (s.99, s.100, s.103A, etc.). There is NO equivalent list of exceptions for s.155. Even if an employee is dismissed for an automatically unfair reason during a redundancy, they still need 2 years' service to claim the statutory redundancy PAYMENT

Not recognising that the basic award (s.119) has a DIFFERENT qualifying period from the redundancy payment (s.155) — the basic award qualifying period is s.108 (reducing to 6 months). The redundancy payment qualifying period is s.155 (staying at 2 years). An employee with 8 months' service who wins an unfair dismissal claim receives a basic award (calculated on 8 months — modest) but NOT a redundancy payment

Overlooking that the s.122(4) set-off only applies where a redundancy payment has been PAID — if the employee has less than 2 years' service and is not entitled to a redundancy payment, there is no set-off. The basic award (s.119) is paid in full. This is a change from the pre-ERA 2025 position where both qualifying periods were aligned

Not considering the impact on settlement calculations — from 1 January 2027, settlement negotiations for redundancy of short-service employees must account for the decoupling. The employer faces: (1) no statutory redundancy pay obligation (employee under 2 years); (2) potential unfair dismissal liability (employee over 6 months); (3) UNCAPPED compensatory award. The settlement calculation is fundamentally different from the pre-ERA 2025 position

Not recognising that continuity of employment may include service with previous employers — ss.210–219 provide that certain types of service with previous employers count towards continuity, including TUPE transfers (s.218(2)), associated employers (s.218(6)), and statutory succession. An employee may have less than 2 years' service with the current employer but more than 2 years' total continuous employment

Assuming that breaks in employment always reset the clock — the statutory presumption of continuity (s.210(5)) means continuity is presumed unless shown to be broken. Seasonal workers, employees on successive fixed-term contracts, and employees who take career breaks may retain continuity through the gaps (Ford v Warwickshire [1983])

Not understanding the policy rationale for the decoupling — the Government's position is that statutory redundancy pay compensates for long-service investment in the employment relationship and is not appropriate for employees with short service. The unfair dismissal right protects against arbitrary or unfair dismissal regardless of service length (from 6 months). These are different rights serving different purposes — the decoupling reflects this distinction

sections.title: Amount of a redundancy payment
sections.summary: The amount of a statutory redundancy payment is calculated using the SAME formula as the basic award for unfair dismissal (s.119): age bands × complete years of continuous service (maximum 20) × capped weekly pay (s.227). Three age bands: 0.5 weeks' pay per year for service when the employee was under 22; 1 week's pay per year for service when aged 22–40; 1.5 weeks' pay per year for service when aged 41 or over. The weekly pay is subject to the s.227 cap (£751 from 6 April 2026, per SI 2026/310). Maximum statutory redundancy payment: £22,530 (30 × £751). The employer must provide a written statement showing how the redundancy payment has been calculated (s.165). The statutory redundancy payment is tax-free up to £30,000 (ITEPA 2003 s.403).
sections.text:
(1) The amount of a redundancy payment shall be calculated by—

(a) determining the period, ending with the relevant date, during which the employee has been continuously employed,

(b) reckoning backwards from the end of that period the number of years of employment falling within that period, and

(c) allowing the appropriate amount for each of those years of employment.

(2) In subsection (1)(c) "the appropriate amount" means—

(a) one and a half weeks' pay for a year of employment in which the employee was not below the age of forty-one,

(b) one week's pay for a year of employment (not within paragraph (a)) in which he was not below the age of twenty-two, and

(c) half a week's pay for a year of employment not within paragraph (a) or (b).

(3) Where twenty years of employment have been reckoned under subsection (1), no account shall be taken under that subsection of any year of employment earlier than those twenty years.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 162 uses IDENTICAL wording to s.119 (basic award). The formula, age bands, service cap, and weekly pay cap are the same. The only differences are: (1) the qualifying period — s.155 requires 2 years for redundancy pay, s.108 requires 6 months (from January 2027) for the basic award; (2) the calculation date — s.162 uses the 'relevant date' (s.145), s.119 uses the 'effective date of termination' (s.97); (3) where both a redundancy payment and a basic award are due, the redundancy payment is SET OFF against the basic award (s.122(4)), preventing double recovery.

dynamicLimits.instruction: The weekly pay cap that governs this calculation changes every 6 April. Eileen MUST cross-reference the s.227 limits table (or the current Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order) before quoting any figure. The relevant date of the redundancy determines which year's cap applies.
dynamicLimits.crossReference: See s.227 for the authoritative limits table with full history.

formula.identicalTo: s.119 (basic award for unfair dismissal) — see s.119 for the complete worked example and step-by-step methodology
formula.step1: Determine the period of continuous employment ending with the relevant date (s.145)
formula.step2: Count backwards from the relevant date — reckon complete years of employment (maximum 20)
formula.step3: For each year, apply the age-band multiplier: 1.5 (aged 41+), 1.0 (aged 22–40), 0.5 (under 22)
formula.step4: Apply the s.227 weekly pay cap (£751 from 6 April 2026)
formula.step5: Multiply total weeks × capped weekly pay = statutory redundancy payment
formula.maximumCalculation: 30 weeks × £751 = £22,530 (from 6 April 2026) — achieved by an employee aged 61+ with 20+ years' service

writtenStatement.obligation: The employer must provide the employee with a written statement indicating how the amount of the redundancy payment has been calculated. This should show: the employee's relevant date, their length of continuous service, the years counted (including the age band for each year), the weekly pay figure used (and whether the statutory cap applied), and the total redundancy payment.
writtenStatement.practicalNote: Failure to provide the written statement is not a separate claim — but the absence of a transparent calculation increases the risk of disputes and undermines the employer's credibility if the calculation is challenged.

taxTreatment.principle: The first £30,000 of a statutory or contractual redundancy payment (and any other termination payment that is not otherwise taxable) is exempt from income tax. Amounts above £30,000 are taxable. The £30,000 exemption applies to the TOTAL termination payment — not to each individual element. Where the employee receives both a statutory redundancy payment and a contractual enhancement, both are aggregated for the purposes of the £30,000 threshold.
taxTreatment.nic: Statutory and contractual redundancy payments within the £30,000 threshold are also exempt from employee National Insurance contributions. Employer NICs apply to termination payments above £30,000 (since 6 April 2020).
taxTreatment.interaction: The statutory redundancy payment (calculated under s.162) is always within the £30,000 threshold — the maximum statutory payment (£22,530 from April 2026) is well below £30,000. The tax issue arises where the employer pays an enhanced/contractual redundancy payment on top of the statutory amount — the combined total may exceed £30,000.

versions.text: [The core formula in s.162(1)–(3) has not been amended since the 1996 Act. The upper age limit (s.162(4)–(5)) was repealed by the Employment Equality (Age) Regulations 2006 (SI 2006/1031). The weekly pay cap is updated annually by the Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order.]
versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; s.162(4)–(5) repealed by SI 2006/1031 (age discrimination); amounts updated annually by statutory instrument
versions.policyRationale: The redundancy payment formula has been in place since the Redundancy Payments Act 1965. The formula was designed to provide progressively higher compensation for older, longer-serving employees — reflecting the greater difficulty they were expected to face in finding new employment. The age-banded structure survived age discrimination legislation because it is exempted under Schedule 9 para.13 of the Equality Act 2010.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does NOT amend s.162. The redundancy payment formula is UNCHANGED. The weekly pay cap will continue to be updated annually by the Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order. The s.155 qualifying period (2 years) is also unchanged. The only ERA 2025 change that affects the redundancy payment landscape is the decoupling of the unfair dismissal qualifying period (6 months) from the redundancy payment qualifying period (2 years) — see s.155.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW for the formula itself (unchanged), but the decoupling creates the new 6–24 month category where employees can claim unfair dismissal but not redundancy pay.

refs: ERA 1996 s.162 (this section — amount of redundancy payment)
ERA 1996 s.119 (basic award — IDENTICAL formula)
ERA 1996 s.135 (right to redundancy payment — the entitlement provision)
ERA 1996 s.145 (the 'relevant date' — the date from which the calculation runs)
ERA 1996 s.155 (qualifying period — 2 years, UNCHANGED by ERA 2025)
ERA 1996 ss.220–226 (calculation of a week's pay — methodology)
ERA 1996 s.227 (maximum amount of a week's pay — £751 from 6 April 2026)
ERA 1996 s.165 (written statement of calculation — employer obligation)
ERA 1996 s.122(4) (redundancy payment set off against basic award — prevents double recovery)
ITEPA 2003 s.403 (tax treatment — first £30,000 tax-free)
EqA 2010 Sch.9 para.13 (exemption for age-banded redundancy payments)
Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order 2026 (SI 2026/310) — current limits

leadingCases.name: Agard v Westminster Kingsway College
leadingCases.principle: For a term-time only worker, the week's pay for redundancy payment purposes is calculated by dividing the annual salary by the number of weeks worked PLUS statutory annual leave entitlement — not by the total number of weeks in the year or the number of weeks worked alone.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the correct method for calculating a week's pay for a term-time only worker was to divide the annual salary by the number of weeks worked plus the statutory annual leave entitlement. This ensures the calculation reflects the employee's actual working pattern.
leadingCases.significance: Important for employers with term-time only, seasonal, or variable-hours workers. The weekly pay calculation must reflect the actual working pattern — not simply divide annual salary by 52 weeks. Getting this wrong is one of the most common calculation errors.

leadingCases.name: MacCulloch v Imperial Chemical Industries plc
leadingCases.principle: Enhanced redundancy schemes that provide higher payments for older employees (mirroring the statutory age-banded formula) are not automatically age discriminatory. Such schemes may be objectively justified as a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim — namely, cushioning the impact of job loss for employees closer to retirement who may face greater difficulty in finding new employment.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that an enhanced redundancy scheme that provided higher payments to older workers, broadly following the statutory formula but with enhanced multipliers, was capable of being objectively justified under age discrimination legislation.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms that age-banded redundancy payments (both statutory and contractual) can survive age discrimination challenge — provided they are proportionate. The statutory formula is specifically exempted by EqA 2010 Sch.9 para.13. Enhanced schemes that mirror the statutory structure are likely to be justified.

leadingCases.name: Bowers v Secretary of State for Employment
leadingCases.principle: Where the employer is insolvent and the employee applies to the Secretary of State for payment from the National Insurance Fund (s.166), the calculation must use the weekly pay cap and service limits applicable at the relevant date — not the date of the NIF application.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the NIF payment is calculated using the same formula and limits that would have applied had the employer been solvent and paid the statutory redundancy payment. The relevant date determines the applicable cap.
leadingCases.significance: Ensures consistency between employer-paid and NIF-paid redundancy payments. The calculation is the same regardless of who pays.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Calculate your statutory redundancy pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The official GOV.UK calculator — applies the formula automatically with current limits.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on statutory and contractual redundancy pay calculation.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Termination payments and tax
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: HMRC guidance on the tax treatment of redundancy payments including the £30,000 exemption.

commonErrors: Assuming the redundancy payment formula is different from the basic award formula — s.162 and s.119 use IDENTICAL wording. The same age bands, the same 20-year service cap, the same weekly pay cap (s.227). The only differences are: the qualifying period (2 years for redundancy pay vs 6 months for basic award from January 2027), the calculation date (relevant date vs EDT), and the set-off under s.122(4). ERA 2025 does NOT amend s.162 — the formula is unchanged

Using the wrong year's weekly pay cap — the s.227 cap changes every 6 April. The RELEVANT DATE determines which year's cap applies. From 6 April 2026: £751/week, maximum £22,530. From 6 April 2025: £719/week, maximum £21,570. An employee made redundant on 5 April 2026 uses the 2025/26 cap; an employee made redundant on 6 April 2026 uses the 2026/27 cap

Including partial years of service in the calculation — only COMPLETE years count. Nine years and eleven months counts as nine years. The final partial year is disregarded. This can make a significant difference where the employee is close to a service anniversary

Calculating the week's pay incorrectly for variable-hours or term-time workers — for employees with no normal working hours, the week's pay is the average over the 12-week reference period (s.224). For term-time workers, Agard v Westminster Kingsway [2011] establishes the correct method: annual salary divided by weeks worked plus statutory annual leave. Dividing by 52 produces an incorrect (lower) figure

Not providing a written statement of the calculation — s.165 requires the employer to provide a written statement showing how the payment was calculated. While not providing the statement is not a standalone claim, it increases the risk of disputes and undermines the employer's credibility

Assuming the statutory redundancy payment is the employer's total financial obligation — the statutory payment is the MINIMUM. The employer may also owe: notice pay (s.86), accrued holiday pay, any contractual or enhanced redundancy payment, pension entitlements, and (if the dismissal is unfair) the compensatory award. The statutory redundancy payment is one element of the total termination cost

Not understanding the tax treatment — the first £30,000 of the total termination payment (statutory + contractual redundancy + any other non-contractual termination payment) is tax-free under ITEPA 2003 s.403. The statutory maximum (£22,530) is always within the threshold. The tax issue arises when contractual enhancements push the total above £30,000. Employer NICs apply to amounts above £30,000 since 6 April 2020

Not recognising that the age-banded formula survives age discrimination challenge — the statutory formula (higher payments for older employees) is specifically exempted from age discrimination by EqA 2010 Sch.9 para.13. Enhanced schemes that mirror the statutory structure are also likely to be justified (MacCulloch v ICI [2008])

Not applying the s.122(4) set-off where both a redundancy payment and basic award are due — where the employee receives a statutory redundancy payment and subsequently wins an unfair dismissal claim, the redundancy payment must be set off against the basic award. Since both use the same formula, the set-off typically extinguishes the basic award. The employee receives: redundancy payment + compensatory award

Not using the GOV.UK calculator as a cross-check — the official GOV.UK calculator (gov.uk/calculate-your-redundancy-pay) applies the formula automatically with the correct year's limits. Employers should use it as a cross-check against manual calculations, particularly in large-scale redundancies where calculation errors across multiple employees can compound

sections.title: Continuity of employment
sections.summary: Provisions governing how continuous employment is computed. Critical for qualifying periods, notice, redundancy, and unfair dismissal.
sections.text:
(1) References in any provision of this Act to a period of continuous employment are to a period computed in accordance with this Chapter.

(2) In any provision of this Act which refers to a period of continuous employment expressed in months or years—
(a) a month means a calendar month, and
(b) a year means a year of twelve calendar months.

(3) In computing an employee's period of continuous employment for the purposes of any provision of this Act, any question whether the employee's employment is of a kind counting towards a period of continuous employment shall be determined week by week.
refs: s.212 ERA 1996 (Weeks Counting)
s.216 ERA 1996 (Strikes)
s.218 ERA 1996 (Change of Employer)

sections.title: Weeks counting in computing period
sections.summary: A week counts towards continuous employment if the employee's relations with the employer are governed by a contract of employment throughout the week.
sections.text:
(1) Any week during the whole or part of which an employee's relations with his employer are governed by a contract of employment counts in computing the employee's period of continuous employment.

(3) Subject to subsection (4), any week during the whole or part of which an employee is—
(a) incapable of work in consequence of sickness or injury,
(b) absent from work on account of a temporary cessation of work, or
(c) absent from work in circumstances such that, by arrangement or custom, he is regarded as continuing in the employment of his employer for any purpose,
counts in computing the employee's period of continuous employment.
refs: s.210 ERA 1996 (Continuity)
Ford v Warwickshire County Council [1983] HL (Temporary Cessation)

sections.title: Limits on amount of a week's pay
sections.summary: The amount of a week's pay for the purposes of calculating the basic award (s.119), statutory redundancy payment (s.162), and various other statutory calculations shall not exceed the prescribed limit. The limit is reviewed annually by the Secretary of State in line with RPI under s.34 of the Employment Relations Act 1999. The current limit is £719 (from 6 April 2025, per SI 2025/348). From 6 April 2026 (SI 2026/310), the limit increases to £751. This provision is the ANCHOR for all annual limit changes — every year, the Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order adjusts this figure and the downstream calculations (maximum basic award, maximum statutory redundancy payment, minimum basic award for auto-unfair dismissals, and maximum compensatory award) follow from it.
sections.text:
(1) The amount of a week's pay calculated in accordance with this Chapter shall not exceed [the amount specified in this subsection].

[The amount is prescribed by the Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order — currently £751 from 6 April 2026 (SI 2026/310)]
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 227 sets the CAP on a week's pay for statutory calculation purposes. The actual week's pay is calculated under ss.220–226 (normal working hours, variable pay, no normal hours, etc.) — but the result is then CAPPED at the s.227 limit. This cap affects: the basic award for unfair dismissal (s.119), the statutory redundancy payment (s.162), the s.93 award for failure to provide written reasons (s.93), the additional award for non-compliance with reinstatement/re-engagement orders (s.117), and various other provisions. The cap does NOT apply to: the compensatory award for unfair dismissal (s.123 — which uses ACTUAL weekly pay, not capped), wrongful dismissal damages (contractual claim — actual pay), or discrimination compensation (uncapped).

dynamicLimits.instruction: Section 227 is the AUTHORITATIVE provision for the weekly pay cap. Eileen MUST cross-reference the current year's Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order before quoting any figure. The limits change every 6 April. If the live limits table has not been updated for the current year, Eileen must inform the user that the figures may be from the previous year and recommend checking the latest Order.
dynamicLimits.annualSource: Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order — published annually by the Secretary of State under s.34 of the Employment Relations Act 1999, linked to September RPI, typically published in February/March, taking effect from 6 April

limitsTable.note: Complete limits table. Eileen should use the row corresponding to the effective date of termination (EDT) — the limits that were in force on the EDT determine the calculation.

years.notes: The LAST increase before ERA 2025 removes the compensatory award cap on 1 January 2027. From that date, the compensatory award will be UNCAPPED — but the weekly pay cap (s.227) and the basic award/redundancy payment formula will continue to use the annual limit.

annualReviewMechanism.mechanism: Under s.34(2), if the Retail Prices Index (RPI) for September of a year is higher or lower than the index for the previous September, the Secretary of State MUST change the specified sums by the same percentage (rounded up or down as specified in s.34(3)). This is a MANDATORY annual adjustment — the Secretary of State has no discretion to freeze or skip the increase. The adjustment is made by statutory instrument (the Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order), typically published in February/March and taking effect from 6 April.
annualReviewMechanism.practicalNote: The RPI-linkage means the limits increase automatically with inflation. The September-to-September RPI comparison determines the percentage change. The Order is published several weeks before the 6 April effective date, giving employers time to update their systems.

cappedByS227: Basic award for unfair dismissal (s.119) — uses capped weekly pay
Statutory redundancy payment (s.162) — uses capped weekly pay (same formula as basic award)
Award for failure to provide written reasons (s.93) — 2 weeks × capped weekly pay
Additional award for non-compliance with reinstatement/re-engagement order (s.117)
Minimum basic award for auto-unfair dismissals (s.120) — set by annual Order
Guarantee payment (s.28) — daily rate set by annual Order
Various other statutory calculations under Part XIV Chapter II
notCappedByS227: Compensatory award for unfair dismissal (s.123) — uses ACTUAL weekly pay, subject to separate compensatory cap in s.124 (which is being REMOVED by ERA 2025 from 1 January 2027)
Wrongful dismissal damages — contractual claim, uses actual contractual pay with no statutory cap
Discrimination compensation (EqA 2010) — uncapped, uses actual loss
Notice pay — based on actual contractual/statutory pay

weeklyPayCalculation.methodology: The week's pay is calculated under ss.220–226 BEFORE the s.227 cap is applied. The methodology depends on the employee's working pattern: (1) s.221 — employee with normal working hours and fixed pay: the amount payable for normal working hours; (2) s.222 — employee with normal working hours and variable pay: the average over the 12-week reference period (or 52 weeks for holiday pay under the Employment Rights (Employment Particulars and Paid Annual Leave) (Amendment) Regulations 2018); (3) s.224 — employee with no normal working hours: the average weekly pay over the 12-week reference period. Only AFTER the calculation under ss.220–226 is the s.227 cap applied.
weeklyPayCalculation.calculationDate: The calculation date depends on the context: for unfair dismissal, it is the EDT (or the date notice was given, if notice was given); for redundancy, it is the relevant date under s.226.

versions.text: [s.227 has been in place since the 1996 Act was enacted. The specific amount is updated annually by the Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order. The annual review mechanism was formalised by the Employment Relations Act 1999 s.34 (linking the adjustment to RPI). The figure has increased every year — from £210 in 1996 to £751 in 2026.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted; amount updated annually by statutory instrument
versions.policyRationale: The weekly pay cap ensures that the basic award and statutory redundancy payment remain proportionate and affordable. Without the cap, high earners would receive very large basic awards/redundancy payments. The cap keeps the formulaic elements of compensation at a level that reflects the statutory minimum, not the employee's full earnings.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 REMOVES the compensatory award cap (s.124(1)) from 1 January 2027. However, the s.227 weekly pay cap is NOT removed — it continues to apply to the basic award (s.119), statutory redundancy payment (s.162), and other formulaic calculations. The practical effect: from 1 January 2027, the compensatory award will be uncapped (based on ACTUAL loss), but the basic award and redundancy payment will continue to use the capped weekly pay figure. The annual Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order will continue to update the s.227 cap each April.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: HIGH — the 2026/27 Order (SI 2026/310) is the LAST Order that will include a compensatory award cap figure. From 1 January 2027, the compensatory award line will no longer be relevant (uncapped). But the weekly pay cap, basic award maximum, and minimum basic award will continue to be updated annually.

refs: ERA 1996 s.227 (this section — maximum amount of a week's pay)
ERA 1996 ss.220–226 (calculation of a week's pay — the methodology before the cap is applied)
ERA 1996 s.119 (basic award — uses capped weekly pay)
ERA 1996 s.162 (statutory redundancy payment — uses capped weekly pay)
ERA 1996 s.93 (written reasons award — 2 weeks × capped weekly pay)
ERA 1996 s.117 (additional award — uses capped weekly pay)
ERA 1996 s.120 (minimum basic award for auto-unfair — set by annual Order)
ERA 1996 s.123 (compensatory award — uses ACTUAL pay, NOT capped by s.227)
ERA 1996 s.124 (compensatory award cap — separate cap, REMOVED by ERA 2025 from 1 January 2027)
Employment Relations Act 1999 s.34 (annual review mechanism — RPI-linked, mandatory adjustment)
Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order 2026 (SI 2026/310) — in force 6 April 2026
Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order 2025 (SI 2025/348) — in force 6 April 2025

leadingCases.name: Boorman v Allmakes Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The s.227 cap applies to the basic award and statutory redundancy payment calculations, but does NOT cap the compensatory award under s.123. The compensatory award is based on the employee's ACTUAL loss, calculated using actual earnings (subject to the separate compensatory cap in s.124, which ERA 2025 is removing).
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed the distinction between the s.227 cap (which applies to the formulaic calculations) and the compensatory award (which is based on actual loss). A high earner who is unfairly dismissed receives a basic award capped at s.227 limits, but a compensatory award based on their actual (uncapped) earnings.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the critical distinction between capped (basic award) and uncapped (compensatory award) elements of unfair dismissal compensation. This distinction becomes even more significant from 1 January 2027 when the compensatory cap is removed entirely.

leadingCases.name: Paggetti v Cobb
leadingCases.principle: The calculation date for the week's pay determines which year's cap applies. The calculation date for unfair dismissal is the EDT (or the date notice was given). An employee dismissed on 5 April uses the previous year's cap; an employee dismissed on 6 April uses the new year's cap.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the relevant date for determining which weekly pay cap applies is the calculation date — which depends on the context (EDT for unfair dismissal, relevant date for redundancy). This prevents any ambiguity about which year's limits apply.
leadingCases.significance: Practical guidance on which year's cap applies. Important for dismissals around the 6 April boundary — the EDT determines whether the old or new year's cap is used.

leadingCases.name: Evans v Malley Organisation Ltd (t/a First Business Support)
leadingCases.principle: Where an employee's pay varies, the week's pay is calculated by averaging over the 12-week reference period under s.222/s.224. Only AFTER the average is calculated is the s.227 cap applied. The cap applies to the final figure, not to each individual week in the reference period.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the s.227 cap is applied to the result of the ss.220–226 calculation, not to the individual components. For variable-pay employees, the average weekly pay is calculated first, then capped.
leadingCases.significance: Clarifies the sequencing: calculate the week's pay under ss.220–226, THEN apply the s.227 cap. Important for employees with variable pay, overtime, or commission.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Calculate your statutory redundancy pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The GOV.UK calculator automatically applies the current year's s.227 cap.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Limits on employment tribunal compensation
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Annual publication of the current limits, including the s.227 weekly pay cap, compensatory award cap, and minimum basic award.

commonErrors: Using the wrong year's limits — the s.227 cap changes every 6 April. The EDT determines which year's cap applies. An employee dismissed on 5 April 2026 uses the 2025/26 cap (£719). An employee dismissed on 6 April 2026 uses the 2026/27 cap (£751). Using the wrong year's figure produces an incorrect calculation (Paggetti v Cobb [2002])

Not knowing the CURRENT limits — the 2026/27 limits (from 6 April 2026, SI 2026/310) are: weekly pay cap £751; maximum basic award £22,530; maximum statutory redundancy pay £22,530; minimum basic award for auto-unfair £9,157; maximum compensatory award £123,543. These replace the 2025/26 limits (£719, £21,570, £21,570, £8,763, £118,223)

Assuming the s.227 cap applies to the compensatory award — the s.227 cap applies to the BASIC AWARD (s.119) and STATUTORY REDUNDANCY PAYMENT (s.162). It does NOT cap the compensatory award (s.123), which is based on ACTUAL weekly pay subject to the separate s.124 compensatory cap. From 1 January 2027, the compensatory cap is REMOVED entirely — making the distinction even more important (Boorman v Allmakes [1995])

Applying the cap to each week individually for variable-pay employees — for employees with variable pay, the week's pay is calculated by taking the average over the reference period under ss.222/224. The s.227 cap is then applied to the RESULT, not to each individual week. Calculate first, cap second (Evans v Malley [2003])

Not understanding that the annual review is MANDATORY — the Secretary of State has no discretion to freeze or skip the annual adjustment. Under Employment Relations Act 1999 s.34, if the September RPI has changed, the limits MUST be adjusted by the same percentage. The adjustment is made by statutory instrument, typically published in February/March for 6 April commencement

Confusing the s.227 cap (weekly pay for formulaic calculations) with the s.124 cap (compensatory award maximum) — these are DIFFERENT caps that serve different purposes. s.227 caps the week's pay used in formulas (basic award, redundancy). s.124 caps the total compensatory award. s.227 will continue to apply after ERA 2025. s.124 is being REMOVED by ERA 2025 from 1 January 2027

Not recognising that the 2026/27 compensatory cap (£123,543) is the LAST — ERA 2025 removes the compensatory award cap from 1 January 2027. The 2026/27 Order is the final Order that includes a compensatory award cap figure. From 1 January 2027, the compensatory award will be uncapped. But the weekly pay cap, basic award maximum, and minimum basic award will continue to be updated annually

Not updating internal systems when the new Order takes effect — HR and payroll systems, redundancy calculators, settlement models, and risk assessment tools must be updated each April to reflect the new limits. Using outdated limits in settlement negotiations or tribunal calculations produces incorrect figures

Not recognising that the cap does NOT apply to wrongful dismissal or discrimination — wrongful dismissal is a contractual claim based on actual pay (no statutory cap). Discrimination compensation under EqA 2010 is uncapped and based on actual loss. The s.227 cap only applies to the statutory formulaic calculations

Assuming the limits only matter for tribunal claims — the s.227 cap also affects: statutory redundancy payments (which employers must pay regardless of whether a claim is brought), the s.93 written reasons award, guarantee payments, and the additional award for non-compliance with reinstatement orders. The cap has practical consequences beyond litigation

Not providing Eileen with the current limits — this provision is the ANCHOR for the dynamicLimits architecture. Eileen must always cross-reference the current year's Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order before quoting any figure. If the live limits table has not been updated, Eileen must flag the staleness to the user

sections.title: Employees, workers etc.
sections.summary: The definitional provision for the entire Employment Rights Act 1996. Defines 'employee' (s.230(1)–(2)), 'worker' (s.230(3)), and 'employer' (s.230(4)–(5)). The distinction between employee and worker determines which statutory rights are available: employees enjoy the full range of ERA rights (unfair dismissal, redundancy, minimum notice, statement of particulars), while workers have a more limited set (wages protection, whistleblowing detriment, working time, national minimum wage). Individuals who are genuinely self-employed and in business on their own account fall outside both definitions. The classification question — employee, worker, or self-employed — has been the subject of the most significant Supreme Court litigation in employment law in the last decade, including Uber BV v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5.
sections.text:
(1) In this Act "employee" means an individual who has entered into or works under (or, where the employment has ceased, worked under) a contract of employment.

(2) In this Act "contract of employment" means a contract of service or apprenticeship, whether express or implied, and (if it is express) whether oral or in writing.

(3) In this Act "worker" (except in the phrases "shop worker" and "betting worker") means an individual who has entered into or works under (or, where the employment has ceased, worked under)—

(a) a contract of employment, or

(b) any other contract, whether express or implied and (if it is express) whether oral or in writing, whereby the individual undertakes to do or perform personally any work or services for another party to the contract whose status is not by virtue of the contract that of a client or customer of any profession or business undertaking carried on by the individual,

and any reference to a worker's contract shall be construed accordingly.

(4) In this Act "employer", in relation to an employee or a worker, means the person by whom the employee or worker is (or, where the employment has ceased, was) employed.

(5) In this Act "employment"—

(a) in relation to an employee, means (except for the purposes of section 171) employment under his contract of employment, and

(b) in relation to a worker, means employment under his contract;

and "employed" shall be construed accordingly.

(6) This section has effect subject to sections 43K and 49B(10); and for the purposes of Part XIII so far as relating to redundancy payments, references to an employee shall be read as references to an employee within the meaning of that Part.

(7) Parts 1 and 2 of the Children and Families Act 2014, where they apply, have effect for the purposes of the definition of "employee" in subsection (1).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 230 creates a three-tier classification: employee (highest level of protection), worker (intermediate protection), and self-employed (no ERA protection). Every right in the ERA depends on which category the individual falls into. The question of classification is determined by the REALITY of the relationship, not by the contractual label — a contract that describes someone as 'self-employed' may be disregarded if the reality is that they are an employee or worker (Autoclenz v Belcher [2011] UKSC 41). The Supreme Court in Uber v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5 confirmed that the statutory definition of 'worker' must be interpreted purposively — in light of the protective purpose of the legislation — and that the tribunal must look at what the individual actually does, not what the contract says they do.

statusFramework.note: The three-category framework is the analytical structure for all employment status questions. Each category carries different statutory rights.

employee.definition: An individual who has entered into or works under a contract of employment — meaning a contract of service or apprenticeship (s.230(1)–(2)).
employee.test: The common law test for employee status has three essential elements: (1) mutuality of obligation — the employer must be obliged to provide work and the employee must be obliged to do it; (2) personal service — the individual must perform the work personally and cannot send a substitute; (3) control — the employer must exercise a sufficient degree of control over the individual. All three must be present. Additional factors (provision of equipment, financial risk, tax treatment, integration into the business) may be relevant but are not determinative.
employee.testSource: Ready Mixed Concrete (South East) Ltd v Minister of Pensions and National Insurance [1968] 2 QB 497 (QBD); Nethermere (St Neots) Ltd v Gardiner [1984] ICR 612 (CA)
employee.rightsInclude: Unfair dismissal (s.94), redundancy payments (Part XI), minimum notice (s.86), statement of particulars (s.1), time off rights (Part VI), maternity/paternity/adoption leave (Part VIII), flexible working (s.80F), plus all worker rights

worker.definition: An individual who works under a contract of employment (s.230(3)(a)) OR any other contract to perform work or services personally for another party whose status is not that of a client or customer (s.230(3)(b)).
worker.limbBTest: The 'limb (b) worker' category catches individuals who are not employees but who personally perform work for another party and are not genuinely in business on their own account. The key question is whether the other party is a 'client or customer' of the individual's own business — if so, the individual is self-employed; if not, the individual is a worker.
worker.limbBSource: Uber BV v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5; Pimlico Plumbers Ltd v Smith [2018] UKSC 29; Clyde & Co LLP v Bates van Winkelhof [2014] UKSC 32
worker.rightsInclude: Wages protection (Part II — s.13), whistleblowing detriment (s.47B), working time (WTR 1998), national minimum wage (NMWA 1998), holiday pay, discrimination protection (EqA 2010), but NOT unfair dismissal, NOT redundancy payments, NOT minimum notice

selfEmployed.definition: An individual who is in business on their own account and provides services to clients or customers. Falls outside both the employee and worker definitions.
selfEmployed.indicatorsOfSelfEmployment: Genuine ability to send a substitute without restriction, financial risk borne by the individual, provision of own equipment, ability to profit from sound management, ability to work for multiple clients simultaneously, no mutuality of obligation, control over how and when work is done, invoicing for services, registration for VAT, self-assessment tax return
selfEmployed.rightsInclude: No ERA rights. No unfair dismissal, no redundancy, no wages protection, no working time rights. May have some health and safety protections. May have discrimination protection if personally performing work (EqA 2010 s.83(2)).

versions.text:
(1) In this Act "employee" means an individual who has entered into or works under (or, where the employment has ceased, worked under) a contract of employment.

(2) In this Act "contract of employment" means a contract of service or apprenticeship, whether express or implied, and (if it is express) whether oral or in writing.

(3) In this Act "worker" (except in the phrases "shop worker" and "betting worker") means an individual who has entered into or works under (or, where the employment has ceased, worked under)—

(a) a contract of employment, or

(b) any other contract, whether express or implied and (if it is express) whether oral or in writing, whereby the individual undertakes to do or perform personally any work or services for another party to the contract whose status is not by virtue of the contract that of a client or customer of any profession or business undertaking carried on by the individual,

and any reference to a worker's contract shall be construed accordingly.

(4) In this Act "employer", in relation to an employee or a worker, means the person by whom the employee or worker is (or, where the employment has ceased, was) employed.

(5) In this Act "employment"—

(a) in relation to an employee, means (except for the purposes of section 171) employment under his contract of employment, and

(b) in relation to a worker, means employment under his contract;

and "employed" shall be construed accordingly.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 1996 as originally enacted
versions.policyRationale: The 1996 Act consolidated the definitions from EPCA 1978. The three-tier framework (employee, worker, self-employed) was established. The worker definition in s.230(3)(b) was carried forward from the Wages Act 1986 and Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992. At this stage, the worker category had limited practical significance — it would become critically important only with the introduction of the National Minimum Wage Act 1998 and Working Time Regulations 1998.

versions.text: [As above, with the insertion of s.230(6) by the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 s.15(1): 'This section has effect subject to sections 43K and 49B(10); and for the purposes of Part XIII so far as relating to redundancy payments, references to an employee shall be read as references to an employee within the meaning of that Part.']
versions.amendedBy: Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (c.23) s.15(1)
versions.policyRationale: PIDA 1998 inserted s.230(6) to ensure that the extended worker definition in s.43K (which covers certain agency workers and NHS workers for whistleblowing purposes) takes effect. This expanded the scope of whistleblowing protection beyond the standard s.230(3) worker definition.

versions.text: [Current version — s.230(6) words substituted by Small Business, Enterprise and Employment Act 2015 s.149(3), and s.230(7) inserted by Children and Families Act 2014 s.139(6), Sch.7 para.41.]
versions.amendedBy: Small Business, Enterprise and Employment Act 2015 (c.26) s.149(3); Children and Families Act 2014 (c.6) s.139(6)
versions.policyRationale: Minor technical amendments. The SBEE Act 2015 updated the cross-reference in s.230(6). The Children and Families Act 2014 inserted s.230(7) to ensure that the employee definition interacts correctly with the shared parental leave and pay provisions in Parts 1 and 2 of that Act.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend the definitions in s.230(1)–(3). However, several ERA 2025 provisions significantly affect employment status in practice: (1) the zero-hours and low-hours contract provisions will create new rights for workers on such contracts, increasing the practical significance of worker status; (2) the Government has committed to consulting on a single status of 'worker' to simplify the three-tier framework, though this is NOT in ERA 2025 itself and remains a longer-term policy ambition; (3) ERA 2025 extends various rights that were previously employee-only to workers, further narrowing the gap between the two categories.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: ERA 2025 zero-hours provisions not yet in force as at March 2026. Single status consultation not yet commenced.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE to HIGH — the practical significance of the employee/worker distinction will change as more rights are extended to workers. Employers relying on the classification of individuals as workers rather than employees to avoid unfair dismissal liability will face increased scrutiny.
pendingAmendment.singleStatusProposal: The Government's Make Work Pay plan committed to moving towards a single status of 'worker' that would replace the current three-tier framework. This would potentially extend unfair dismissal rights to all workers, not just employees. However, this is a long-term ambition requiring primary legislation beyond ERA 2025 and has not yet been consulted upon.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 does not change the definitions in s.230. The three-tier framework (employee/worker/self-employed) remains in force. However, the Government's stated policy direction is towards a simpler two-tier framework (worker/self-employed), which would extend employee-level protections to all workers. This would be a fundamental change requiring further primary legislation. Employers should monitor the consultation on single status closely — if implemented, it would transform the exposure landscape for every business using worker or contractor arrangements.
refs: ERA 1996 s.230(1)–(2) (employee = individual under a contract of employment = contract of service)
ERA 1996 s.230(3)(a) (worker includes employees)
ERA 1996 s.230(3)(b) (worker includes individuals personally performing work who are not genuinely self-employed — 'limb (b) workers')
ERA 1996 s.230(4)–(5) (employer, employment — defined by reference to the individual's status)
ERA 1996 s.43K (extended worker definition for whistleblowing purposes — agency workers, NHS)
ERA 1996 s.94 (right not to be unfairly dismissed — employees only)
ERA 1996 s.13 (wages protection — workers and employees)
